- Ō£ģ **0-D:** All 3 in nanoscale. Examples: **Quantum dots, CŌéåŌéĆ, gold nanoparticles**. Used in LEDs, bio-imaging.
- Ō£ģ **1-D:** 2 in nanoscale, 1 long. Examples: **CNT, Si nanowires, Ag nanorods**. Aspect ratio is critical.
- Ō£ģ **2-D:** 1 in nanoscale, 2 large. Examples: **Graphene, MoSŌéé, thin films**. Used in flexible electronics.
- Ō£ģ **3-D:** Bulk, built from nano-units. Examples: **Nanocomposites, aerogels, nanocrystalline metals**. No quantum confinement.
- Ō£ģ **SA:V ratios:** Sphere `3/r`, cylinder `2/r`, thin film `1/t`.
- Ō£ģ **SSA formula (sphere):** `SSA = 6/(Žüd)`. Always carry units.
- Ō£ģ **Surface atom fraction (FCC sphere):** `f = 3a/(4r)`.
- Ō£ģ **Density of states scaling:** `g(E) ŌłØ E^(d/2 - 1)` for `d = 0, 1, 2, 3`.
- Ō£ģ **Quantum confinement:** 0-D has 3-D confinement; 1-D has 2-D confinement; 2-D has 1-D confinement.
- Ō£ģ **Originators:** Gleiter (1995) for nanostructured materials; Siegel & Thomas for dimensional classification.
- Ō£ģ **Key takeaway:** The smaller the size, the higher the SA:V, the more surface atoms, the more active the material ŌĆö this is the *engineer's* reason to care about nanomaterials.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electromagnetic Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Electromagnetic Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 ŌĆö Electromagnetic Spectrum

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electromagnetic Spectrum (EMS):** The electromagnetic spectrum is the continuous range of all possible frequencies (or wavelengths) of electromagnetic radiation, classified into distinct bands based on wavelength, frequency, and photon energy. Mathematically, it spans frequencies from below **3 Hz** (extremely low frequency, ELF) to above **$10^{30}$ Hz** (high-energy gamma rays), corresponding to wavelengths from thousands of kilometers down to sub-atomic scales.

In the context of **Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology (PECHT833)**, the electromagnetic spectrum is critically important because nanomaterials exhibit **size- and shape-dependent interactions** with electromagnetic radiation across this entire spectrum. When a nanostructure's dimensions become comparable to a specific wavelength of light (or a sub-multiple like $\lambda/2$, $\lambda/4$), strong **resonance phenomena** occur ŌĆö these are exploited in plasmonics, photonics, and biomedical imaging.

### 1.2 The WaveŌĆōParticle Duality of EM Radiation

Electromagnetic radiation simultaneously exhibits **wave nature** and **particle nature**:

- **Wave Nature:** Characterized by wavelength ($\lambda$), frequency ($\nu$), and propagation speed ($c$).
- **Particle Nature:** Composed of discrete quanta called **photons**, each carrying energy $E = h\nu$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (commonly approximated as $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s).
> **Planck's Constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Ocean of Light

Imagine standing on the shore of an ocean. You see waves of vastly different sizes ŌĆö from tiny ripples (capillary waves) just millimeters tall, to massive tsunamis hundreds of meters long. Although they are all *ocean waves*, they behave very differently: small ripples get absorbed by a pebble, while giant waves crash past it.

The **electromagnetic spectrum** is exactly this ŌĆö a single "ocean" of transverse oscillating electric ($\vec{E}$) and magnetic ($\vec{B}$) fields, but the "wave sizes" (wavelengths) vary by more than **20 orders of magnitude**:

- **Radio waves** are the gentle, kilometer-long swells that easily bend around buildings.
- **Visible light** (~400ŌĆō700 nm) is the "human-sized" wave we can directly perceive ŌĆö our eyes evolved to detect it because the Sun's peak emission lies here, and most natural nanostructures (cells, molecules) happen to be of comparable size.
- **X-rays and gamma rays** are violent, subatomic-scale ripples that smash through matter.

**Why is this relevant to nanotechnology?** Because when we shrink materials into the **1ŌĆō100 nm range**, we create artificial structures whose dimensions match the wavelength of visible light, infrared, or ultraviolet. These nanostructures essentially become "engineered pebbles" in our ocean ŌĆö and we can tune their size, shape, and composition to selectively interact with specific "wave sizes" of the spectrum. This is the foundation of **nanophotonics**, **plasmonics**, and **metamaterials**.

### 1.4 The Complete Family of EM Bands

The electromagnetic spectrum is conventionally divided into seven (sometimes eight) principal regions. From lowest to highest energy:

| Band | Wavelength Range | Frequency Range | Photon Energy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Radio Waves | $> 1$ m | $< 300$ MHz | $< 1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ eV |
| Microwaves | $1$ mm ŌĆō $1$ m | $300$ MHz ŌĆō $300$ GHz | $1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ ŌĆō $1.24 \times 10^{-3}$ eV |
| Infrared (IR) | $700$ nm ŌĆō $1$ mm | $300$ GHz ŌĆō $430$ THz | $1.24 \times 10^{-3}$ ŌĆō $1.65$ eV |
| Visible Light | $400$ nm ŌĆō $700$ nm | $430$ THz ŌĆō $750$ THz | $1.65$ ŌĆō $3.10$ eV |
| Ultraviolet (UV) | $10$ nm ŌĆō $400$ nm | $750$ THz ŌĆō $30$ PHz | $3.10$ ŌĆō $124$ eV |
| X-rays | $0.01$ nm ŌĆō $10$ nm | $30$ PHz ŌĆō $30$ EHz | $124$ eV ŌĆō $124$ keV |
| Gamma Rays | $< 0.01$ nm | $> 30$ EHz | $> 124$ keV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visible Sub-bands (in order of increasing frequency / decreasing wavelength):**
> Red ŌåÆ Orange ŌåÆ Yellow ŌåÆ Green ŌåÆ Blue ŌåÆ Indigo ŌåÆ Violet (VIBGYOR).
> Red light ($\lambda \approx 700$ nm) carries the lowest visible photon energy; violet ($\lambda \approx 400$ nm) carries the highest.

### 1.5 Boundary Regions (Transition Zones)

Between major bands lie scientifically rich transition regions that often have unique nanomaterial applications:

- **Terahertz (THz) Radiation** ($0.1$ mm ŌĆō $1$ mm; $0.3$ ŌĆō $3$ THz): Sits between microwaves and IR. Used in security imaging and studying molecular vibrations.
- **Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV)** ($10$ nm ŌĆō $121$ nm): Critical for next-generation semiconductor lithography at sub-7 nm nodes.
- **Soft X-rays** ($0.1$ nm ŌĆō $10$ nm): Used in X-ray diffraction (XRD) for characterizing crystal structures of nanomaterials.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundamental Governing Equations

The behavior of the electromagnetic spectrum rests on four Maxwell's equations (in vacuum / source-free form), plus the dispersion relation:

#### (i) Maxwell's Equations (Vacuum, Source-Free)

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$$

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial \vec{B}}{\partial t}$$

$$\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the **permittivity of free space** and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the **permeability of free space**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These four equations *unify* electricity, magnetism, and optics into a single framework. They mathematically *prove* the existence of electromagnetic waves traveling at speed $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$.

#### (ii) Wave Equation for $\vec{E}$ (in vacuum)

Taking the curl of Faraday's law and substituting Amp├©re-Maxwell's law:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

For a plane-wave solution $\vec{E}(z,t) = E_0 \cos(kz - \omega t)\hat{x}$, this yields:

$$\omega^2 = c^2 k^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \lambda \nu$$

### 2.2 The Six Master Relations of the EM Spectrum

Every problem in this topic reduces to these six formulas. Master them.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wave speed | $c = \lambda \nu$ | m/s | Universal in vacuum |
| 2 | Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J (or eV) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs |
| 3 | Wave number | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\nu}{c}$ | m$^{-1}$ | Used heavily in IR/Raman spectroscopy |
| 4 | Momentum | $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda} = \dfrac{E}{c}$ | kg┬Ęm/s | Explains radiation pressure / Compton effect |
| 5 | Energy in eV | $E(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | eV | Quick conversion using $hc \approx 1240$ eV┬Ęnm |
| 6 | Photon flux | $\Phi = \dfrac{P}{h\nu}$ | photons/s | $P$ = optical power in watts |

### 2.3 Engineering Utility Across the Spectrum

The choice of EM band dictates the application:

- **Radio & Microwave:** MRI imaging, radar, telecommunications, wireless charging. Nanomaterial relevance: **metamaterial antennas**, **RF shielding nanocomposites**.
- **Infrared:** Thermal imaging, night vision, fiber-optic communication (1550 nm telecom window). Nanomaterial relevance: **quantum dot IR detectors**, **black-body absorbers** (carbon nanotubes, graphene).
- **Visible:** Displays, LEDs, lasers, solar cells. Nanomaterial relevance: **plasmonic color generation** by gold/silver nanoparticles, **quantum dot displays**.
- **UV:** Sterilization, photolithography, water purification. Nanomaterial relevance: **ZnO/TiO$_2$ photocatalytic nanoparticles**.
- **X-ray:** Medical imaging, crystallography. Nanomaterial relevance: **XRD characterization**, **gold nanoshells** for cancer therapy.
- **Gamma:** Sterilization, radiotherapy. Nanomaterial relevance: **radio-labeled nanoparticles** for theranostics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Rule for Nanomaterial Applications:** The nanomaterials feature size $d$ should satisfy $d \sim \lambda/n$ (where $n$ is the medium refractive index) for maximum light-matter coupling via localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). For visible light in water ($n \approx 1.33$): $d \approx 300$ŌĆō$525$ nm. This is why noble-metal nanoparticles in the 10ŌĆō100 nm range are central to visible-light plasmonics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö From Maxwell's Equations to $c = \lambda \nu$

This is a foundational derivation for KTU. Each step is written out fully.

**Step 1:** Take the curl of Faraday's law (Equation 3 above):

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 2:** Apply the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$:

$$\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 3:** Use Gauss's law for electricity in vacuum ($\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$), so the first term vanishes:

$$-\nabla^2 \vec{E} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$$

**Step 4:** Substitute Amp├©re-Maxwell's law for the RHS curl:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}\right)$$

**Step 5:** Since $\mu_0$ and $\varepsilon_0$ are constants in vacuum:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \frac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with the standard wave equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{v^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$. The wave speed is therefore:

$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}$$

**Step 7:** Plug in $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m:

$$\begin{aligned}
c &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(8.854 \times 10^{-12})}} \\
&= \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.112 \times 10^{-17}}} \\
&= \frac{1}{3.336 \times 10^{-9}} \\
&\approx 2.998 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8:** For any 1-D harmonic plane wave $E(z,t) = E_0 \cos(kz - \omega t + \phi)$, the spatial periodicity is the wavelength $\lambda$ (so $k = 2\pi/\lambda$) and the temporal periodicity is the period $T = 1/\nu$ (so $\omega = 2\pi\nu$). Substitution into the wave equation forces:

$$\omega = ck \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2\pi\nu = c \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{c = \lambda \nu}$$

This is the most fundamental relation of the electromagnetic spectrum.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö The EnergyŌĆōWavelength Conversion $E = hc/\lambda$

**Step 1:** Start from the PlanckŌĆōEinstein relation for a photon:

$$E = h\nu$$

**Step 2:** Replace $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$E = h \cdot \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the constants $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs and $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$hc = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8}) = 1.986 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J┬Ęm}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to the more convenient unit eV┬Ęnm by using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J and $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9}$ m:

$$hc = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{-9}} \text{ eV┬Ęnm} \approx 1240 \text{ eV┬Ęnm}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore the working formula is:

$$\boxed{E(\text{eV}) = \frac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A quantum dot (CdSe) emits green light at $\lambda = 530$ nm. Find (a) its frequency, (b) photon energy in eV, and (c) photon momentum.

**(a) Frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\nu &= \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{530 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}} \\
&= 5.66 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} \\
&= 566 \text{ THz}
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Photon Energy (in eV):**

$$E = \frac{1240 \text{ eV┬Ęnm}}{530 \text{ nm}} = 2.34 \text{ eV}$$

**Cross-check in Joules:**

$$E = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(5.66 \times 10^{14}) = 3.75 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

Converting: $3.75 \times 10^{-19} / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 2.34$ eV Ō£ō

**(c) Photon Momentum:**

$$p = \frac{h}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{530 \times 10^{-9}} = 1.25 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg┬Ęm/s}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Energy Equivalence Table

The following table maps representative wavelengths across the spectrum to their photon energies ŌĆö essential for solving KTU numericals quickly.

| Spectral Band | Representative $\lambda$ | Frequency $\nu$ (Hz) | Photon Energy $E$ | Quantum/Nano Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Radio | $1$ m | $3 \times 10^{8}$ | $1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ eV | NMR, MRI contrast agents |
| Microwave | $1$ cm | $3 \times 10^{10}$ | $1.24 \times 10^{-4}$ eV | Dielectric heating of nanofluids |
| Far-IR | $10\ \mu$m | $3 \times 10^{13}$ | $0.124$ eV | Molecular vibrations (IR spectroscopy) |
| Near-IR | $1\ \mu$m | $3 \times 10^{14}$ | $1.24$ eV | Telecom fiber optics |
| Red | $700$ nm | $4.29 \times 10^{14}$ | $1.77$ eV | Si band-gap absorption |
| Green | $530$ nm | $5.66 \times 10^{14}$ | $2.34$ eV | CdSe quantum dot emission |
| Blue | $470$ nm | $6.38 \times 10^{14}$ | $2.64$ eV | GaN LED emission |
| UV-A | $365$ nm | $8.22 \times 10^{14}$ | $3.40$ eV | Photolithography (i-line) |
| EUV | $13.5$ nm | $2.22 \times 10^{16}$ | $91.8$ eV | 7-nm node semiconductor lithography |
| Soft X-ray | $1$ nm | $3 \times 10^{17}$ | $1.24$ keV | XRD of nanocrystals |
| Hard X-ray | $0.1$ nm | $3 \times 10^{18}$ | $12.4$ keV | Medical CT imaging |
| Gamma | $0.001$ nm | $3 \times 10^{20}$ | $1.24$ MeV | $^{60}$Co sterilization |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Spectral Order (low ŌåÆ high energy):**
> **R**oyal **M**artians **I**nvaded **V**enus **U**sing **X**-ray **G**uns
> (Radio ŌåÆ Microwave ŌåÆ IR ŌåÆ Visible ŌåÆ UV ŌåÆ X-ray ŌåÆ Gamma)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Complete Electromagnetic Spectrum Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["EM Spectrum Master Source: Accelerated Charges"] --> B["Wavelength Increases Frequency Decreases"]
    A --> C["Wavelength Decreases Frequency Increases"]

    B --> D["Radio Waves"]
    D --> D1["VLF 3 to 30 kHz"]
    D --> D2["AM Broadcast 540 to 1600 kHz"]
    D --> D3["FM TV 88 to 108 MHz"]

    B --> E["Microwaves 1 mm to 1 m"]
    E --> E1["Radar 1 to 100 GHz"]
    E --> E2["WiFi 2.4 5 GHz"]
    E --> E3["Satellite Comms 4 to 12 GHz"]

    B --> F["Infrared 700 nm to 1 mm"]
    F --> F1["Near IR 0.7 to 5 microns"]
    F --> F2["Mid IR 5 to 30 microns"]
    F --> F3["Far IR 30 to 1000 microns"]

    B --> G["Visible Light 400 to 700 nm"]
    G --> G1["Red 620 to 700 nm"]
    G --> G2["Orange 590 to 620 nm"]
    G --> G3["Yellow 560 to 590 nm"]
    G --> G4["Green 520 to 560 nm"]
    G --> G5["Blue 450 to 520 nm"]
    G --> G6["Violet 400 to 450 nm"]

    C --> H["Ultraviolet 10 to 400 nm"]
    H --> H1["UVA 315 to 400 nm"]
    H --> H2["UVB 280 to 315 nm"]
    H --> H3["UVC 100 to 280 nm"]
    H --> H4["EUV 10 to 121 nm"]

    C --> I["X-rays 0.01 to 10 nm"]
    I --> I1["Soft X-ray 0.1 to 10 nm"]
    I --> I2["Hard X-ray 0.01 to 0.1 nm"]

    C --> J["Gamma Rays below 0.01 nm"]
    J --> J1["Nuclear Transitions"]
    J --> J2["Cosmic Sources"]

    G --> K["Nanomaterial Resonance Window d equals lambda"]
    K --> K1["Plasmonic NPs 10 to 100 nm"]
    K --> K2["Quantum Dots 2 to 10 nm"]
    K --> K3["Photonic Crystals 100 to 500 nm"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö LightŌĆōMatter Interaction Hierarchy in Nanomaterials

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source["Incident EM Wave"] --> P1["Photon Energy Matches Bandgap"]
    Source --> P2["Photon Energy Exceeds Work Function"]
    Source --> P3["Photon Wavelength Matches Particle Size"]
    Source --> P4["Photon Frequency Matches Molecular Vibration"]

    P1 --> Out1["Absorption and Emission Quantum Dots"]
    P2 --> Out2["Photoelectric Effect Photodetectors"]
    P3 --> Out3["Surface Plasmon Resonance Au Ag NPs"]
    P4 --> Out4["IR Absorption Spectroscopy Nanomaterial Identification"]

    Out1 --> App1["LEDs Lasers Bio Imaging"]
    Out2 --> App2["Solar Cells Photocatalysis"]
    Out3 --> App3["Sensors SERS Therapy"]
    Out4 --> App4["Chemical Analysis Drug Detection"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram ŌĆö Photon Energy Decay Cascade (Visual to Radio)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top["High Energy Gamma Rays MeV Scale"] --> Mid1["X Rays keV Scale"]
    Mid1 --> Mid2["UV eV Scale"]
    Mid2 --> Mid3["Visible 1.65 to 3.1 eV"]
    Mid3 --> Mid4["IR meV Scale"]
    Mid4 --> Mid5["MicroeV Scale Microwaves"]
    Mid5 --> Bot["Low Energy Radio Waves nano eV Scale"]

    Mid3 -.-> Nano["Nanomaterial Interaction Zone Plasmonic Photonic Quantum"]
    Nano --> Nano1["Gold Nanoparticles 10 to 50 nm"]
    Nano --> Nano2["Quantum Dots 2 to 10 nm"]
    Nano --> Nano3["Photonic Crystals 100 to 500 nm"]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description ŌĆö Photon Energy vs Wavelength

Since the photon energy is an **inverse function** of wavelength, the spectrum can be visualized on a logarithmic axis. The classic schematic has:

- **X-axis (logarithmic):** Wavelength $\lambda$ ŌĆö from $10^{-12}$ m (gamma) on the left to $10^{4}$ m (radio) on the right.
- **Y-axis (logarithmic):** Photon energy $E$ ŌĆö decreasing as $\lambda$ increases.
- A **narrow visible window** appears as a tiny rainbow-colored sliver between IR and UV.
- A **shaded "Nanomaterial Window"** marking $\lambda = 1$ŌĆō$1000$ nm, highlighting where nanostructure dimensions naturally couple with the spectrum.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. Define electromagnetic spectrum. List its major regions in order of increasing wavelength.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The electromagnetic spectrum is the continuous distribution of electromagnetic radiation arranged according to wavelength or frequency. Major regions in order of **increasing wavelength** (decreasing frequency and energy) are:
> **Gamma rays ŌåÆ X-rays ŌåÆ Ultraviolet ŌåÆ Visible light ŌåÆ Infrared ŌåÆ Microwaves ŌåÆ Radio waves.**
> [3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for ordered list]

**Q2. A laser emits radiation at 632.8 nm. Calculate the photon energy in eV.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Using $E(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$:
> $E = \dfrac{1240}{632.8} = 1.96$ eV.
> [3 marks: 1 for formula + 2 for substitution and final answer]

---

### Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Question

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Module 1 Choice Pattern]**

**(a) Derive the wave equation for the electric field in vacuum starting from Maxwell's equations. Show that electromagnetic waves travel at speed $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$.** [7 marks]

**(b) A silicon quantum dot of diameter 5 nm absorbs photons at the silicon bandgap energy of 1.12 eV. Calculate (i) the absorbed photon wavelength, (ii) the corresponding frequency, (iii) the photon momentum, and (iv) the energy in Joules. State one application of such quantum dots.** [7 marks]

> **Model Solution (a):**
> Starting from the four Maxwell equations (Gauss's laws, Faraday's law, Amp├©re-Maxwell law), take the curl of Faraday's law:
> $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = -\dfrac{\partial}{\partial t}(\nabla \times \vec{B})$
> Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{E}) = \nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{E}) - \nabla^2 \vec{E}$, and substituting $\nabla \cdot \vec{E} = 0$ (vacuum) along with $\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial \vec{E}}{\partial t}$:
> $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$
> Comparing with the standard wave equation $\nabla^2 \vec{E} = \dfrac{1}{v^2}\dfrac{\partial^2 \vec{E}}{\partial t^2}$ yields $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}}$. Substituting values gives $v = c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. [7 marks]

> **Model Solution (b):**
> (i) $\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{E(\text{eV})} = \dfrac{1240}{1.12} = 1107$ nm $\approx 1.107\ \mu$m (near-IR). [2 marks]
> (ii) $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{1107 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.71 \times 10^{14}$ Hz. [2 marks]
> (iii) $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1107 \times 10^{-9}} = 5.98 \times 10^{-28}$ kg┬Ęm/s. [1.5 marks]
> (iv) $E = 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 1.79 \times 10^{-19}$ J. [1 mark]
> **Application:** Silicon quantum dots are used in **next-generation solar cells, light-emitting diodes, and bio-imaging** due to their size-tunable emission and biocompatibility. [0.5 mark]

#### Question B ŌĆö Full 14-Mark Alternative

**(a) Explain the electromagnetic spectrum in detail. Discuss the properties and applications of any four regions in the context of nanomaterials.** [7 marks]

**(b) Calculate the number of photons emitted per second by a 1 mW He-Ne laser operating at 632.8 nm. If these photons fall on a gold nanoparticle of diameter 20 nm, briefly describe the resonance phenomenon that occurs.** [7 marks]

> **Model Solution (a):**
> The electromagnetic spectrum is the entire range of EM radiation classified by wavelength/frequency. Discussing four regions:
> **(1) Visible Light:** Wavelengths 400ŌĆō700 nm. Noble-metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag) of size 10ŌĆō100 nm exhibit **localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)** in this region, producing intense colors used in biosensors and anti-counterfeit inks. [1.75 marks]
> **(2) Ultraviolet:** 10ŌĆō400 nm. ZnO and TiO$_2$ nanoparticles absorb UV photons, generating electron-hole pairs that drive photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. Also used in **EUV lithography** for sub-7 nm semiconductor patterning. [1.75 marks]
> **(3) Infrared:** 700 nmŌĆō1 mm. Quantum dots (PbS, PbSe) of size 2ŌĆō10 nm have size-tunable bandgaps in the IR, used in **night-vision devices** and **telecom-band photodetectors**. [1.75 marks]
> **(4) X-ray:** 0.01ŌĆō10 nm. Used in **X-ray diffraction (XRD)** to determine the crystal structure of nanomaterials via Bragg's law. Also, gold nanoshells absorb near-IR to X-ray for **photothermal cancer therapy**. [1.75 marks]

> **Model Solution (b):**
> Photon energy: $E = \dfrac{1240}{632.8} = 1.96$ eV $= 1.96 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.14 \times 10^{-19}$ J. [2 marks]
> Photon flux: $\Phi = \dfrac{P}{E} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{3.14 \times 10^{-19}} = 3.18 \times 10^{15}$ photons/s. [3 marks]
> **Resonance Phenomenon:** A 20 nm gold nanoparticle has a diameter comparable to ~$\lambda$/30 for red light. The free conduction electrons collectively oscillate at a specific frequency matching the incident photons, producing **localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR)**. This concentrates the electromagnetic field at the nanoparticle surface by factors of $10^2$ŌĆō$10^3$, leading to enhanced absorption and scattering visible as a deep red color. [2 marks]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong units in final answer:** Always convert $\lambda$ to **meters** before using $c = \lambda \nu$, or use the $E(\text{eV}) = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$ shortcut.
> 2. **Forgetting $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ conversion:** eV and Joules are *not* interchangeable without this factor.
> 3. **Confusing wave number with frequency:** $\tilde{\nu} = 1/\lambda$ (in m$^{-1}$) is **not** the same as $\nu = c/\lambda$ (in Hz).
> 4. **Misordering the spectrum:** Energy increases from radio ŌåÆ gamma; wavelength decreases in the same direction. Writing either in the wrong order costs a mark.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU examiners award partial marks for each algebraic transition. Writing the final wave equation without showing "curl of curl ŌåÆ substitution ŌåÆ comparison" loses 3ŌĆō4 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Definition:** The EM spectrum is the continuous range of all EM radiation, classified by wavelength from $\sim 10^{-12}$ m (gamma) to $> 10^{4}$ m (radio).
- [ ] **Core formula #1:** $c = \lambda \nu$ where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum.
- [ ] **Core formula #2:** $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$, with $E(\text{eV}) = 1240/\lambda(\text{nm})$.
- [ ] **Core formula #3:** Photon momentum $p = h/\lambda = E/c$.
- [ ] **Order (low ŌåÆ high energy):** Radio < Microwave < IR < Visible < UV < X-ray < Gamma.
- [ ] **VIBGYOR:** Visible spectrum in increasing frequency order.
- [ ] **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs.
- [ ] **Speed of light derivation:** Maxwell's equations $\Rightarrow$ $c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$.
- [ ] **Nanomaterial relevance:** Structures of size $d \sim \lambda/n$ couple resonantly with light ŌĆö basis of plasmonics, quantum dots, photonic crystals.
- [ ] **Photolithography clue:** Modern semiconductor nodes use EUV at $\lambda = 13.5$ nm to pattern features smaller than the diffraction limit.
- [ ] **Quick conversions:** $1$ eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J; $hc \approx 1240$ eV┬Ęnm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Particle Size and Its Significance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Particle Size and Its Significance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Particle Size

In the context of **Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology**, **particle size** refers to the effective diameter (or characteristic linear dimension) of an individual particle in a bulk or dispersed system. When this size is reduced to the **nanometer scale (typically 1 nm to 100 nm)**, the physical, chemical, mechanical, optical, magnetic, and electronic properties of the material undergo a fundamental transformation due to the dominance of surface atoms and quantum confinement effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A **nanoparticle** is defined as a particle with at least one dimension less than or equal to **100 nm** (as per ISO/TS 80004 and KTU-aligned definition). At this scale, the **fraction of surface atoms** becomes comparable to the fraction of bulk atoms, which dramatically alters the material's behavior.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Sugar Cube vs. Powder" Concept

Imagine a single **sugar cube** of side 1 cm. It dissolves slowly in water. Now, grind the same cube into ultra-fine powder. The powder dissolves almost instantly. Why?

- The **mass is identical**, but the **total surface area exposed** is enormous.
- More surface area = more atoms in contact with surroundings = faster reactions.
- This simple observation is the **heart of nanoscience**: properties change not because the atoms change, but because the **geometry of arrangement** changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Anchor:** Reducing particle size does **not** change the chemistry of atoms, but it **dramatically amplifies the surface-to-volume ratio**, which controls nearly all technologically interesting properties of nanomaterials.

### 1.3 Particle Size Classification (Hierarchical View)

| Size Regime | Typical Range | Behavior Dominated By |
|-------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Bulk / Macro | > 1 ┬Ąm | Bulk physics (classical mechanics) |
| Micro | 100 nm ŌĆō 1 ┬Ąm | Surface effects begin to matter |
| Nano | 1 nm ŌĆō 100 nm | **Surface + Quantum effects dominant** |
| Atomic / Molecular | < 1 nm | Quantum chemistry, single-atom physics |

### 1.4 The Three Key Metrics that Define "Significance"

The significance of particle size in nanotechnology is captured by three fundamental geometric parameters:

1. **Surface-to-Volume Ratio (S/V)** ŌĆō increases inversely with particle size.
2. **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** ŌĆō surface area per unit mass (m┬▓/g).
3. **Volume Fraction (Žå)** ŌĆō relative packing density in a composite/nanodispersion.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always start any answer on "significance of particle size" by stating that **as size decreases, the surface-to-volume ratio increases**, leading to property changes such as lowered melting point, altered band gap, enhanced catalytic activity, and modified mechanical strength.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Particle Size Matters: The "Why" and "How"

#### A. Surface-to-Volume Ratio (S/V)
For a spherical particle of radius *r*:
- Surface Area, $S = 4\pi r^2$
- Volume, $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$
- Hence, $S/V = \frac{3}{r}$

**Why it matters:** As *r* decreases, the fraction of atoms lying *on* the surface (rather than inside) increases rapidly. Surface atoms have **dangling bonds** and higher energy, making them chemically and catalytically more reactive.

#### B. Surface Atom Fraction (f_s)
For a spherical particle of diameter *D* and atomic diameter *d*:

$$f_s = \frac{N_s}{N_{total}} \approx \frac{6d}{D}$$

where $N_s$ is the number of surface atoms and $N_{total}$ is the total number of atoms.

**Engineering Insight:** When *D* = 10 nm, nearly **20% of atoms are surface atoms**. When *D* = 1 nm, **virtually all atoms are surface atoms**. This is why nanocatalysts (e.g., Pt nanoparticles in fuel cells) are extraordinarily efficient.

#### C. Specific Surface Area (SSA)
Specific surface area is the total surface area per unit mass:

$$SSA = \frac{S}{m} = \frac{S}{\rho \cdot V} = \frac{3}{\rho \cdot r}$$

where $\rho$ is the density of the material. Units: **m┬▓/g** or **m┬▓/kg**.

#### D. Number of Particles per Unit Mass (N_p)

$$N_p = \frac{m_{total}}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$$

This is critical in dosage calculations for drug delivery and in determining the number of catalytic active sites.

#### E. Volume Fraction (Žå) of Nanoparticles in a Matrix

$$\phi = \frac{V_{nanoparticles}}{V_{total}}$$

Used in nanocomposite design to tune mechanical/thermal/electrical properties.

### 2.2 Property-Size Relationships (The Real Significance)

| Property | Effect of Decreasing Size (Bulk ŌåÆ Nano) | Origin |
|----------|------------------------------------------|--------|
| **Melting Point** | Decreases significantly | Increased surface energy |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Increases (Hall-Petch effect, then inverse Hall-Petch) | Grain boundary interactions |
| **Optical Absorption** | Blue/red shift; color changes | Quantum confinement |
| **Electrical Conductivity** | Can change metalŌåÆinsulator | Quantum size effect |
| **Magnetic Coercivity** | Increases up to a critical size | Single-domain formation |
| **Catalytic Activity** | Dramatically increases | Higher fraction of active surface sites |
| **Chemical Reactivity** | Increases | High surface energy |

### 2.3 The Quantum Size Effect (Critical for KTU 2024)

When the particle size becomes comparable to or smaller than the **Bohr exciton radius** of the material, quantum confinement occurs. The energy levels become discrete:

$$E_{gap}(r) = E_{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu r^2}$$

where:
- $\hbar$ = reduced Planck's constant = **1.054 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│Ōü┤ J┬Ęs**
- $\mu$ = reduced effective mass of the exciton
- $r$ = radius of the nanoparticle
- $E_{bulk}$ = band gap of the bulk material

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation shows that **band gap energy increases as 1/r┬▓** as particle size shrinks. This is why CdSe nanoparticles (bulk band gap Ōēł 1.74 eV) glow **green at 3 nm** and **red at 8 nm**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | $S/V = 3/r$ (sphere) | r = radius | mŌü╗┬╣ | Quantifies surface dominance |
| 2 | $SSA = 3/(\rho r)$ | Žü = density | m┬▓/kg | Catalyst design, drug loading |
| 3 | $f_s \approx 6d/D$ | d = atomic diameter, D = particle diameter | dimensionless | Surface atom fraction |
| 4 | $E_{gap}(r) = E_{bulk} + \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu r^2}$ | Quantum confinement energy | eV | Optoelectronics, LEDs |
| 5 | $N_p = \dfrac{m}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$ | Number of particles per mass | particles/g | Dosage, formulation |
| 6 | $\phi = V_{nano}/V_{total}$ | Volume fraction | dimensionless | Nanocomposite design |
| 7 | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{2 \gamma_{sl} \cdot T_m \cdot V_m}{r \cdot \Delta H_f}$ | Melting point depression | K | GibbsŌĆōThomson relation |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Catalysis:** Pt nanoparticles of 2ŌĆō5 nm in automotive catalytic converters maximize SSA for pollutant conversion.
- **Drug Delivery:** 50ŌĆō100 nm polymeric nanoparticles exploit the **Enhanced Permeability and Retention (EPR)** effect to target tumors.
- **Energy Storage:** Nano-LiCoOŌéé cathodes in Li-ion batteries shorten LiŌü║ diffusion paths, improving charge rates.
- **Cosmetics:** TiOŌéé and ZnO nanoparticles provide UV protection while remaining transparent on skin.
- **Sensors:** Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) substrates rely on Ag/Au nanoparticles of 10ŌĆō50 nm.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Surface-to-Volume Ratio for a Sphere

**Goal:** Show that as particle radius decreases, surface-to-volume ratio increases inversely.

**Starting point ŌĆö Geometry of a sphere:**

$$S = 4\pi r^2, \quad V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 1: Form the ratio.**

$$\frac{S}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$$

**Step 2: Cancel common terms ($4\pi$ and $r^2$).**

$$\frac{S}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot 3}{4\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

$$\boxed{\frac{S}{V} = \frac{3}{r}}$$

This shows that halving the radius **doubles** the S/V ratio. For *r* = 1 ┬Ąm, S/V = 3 ├Ś 10ŌüČ mŌü╗┬╣. For *r* = 1 nm, S/V = 3 ├Ś 10Ōü╣ mŌü╗┬╣ ŌĆö a **1000-fold increase**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Specific Surface Area from First Principles

**Goal:** Express SSA in terms of density and radius.

**Step 1: Start from mass-volume relation.**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

**Step 2: Define SSA as surface per unit mass.**

$$SSA = \frac{S}{m} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$SSA = \frac{4\pi r^2 \cdot 3}{4\pi r^3 \cdot \rho} = \frac{3}{\rho \cdot r}$$

$$\boxed{SSA = \frac{3}{\rho \cdot r}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical illustration for KTU exam-type problems.**

For gold (Au) nanoparticles, Žü = **19.3 g/cm┬│ = 1.93 ├Ś 10Ōü┤ kg/m┬│**, r = 5 nm = 5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗Ōü╣ m:

$$SSA = \frac{3}{1.93 \times 10^4 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{3}{9.65 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 3.11 \times 10^4 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}$$

Converting to m┬▓/g: SSA Ōēł **31.1 m┬▓/g**. This is the order of magnitude typical for catalytically active gold nanoparticles.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Surface Atom Fraction (Geometric Counting Model)

**Goal:** Derive the simple expression $f_s \approx 6d/D$ for a cubic/cuboidal particle model.

**Step 1: Assume a cubic particle of side D containing atoms of diameter d.**

Number of atoms along one edge: $n = D/d$
Total number of atoms in a simple cubic model: $N_{total} = n^3 = (D/d)^3$

**Step 2: Count surface atoms (top, bottom, front, back, left, right faces).**

Each face contains $n^2$ atoms. There are 6 faces, so:

$$N_s = 6n^2 - (\text{corner corrections}) \approx 6n^2$$

**Step 3: Form the fraction.**

$$f_s = \frac{N_s}{N_{total}} = \frac{6n^2}{n^3} = \frac{6}{n} = \frac{6d}{D}$$

$$\boxed{f_s \approx \frac{6d}{D}}$$

**Step 4: Numerical check.**

For a 10 nm particle with d Ōēł 0.3 nm: $f_s = 6 \times 0.3 / 10 = 0.18$, i.e., **18% of atoms are on the surface**. This validates the earlier statement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Quantum Confinement Energy (Particle in a Box Analogy)

**Goal:** Derive the size-dependent band gap energy for a nanoparticle.

**Step 1: Model the nanoparticle as a 3D infinite potential well of size L (= particle diameter).**

For an electron, the ground state energy is:

$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m L^2}(n_x^2 + n_y^2 + n_z^2)$$

**Step 2: For the ground state, $n_x = n_y = n_z = 1$:**

$$E_{ground} = \frac{3\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m L^2}$$

**Step 3: The band gap of the nanoparticle is bulk band gap + confinement energy of electron + hole.**

The electron and hole have effective masses $m_e^*$ and $m_h^*$, with reduced mass $\mu = \frac{m_e^* m_h^*}{m_e^* + m_h^*}$.

**Step 4: Final band gap expression:**

$$E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu r^2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu r^2}}$$

**Step 5: Engineering interpretation.**

- The $1/r^2$ dependence means halving the particle size **quadruples** the confinement energy.
- A bulk semiconductor (e.g., CdS) with $E_{gap}^{bulk} = 2.4$ eV becomes strongly blue-shifted at small *r*.
- This is the foundation of **tunable quantum dot LEDs and biolabels**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Melting Point Depression (GibbsŌĆōThomson Equation)

**Goal:** Show that the melting temperature of nanoparticles is lower than the bulk.

**Step 1: Start from classical thermodynamics ŌĆö at equilibrium, chemical potentials of solid and liquid are equal.**

For a curved surface, the GibbsŌĆōThomson effect gives:

$$\Delta T_m = T_m^{bulk} - T_m^{nano} = \frac{2 \gamma_{sl} \cdot V_m \cdot T_m^{bulk}}{r \cdot \Delta H_f}$$

**Step 2: Symbols:**
- $\gamma_{sl}$ = solidŌĆōliquid interfacial energy (J/m┬▓)
- $V_m$ = molar volume (m┬│/mol)
- $\Delta H_f$ = bulk enthalpy of fusion (J/mol)
- $r$ = particle radius (m)

**Step 3: Final form:**

$$\boxed{T_m^{nano} = T_m^{bulk}\left(1 - \frac{2 \gamma_{sl} V_m}{r \Delta H_f}\right)}$$

**Step 4: Real-world relevance.**

- Bulk gold melts at **1064 ┬░C**; 2 nm Au nanoparticles melt at around **400 ┬░C** ŌĆö a dramatic drop.
- Important in **sintering of nanoceramics, soldering of nanopastes, and nanofabrication**.

---

### 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Derivation | Key Result | Particle Size Dependence | Practical Implication |
|------------|------------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| S/V Ratio | $S/V = 3/r$ | $1/r$ | Surface-driven phenomena |
| Specific Surface Area | $SSA = 3/(\rho r)$ | $1/r$ | Catalyst/drug efficiency |
| Surface Atom Fraction | $f_s \approx 6d/D$ | $1/D$ | Reactivity tuning |
| Quantum Confinement | $E_{gap} \propto 1/r^2$ | $1/r^2$ | Tunable optics, LEDs |
| Melting Point Depression | $\Delta T_m \propto 1/r$ | $1/r$ | Sintering, low-T processing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Particle Size Hierarchy: Macro ŌåÆ Micro ŌåÆ Nano ŌåÆ Atomic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BULK MATERIAL\nD greater than 1000 nm] --> B[MICROSCOPIC\nD = 100 nm to 1000 nm]
    B --> C[NANOSCALE\nD = 1 nm to 100 nm]
    C --> D[ATOMIC CLUSTER\nD less than 1 nm]
    A --> A1[Surface atoms less than 1 percent\nBulk physics dominates]
    B --> B1[Surface atoms 1 to 5 percent\nMixed bulk and surface behavior]
    C --> C1[Surface atoms 10 to 50 percent\nSurface and quantum effects dominate]
    D --> D1[Every atom is essentially surface\nMolecular chemistry regime]
    style A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#d84315,stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Surface-to-Volume Ratio Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Particle Geometry] --> B{Shape Selection}
    B --> C1[Spherical]
    B --> C2[Cubic]
    B --> C3[Rod or Tube]
    B --> C4[Plate or Sheet]
    C1 --> D1[S over V equals 3 over r]
    C2 --> D2[S over V equals 6 over L]
    C3 --> D3[S over V depends on aspect ratio]
    C4 --> D4[S over V equals 2 over t for thin film]
    D1 --> E[Surface atom fraction increases]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Property changes:\nMelting, Optical, Magnetic, Catalytic]
    style A fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Property Modification Workflow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Reduce Particle Size to Nano Regime] --> P2[Increase in Surface to Volume Ratio]
    P2 --> P3[Increase in Surface Energy]
    P3 --> P4A[Catalytic Activity Rises]
    P3 --> P4B[Melting Point Drops]
    P3 --> P4C[Mechanical Strength Changes]
    P2 --> P5[Quantum Confinement Activated]
    P5 --> P6A[Band Gap Widens]
    P5 --> P6B[Optical Absorption Shifts]
    P5 --> P6C[Electrical Conduction Modifies]
    P1 --> P7[Greater Fraction of Surface Atoms]
    P7 --> P8A[Enhanced Chemical Reactivity]
    P7 --> P8B[Modified Magnetic Coercivity]
    style P1 fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Nanomaterial Dimensional Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nanomaterial Classification] --> B[0 Dimensional]
    A --> C[1 Dimensional]
    A --> D[2 Dimensional]
    A --> E[3 Dimensional]
    B --> B1[All dimensions in nano\nExamples: Quantum dots, Nanoparticles]
    C --> C1[Two dimensions in nano\nExamples: Nanotubes, Nanowires, Nanorods]
    D --> D1[One dimension in nano\nExamples: Graphene, Thin films, Nanosheets]
    E --> E1[Zero dimensions in nano at bulk level\nExamples: Nanocomposites, Bulk nanostructured materials]
    style A fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Significance Flow: From Particle Size to Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smaller Particle Size] --> B[Higher Surface to Volume Ratio]
    B --> C[Greater Number of Surface Atoms]
    C --> D[More Active Sites]
    D --> E1[Catalysis]
    D --> E2[Sensors]
    D --> E3[Drug Delivery]
    B --> F[Lower Melting Point]
    F --> G1[Low Temperature Sintering]
    F --> G2[Nano Inks and Pastes]
    C --> H[Stronger Surface Plasmon Resonance]
    H --> I1[Optical Applications]
    H --> I2[SERS Substrates]
    B --> J[Quantum Confinement]
    J --> K1[LEDs and Displays]
    J --> K2[Solar Cells]
    J --> K3[Biological Labels]
    style A fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#b39ddb,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
Define **nanoparticle** as per KTU/ISO standards. Why is particle size critical in nanotechnology?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A nanoparticle is a particle of matter with at least one dimension Ōēż 100 nm. Particle size is critical because at the nanoscale, the surface-to-volume ratio increases dramatically, leading to a substantial fraction of atoms residing on the surface. This results in altered mechanical, optical, magnetic, electrical, and chemical properties compared to the bulk, which forms the foundation of nanotechnology applications in catalysis, drug delivery, sensors, and energy devices. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024 Model]**
What is **specific surface area (SSA)**? Show that for a spherical particle, $SSA = \dfrac{3}{\rho r}$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific surface area is the total surface area of a particle per unit mass, expressed in m┬▓/g. For a sphere of radius *r* and density *Žü*:

$$S = 4\pi r^2, \quad m = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$$

$$SSA = \frac{S}{m} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{\rho r}$$

This shows that SSA scales inversely with particle radius. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice)

> **Instructions:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **surface-to-volume ratio** and **specific surface area** of a spherical nanoparticle. How does the surface atom fraction change with particle size? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Derive S/V for a sphere.** 
For radius *r*: $S = 4\pi r^2$, $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$.

$$\frac{S}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2: Derive SSA.**
With mass $m = \rho V$:

$$SSA = \frac{S}{m} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{\rho r}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Surface atom fraction.**
Modeling the particle as a cube of side *D* with atomic diameter *d*:

$$f_s = \frac{6n^2}{n^3} = \frac{6}{n} = \frac{6d}{D}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 4: Interpretation and numerical check.**
At *D* = 10 nm, *d* = 0.3 nm: $f_s = 0.18$ (i.e., 18% surface atoms). As size decreases, this fraction rises sharply, leading to increased surface energy, catalytic activity, and chemical reactivity. **(1 mark)**

---

**(b)** With suitable derivations, explain the **quantum size effect** and its effect on the **optical and electronic properties** of nanomaterials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Statement of the quantum size effect.**
When the particle size becomes comparable to or smaller than the Bohr exciton radius, the charge carriers (electrons and holes) are spatially confined, leading to discrete energy levels and a widening of the band gap. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Derivation.**
Using the particle-in-a-box model:

$$E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2\mu r^2}$$

where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass and $r$ is the particle radius. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3: Optical implications.**
- Band gap increases as *r* decreases ŌåÆ absorption/emission shifts to **shorter wavelengths (blue shift)**.
- Example: CdSe quantum dots emit red at ~8 nm and green at ~3 nm.
- This enables tunable LEDs, lasers, and biolabels. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 4: Electronic implications.**
- Effective band gap widening can transform a semiconductor into an insulator.
- Conductivity drops because fewer electrons can be thermally excited across the gap.
- Single-electron tunneling and Coulomb blockade phenomena appear. **(1.5 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Jun 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of particle size in nanotechnology. Discuss how particle size affects **(i) mechanical, (ii) optical, and (iii) magnetic** properties of materials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:**
Particle size is a fundamental parameter in nanotechnology. Reducing particle size to the nanoscale alters the surface-to-volume ratio, the number of surface atoms, and introduces quantum confinement, leading to significant property changes. **(1 mark)**

**(i) Mechanical Properties:**
- **Hall-Petch strengthening:** Yield strength increases as grain size decreases because grain boundaries impede dislocation motion: $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k d^{-1/2}$.
- **Inverse Hall-Petch effect:** Below ~10 nm, grain boundary sliding dominates, and strength decreases.
- Nanomaterials can exhibit superplasticity at lower temperatures. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Optical Properties:**
- **Quantum confinement** widens the band gap, causing a **blue shift** in absorption/emission.
- Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) in metal nanoparticles (Au, Ag) produces intense colors used in sensors.
- Nanoparticles of TiOŌéé and ZnO become transparent while retaining UV absorption. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Magnetic Properties:**
- Below the **single-domain critical size**, each particle has uniform magnetization, eliminating domain wall motion.
- This results in **high coercivity** (used in hard drives).
- Below a few nm, **superparamagnetism** appears ŌĆö particles lose stable magnetization due to thermal fluctuations, used in MRI contrast agents and hyperthermia. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b)** Derive the **GibbsŌĆōThomson equation** for melting point depression of nanoparticles. Why is this important in **nano-sintering** and **nano-paste technology**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Thermodynamic origin.**
For a small particle, the high surface energy of the curved solidŌĆōliquid interface shifts the chemical potential equilibrium, lowering the melting point. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Derivation.**

Starting from the ClausiusŌĆōClapeyron equation applied to a small particle with surface energy contribution:

$$\Delta T_m = T_m^{bulk} - T_m^{nano} = \frac{2 \gamma_{sl} V_m T_m^{bulk}}{r \Delta H_f}$$

$$\boxed{T_m^{nano} = T_m^{bulk}\left(1 - \frac{2 \gamma_{sl} V_m}{r \Delta H_f}\right)}$$ **(4 marks)**

**Step 3: Importance in nano-sintering and nano-pastes.**
- Nanoparticles of metals (Ag, Cu) and ceramics can be densified at **temperatures 100ŌĆō400 ┬░C below** their bulk melting point.
- Enables **low-temperature processing** of electronic interconnects, printable circuits, and brazing of heat-sensitive components.
- Used in conductive inks for flexible electronics and RFID antennas. **(2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing S/V with SSA:** Many students write SSA = 3/r (forgetting density). Always include Žü in the formula: $SSA = 3/(\rho r)$.
> 2. **Units oversight:** Forget to mention units in the final answer. Always write m┬▓/g or m┬▓/kg.
> 3. **Quantum size effect misuse:** Stating "particles behave like atoms" ŌĆö KTU examiners want the *band gap widening* explanation with the $\hbar^2 \pi^2 / 2\mu r^2$ term.
> 4. **Surface atom fraction derivation:** Skipping the cubic model derivation; merely stating "$f_s$ increases as size decreases" gets **only 1 mark out of 3**.
> 5. **GibbsŌĆōThomson derivation:** Examiners deduct marks if you fail to clearly state all four variables ($\gamma_{sl}, V_m, \Delta H_f, r$) and their units.
> 6. **Naming the three classification types (0D, 1D, 2D):** Many answers reverse them. Remember: 0D means **all** three dimensions are in the nano range.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Nanoparticle definition:** At least one dimension Ōēż 100 nm (KTU/ISO standard).
- **Surface-to-volume ratio** for a sphere: $S/V = 3/r$ ŌĆö **inversely proportional to radius**.
- **Specific surface area (SSA):** $SSA = 3/(\rho r)$ ŌĆö units m┬▓/g; the basis of catalyst design.
- **Surface atom fraction:** $f_s \approx 6d/D$ ŌĆö at 10 nm, ~20% atoms are surface atoms.
- **Quantum size effect:** Band gap widens as $E_{gap} \propto 1/r^2$ ŌĆö causes blue shift in optical spectra.
- **Melting point depression:** GibbsŌĆōThomson equation, $\Delta T_m \propto 1/r$ ŌĆö vital for nano-sintering.
- **Mechanical:** Hall-Petch strengthening ŌåÆ inverse Hall-Petch at < 10 nm.
- **Magnetic:** Single-domain (high coercivity) ŌåÆ superparamagnetic (very small particles).
- **Classification of nanomaterials:** 0D (all 3 in nano), 1D (2 in nano), 2D (1 in nano), 3D (bulk nanostructured).
- **Engineering relevance:** Catalysis, drug delivery, sensors, energy storage, optoelectronics, cosmetics.
- **Always** state the *physical reason* (surface dominance or quantum confinement) for any property change.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Exam Tip:** A 14-mark question on "significance of particle size" almost always requires **(a)** a derivation (S/V or SSA) **+** interpretation, and **(b)** property discussion with at least one **quantum size effect** mention. Master the formulas and you will secure 12+ marks consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physics of Nanomaterials: Size Effect on Thermal, Electrical, Electronic, Mechanical, Optical and Magnetic Properties of Nanomaterials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Physics of Nanomaterials: Size Effect on Thermal, Electrical, Electronic, Mechanical, Optical and Magnetic Properties of Nanomaterials # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Does "Size Effect" Mean in Nanomaterials? **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** The **size effect** in nanomaterials refers to the systematic and often dramatic deviation of physical, chemical, mechanical, and electronic properties of a material when at least one of its dimensions is reduced below a critical length scale ŌĆö typically **below 100 nm**. This critical threshold is governed by characteristic physical lengths such as the **de Broglie wavelength** (Ōēł 1ŌĆō100 nm), the **electron mean free path** (Ōēł 10ŌĆō100 nm in metals), the **exciton Bohr radius** (Ōēł 1ŌĆō10 nm in semiconductors), and the **domain wall width** (Ōēł 10ŌĆō50 nm in magnetic materials). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** > *"When the physical size of a particle approaches or becomes smaller than a characteristic length scale of a property (e.g., mean free path, Bohr radius, magnetic domain size), that property transitions from bulk behavior to size-dependent behavior. This is the essence of the nanosize effect."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Crowd at a Concert" Analogy Imagine a huge crowd at a stadium concert (this represents **bulk material** ŌĆö millions of atoms behaving in an averaged, predictable way). Now shrink that crowd to just 50 people standing in a small room (**nanomaterial** ŌĆö only hundreds to thousands of atoms). What changes? - **The walls (surface) become incredibly important** ŌĆö almost everyone is "near the wall." This is the **surface-to-volume ratio effect**. - **Personal interactions (quantum effects) start to dominate** over crowd behavior (classical bulk averaging). - **Specific individuals (defects, surface atoms)** now disproportionately influence the entire group, unlike in the bulk where they get "diluted." **Key Insight:** In nanomaterials, **surface atoms** (typically 20ŌĆō80% of total atoms) dominate over **bulk atoms**, fundamentally changing how the material interacts with heat, light, electricity, and magnetic fields. ## 1.3 The Six Critical Size-Dependent Properties ŌĆö Overview > [!NOTE] > **The Six Pillars of Nanosize Effects (Module 1 Core):** | # | Property | Characteristic Length Scale | Bulk Behavior | Nano Behavior | |---|----------|----------------------------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | **Thermal** | Phonon mean free path | Constant melting point | Melting point depression | | 2 | **Electrical** | Electron mean free path | Ohm's law | Coulomb blockade / quantized conductance | | 3 | **Electronic** | Exciton Bohr radius | Continuous bands | Discretized energy levels | | 4 | **Mechanical** | Dislocation loop length | Hall-Petch strengthening | Inverse Hall-Petch | | 5 | **Optical** | Wavelength of light | Fixed color | Size-tunable color (plasmonics) | | 6 | **Magnetic** | Magnetic domain size | Ferromagnetism | Superparamagnetism | ## 1.4 The Universal "Scaling Equation" ŌĆö Surface-to-Volume Ratio The foundational geometric reason for all size effects is the **surface-to-volume ratio (S/V)** which scales as **1/r** for a sphere of radius **r**: $$\frac{S}{V} = \frac{4\pi r^2}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \frac{3}{r}$$ This means as **r decreases**, surface atoms dominate, and bulk-averaged properties break down. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Size Effect on Thermal Properties ### 2.1.1 Melting Point Depression (Pawlow Model ŌĆö 1909) **Why?** Surface atoms have fewer nearest neighbors ŌåÆ higher surface energy ŌåÆ lower thermal stability. The **Pawlow Equation** gives the melting point depression for a spherical nanoparticle of radius **r**: $$T_m(r) = T_{m,\infty} \left[ 1 - \frac{2}{\rho_s L r} \left( \sigma_s - \sigma_l \left( \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l} \right)^{2/3} \right) \right]$$ Where: - $T_{m,\infty}$ = bulk melting temperature (**K**) - $\rho_s, \rho_l$ = densities of solid and liquid phases (**kg/m┬│**) - $L$ = latent heat of fusion (**J/kg**) - $\sigma_s, \sigma_l$ = surface energies of solid and liquid (**J/m┬▓**) - $r$ = particle radius (**m**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simplified form (KTU exam-favorite):** > $$T_m(r) \approx T_{m,\infty} \left( 1 - \frac{\beta}{r} \right)$$ > where **╬▓** is a material-specific constant. Note the **1/r dependence** ŌĆö smaller particles melt at lower temperatures! **Engineering Utility:** Used in **soldering pastes for nanoelectronics**, **drug delivery** (controlled melting of lipid nanoparticles), and **low-temperature sintering of ceramics**. ### 2.1.2 Specific Heat Capacity Reduction At the nanoscale, specific heat ($C_p$) decreases because surface atoms have **lower coordination numbers** and thus fewer vibrational modes (phonon density of states is truncated). $$C_p(r) = C_{p,\infty} - \frac{\alpha}{r}$$ ### 2.1.3 Thermal Conductivity Reduction (Phonon Boundary Scattering) In nanowires and thin films, when the characteristic dimension $d$ becomes comparable to the phonon mean free path $\Lambda$: $$\kappa = \kappa_{\infty} \left( 1 + \frac{4 \Lambda}{3 d} \right)^{-1}$$ When $d \ll \Lambda$, thermal conductivity drops dramatically ŌĆö useful for **thermoelectric materials** (e.g., BiŌééTeŌéā nanowires). ## 2.2 Size Effect on Electrical Properties ### 2.2.1 Coulomb Blockade When a nanoparticle (acting as an "island") is small enough, adding a single electron requires a charging energy: $$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2 C}$$ where $C$ is the capacitance of the island. If $E_C \gg k_B T$, electron transport becomes **discrete, one-electron-at-a-time**. **Condition for Coulomb Blockade:** $$\frac{e^2}{2C} > k_B T$$ This is the basis of **single-electron transistors (SETs)** and **quantum dots for qubits**. ### 2.2.2 Quantized Conductance (Landauer Formula) In a 1-D ballistic channel (e.g., carbon nanotube), conductance is quantized in units of: $$G_0 = \frac{2e^2}{h} = \frac{1}{12.9 \text{ k}\Omega}$$ The **2** comes from spin degeneracy. Conductance becomes: $$G = N \cdot G_0 = N \cdot \frac{2e^2}{h}$$ where $N$ is an integer (number of modes). ## 2.3 Size Effect on Electronic Properties ŌĆö Quantum Confinement This is the **most important size effect in semiconductor nanocrystals (quantum dots)**. ### 2.3.1 The Particle-in-a-Box (Quantum Dot) Model For a spherical quantum dot of radius $R$, the bandgap increases from bulk value $E_g^{\infty}$ to: $$E_g(R) = E_g^{\infty} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}$$ where $\mu$ is the reduced effective mass of the electron-hole pair: $$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Result ŌĆö The Brus Equation (more accurate form):** > $$E_g(R) = E_g^{\infty} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 R^2} \left( \frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*} \right) - \frac{1.8 e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R}$$ > The first correction term = **quantum confinement (kinetic energy)** ŌĆö increases with decreasing R. > The second correction term = **Coulomb attraction** ŌĆö slightly decreases the bandgap. **Engineering Utility:** Size-tunable LEDs, **CdSe quantum dots** (red ŌåÆ green as size shrinks from 10 nm to 2 nm), solar cells, bio-imaging tags. ## 2.4 Size Effect on Mechanical Properties ### 2.4.1 Hall-Petch Strengthening (Strengthening with decreasing grain size) For grain size $d$ in the range **100 nm > d > 10 nm**: $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{d}}$$ Smaller grains ŌåÆ more grain boundaries ŌåÆ block dislocation motion ŌåÆ harder material. ### 2.4.2 Inverse Hall-Petch Effect (Below ~10 nm) When grains drop below a critical size (Ōēł 10 nm), **grain boundary sliding** dominates over dislocation pile-up, and the relationship **reverses**: $$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_{HP}}{\sqrt{d}} \quad \text{(becomes negative slope below } d_c\text{)}$$ The material becomes **softer** with decreasing grain size. This is observed in nanocrystalline Cu, Ni, Pd. ### 2.4.3 Superplasticity Nanocrystalline materials (grain size < 100 nm) can exhibit **superplasticity** ŌĆö elongations > 1000% before fracture ŌĆö because grain boundary diffusion becomes the dominant deformation mechanism. ## 2.5 Size Effect on Optical Properties ### 2.5.1 Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) in Metallic Nanoparticles When the size of a metal nanoparticle (Au, Ag) approaches the wavelength of light, conduction electrons collectively oscillate. The **Mie Theory** gives the SPR frequency: $$\omega_{SPR} = \sqrt{\frac{\omega_p^2}{1 + 2\varepsilon_m} - \gamma^2}$$ where: - $\omega_p$ = bulk plasma frequency - $\varepsilon_m$ = dielectric constant of surrounding medium - $\gamma$ = damping factor **For small particles (quasi-static limit):** $$\omega_{SPR} \approx \frac{\omega_p}{\sqrt{1 + 2\varepsilon_m}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Gold nanoparticle solution colors:** > - 20 nm particles ŌåÆ **red** (SPR at ~520 nm) > - 100 nm particles ŌåÆ **purple/blue** (SPR red-shifts with size) > This is the origin of the famous **Lycurgus Cup** (4th century Roman) and stained glass windows. ### 2.5.2 Photoluminescence Shift in Quantum Dots As size decreases, bandgap increases ŌåÆ emission wavelength blue-shifts: $$\lambda_{emission}(R) = \frac{hc}{E_g(R)}$$ ## 2.6 Size Effect on Magnetic Properties ### 2.6.1 Single-Domain Limit Below a critical size $D_c$, a magnetic particle becomes a **single domain** (no domain walls). For Fe: $D_c \approx 15$ nm; for Co: $D_c \approx 70$ nm; for Ni: $D_c \approx 55$ nm. ### 2.6.2 Superparamagnetism For very small ferromagnetic particles (typically < 10 nm), thermal energy $k_B T$ overcomes the anisotropy energy barrier $K_u V$, causing spontaneous magnetization reversal: **N├®el-Arrhenius relaxation time:** $$\tau = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T}\right)$$ where $\tau_0 \approx 10^{-9}$ s. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Superparamagnetic condition:** > $$K_u V < 25 \, k_B T$$ > When this holds, the particle has **no stable magnetization** ŌåÆ behaves like a paramagnet but with a *giant* magnetic moment (10┬│ŌĆō10ŌüĄ ╬╝_B). **Engineering Utility:** - **Hard disk drives** ŌĆö pushing bit density toward superparamagnetic limit. - **MRI contrast agents** ŌĆö superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs). - **Magnetic data storage** ŌĆö thermal stability of bits. ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Key Equation | Condition | Unit | |---|----------|--------------|-----------|------| | 1 | Surface-to-volume ratio | $S/V = 3/r$ (sphere) | $r < 100$ nm | mŌü╗┬╣ | | 2 | Melting point depression | $T_m(r) = T_{m,\infty}(1 - \beta/r)$ | $r < 20$ nm | K | | 3 | Coulomb charging energy | $E_C = e^2/(2C)$ | $E_C \gg k_B T$ | J | | 4 | Conductance quantum | $G_0 = 2e^2/h = 1/(12.9$ k╬®$)$ | 1-D ballistic | S | | 5 | Quantum dot bandgap (Brus) | $E_g(R) = E_g^{\infty} + \hbar^2\pi^2/(2\mu R^2)$ | $R < a_B$ | eV | | 6 | Hall-Petch | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_{HP}/\sqrt{d}$ | $10 \text{ nm} < d < 100 \text{ nm}$ | MPa | | 7 | Inverse Hall-Petch | Softening at $d < 10$ nm | $d < 10$ nm | MPa | | 8 | SPR frequency | $\omega_{SPR} \approx \omega_p/\sqrt{1+2\varepsilon_m}$ | $r \ll \lambda_{light}$ | rad/s | | 9 | Superparamagnetic limit | $K_u V < 25 k_B T$ | $d < 10$ nm typically | J | | 10 | N├®el relaxation | $\tau = \tau_0 \exp(K_u V / k_B T)$ | Single-domain particle | s | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Melting Point Depression (Pawlow Model) ### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate At equilibrium melting, the **chemical potential of the solid** equals the **chemical potential of the liquid**: $$\mu_s(T_m, r) = \mu_l(T_m, r)$$ The chemical potential for a particle of radius $r$ includes a surface energy contribution: $$\mu_s(T,r) = \mu_s^{\infty}(T) + \frac{2\sigma_s V_s}{r}$$ $$\mu_l(T,r) = \mu_l^{\infty}(T) + \frac{2\sigma_l V_l}{r}$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation **Step 1:** Set them equal: $$\mu_s^{\infty}(T_m) + \frac{2\sigma_s V_s}{r} = \mu_l^{\infty}(T_m) + \frac{2\sigma_l V_l}{r}$$ **Step 2:** Rearrange to separate surface terms: $$\mu_l^{\infty}(T_m) - \mu_s^{\infty}(T_m) = \frac{2V}{r}\left(\sigma_s - \sigma_l \cdot \frac{V_l}{V_s}\right)$$ Since densities $\rho_s = M/V_s$ and $\rho_l = M/V_l$, we have $V_l/V_s = \rho_s/\rho_l$. **Step 3:** At the bulk melting point $T_{m,\infty}$, the bulk chemical potentials are equal: $$\mu_l^{\infty}(T_{m,\infty}) - \mu_s^{\infty}(T_{m,\infty}) = 0$$ **Step 4:** Linearize the difference in chemical potentials around $T_{m,\infty}$ using a Taylor expansion. Recall that $\partial \mu / \partial T \vert_P = -S$, where $S$ is molar entropy. Also, at the melting point, $\Delta H_m = T_{m,\infty} \Delta S_m = L M$ (latent heat per mole). $$\mu_l^{\infty}(T_m) - \mu_s^{\infty}(T_m) \approx -\Delta S \cdot (T_m - T_{m,\infty}) = -\frac{L \rho_s}{T_{m,\infty}}(T_m - T_{m,\infty})$$ **Step 5:** Combine steps 2 and 4: $$-\frac{L \rho_s}{T_{m,\infty}}(T_m - T_{m,\infty}) = \frac{2}{r}\left(\sigma_s - \sigma_l \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $T_m(r)$: $$T_m(r) = T_{m,\infty} - \frac{2 T_{m,\infty}}{L \rho_s r}\left(\sigma_s - \sigma_l \cdot \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)$$ **Step 7:** Final compact form: $$\boxed{T_m(r) = T_{m,\infty}\left[1 - \frac{2}{\rho_s L r}\left(\sigma_s - \sigma_l \left(\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_l}\right)^{2/3}\right)\right]}$$ > [!NOTE] > The $(\rho_s/\rho_l)^{2/3}$ factor arises from equating volumes of the solid and liquid phases on a per-atom basis. The **negative sign** confirms that $T_m(r) < T_{m,\infty}$ ŌĆö nanoparticles melt at lower temperatures. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantum Confinement Energy in a Quantum Dot ### 3.2.1 Physical Setup A spherical nanocrystal (radius $R$) acts as a 3-D spherical potential well. Both electron (in conduction band) and hole (in valence band) are confined. We solve the Schr├Čdinger equation: $$\left[-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\nabla^2 + V(r)\right]\Psi(\vec{r}) = E\Psi(\vec{r})$$ with $V(r) = 0$ for $r < R$ and $V(r) = \infty$ for $r > R$ (infinite well approximation). ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Separation of variables in spherical coordinates gives: $$\Psi(r,\theta,\phi) = R_{n,\ell}(r) Y_{\ell}^{m}(\theta,\phi)$$ **Step 2:** The radial equation is the spherical Bessel equation. Solutions are the spherical Bessel functions $j_\ell(k r)$ with boundary condition: $$j_\ell(k R) = 0$$ **Step 3:** The lowest root occurs for $\ell = 0$ (s-state), where $j_0(x) = \sin(x)/x$. The first zero is at $x = \pi$, giving: $$k R = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \pi/R$$ **Step 4:** The confinement energy (kinetic energy of the lowest state): $$E_{conf} = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m^*} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m^* R^2}$$ **Step 5:** Apply this to BOTH the electron (mass $m_e^*$) in the conduction band AND the hole (mass $m_h^*$) in the valence band: $$\Delta E_{e} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m_e^* R^2} \quad \text{and} \quad \Delta E_{h} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m_h^* R^2}$$ **Step 6:** Total shift in bandgap: $$\Delta E_g = \Delta E_e + \Delta E_h = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 R^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right)$$ **Step 7:** Introduce the reduced effective mass $\mu$ where $1/\mu = 1/m_e^* + 1/m_h^*$: $$\boxed{E_g(R) = E_g^{\infty} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 \mu R^2}}$$ **Step 8:** Add the Coulomb correction (electron-hole attraction, in a dielectric medium): The Coulomb energy for charges $+e$ and $-e$ separated by distance $\approx R$ in a medium of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ is: $$E_{Coulomb} = -\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R}$$ The numerical prefactor **1.8** in the Brus equation arises from averaging over the actual wavefunctions (not point charges). The final **Brus Equation**: $$\boxed{E_g(R) = E_g^{\infty} + \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 R^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right) - \frac{1.8 e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r R}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation of each term:** > - **1st correction (positive, $\propto 1/R^2$):** Confinement raises energy ŌĆö dominant for very small R. > - **2nd correction (negative, $\propto 1/R$):** Coulomb attraction lowers energy ŌĆö subdominant. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Surface Plasmon Resonance Condition ### 3.3.1 Quasi-Static Approximation (small particle, $r \ll \lambda$) Consider a metal sphere of radius $r$ in a uniform external electric field $\vec{E}_0 = E_0 \hat{z}$. Inside the sphere, the field is uniform: $$\vec{E}_{in} = \frac{3\varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon + 2\varepsilon_m}\vec{E}_0$$ where $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_1 + i\varepsilon_2$ is the complex dielectric function of the metal, and $\varepsilon_m$ is the dielectric of the surrounding medium. ### 3.3.2 Resonance Condition Resonance occurs when the denominator vanishes: $$\varepsilon_1(\omega) + 2\varepsilon_m = 0$$ For a Drude metal, $\varepsilon_1(\omega) = 1 - \omega_p^2/\omega^2$: $$1 - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2} + 2\varepsilon_m = 0$$ Solving: $$\omega^2 = \frac{\omega_p^2}{1 + 2\varepsilon_m}$$ $$\boxed{\omega_{SPR} = \frac{\omega_p}{\sqrt{1 + 2\varepsilon_m}}}$$ **Step-by-step implications:** - $\omega_p = \sqrt{n e^2/(m \varepsilon_0)}$ ŌåÆ depends on free electron density. - For Ag in vacuum: $\omega_p \approx 9$ eV ŌåÆ $\omega_{SPR} \approx 3.5$ eV ŌåÆ $\lambda \approx 350$ nm. - For Au in water: $\lambda_{SPR} \approx 520$ nm (green light absorbed ŌåÆ red light transmitted). - Surrounding medium with higher $\varepsilon_m$ ŌåÆ SPR red-shifts. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Superparamagnetic Limit ### 3.4.1 Energy Barrier for Magnetization Reversal For a single-domain particle with uniaxial anisotropy constant $K_u$ and volume $V$, the energy density is: $$E(\theta) = K_u \sin^2\theta$$ where $\theta$ is the angle between magnetization and easy axis. The energy barrier between two stable states ($\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$) is: $$\Delta E = K_u V$$ ### 3.4.2 N├®el Relaxation Time The probability of thermally activated switching per unit time is given by Boltzmann statistics: $$\frac{1}{\tau} = f_0 \exp\left(-\frac{\Delta E}{k_B T}\right)$$ where $f_0 = 1/\tau_0 \approx 10^{9}$ Hz (attempt frequency). Therefore: $$\boxed{\tau = \tau_0 \exp\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T}\right)}$$ ### 3.4.3 Superparamagnetic Criterion A particle is considered superparamagnetic when $\tau < 100$ s (typical measurement time). Setting $\tau = 100$ s and $\tau_0 = 10^{-9}$ s: $$100 = 10^{-9} \exp\left(\frac{K_u V}{k_B T}\right)$$ $$\ln(10^{11}) = \frac{K_u V}{k_B T}$$ $$\frac{K_u V}{k_B T} \approx 25$$ $$\boxed{K_u V < 25 \, k_B T \quad \text{(superparamagnetic limit)}}$$ ### 3.4.4 Numerical Example: 10 nm FeŌéāOŌéä Particle For magnetite: $K_u \approx 1.1 \times 10^4$ J/m┬│, $V = (4/3)\pi(5 \times 10^{-9})^3 \approx 5.24 \times 10^{-25}$ m┬│. $K_u V \approx 5.76 \times 10^{-21}$ J Ōēł 4.4 $k_B T$ at 300 K. This is **below 25 $k_B T$** ŌåÆ the particle is superparamagnetic at room temperature. To stabilize, one must either increase $K_u$ (use Co, FePt alloys) or reduce T (use low-temperature operation). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Architecture Diagram ŌĆö Size-Dependent Properties of Nanomaterials ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Nanomaterial
