> [!NOTE]
> **The Flashlight Through a Window Analogy:**
> Imagine you are standing inside a dark room holding a thin sheet of frosted glass up to a bright flashlight. If you place a tiny insect wing behind the glass, the light silhouette reveals the veins and microstructure of the wing in extreme detail because the glass is thin enough to let light pass, but also structured enough to diffract it.
>
> In TEM:
> - The **flashlight** is replaced by a **high-voltage electron beam** (electrons have a de Broglie wavelength ~100,000├Ś shorter than visible light).
> - The **frosted glass** is the **ultra-thin specimen** (~50 nm thick).
> - The **silhouette/vein pattern** projected on the screen is the **TEM image** you record.
> - The **diffraction (rainbow-like splitting)** is the **electron diffraction pattern** that reveals crystal structure.
>
> Because electrons have such tiny wavelengths, you can "see" individual atomic columns ŌĆö something impossible with optical microscopes due to the diffraction limit of visible light.

## 1.3 Why TEM is Critical for Nanotechnology

| Property | Optical Microscope | SEM | **TEM** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength used | ~550 nm (visible) | ~0.01 nm (eŌü╗) | **~0.0017 nm ŌĆō 0.0087 nm (eŌü╗)** |
| Best resolution | ~200 nm | ~1ŌĆō10 nm | **Ōēł 0.05 ŌĆō 0.2 nm** |
| Information source | Reflected light | Surface eŌü╗ | **Transmitted + diffracted eŌü╗** |
| Magnification | ├Ś1000 | ├Ś100,000 | **├Ś1,000,000 ŌĆō ├Ś50,000,000** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU benchmark value:** At an accelerating voltage of **200 kV**, the relativistically corrected electron wavelength is approximately **╬╗ Ōēł 0.00251 nm (2.51 pm)**, which is the basis for atomic-resolution imaging.

## 1.4 Core Physical Constants Used in TEM

- Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│Ōü┤ J┬Ęs**
- Electron rest mass: **mŌéĆ = 9.109 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│┬╣ kg**
- Electron charge: **e = 1.602 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬╣Ōü╣ C**
- Speed of light: **c = 3.0 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s**
- Operating vacuum: **Ōēł 10Ōü╗ŌüĄ to 10Ōü╗ŌüĖ Pa**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö The "Why" and "How"

### Why electrons?
Optical microscopes are limited by the **Abbe diffraction limit**:

$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}$$

For visible light (╬╗ Ōēł 550 nm), the best resolution is ~200 nm ŌĆö useless for nanostructures. To resolve sub-nanometer features, we need **╬╗ << atomic spacing (~0.2 nm)**. Accelerated electrons satisfy this.

### How the image forms?
1. The **electron gun** (thermionic/FEG) emits electrons.
2. Electrons are accelerated to 60ŌĆō300 kV and acquire a tiny **de Broglie wavelength**.
3. **Condenser lenses** focus the beam onto the specimen.
4. Electrons interact with the specimen via elastic scattering (diffraction) and inelastic scattering (energy loss).
5. The **objective lens** forms the first intermediate image and diffraction pattern.
6. **Intermediate and projector lenses** magnify the image onto a **fluorescent screen / CCD camera**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU ESE numerical/derivation questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_0\,e\,V}}$ | Non-relativistic de Broglie wavelength | m |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_0\,e\,V\!\left(1+\dfrac{eV}{2m_0c^2}\right)}}$ | **Relativistically corrected** wavelength (mandatory for V > 50 kV) | m |
| 3 | $\lambda \approx \dfrac{1.227}{\sqrt{V\!\left(1+0.978\times10^{-6}V\right)}}$ (nm) | Practical form (V in volts) | nm |
| 4 | $d = \dfrac{0.61\,\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}$ | **Rayleigh resolution criterion** | m |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{D}{f}$ | Magnification of a thin lens | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{v}+\dfrac{1}{u}$ | Thin-lens equation | mŌü╗┬╣ |
| 7 | $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$ | **Bragg's law** for electron diffraction | m |
| 8 | $r_s = C_s\,\alpha^3$ | Radius of spherical aberration disk | m |
| 9 | $r_c = C_c\,\dfrac{\Delta E}{E}\,\alpha$ | Radius of chromatic aberration disk | m |
| 10 | $R = \dfrac{V}{\Delta V}$ | Energy resolution of electron source | dimensionless |
| 11 | $d_{kl} = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}}$ | Interplanar spacing (cubic) | m |
| 12 | $\xi_g = \dfrac{\pi V_c \cos\theta}{\lambda F_g}$ | Extinction distance (TEM contrast) | m |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's hint:** For numerical problems, ALWAYS use the **relativistic** de Broglie formula when V Ōēź 50 kV. The non-relativistic version gives wrong answers above this threshold.

## 2.3 ElectronŌĆōSpecimen Interactions (Engineering Utility)

When the high-energy electron beam strikes the ultra-thin specimen, several events occur:

- **Elastic scattering** ŌĆö no energy loss; produces **diffraction contrast** (Bragg scattering). Used for crystal structure, defects, grain boundaries.
- **Inelastic scattering** ŌĆö energy lost to specimen; produces:
  - Secondary electrons (low energy)
  - Characteristic X-rays (for EDS)
  - Plasmon losses (~15 eV)
  - Phonon excitation
  - Cathodoluminescence
- **Unscattered (direct) electrons** ŌĆö form **Bright Field (BF)** images.
- **Scattered electrons** ŌĆö form **Dark Field (DF)** images.
- **Diffracted beams** ŌĆö form **Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED)** patterns, revealing crystal symmetry and lattice parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** TEM is indispensable for:
> - Verifying quantum dot sizes in optoelectronic devices.
> - Characterizing grain boundaries in nanoceramics.
> - Identifying dislocations and stacking faults in semiconductor nanowires.
> - Confirming crystal phase (FCC/BCC/HCP) of metallic nanoparticles via SAED.
> - Measuring d-spacings in 2D materials (graphene, MoSŌéé).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Relativistic de Broglie Wavelength of an Electron

This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 3 for TEM. It is routinely asked for 7 marks.

### Starting Point ŌĆö Total Relativistic Energy

The total energy $E$ of an accelerated electron equals the kinetic energy gained from the accelerating voltage $V$:

$$E = eV$$

By Einstein's relativistic energyŌĆōmomentum relation:

$$E^2 = (p\,c)^2 + (m_0 c^2)^2$$

Solving for momentum $p$:

$$p\,c = \sqrt{E^2 - (m_0 c^2)^2} = \sqrt{(eV)^2 + 2\,eV\,m_0 c^2}$$

(Using the identity $A^2 - B^2 = (A-B)(A+B)$ and $A = m_0c^2 + eV$, $B = m_0c^2$.)

So:

$$p = \sqrt{2\,m_0 eV\!\left(1 + \frac{eV}{2\,m_0 c^2}\right)}$$

### Applying the de Broglie Relation

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

Substituting:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2\,m_0 eV\!\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2\,m_0 c^2}\right)}}$$

### Numerical Form (Vacuum, V in volts)

Plugging in physical constants:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs
- $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

$$\lambda \approx \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V\!\left(1 + 0.978\times 10^{-6}\,V\right)}} \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Benchmark Results (memorize for Part A):**
> - At **V = 100 kV**: ╬╗ Ōēł **0.00370 nm (3.70 pm)**
> - At **V = 200 kV**: ╬╗ Ōēł **0.00251 nm (2.51 pm)**
> - At **V = 300 kV**: ╬╗ Ōēł **0.00197 nm (1.97 pm)**

### Worked Example (KTU Typical Numerical)

> **Q:** Calculate the wavelength of an electron accelerated through 80 kV. Compare with visible light.
> **Solution:** Using the non-relativistic form (since 80 kV Ōēł 0.16 mc┬▓, correction is small but we use relativistic for accuracy):

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{80000\!\left(1+0.978\times 10^{-6}\times 80000\right)}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{80000\,(1+0.0782)}} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{86256}} \approx \frac{1.227}{293.7}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.00418 \text{ nm}}$$

Compared to visible light (╬╗ Ōēł 550 nm), this is **~131,500├Ś shorter** ŌĆö explaining why TEM achieves sub-├ģngstrom resolution.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Resolving Power of TEM

The minimum resolvable distance is governed by:

$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61\,\lambda}{n\sin\alpha}$$

For an electromagnetic lens, $n \approx 1$ and $\alpha \approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ rad.

For 200 kV electrons (╬╗ = 0.00251 nm) and ╬▒ = 10Ōü╗┬▓ rad:

$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61 \times 0.00251}{1 \times 0.01} \approx 0.153 \text{ nm}$$

Thus **atomic resolution (Ōēł 0.1 nm)** is achievable in modern TEM.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Camera Length and SAED Pattern Indexing

In Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED), the camera constant $L\lambda$ relates ring/spot radius $R$ to interplanar spacing $d$:

$$R\,d = L\,\lambda$$

This arises from Bragg's law combined with the small-angle approximation in TEM geometry:

$$2d\sin\theta = \lambda \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 2d\theta \approx \lambda \;\;\text{(for small }\theta)$$

Since the diffraction angle $\theta \approx R/L$ (where $L$ is the camera length):

$$\frac{R}{L} = \frac{\lambda}{2d} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R = \frac{L\lambda}{2d}$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{R\,d = L\lambda}$$

> **Worked KTU Example:** A TEM is operated at 200 kV (╬╗ = 2.51 pm) with a camera length L = 100 cm. The (111) diffraction spot of an FCC metal appears at radius R = 12.5 mm. Find the lattice parameter a.
>
> $$d_{111} = \frac{L\lambda}{R} = \frac{(1.0)(2.51\times 10^{-12})}{12.5\times 10^{-3}} = 2.008\times 10^{-10}\text{ m}$$
>
> For cubic: $a = d_{111}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2} = 2.008\times 10^{-10} \times \sqrt{3}$
>
> $$\boxed{a \approx 3.478 \text{ ├ģ}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Imaging Modes of TEM

| Mode | Aperture Used | Electrons Selected | Contrast Type | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bright Field (BF)** | Objective aperture on optic axis | Direct (undiffracted) beam | MassŌĆōthickness + diffraction | General morphology |
| **Dark Field (DF)** | Objective aperture on diffracted beam | One specific diffracted beam | Diffraction contrast | Defect/imaging |
| **High-Resolution TEM (HRTEM)** | Large aperture or none | Multiple beams interfere | Phase contrast | Atomic column imaging |
| **SAED** | Selected-area aperture | Diffracted beams form spots/rings | Reciprocal space | Crystal structure/phase ID |
| **STEM (Scanning TEM)** | ŌĆö | Convergent beam rastered | Z-contrast (HAADF) | Atomic number mapping |
| **EELS** | ŌĆö | Energy-loss electrons | Inelastic | Elemental/electronic states |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a TEM Column

The following Mermaid block represents the **electron-optical path** through a TEM, from source to detector. It satisfies the KTU 2024 expectation of understanding component sequence.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EGUN Electron Gun W or LaB6 or FEG] --> B[ANOD Anode Accelerating Voltage 60 to 300 kV]
    B --> C[CL1 Condenser Lens 1 Beam size control]
    C --> D[CL2 Condenser Lens 2 Beam convergence]
    D --> E[SA Selector Aperture]
    E --> F[SPC Ultra thin Specimen less than 100 nm]
    F --> G[OL Objective Lens Primary image formation]
    G --> H[OA Objective Aperture BF or DF selection]
    H --> I[IL Intermediate Lens Magnification stage 1]
    I --> J[PL Projector Lens Magnification stage 2]
    J --> K[FS Fluorescent Screen]
    J --> L[CCD CCD Camera Digital recording]
    F -.-> M[SAED Selected Area Diffraction Mode]
    M --> J
    G --> N[VAC High Vacuum System 10 to the power minus 5 to minus 8 Pa]
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram ŌĆö Image Formation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Electron Source] --> P2[Condenser System]
    P2 --> P3[Specimen Stage]
    P3 --> P4[Objective Lens]
    P4 --> P5[Intermediate and Projector Lenses]
    P5 --> P6[Detector Screen or CCD]
    P4 --> P7[SAED Diffraction Plane]
    P7 --> P6
```

## 4.3 Schematic of ElectronŌĆōSpecimen Interaction Outcomes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Incident Electron Beam 200 kV] --> Q2[Ultra thin Specimen]
    Q2 --> R1[Unscattered electrons BF image]
    Q2 --> R2[Elastically scattered electrons DF and diffraction]
    Q2 --> R3[Inelastically scattered electrons]
    R3 --> S1[Secondary electrons]
    R3 --> S2[Characteristic X rays for EDS]
    R3 --> S3[Plasmon losses for EELS]
    R3 --> S4[Cathodoluminescence photons]
    Q2 --> R4[Transmitted electrons main TEM signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation note for KTU students:** Of every 10,000 incident electrons in TEM, almost all pass through the thin foil because the specimen is so thin. Only a small fraction undergo scattering events ŌĆö but this fraction *carries all the structural and chemical information*. The vacuum system ensures that stray electrons do not interfere with the imaging beam.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define Transmission Electron Microscopy. Why is an ultra-thin specimen required in TEM?**

**Model Answer:**
Transmission Electron Microscopy is a characterization technique in which a high-energy electron beam (typically 60ŌĆō300 kV) is transmitted through an ultra-thin specimen (< 100 nm). The transmitted and diffracted electrons are used to form high-resolution images and diffraction patterns revealing morphology, crystal structure, and atomic arrangement at sub-nanometer scales.

An ultra-thin specimen is required because:
1. **Electrons have low penetration depth** ŌĆö a thick specimen would absorb/scatter electrons excessively, preventing transmission.
2. **Single-scattering approximation** must hold for clean image interpretation; thin foils ensure this.
3. **High-resolution imaging** demands minimal chromatic broadening from plural scattering.
4. **Vacuum compatibility** ŌĆö thin foils can be mounted on 3 mm copper grids.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model]**
**What is the role of the objective lens in a TEM?**

**Model Answer:**
The objective lens is the **most critical lens** in a TEM. It is the first lens after the specimen and performs three essential functions:
1. Forms the **first magnified image** of the specimen.
2. Forms the **diffraction pattern** in the back focal plane.
3. Determines the **ultimate resolution** of the microscope.

Any spherical ($C_s$) or chromatic ($C_c$) aberration in the objective lens directly limits the resolution. Hence, modern TEMs use objective lens aberration correctors.

---

## 5.2 PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Forgetting the **relativistic correction** for V Ōēź 50 kV ŌĆö costs 2ŌĆō3 marks.
> - Not mentioning **why vacuum is required** (electron mean free path) ŌĆö common 1-mark loss.
> - Confusing **BF vs DF image formation** ŌĆö read the aperture position carefully.
> - In SAED indexing, students often forget the cubic factor $\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ ŌĆö losing 3 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option 1**

#### (a) Derive the relativistic de Broglie wavelength expression for an electron accelerated through a potential V. Hence calculate the wavelength at 200 kV. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Total energy from acceleration:**
The electron gains kinetic energy equal to $eV$, which by relativity is the *total* energy minus rest energy:
$$E_{total} = m_0c^2 + eV$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Relativistic energyŌĆōmomentum relation:**
$$E_{total}^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_0c^2)^2$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute and simplify:**
$$(m_0c^2 + eV)^2 = (pc)^2 + (m_0c^2)^2$$

$$(pc)^2 = (m_0c^2 + eV)^2 - (m_0c^2)^2$$

Using $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$:

$$(pc)^2 = [(m_0c^2 + eV) - m_0c^2][(m_0c^2 + eV) + m_0c^2] = eV(2m_0c^2 + eV)$$

$$p = \sqrt{\frac{eV(2m_0c^2 + eV)}{c^2}} = \sqrt{2m_0eV\!\left(1 + \frac{eV}{2m_0c^2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö de Broglie wavelength:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_0eV\!\left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2m_0c^2}\right)}}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Numerical evaluation at V = 200 kV:**
Using the practical form:
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{200000\,(1 + 0.978\times 10^{-6}\times 200000)}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{200000\,(1 + 0.1956)}} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{239120}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{488.99}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 2.51 \text{ pm}}$$

#### (b) Describe the major components of a TEM with their functions. Why is a high vacuum essential? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Electron gun** (W/LaBŌéå/FEG) | Source of electrons via thermionic or field emission |
| **Anode + accelerating stack** | Accelerates electrons to 60ŌĆō300 kV |
| **Condenser lenses (C1, C2)** | Demagnify the source and control beam convergence |
| **Specimen holder** | Holds 3 mm grid; allows translation, tilt, heating, cooling |
| **Objective lens** | Forms first image and diffraction pattern; determines resolution |
| **Objective aperture** | Selects transmitted (BF) or scattered (DF) electrons |
| **Intermediate + projector lenses** | Provide variable magnification (├Ś50 to ├Ś1,500,000) |
| **Fluorescent screen** | Converts electron image to visible light |
| **CCD camera** | Digital recording |
| **Vacuum system** | Maintains 10Ōü╗ŌüĄŌĆō10Ōü╗ŌüĖ Pa pressure |

**Why high vacuum is essential:**
1. Electrons have a **finite mean free path** in air (~ ╬╝m). Air molecules would scatter the beam.
2. Prevents **oxidation/contamination** of the specimen and electron gun filament.
3. Avoids **electrical breakdown** in the high-voltage column.
4. Ensures **clean diffraction patterns** without gas scattering halos.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Internal Choice Option 2**

#### (a) Explain the principle of Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED). How is it used to determine crystal structure? (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
SAED uses a **selected-area aperture** placed in the image plane of the objective lens. The aperture restricts the diffraction pattern to a specific region of the specimen (typically 0.1ŌĆō1 ╬╝m diameter). Electrons diffracted from this region form a pattern of spots (single crystal) or rings (polycrystalline) on the screen.

**Formation of diffraction pattern:**
- The back focal plane of the objective lens contains the **Fourier transform** of the specimen's exit wavefunction.
- For a single crystal: **regular array of spots** at positions satisfying the Laue condition.
- For polycrystalline: **concentric rings** because many orientations are sampled.

**Determination of crystal structure:**

1. **Measure ring radii R** or spot positions.
2. Use camera constant $L\lambda$:
$$R\,d_{hkl} = L\lambda$$
3. Compute $d_{hkl}$ for each ring.
4. Compare ratio $d_n/d_1$ for successive rings:

| Crystal | Ratios $d_n/d_1$ |
|---|---|
| FCC | $1 : 1/\sqrt{2} : 1/\sqrt{3} : 1/\sqrt{4} : 1/\sqrt{5}$ |
| BCC | $1 : 1/\sqrt{2} : 1/\sqrt{3} : 1/\sqrt{4} : 1/\sqrt{5}$ |
| HCP | Different ratios (1, 1/ŌłÜ3, 1/ŌłÜ4, 1/ŌłÜ7, ...) |

5. Match ratios ŌåÆ identify structure (FCC/BCC/HCP).
6. Compute lattice parameter:
$$a = d_{hkl}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$$

#### (b) An electron is accelerated through 120 kV. Compute (i) its de Broglie wavelength, (ii) its velocity. Compare the result with the non-relativistic case. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Relativistic wavelength at V = 120 kV:**

Using the practical formula:
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V(1+0.978\times10^{-6}V)}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{120000\,(1+0.978\times10^{-6}\times 120000)}}$$

$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{120000\,(1+0.1174)}} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{134085}} = \frac{1.227}{366.18}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{rel} \approx 3.351 \times 10^{-3} \text{ nm} = 3.35 \text{ pm}}$$

**(ii) Relativistic velocity:**

Using the relativistic momentum we derived earlier and $\lambda = h/p$:

$$v_{rel} = \frac{pc^2}{E_{total}} = \frac{pc^2}{m_0c^2 + eV}$$

From $p = h/\lambda = (6.626\times10^{-34})/(3.351\times10^{-12}) = 1.977\times10^{-22}$ kg┬Ęm/s

$$E_{total} = m_0c^2 + eV = 8.187\times10^{-14} + (1.602\times10^{-19})(120000)$$

$$E_{total} = 8.187\times10^{-14} + 1.922\times10^{-14} = 1.011\times10^{-13} \text{ J}$$

$$v_{rel} = \frac{(1.977\times10^{-22})(9\times10^{16})}{1.011\times10^{-13}} = \frac{1.779\times10^{-5}}{1.011\times10^{-13}}$$

$$\boxed{v_{rel} \approx 1.760 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.587c}$$

**Non-relativistic comparison:**
$$v_{nr} = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{2(1.602\times10^{-19})(120000)}{9.109\times10^{-31}}}$$

$$v_{nr} = \sqrt{4.22\times10^{16}} \approx 2.054\times10^{8} \text{ m/s} \approx 0.685c$$

$$\lambda_{nr} = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{120000}} = \frac{1.227}{346.41} \approx 3.54 \text{ pm}$$

**Error percentage:**
$$\Delta\lambda = \frac{3.54 - 3.35}{3.35} \times 100\% \approx 5.7\%$$

This justifies why the relativistic formula is mandatory above 50 kV.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **TEM uses transmitted electrons** ŌĆö specimen MUST be < 100 nm thick.
- [ ] **de Broglie wavelength** of 100 kV electrons Ōēł **3.70 pm**; of 200 kV electrons Ōēł **2.51 pm**.
- [ ] **ALWAYS use relativistic formula** for V Ōēź 50 kV in KTU problems.
- [ ] **Rayleigh criterion**: $d = 0.61\lambda / (n\sin\alpha)$ governs resolution.
- [ ] **Objective lens** is the heart of TEM ŌĆö sets the resolution limit.
- [ ] **BF image** uses direct beam; **DF image** uses one diffracted beam.
- [ ] **SAED camera equation**: $R \cdot d_{hkl} = L\lambda$.
- [ ] **FCC ring ratios**: $d_n/d_1 = 1 : 1/\sqrt{2} : 1/\sqrt{3} : 1/\sqrt{4} \ldots$ ŌĆö memorize this.
- [ ] **High vacuum (10Ōü╗ŌüĄ to 10Ōü╗ŌüĖ Pa)** is mandatory for electron mean free path and filament life.
- [ ] **HRTEM** uses phase contrast from multiple beams ŌĆö gives atomic column images.
- [ ] **STEM-HAADF** gives Z-contrast ŌĆö heavy atoms appear brighter.
- [ ] **EELS** detects inelastic losses for chemical/electronic state analysis.
- [ ] **Spherical aberration** $r_s = C_s\alpha^3$ ŌĆö corrected by hexapole correctors in modern TEM.
- [ ] **Bragg's law** $2d\sin\theta = n\lambda$ is the foundation of all electron diffraction.
- [ ] **Comparison tip:** SEM sees surface; TEM sees internal structure at atomic scale.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line exam mnemonic:** **"Electrons Enter, Lenses Image, Objective Decides"** ŌĆö remember the sequence **Gun ŌåÆ Condenser ŌåÆ Specimen ŌåÆ Objective ŌåÆ Projector ŌåÆ Screen** for any TEM diagram question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)** is a high-resolution type of **scanning probe microscopy (SPM)** that operates in real space at the nanometer and atomic scale, with demonstrated resolution on the order of fractions of a nanometer (sub-nanometer, typically **0.1 nm to 1 nm lateral** and **0.01 nm vertical**). It achieves this by sensing the **interatomic forces** (predominantly short-range **van der Waals**, **repulsive**, **capillary**, **chemical**, and **electrostatic** forces) between a sharp probe tip (radius of curvature typically **2 nm ŌĆō 30 nm**) attached to the end of a flexible microcantilever (spring constant **0.01 N/m to 100 N/m**) and the sample surface. Unlike Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM), which requires conductive samples, AFM can image **conductors, insulators, biological specimens, polymers, and ceramics** in ambient, vacuum, or liquid environments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** AFM belongs to the family of Scanning Probe Microscopies (SPMs). The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes understanding the difference between Contact Mode, Non-Contact Mode, and Tapping (Intermittent Contact) Mode ŌĆö a frequent 7-mark and 14-mark exam question. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The Blind Man's Cane Reading Braille Imagine a **blind person reading a Braille page**: - The **finger** acts as the **AFM tip** (the sharp probe). - The **hand's muscles and nerves** act as the **cantilever and piezoelectric sensor**, registering every small bump and dip. - As the finger moves across the page, the brain maps out every dot, recreating the surface topography. In AFM, instead of a finger, a **silicon or silicon nitride microcantilever with a nanometer-sharp tip** is raster-scanned across a sample using a **piezoelectric scanner** (precision **< 0.01 nm**). The cantilever bends in response to surface forces, and a **laser-photodiode detector** measures this deflection with sub-├ģngstrom precision. A **feedback loop** then adjusts the tipŌĆōsample distance to maintain a constant force (or amplitude/phase), producing a 3D topographical map. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used Throughout AFM Physics:** > - Boltzmann Constant: **$k_B = 1.380649 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$** > - Planck's Constant: **$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$** > - Young's Modulus of Silicon: **$E_{Si} \approx 130 - 190 \, \text{GPa}$** > - Poisson's Ratio of Silicon: **$\nu \approx 0.27$** > - Typical Tip Radius: **$R_{tip} \approx 2 - 30 \, \text{nm}$** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle ŌĆö The "Why" and "How" The AFM system operates through five interlocking subsystems: **Step 1 ŌĆö The Tip-Sample Interaction (Why does the cantilever bend?)** - The tip experiences an interatomic force **$F_{ts}$** as a function of tipŌĆōsample separation **$z$**. This force has contributions from: - Short-range **Pauli repulsive forces** (when $z < 0.5$ nm, $F \sim 10^{-8}$ N) - Long-range **van der Waals attractive forces** ($F \sim 10^{-12}$ N at $z \approx 1$ nm) - **Capillary forces** from ambient water layers ($F \sim 10^{-8}$ N) - **Electrostatic** and **chemical** forces depending on sample. **Step 2 ŌĆö Mechanical Transduction (How is force converted to a measurable signal?)** - The cantilever behaves as a Hookean spring. The applied force deflects it by $\Delta z$ according to **Hooke's Law**: $F = k \cdot \Delta z$, where **$k$** is the **cantilever spring constant** (typically 0.01 to 100 N/m). - This deflection is **optically amplified**: a laser beam reflects off the back of the cantilever onto a **position-sensitive photodetector (PSPD)** with a four-quadrant photodiode. The angular change of the reflected beam yields sub-├ģngstrom vertical sensitivity. **Step 3 ŌĆö Piezoelectric Scanning (How is the sample moved?)** - A **piezoelectric tube scanner** controls the tipŌĆōsample position with **sub-nanometer precision** in $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes. - Applying a voltage $V$ produces a displacement $d = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot L$ (where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient). **Step 4 ŌĆö Feedback Control (How is constant interaction maintained?)** - The photodiode signal is compared to a **setpoint** in a **Proportional-Integral (PI) feedback controller**. - The controller adjusts the $z$-piezo voltage to maintain a constant cantilever deflection (Contact Mode), oscillation amplitude (Tapping Mode), or frequency shift (Non-Contact/Frequency Modulation Mode). - The feedback output IS the topography image. **Step 5 ŌĆö Image Reconstruction (How is a 3D image formed?)** - The raster-scanned ($x,y$) position is correlated with the feedback $z$-signal, producing a 2D topograph. Each pixel represents the height of the surface at that location. ### 2.2 Three Primary Operating Modes | Mode | Tip-Sample Gap | Force Regime | Cantilever Oscillation | Typical Application | |------|----------------|--------------|------------------------|---------------------| | **Contact Mode** | $0.1 - 0.5 \, \text{nm}$ | Repulsive ($10^{-8}$ to $10^{-9}$ N) | Not oscillating | Hard, flat samples; atomic resolution on mica/HOPG | | **Tapping Mode** (Intermittent Contact) | $0.5 - 2 \, \text{nm}$ | Cyclic (repulsive ŌåÆ attractive) | Near resonance amplitude $A_0 \sim 10 - 100 \, \text{nm}$ | Soft samples, polymers, biological specimens | | **Non-Contact Mode** | $1 - 10 \, \text{nm}$ | Attractive (long-range vdW) | At resonance $f_0$, $A \sim 1 - 5 \, \text{nm}$ | Ultra-clean surfaces under UHV | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells** to avoid breaking markdown table syntax (per protocol). | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value | |---------|-------------|----------------------| | $F = k \cdot \Delta z$ | Hooke's Law for cantilever deflection | $F$ in N, $k$ in N/m, $\Delta z$ in m | | $k = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$ | Rectangular cantilever spring constant | $E$: Young's modulus (Pa), $w$: width, $t$: thickness, $L$: length | | $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$ | Cantilever resonance frequency | $m^{*}$: effective mass (kg), typically $50 - 500 \, \text{kHz}$ | | $F_{vdW}(D) = \dfrac{-A_H \cdot R}{6 D^{2}}$ | Van der Waals force between sphere (tip) and flat surface | $A_H$: Hamaker constant ($\sim 10^{-19}$ J), $R$: tip radius, $D$: gap | | $U_{LJ}(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$ | Lennard-Jones 6-12 potential | $\varepsilon$: well depth, $\sigma$: zero-crossing distance | | $F_{LJ}(r) = -\dfrac{dU}{dr} = 24\varepsilon \left[ \dfrac{2\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$ | Lennard-Jones interatomic force | N | | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0}{2k} \cdot \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | Frequency shift in Non-Contact Mode (force gradient detection) | Hz | | $F_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_B T \cdot k}{\pi \cdot Q \cdot f_0}}$ | Minimum detectable force (thermal noise limit) | N | | $\delta_{cantilever} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}$ | Static cantilever tip deflection | m | | $A_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_B T \cdot Q}{\pi \cdot k \cdot f_0}}$ | Minimum detectable amplitude | m | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Spring Constant for a Rectangular Beam The AFM cantilever is modeled as an **end-loaded rectangular beam** of length $L$, width $w$, and thickness $t$. For small deflections, EulerŌĆōBernoulli beam theory applies. **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the bending moment.** For a concentrated load $F$ at the free end of a cantilever fixed at $x=0$, the bending moment at position $x$ is: $$M(x) = F(L - x)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the EulerŌĆōBernoulli equation.** The local radius of curvature is related to the bending moment and flexural rigidity $EI$: $$EI \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}z}{dx^{2}} = M(x) = F(L - x)$$ where $I = \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}$ is the second moment of area for a rectangular cross-section. **Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate once to obtain slope.** $$EI \cdot \dfrac{dz}{dx} = F \left( Lx - \dfrac{x^{2}}{2} \right) + C_1$$ The boundary condition at the fixed end ($x = 0$, slope = 0) gives $C_1 = 0$. **Step 4 ŌĆö Integrate again to obtain deflection.** $$EI \cdot z(x) = F \left( \dfrac{Lx^{2}}{2} - \dfrac{x^{3}}{6} \right) + C_2$$ The boundary condition at $x = 0$ gives $C_2 = 0$. **Step 5 ŌĆö Evaluate deflection at the free end ($x = L$).** $$\delta = z(L) = \dfrac{F}{EI} \left( \dfrac{L^{3}}{2} - \dfrac{L^{3}}{6} \right) = \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3EI}$$ Substituting $I = \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}$: $$\delta = \dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot \dfrac{wt^{3}}{12}} = \dfrac{4 F L^{3}}{E w t^{3}}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Solve for the spring constant $k = F/\delta$.** $$k = \dfrac{F}{\delta} = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$$ This is the **standard rectangular cantilever spring constant formula** universally used in AFM design. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Interpretation:** The spring constant scales as $t^{3}/L^{3}$. A 10├Ś thinner or 10├Ś longer cantilever makes $k$ **1000├Ś softer**. This is why soft cantilevers (used in biological AFM) are long and thin. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Van der Waals Force Between Spherical Tip and Flat Surface The AFM tip is approximated as a **sphere of radius $R$**, and the sample as an infinite flat plane. We integrate the Lennard-Jones pair potential over all atoms in both bodies (Hamaker summation). **Step 1 ŌĆö Hamaker pairwise summation.** The interaction energy between a sphere of radius $R$ and a flat surface separated by distance $D$ is: $$U_{vdW}(D) = -\dfrac{A_H \cdot R}{6 D}$$ where $A_H$ is the **Hamaker constant** (typically $A_H \approx 6 \times 10^{-20}$ to $1 \times 10^{-19}$ J for most solid-air systems). **Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiate to obtain force.** Force is the negative gradient of potential energy: $$F_{vdW}(D) = -\dfrac{dU}{dD} = -\dfrac{d}{dD}\left[-\dfrac{A_H R}{6 D}\right]$$ $$F_{vdW}(D) = -\dfrac{A_H R}{6} \cdot \left( -\dfrac{1}{D^{2}} \right) \cdot (-1) = -\dfrac{A_H \cdot R}{6 D^{2}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Sign convention.** The negative sign indicates that the force is **attractive** (pulls tip toward surface). The magnitude is: $$\vert F_{vdW} \vert = \dfrac{A_H \cdot R}{6 D^{2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Estimate:** With $A_H = 10^{-19}$ J, $R = 20$ nm, $D = 0.5$ nm: $\vert F \vert = \dfrac{10^{-19} \times 20 \times 10^{-9}}{6 \times (0.5 \times 10^{-9})^{2}} \approx 1.33 \times 10^{-9}$ N (Ōēł 1.3 nN) ŌĆö typical AFM operating force in non-contact regime. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Resonance Frequency of AFM Cantilever The cantilever is modeled as a **simple harmonic oscillator** with effective mass $m^{*}$ and spring constant $k$. **Step 1 ŌĆö Equation of motion.** Newton's second law for free oscillation (no damping): $$m^{*} \ddot{x} + kx = 0$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Assume harmonic solution.** Let $x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega t)$. Then $\ddot{x} = -\omega^{2} x$. $$-m^{*} \omega^{2} X_0 \cos(\omega t) + k X_0 \cos(\omega t) = 0$$ $$(k - m^{*}\omega^{2}) X_0 \cos(\omega t) = 0$$ For non-trivial solutions: $$\omega^{2} = \dfrac{k}{m^{*}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Convert angular frequency to Hz.** $$f_{0} = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$$ For a rectangular cantilever, the **effective mass** is approximately $m^{*} \approx 0.24 \rho L w t$, where $\rho$ is the density of the cantilever material. > [!NOTE] > **Example:** For a silicon cantilever ($E = 170$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m┬│) with $L = 200$ ┬Ąm, $w = 40$ ┬Ąm, $t = 1$ ┬Ąm: > $k = \dfrac{(170 \times 10^{9})(40 \times 10^{-6})(10^{-6})^{3}}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}} \approx 0.21$ N/m > $m^{*} \approx 0.24 \times 2330 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{-6} \approx 4.47 \times 10^{-12}$ kg > $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.21}{4.47 \times 10^{-12}}} \approx 34.5$ kHz --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Lennard-Jones Force Curve (Theoretical Force-Distance Curve) The total interaction between tip and sample is captured by the **Lennard-Jones 6-12 potential**: $$U(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Differentiate to find force.** $$F(r) = -\dfrac{dU}{dr} = -4\varepsilon \left[ -12 \dfrac{\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} + 6 \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$$ $$F(r) = 24\varepsilon \left[ \dfrac{2\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}} \right]$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Find equilibrium (zero-force) separation.** Setting $F(r_0) = 0$: $$\dfrac{2\sigma^{12}}{r_0^{13}} = \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r_0^{7}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_0 = 2^{1/6} \sigma \approx 1.122 \, \sigma$$ This is the **equilibrium contact distance**. **Step 3 ŌĆö Find minimum potential depth.** Substituting $r_0$ back into $U(r)$: $$U_{min} = U(2^{1/6}\sigma) = 4\varepsilon\left[\left(\dfrac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{2^{1/6}\sigma}\right)^{6}\right]$$ $$U_{min} = 4\varepsilon\left[2^{-2} - 2^{-1}\right] = 4\varepsilon\left[\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right] = -\varepsilon$$ So the **minimum potential energy is $-\varepsilon$**, confirming that $\varepsilon$ is the well depth. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: AFM vs. Competing SPM/EM Techniques | Parameter | **AFM** | **STM** | **SEM** | **TEM** | |-----------|---------|---------|---------|---------| | Max. Resolution | 0.1 nm lateral | 0.01 nm lateral | 1 nm | 0.05 nm | | Sample Type | Any (C/Insulator) | Conductive only | Conductive (coated insulators) | Ultra-thin (<100 nm) | | Environment | Air, liquid, vacuum | Mostly vacuum | Vacuum | High vacuum | | 3D Topography | **Yes (true 3D)** | Yes (with care) | No (2D projection) | No (2D projection) | | Typical Scan Time | Minutes | Minutes | Seconds | Minutes-Hours | | Sample Damage | Minimal (in non-contact) | Possible | High (electron beam damage) | High | | Cost | Moderate | Moderate | High | Very High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 AFM Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture Flow The following Mermaid diagram represents the complete functional architecture of an Atomic Force Microscope, illustrating the **signal flow** between the mechanical, optical, electronic, and computational subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Piezoelectric XY Scanner"] -->|"Moves tip/sample in X-Y raster"| B["AFM Tip on Cantilever"] B -->|"Tip-sample interatomic force F_ts"| C["Microcantilever Deflection"] C -->|"Laser beam reflection"| D["Position Sensitive Photodiode PSPD"] D -->|"Voltage signal V_pd"| E["Signal Amplifier and Filter"] E -->|"V_signal"| F["Feedback Controller PI"] G["User Setpoint V_set"] --> F F -->|"Error signal e = V_set - V_signal"| H["High Voltage Amplifier"] H -->|"Z-piezo voltage V_z"| I["Piezoelectric Z Scanner"] I -->|"Adjusts tip-sample gap Z"| B F -->|"Topography signal Z_feedback"| J["Data Acquisition ADC"] A -->|"XY position sync"| J J -->|"Digital topography map"| K["Computer Display 3D Image"] L["Cantilever Excitation AC Source"] -->|"dither at f_0"| B E -->|"Amplitude / phase signal"| F ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram:** The closed-loop pathway runs B ŌåÆ C ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ E ŌåÆ F ŌåÆ H ŌåÆ I ŌåÆ B. The topography signal is taken from the **feedback output** $V_z$ required to maintain the setpoint, NOT from the raw cantilever deflection. The data acquisition is synchronized with the XY scanner position. ### 4.2 Operational Mode Selection ŌĆö Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Start: Choose AFM Mode"] --> Q{"Sample stiffness and type?"} Q -->|"Hard inorganic
Mica, HOPG, Silicon"| R["Contact Mode"] Q -->|"Soft polymer
Biological sample"| S["Tapping Mode"] Q -->|"Atomically flat UHV
Atomic resolution"| T["Non-Contact Mode"] R --> R1["Constant deflection
F setpoint ~ nN"] S --> S1["Constant amplitude
A setpoint ~ 80% A0"] T --> T1["Constant frequency shift
delta f setpoint ~ -50 Hz"] ``` ### 4.3 Force-Distance Curve Conceptual Plot (Theoretical Reference) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Large separation D > 10 nm
Zero force"] -->|"Approach tip"| B["D approx 1 to 5 nm
Van der Waals attraction grows"] B -->|"Continue approach"| C["Jump-to-contact
at gradient > k
Mechanical instability"] C -->|"D approx 0.2 to 0.5 nm
Repulsive regime"| D["Contact minimum
Equilibrium r0 approx 1.122 sigma"] D -->|"Withdraw tip"| E["Adhesion
Capillary and chemical bonds
Hysteresis loop"] E -->|"Snap-out of contact"| F["Free cantilever
Zero force"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Examiner Tip:** The "jump-to-contact" and "snap-out" features are unstable branches that arise because the cantilever spring constant $k$ is finite. If $\partial F / \partial z > k$, the system becomes mechanically unstable and the tip snaps uncontrollably. ### 4.4 AFM Cantilever Schematic ŌĆö Physical Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Substrate Chip"] --> B["Cantilever Beam
L approx 100 to 300 um"] B --> C["Integrated Sharp Tip
R approx 2 to 30 nm
Height approx 10 to 15 um"] B --> D["Reflective Coating on Back
Gold or Aluminum
Thickness approx 20 to 40 nm"] E["Sample Surface"] -->|"Raster scanned
under tip"| C F["Incident Laser Beam"] -->|"Strikes reflective back"| D D -->|"Reflected beam to PSPD"| G["Four-Quadrant Photodiode"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Physical Layout:** The reflective coating on the back of the cantilever is critical ŌĆö it provides the optical mirror surface required for laser-based deflection sensing. The tip is fabricated at the free end of the cantilever using silicon etching or focused ion beam (FIB) milling. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### **PART A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024 Model]** **"Differentiate between Contact Mode and Tapping Mode of AFM operation."** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **Contact Mode** | **Tapping Mode** | |-----------|------------------|------------------| | Tip-sample gap | $0.1 - 0.5$ nm (in contact) | $0.5 - 2$ nm (intermittent) | | Force regime | Repulsive ($10^{-8}$ N) | Cyclic repulsiveŌĆōattractive | | Cantilever state | Not oscillating; static deflection | Oscillating near resonance | | Setpoint | Constant deflection | Constant amplitude (typically 80% of free amplitude) | | Lateral (shear) force | High ŌĆö can drag soft samples | Negligible ŌĆö eliminates friction | | Sample suitability | Hard, robust samples | Soft, fragile, biological | | Image information | Height only | Height, Phase, Amplitude | **(3 Marks awarded for tabulated comparison with at least 4 distinguishing points.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023 Model]** **"What is the role of the piezoelectric scanner in AFM?"** (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The **pieelectric scanner** (typically a piezoelectric tube or stack actuator) serves three critical functions in AFM: 1. **Coarse Positioning:** Moves the tip or sample holder over macroscopic distances (mm range) for initial alignment. 2. **Fine XY Scanning:** Provides the **raster-scan motion** in the lateral plane with sub-nanometer precision, controlled by applied voltages $V_x(t)$ and $V_y(t)$. 3. **Fine Z Control:** Modulates the tipŌĆōsample separation along the vertical axis using $V_z(t)$ as dictated by the feedback loop, with displacement given by $d = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot L$ where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient. Without the piezoelectric scanner, atomic-scale imaging would be impossible due to mechanical vibrations and lack of sub-nanometer positional control. --- ### **PART B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice Modeled After KTU ESE)** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3(a) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö KTU Model 2024]** **"With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM). Discuss the role of the cantilever and the laser-photodiode detection system."** (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Construction:** The AFM consists of the following subsystems (refer to Section 4.1 diagram): 1. **Microcantilever with Sharp Tip:** Fabricated from silicon or silicon nitride using MEMS techniques. Tip radius typically 2ŌĆō30 nm; spring constant $k = 0.01 - 100$ N/m. 2. **Piezoelectric Scanner:** A tube or stack that positions the sample in $x$, $y$, $z$ with sub-nanometer precision. 3. **Laser Source:** A low-power laser diode (typically 1 mW, $\lambda = 630 - 670$ nm) focused on the back of the cantilever. 4. **Position-Sensitive Photodiode (PSPD):** A four-quadrant photodiode that detects the reflected laser position. 5. **Feedback Electronics:** A PI controller that compares the photodiode signal to a setpoint and adjusts the $z$-piezo voltage. 6. **Computer and Display:** Acquires and reconstructs the topography image. **Working Principle:** The tip is brought into proximity of the sample surface until **interatomic forces** between tip and sample atoms deflect the cantilever. This deflection changes the angle of the reflected laser beam, which produces a measurable displacement $\Delta V$ on the PSPD. The feedback loop adjusts the $z$-piezo to maintain constant $V_{PSPD}$ (setpoint), and the required $z$-correction at each $(x,y)$ position forms the topography image. **Role of the Cantilever:** The cantilever acts as a **force transducer**. It converts the tipŌĆōsample force $F_{ts}$ into a measurable mechanical deflection $\Delta z = F_{ts}/k$. For soft cantilevers ($k \sim 0.01$ N/m), a force of 1 nN produces a 100 nm deflection ŌĆö easily measurable. **Role of the Laser-Photodiode System:** The optical lever amplifies small cantilever deflections. A deflection $\Delta z$ at the tip produces a beam displacement $\Delta x \approx 2L_{OD} \cdot \Delta z / L_c$ on the PSPD, where $L_{OD}$ is the optical lever arm (typically 4ŌĆō8 cm) and $L_c$ is the cantilever length. This optical amplification yields sub-├ģngstrom vertical resolution. **(7 Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for principle, 1 for cantilever, 2 for laser-photodiode.)** --- **Q3(b) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö KTU Model 2024]** **"Explain the Lennard-Jones potential curve and its significance in AFM. Derive the equilibrium separation and minimum energy."** (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** The Lennard-Jones 6-12 potential models the **interatomic interaction** between tip and sample atoms: $$U(r) = 4 \varepsilon \left[ \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$ where: - $\varepsilon$ = depth of the potential well (energy) - $\sigma$ = distance at which $U(r) = 0$ - $r$ = interatomic separation - The $r^{-12}$ term models short-range **Pauli repulsion** - The $r^{-6}$ term models long-range **van der Waals attraction** **Significance in AFM:** The LJ curve gives the **force-distance relationship** that governs all AFM tipŌĆōsample interactions. The **attractive well** determines the non-contact regime; the **repulsive wall** governs contact-mode imaging. The balance of these forces determines image contrast. **Derivation of Equilibrium Separation $r_0$:** The corresponding force is: $$F(r) = -\dfrac{dU}{dr} = 24\varepsilon\left[\dfrac{2\sigma^{12}}{r^{13}} - \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r^{7}}\right]$$ Setting $F(r_0) = 0$: $$\dfrac{2\sigma^{12}}{r_0^{13}} = \dfrac{\sigma^{6}}{r_0^{7}}$$ $$2\sigma^{6} = r_0^{6}$$ $$\boxed{r_0 = 2^{1/6}\,\sigma \approx 1.122\,\sigma}$$ **Derivation of Minimum Energy:** Substituting $r_0 = 2^{1/6}\sigma$: $$U_{min} = 4\varepsilon\left[\left(\dfrac{1}{2^{1/6}}\right)^{12} - \left(\dfrac{1}{2^{1/6}}\right)^{6}\right] = 4\varepsilon\left[\dfrac{1}{4} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right] = -\varepsilon$$ $$\boxed{U_{min} = -\varepsilon}$$ **(7 Marks: 2 for LJ explanation, 2 for equilibrium derivation, 2 for minimum energy derivation, 1 for AFM significance.)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **Q4(a) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö KTU Model 2024 Alternate]** **"Discuss the three operating modes of AFM with their force regimes and typical applications. Why is Tapping Mode preferred for biological samples?"** (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Mode 1 ŌĆö Contact Mode:** - **Tip-sample gap:** $0.1 - 0.5$ nm (interpenetration of electron clouds) - **Force regime:** Purely **repulsive** (Pauli + ionic repulsion), $F \sim 10^{-8}$ to $10^{-9}$ N - **Setpoint:** Constant cantilever deflection - **Advantages:** High scan speeds; true atomic resolution on flat surfaces - **Drawbacks:** High lateral (shear) forces can damage soft samples; tip wear is significant - **Applications:** Atomic resolution on HOPG, mica; friction/lateral force microscopy **Mode 2 ŌĆö Tapping Mode (Intermittent Contact):** - **Tip-sample gap:** $0.5 - 2$ nm - **Force regime:** **Cyclic** ŌĆö alternately repulsive and attractive during each oscillation cycle - **Setpoint:** Constant oscillation amplitude (typically 80% of free amplitude $A_0$) - **Cantilever oscillates** at or near its resonance frequency $f_0$ - **Advantages:** Eliminates lateral forces; preserves sharp tips; works on soft samples - **Applications:** Polymers, biomolecules, DNA, proteins, thin films **Mode 3 ŌĆö Non-Contact Mode:** - **Tip-sample gap:** $1 - 10$ nm (never touches surface) - **Force regime:** Purely **attractive** (long-range van der Waals + electrostatic) - **Setpoint:** Constant frequency shift $\Delta f$ (force-gradient detection) - **Cantilever oscillates** at resonance with very small amplitude ($A \sim 1 - 5$ nm) - **Advantages:** No tip wear; true non-destructive imaging; atomic resolution under UHV - **Drawbacks:** Requires ultra-clean surfaces; very slow scan speeds; sensitive to contamination layers - **Applications:** Atomic resolution on Si(111)-(7├Ś7), insulators under vacuum **Why Tapping Mode for Biological Samples:** Biological samples (cells, proteins, DNA) are **soft, hydrated, and loosely bound to substrates**. In Contact Mode, the lateral shear force would drag or tear the sample. Tapping Mode eliminates lateral forces because the tip only intermittently touches the surface, allowing gentle imaging without destroying fragile biological structures. **(7 Marks: 2 for each mode description, 1 for biological preference.)** --- **Q4(b) [KTU University Exam ŌĆö KTU Model 2024 Alternate]** **"Derive an expression for the spring constant of a rectangular AFM cantilever. Given a silicon cantilever with length $L = 200 \, \mu\text{m}$, width $w = 40 \, \mu\text{m}$, thickness $t = 1 \, \mu\text{m}$, and Young's modulus $E = 170 \, \text{GPa}$, calculate the spring constant and resonance frequency (assume $\rho_{Si} = 2330 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$)."** (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** From Euler-Bernoulli beam theory for an end-loaded rectangular cantilever (see Section 3.1): $$\boxed{k = \dfrac{E \cdot w \cdot t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}}$$ **Numerical Calculation of $k$:** Substituting $E = 170 \times 10^{9}$ Pa, $w = 40 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $t = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $L = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m: $$k = \dfrac{(170 \times 10^{9})(40 \times 10^{-6})(10^{-6})^{3}}{4 (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}$$ **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute numerator.** $$(170 \times 10^{9}) \times (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^{-18}) = 6.8 \times 10^{-13}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Compute denominator.** $$4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 4 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 3.2 \times 10^{-14}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Divide.** $$k = \dfrac{6.8 \times 10^{-13}}{3.2 \times 10^{-14}} = 21.25 \, \text{N/m}$$ $$\boxed{k \approx 0.21 \, \text{N/m}}$$ **Calculation of Resonance Frequency:** The effective mass is $m^{*} = 0.24 \rho L w t$: $$m^{*} = 0.24 \times 2330 \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times (40 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^{-6})$$ $$m^{*} = 0.24 \times 2330 \times 8 \times 10^{-15} = 4.47 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{kg}$$ **Resonance frequency:** $$f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{0.21}{4.47 \times 10^{-12}}}$$ $$\dfrac{k}{m^{*}} = 4.70 \times 10^{10} \, \text{s}^{-2}$$ $$\sqrt{4.70 \times 10^{10}} = 2.168 \times 10^{5} \, \text{rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{f_0 \approx 34.5 \, \text{kHz}}$$ **(7 Marks: 3 for derivation, 2 for $k$ calculation, 2 for $f_0$ calculation.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ŌĆö Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Contact and Tapping Modes:** Students often mix up "constant deflection" with "constant amplitude." In Contact Mode the cantilever is **static**; in Tapping Mode it **oscillates** and amplitude is the controlled variable. > 2. **Sign of Forces:** Van der Waals forces are **attractive** (negative); Pauli/repulsive forces are **positive**. Do not write $F_{vdW}$ as positive without explaining the convention. > 3. **Cantilever Formula:** The $k \propto t^{3}/L^{3}$ scaling is the most-tested dimensional analysis. Writing $k \propto t/L$ (linear) costs full marks. > 4. **Lennard-Jones Derivation:** Skipping the step where you set $F(r_0) = 0$ and solve for $r_0$ loses 2 marks immediately. Always show the equilibrium condition. > 5. **Units:** Mixing nm and m in force calculations is the #1 numerical error. Always convert: $1 \, \text{nm} = 10^{-9}$ m. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist** - [ ] **AFM** is a **Scanning Probe Microscope** that images surfaces by sensing interatomic forces via a sharp tip on a flexible cantilever. - [ ] AFM works on **conductors AND insulators** ŌĆö unlike STM. - [ ] Three modes: **Contact (repulsive), Tapping (cyclic), Non-Contact (attractive)**. - [ ] **Cantilever spring constant:** $k = \dfrac{E w t^{3}}{4 L^{3}}$ ŌĆö scales with $t^{3}/L^{3}$. - [ ] **Resonance frequency:** $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^{*}}}$, typically $50 - 500$ kHz. - [ ] **Van der Waals force (sphere-flat):** $F_{vdW} = -\dfrac{A_H R}{6 D^{2}}$. - [ ] **Lennard-Jones potential:** $U(r) = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^{6}]$. - [ ] **Equilibrium separation:** $r_0 = 2^{1/6}\sigma$; **minimum energy:** $U_{min} = -\varepsilon$. - [ ] **Optical lever amplification:** $\Delta x_{PSPD} = 2 L_{OD} \cdot \Delta z_{tip} / L_c$. - [ ] **Thermal noise limit:** $F_{min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 k_B T k}{\pi Q f_0}}$. - [ ] **Tapping Mode preferred** for biological samples to eliminate destructive lateral forces. - [ ] **Non-Contact Mode** requires UHV for atomic resolution on Si(111)-(7├Ś7) reconstructions. - [ ] **Hamaker constant** $A_H \sim 10^{-19}$ J for most air-solid systems. - [ ] **Piezoelectric scanner** provides sub-nm $xyz$ positioning via applied voltage. - [ ] **Feedback loop output** = topography image; the **setpoint** determines imaging force. - [ ] **True 3D topography** ŌĆö AFM gives height information directly, unlike SEM/TEM which give 2D projections. - [ ] **AFM resolution:** lateral $\sim 0.1$ nm; vertical $\sim 0.01$ nm. - [ ] **Force gradient detection** in NC-AFM: $\Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0}{2k}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$.

topics.title: Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) - Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM) - Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)** is a non-optical, surface-sensitive analytical technique that exploits the **quantum mechanical phenomenon of electron tunneling** between a sharp, atomically terminated metallic tip and a conducting (or semi-conducting) sample surface separated by a sub-nanometer vacuum gap (typically **0.3 nm to 1.0 nm**). The magnitude of the resulting **tunneling current** is recorded as the tip is rastered across the surface, allowing three-dimensional topographical imaging with **sub-├ģngstr├Čm vertical resolution** and **atomic-scale lateral resolution**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context (KTU High-Weight Point):**
> - Invented in **1981** by **Gerd Binnig** and **Heinrich Rohrer** at the IBM Zurich Research Laboratory.
> - Awarded the **Nobel Prize in Physics in 1986** (shared with Ernst Ruska, inventor of the electron microscope).
> - First instrument capable of directly imaging individual atoms on a conducting surface in **real space**.



> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** STM belongs to the broader family of **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM)** ŌĆö instruments that "feel" the surface with a physical probe rather than illuminate it with photons or electrons. Other SPM variants include AFM, MFM, and SNOM.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆö The "Tape Measure Made of Electrons"

Imagine trying to read a Braille book blindfolded. You would use a fingertip that detects tiny bumps and dips. Now imagine a fingertip so incredibly fine that it could "feel" individual atoms ŌĆö this is the role of the **STM tip**.

The clever part is *how* it feels. When the tip is brought extremely close to the surface (closer than the width of a single atom), electrons from the tip can **magically jump across the empty gap** into the sample ŌĆö a quantum trick called **tunneling**. This "jumping" produces a tiny, measurable electric current. Because the probability of this jump depends **exponentially** on the gap distance, even a tiny change in the height of the surface (say, **0.1 nm** ŌĆö the size of a single atom) causes a dramatic change in current. By mapping this current, the microscope essentially draws a contour map of the atomic landscape.

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Tip-Sample Gap ($d$)** | **0.3 ŌĆō 1.0 nm** | Determines exponential current change |
| **Tunneling Current ($I_t$)** | **0.1 nA ŌĆō 10 nA** | The actual signal measured |
| **Bias Voltage ($V_b$)** | **0.01 V ŌĆō 3 V** | Drives electrons across the barrier |
| **Work Function ($\Phi$)** | **4 ŌĆō 5 eV** | Sets the barrier height for tunneling |
| **Lateral Resolution** | **~ 0.1 nm** | Atomic-scale imaging possible |
| **Vertical Resolution** | **~ 0.01 nm** | Sub-picometer surface sensing |

## 1.3 Operational Requirements

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Mandatory Conditions for STM Operation:**
> 1. **Conducting Sample:** Sample must be electrically conductive (metals, doped semiconductors, or thin films on conducting substrates). Insulators cannot be imaged directly.
> 2. **Atomically Sharp Tip:** Usually a **tungsten (W)** or **platinum-iridium (Pt-Ir)** wire electrochemically etched to a single-atom apex.
> 3. **Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV) and Vibration Isolation:** Ambient vibrations and surface contamination destroy atomic resolution. Typical operating pressure: **< 10Ōü╗Ōü╣ Torr**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Quantum Tunneling

The classical view of physics forbids an electron with energy $E$ from crossing a potential barrier of height $\Phi$ where $E < \Phi$. However, in quantum mechanics, the electron is described by a **wave function** $\psi(x)$, and at a potential discontinuity, the wave function does **not** abruptly drop to zero ŌĆö instead it **decays exponentially** inside the classically forbidden region.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

- **Step 1 ŌĆö Classical Picture:** A particle of energy $E$ approaching a barrier of height $\Phi > E$ must reflect back. Transmission coefficient $T = 0$.

- **Step 2 ŌĆö Quantum Picture:** The Schr├Čdinger equation in the barrier region yields solutions of the form $\psi(x) = \psi_0 e^{-\kappa x}$, where $\kappa$ is the **inverse decay length** of the wave function.

- **Step 3 ŌĆö Wave Function Penetration:** Because $\psi(x)$ is non-zero at the far side of the barrier, there is a finite probability of finding the electron there. The probability density is $\vert \psi \vert^2 \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$, which is non-zero for finite $d$.

- **Step 4 ŌĆö Tunneling Current Origin:** When a small bias $V_b$ is applied, occupied electronic states in the tip (filled up to $E_F$) align with unoccupied states in the sample (above $E_F$ of the sample). Electrons tunnel elastically, producing a measurable current $I_t$.

- **Step 5 ŌĆö Exponential Sensitivity:** Since the current depends on $e^{-2\kappa d}$, a change in gap of just **0.1 nm** changes the current by an order of magnitude. This is the *fingerprint* of STM sensitivity.

### Definition of the Decay Constant $\kappa$

For a rectangular barrier of height $\Phi$ and an electron of mass $m_e$, solving the time-independent Schr├Čdinger equation yields:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \Phi}}{\hbar}$$

Substituting numerical values with $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg and $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs:

$$\kappa = 0.512 \, \sqrt{\Phi \, [\text{eV}]} \quad \text{(in units of ├ģ}^{-1}\text{)}$$

For a typical work function $\Phi \approx 4$ eV: $\kappa \approx 1.024$ ├ģŌü╗┬╣, meaning the wave function decays by a factor of $e$ every **~0.1 nm**.

## 2.2 Bardeen's Transfer Hamiltonian Formalism

The rigorous theoretical framework of STM is based on **John Bardeen's Transfer Hamiltonian Theory (1961)**. The tunneling current between two weakly coupled electrodes (tip and sample) is given by:

$$I_t = \frac{2\pi e}{\hbar} \sum_{\mu,\nu} \left[ f(E_\mu) \bigl(1 - f(E_\nu + eV)\bigr) - f(E_\nu)\bigl(1 - f(E_\mu - eV)\bigr) \right] \cdot \left\vert M_{\mu\nu} \right\vert^2 \cdot \delta(E_\mu - E_\nu)$$

Where:
- $f(E)$ = Fermi-Dirac occupation function.
- $M_{\mu\nu}$ = tunneling matrix element between tip state $\mu$ and sample state $\nu$.
- $V$ = applied bias voltage.

**Physical interpretation:** At low temperature and small bias, this reduces to a simple proportionality between the current and the **Local Density of States (LDOS)** of the sample at the Fermi level.

## 2.3 Tersoff-Hamann Limit (The Working Approximation)

For an **s-wave (spherically symmetric) tip**, Tersoff and Hamann (1983) showed that the tunneling current simplifies elegantly to:

$$I_t \propto V_b \cdot \rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F)$$

**Key insight:** At constant small bias, the STM image is essentially a **contour map of constant Local Density of States (LDOS) at the Fermi level** evaluated at the tip position $\vec{r}_t$. This is why STM can resolve individual atoms ŌĆö the LDOS peaks sharply above surface atoms.

## 2.4 Simmons' Tunneling Current Approximation

For practical numerical estimation of current, the **Simmons model (1963)** for a rectangular barrier of average height $\bar{\Phi}$, width $d$, and small applied bias $V$ gives:

$$J = \frac{e^2}{h \cdot d^2} \left[ \bar{\Phi} - \frac{eV}{2} \right] \exp\left[ -\frac{4\pi d}{h} \sqrt{2 m_e \left(\bar{\Phi} - \frac{eV}{2}\right)} \right]$$

Or in a more commonly quoted compact form:

$$I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp\left(- \kappa \sqrt{\Phi} \cdot d\right)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **must-memorize** cheat sheet for the KTU End Semester Examination.

| # | Formula / Relation | Physical Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_e \Phi}}{\hbar}$ | Inverse decay length of evanescent wave | $\kappa \approx 1 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1}$ for $\Phi = 4$ eV |
| 2 | $I_t \propto V_b \cdot e^{-2\kappa d}$ | Tunnel current vs. gap and bias | $I_t \approx 0.1 - 10$ nA |
| 3 | $I_t \propto \rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F)$ | Tersoff-Hamann: current ŌłØ sample LDOS | LDOS in states/eV┬Ę├ģ┬│ |
| 4 | $\Delta I / I \approx -2\kappa \cdot \Delta d$ | Current change for gap change | $\sim 10\times$ per 0.1 nm |
| 5 | $\Delta d_{piezo} = d_{31} \cdot V_{piezo}$ | Piezo extension per applied volt | $d_{31} \approx 2 - 5$ pm/V |
| 6 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Resonance frequency of tip assembly | ~ 1 kHz for cantilever |
| 7 | $E_{tunnel} = e V_b$ | Energy window of probed electronic states | meV to eV scale |
| 8 | $\Delta x_{\text{lateral}} = V_x / k_x$ | Lateral scan size from piezo voltage | nm to ╬╝m range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a numerical STM problem is asked, the relation $\dfrac{\partial I_t / I_t}{\partial d} = -2\kappa$ gives the *current sensitivity* to gap variations. For a 10% change in current, the gap must change by only **~ 0.005 nm** ŌĆö explaining the remarkable vertical resolution.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor Industry:** Atomic-scale inspection of wafer surfaces, defect mapping, and growth monitoring of quantum dots and 2D materials.
- **Catalysis Research:** Visualizing catalytic sites on platinum and gold surfaces; understanding single-atom catalysis.
- **Molecular Electronics:** Manipulation of single atoms and molecules (e.g., IBM's quantum corral of 48 Fe atoms on Cu(111)).
- **Data Storage (Research):** Demonstrated **atomic-bit data storage** at densities exceeding **100 Tbit/in┬▓** (concept demonstrated; not commercial).
- **Surface Science:** Determining surface reconstructions, adsorbate positions, and surface electronic structure (combined with STS ŌĆö Scanning Tunneling Spectroscopy).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Decay of the Wave Function in a 1D Rectangular Barrier

Consider an electron of energy $E$ moving along the $x$-axis, encountering a rectangular potential barrier of height $\Phi$ (where $E < \Phi$) extending from $x = 0$ to $x = d$.

**Step 1: Write the time-independent Schr├Čdinger equation (TISE).**

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi(x)}{dx^2} + V(x) \psi(x) = E \psi(x)$$

**Step 2: Inside the barrier, $V(x) = \Phi$, so rearranging:**

$$\frac{d^2 \psi(x)}{dx^2} = \frac{2 m_e (\Phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi(x) = \kappa^2 \psi(x)$$

where we define:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e (\Phi - E)}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 3: General solution inside the barrier ($0 \leq x \leq d$).**

$$\psi(x) = A \, e^{-\kappa x} + B \, e^{+\kappa x}$$

**Step 4: Apply physical boundary condition at $x \to \infty$.**

Since the wave function must remain finite, $B = 0$. Therefore:

$$\psi(x) = A \, e^{-\kappa x} \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq d$$

**Step 5: Probability density inside and beyond the barrier.**

The probability of finding the electron at position $x$ is:

$$\vert \psi(x) \vert^2 = \vert A \vert^2 \, e^{-2\kappa x}$$

**Step 6: Transmission probability through the barrier (ratio of squared amplitudes).**

The transmission coefficient is:

$$T = \frac{\vert \psi(d) \vert^2}{\vert \psi(0) \vert^2} = e^{-2\kappa d}$$

**Step 7: Substitute back the definition of $\kappa$:**

$$T = \exp\left[-\frac{2 d}{\hbar} \sqrt{2 m_e (\Phi - E)}\right]$$

**Result:** The transmission probability decays **exponentially** with barrier width $d$ and the square root of barrier height. This is the foundation of STM sensitivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Sanity Check:** For $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $\Phi - E \approx 3$ eV, $d = 0.5$ nm:
> $$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 3 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{(1.055 \times 10^{-34})^2 \cdot ... } \Rightarrow T \approx 0.4$$
> A 0.1 nm increase in $d$ drops $T$ by an order of magnitude.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tunneling Current from Tersoff-Hamann Theory

**Step 1: Start from Bardeen's first-order time-dependent perturbation result.**

$$I_t = \frac{2\pi e}{\hbar} \sum_{\mu,\nu} \left\vert M_{\mu\nu} \right\vert^2 \, \delta(E_\mu - E_\nu) \, [f(E_\mu) - f(E_\nu + eV)]$$

**Step 2: Replace the matrix element using Bardeen's approximation.**

For two electrodes weakly coupled, the matrix element for tunneling from tip state $\mu$ to sample state $\nu$ is:

$$\left\vert M_{\mu\nu} \right\vert^2 \propto \left\vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \right\vert^2$$

where $\psi_\nu$ is the sample wave function evaluated at the position of the tip.

**Step 3: At $T = 0$ K and small bias $V$, the occupation function difference becomes a delta spike near $E_F$.**

$$f(E_\mu) - f(E_\nu + eV) \approx eV \cdot \delta(E_F - E)$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the current expression.**

$$I_t = \frac{2\pi e^2 V}{\hbar} \sum_\nu \left\vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \right\vert^2 \delta(E_\nu - E_F)$$

**Step 5: Recognize the sum as the LDOS of the sample at the Fermi level.**

By definition:

$$\rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F) = \sum_\nu \left\vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \right\vert^2 \delta(E_\nu - E_F)$$

**Step 6: Final working equation:**

$$\boxed{I_t \propto V_b \cdot \rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F)}$$

**Conclusion:** The STM image at constant current and small bias is mathematically a **Fermi-level LDOS map** of the sample at the tip apex position.

## 3.3 Derivation: Operating Modes ŌĆö Constant Current vs. Constant Height

**Mode A ŌĆö Constant Current Mode (Most Common):**

The feedback loop continuously adjusts the vertical piezo voltage $V_z$ to keep $I_t$ constant at a setpoint $I_0$. The recorded $V_z(x, y)$ is plotted as a 2D image, which represents the surface topography.

$$\text{Image}(x, y) = V_z(x, y) \big\vert_{I_t = I_0}$$

**Mode B ŌĆö Constant Height Mode (Faster, riskier):**

The tip is held at fixed $V_z$ and the current $I_t(x, y)$ is recorded directly. This is faster but can crash the tip if it encounters a tall feature.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Constant Current | Constant Height |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback loop | Active | Disabled |
| Variable recorded | $V_z$ (height) | $I_t$ (current) |
| Scan speed | Slower | Faster |
| Tip crash risk | Low | High |
| Best use | Rough, unknown surfaces | Atomically flat, well-known surfaces |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Pt-Ir STM tip with work function $\Phi = 5.0$ eV is brought to a distance of $d = 0.5$ nm from a gold (Au) sample of work function $\Phi_s = 5.1$ eV. A bias of $V_b = 100$ mV is applied. Estimate: (a) the decay constant $\kappa$, (b) the fractional change in current if the gap increases by $\Delta d = 0.05$ nm.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) ŌĆö Decay constant:**

$$\kappa = 0.512 \sqrt{\Phi_{\text{eff}} \, [\text{eV}]} \quad \text{with } \Phi_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\Phi_{tip} + \Phi_{sample}}{2} = \frac{5.0 + 5.1}{2} = 5.05 \text{ eV}$$

$$\kappa = 0.512 \times \sqrt{5.05} = 0.512 \times 2.247 = 1.151 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1} = 11.51 \text{ nm}^{-1}$$

**Part (b) ŌĆö Fractional current change:**

Using $I \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$:

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = e^{-2\kappa \Delta d} - 1 = e^{-2 \times 11.51 \times 0.05} - 1 = e^{-1.151} - 1 = 0.316 - 1 = -0.684$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta I}{I} \approx -68.4\%}$$

**Interpretation:** A mere **0.05 nm** (half an atom) gap change drops the current by **~68%**, confirming the extraordinary vertical sensitivity of STM.

## 3.5 Derivation: Piezo Scanner Response (Scanning Coordinate Generation)

The tip is mounted on a **piezoelectric tube scanner** (PZT ŌĆö lead zirconate titanate). The displacement per applied volt is given by the inverse piezoelectric coefficient $d_{31}$ (lateral) or $d_{33}$ (longitudinal):

$$d_{piezo} = d_{31} \cdot V_{applied}$$

For a typical PZT material, $d_{31} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V = **2.3 pm/V**.

**Example:** To scan a 100 nm ├Ś 100 nm area, required lateral voltage:

$$V_x = \frac{\Delta x}{d_{31}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9}}{2.3 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 43{,}500 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is why **high-voltage piezo amplifiers** (typically ┬▒250 V) are mandatory in STM electronics ŌĆö to achieve micron-scale scanning ranges.

## 4.1 Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Architecture of an STM System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the physical and electronic signal flow of a complete STM instrument. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric and free of reserved keywords.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    preampC -->|voltage signal| compD[Comparator Reference Setpoint I0]

    ctrlE -->|correction voltage Vz| piezofF[Z Piezoelectric Tube Scanner PZT]
```

## 4.4 Schematic ŌĆö TipŌĆōSample Tunnel Junction and Energy Level Diagram

The following block diagram represents the energy landscape and bias-dependent alignment of electronic states.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tipA1[STM Tip Atomically Sharp Metal]
        EFt1[E_Fermi of Tip]:::ef
        OCCt1[Occupied States below E_F]:::occ
        EMT1[Empty Conduction States above E_F]:::empty
    end
    subgraph gapA2[Vacuum Gap d = 0.3 to 1.0 nm Barrier Height = Phi]
        barrier[Exponentially Decaying Evanescent Wave Function psi x = psi0 exp minus kappa x]:::barrier
    end
    subgraph sampleA3[Conductive Sample Au or Graphene or Si 111]
        EFs1[E_Fermi of Sample]:::ef
        OCCs1[Occupied States below E_F of Sample]:::occ
        EMS1[Empty Conduction States above E_F of Sample]:::empty
    end
    tipA1 -->|electrons tunnel elastically| gapA2
    gapA2 -->|electrons enter unoccupied states| sampleA3
    Vbias1[Applied Bias V_b shifts tip states relative to sample]:::bias --> tipA1
    Vbias1 --> sampleA3
    classDef ef fill fff2cc,stroke cc6600,color 000
    classDef occ fill cce5ff,stroke 003399,color 000
    classDef empty fill e5ccff,stroke 330099,color 000
    classDef barrier fill ffcccc,stroke 990000,color 000
    classDef bias fill d5e8d4,stroke 336600,color 000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** When $V_b > 0$ (tip positive), electrons tunnel **from occupied sample states** to **empty tip states**. When $V_b < 0$ (tip negative), electrons tunnel **from occupied tip states** to **empty sample states**. The sign of $V_b$ therefore selects whether filled or empty states of the sample are imaged.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
**Define tunneling current in STM. How does it depend on the tipŌĆōsample separation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Tunneling current is the net flow of electrons across a thin vacuum (or insulating) barrier between two conducting electrodes (the STM tip and the sample) due to the **quantum mechanical tunneling effect**. It arises because the electron wave function does not terminate abruptly at the barrier boundary but decays exponentially inside the forbidden region, leaving a finite probability for the electron to appear on the other side.

The tunneling current depends on the tipŌĆōsample separation $d$ as:

$$I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp(-2\kappa d) \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \Phi}}{\hbar}$$

Thus, the current decreases **exponentially** with increasing gap $d$. A change in $d$ of just **0.1 nm** typically changes $I_t$ by an order of magnitude. This extreme sensitivity is the fundamental reason for STM's sub-├ģngstr├Čm vertical resolution. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three mandatory conditions for successful STM imaging of a sample surface.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Conducting Sample:** The sample must be electrically conductive so that electrons can flow into/out of it during tunneling. Insulators cannot be imaged directly without modification.
2. **Atomically Sharp Tip:** The tip (typically W or Pt-Ir) must terminate in a single atom or atomic cluster to provide localized tunneling and achieve atomic resolution.
3. **Clean, Vibration-Isolated Environment:** Surface contamination (oxides, adsorbates) and ambient vibrations must be minimized ŌĆö operation is usually in **ultra-high vacuum (UHV < 10Ōü╗Ōü╣ Torr)** with stacked vibration dampers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of Scanning Tunneling Microscopy (STM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the piezoelectric scanner and the feedback loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **tunneling current** using Bardeen's transfer Hamiltonian approach and show that in the TersoffŌĆōHamann limit, the current is proportional to the local density of states (LDOS) of the sample at the Fermi level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle of Operation (7 marks):**

STM works on the principle of **quantum mechanical tunneling** of electrons between a sharp metallic tip and a conducting sample separated by a sub-nanometer gap.

- **Tip:** A tungsten or Pt-Ir wire etched to a single-atom point.
- **Piezoelectric Scanner:** A PZT (lead zirconate titanate) tube that expands or contracts by a few picometers per applied volt. Three pairs of electrodes control the tip's **x**, **y**, and **z** motion with sub-├ģngstr├Čm precision.
- **Bias Voltage ($V_b$):** Applied between tip and sample to align filled and empty electronic states and drive the tunneling current.
- **Tunneling Current ($I_t$):** Measured by a transimpedance preamplifier. Typical magnitude: **0.1ŌĆō10 nA**.
- **Feedback Loop:** A PID controller continuously adjusts the $z$-piezo voltage so that $I_t$ matches a user-defined setpoint $I_0$ (constant current mode). The piezo voltage $V_z(x, y)$ is recorded and rendered as the **STM topographic image**.

**Block diagram:** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)*

The piezo scanner's role is to provide **precise three-dimensional positioning** of the tip with picometer accuracy. The feedback loop's role is to **maintain a constant tip-sample interaction** as the tip scans across surface features, ensuring that the recorded $V_z$ accurately tracks the surface topography. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Derivation of Tunneling Current (7 marks):**

Starting from Bardeen's first-order time-dependent perturbation theory, the elastic tunneling current between tip and sample is:

$$I_t = \frac{2\pi e}{\hbar} \sum_{\mu,\nu} \left\vert M_{\mu\nu} \right\vert^2 \left[ f(E_\mu) - f(E_\nu + eV_b) \right] \delta(E_\mu - E_\nu)$$

**Tersoff-Hamann Approximations:**

1. Replace the matrix element: $\vert M_{\mu\nu} \vert^2 \propto \vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \vert^2$ (s-wave tip).
2. At $T \to 0$ K and small bias: $f(E_\mu) - f(E_\nu + eV) \approx eV_b \, \delta(E - E_F)$.

Substituting:

$$I_t = \frac{2\pi e^2 V_b}{\hbar} \sum_\nu \vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \vert^2 \delta(E_\nu - E_F)$$

The summation is, by definition, the **local density of states of the sample evaluated at the tip position** at the Fermi level:

$$\rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F) = \sum_\nu \vert \psi_\nu(\vec{r}_t) \vert^2 \delta(E_\nu - E_F)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_t \propto V_b \cdot \rho_s(\vec{r}_t, E_F)}$$

**Conclusion:** The STM image in constant current mode is a real-space **contour map of constant LDOS** at $E_F$, providing both geometric and electronic information about the surface. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice: [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **two operating modes of STM** ŌĆö constant current mode and constant height mode ŌĆö with their advantages, limitations, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pt-Ir STM tip with work function $\Phi = 4.5$ eV is positioned at a distance of $d = 0.6$ nm from a graphite (HOPG) surface with work function $\Phi_s = 4.6$ eV. A bias voltage of $V_b = 50$ mV is applied. Calculate (i) the decay constant $\kappa$, and (ii) the percentage change in tunneling current if the gap increases by $\Delta d = 0.08$ nm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operating Modes (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Constant Current Mode | Constant Height Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Working principle** | Feedback adjusts $z$-piezo to keep $I_t$ fixed at $I_0$ | Tip held at fixed $z$; $I_t(x, y)$ recorded directly |
| **Signal recorded** | Piezo voltage $V_z(x, y)$ (height profile) | Tunneling current $I_t(x, y)$ |
| **Scan speed** | Slower (limited by feedback bandwidth) | Faster (no feedback adjustment) |
| **Advantage** | Safe for rough/unknown surfaces; high dynamic range | Higher scan rate; captures fast dynamics |
| **Disadvantage** | Limited by feedback response time | Risk of tip crash on tall features |
| **Best application** | General-purpose atomic imaging | Atomically flat, well-characterized surfaces |

In **constant current mode**, the recorded image is a true **topographic map** of the surface. In **constant height mode**, the recorded image is a map of **electronic structure (LDOS variation)** at the tip height. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\Phi_{tip} = 4.5$ eV, $\Phi_{sample} = 4.6$ eV
- $d = 0.6$ nm, $V_b = 50$ mV, $\Delta d = 0.08$ nm

**Step 1: Average work function (effective barrier height).**

$$\Phi_{eff} = \frac{\Phi_{tip} + \Phi_{sample}}{2} = \frac{4.5 + 4.6}{2} = 4.55 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 2: Compute decay constant.**

$$\kappa = 0.512 \sqrt{\Phi_{eff}} = 0.512 \times \sqrt{4.55} = 0.512 \times 2.133 = 1.092 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1} = 10.92 \text{ nm}^{-1}$$

**Step 3: Compute fractional current change.**

Using $I \propto e^{-2\kappa d}$:

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I} = e^{-2\kappa \Delta d} - 1 = e^{-2 \times 10.92 \times 0.08} - 1 = e^{-1.747} - 1$$

$$e^{-1.747} = 0.1743 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta I}{I} = 0.1743 - 1 = -0.8257$$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Delta I}{I} \approx -82.6\%}$$
