\sigma_{sample}) \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}}$$ **Physical meaning:** - **More shielded** (higher electron density) ŌåÆ larger $\sigma$ ŌåÆ lower $\delta$ (upfield) - **Less shielded** (electron-withdrawing groups) ŌåÆ smaller $\sigma$ ŌåÆ higher $\delta$ (downfield) --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV: Bloch Equations and TŌéü, TŌéé Relaxation The phenomenological Bloch equations describe macroscopic magnetization $M$ in the rotating frame: $$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M \times B)_{x} - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$ $$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (M \times B)_{y} - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$ $$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (M \times B)_{z} - \frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$ After a 90┬░ pulse, the longitudinal recovery solution: $$M_z(t) = M_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/T_1}\right)$$ Transverse decay: $$M_{xy}(t) = M_0 \, e^{-t/T_2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Key Insight:** In **nanomaterials**, TŌéé becomes very short due to inhomogeneous broadening from large surface-to-volume ratios and susceptibility variations ŌĆö this is why solid-state NMR of nanoparticles requires **Magic Angle Spinning (MAS)** at 10ŌĆō100 kHz. --- # Section 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an NMR Spectrometer The following Mermaid block diagram maps the signal flow and component interaction of a modern FT-NMR spectrometer used in nanomaterial characterization. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Magnet Assembly] M1[Superconducting Coil] M2[Liquid He Cryostat 4K] M3[Shim Coils B0 Homogenizer] M4[Sample Probe and Rotor] end subgraph S2[RF Electronics] R1[Frequency Synthesizer] R2[Pulse Programmer and Gate] R3[RF Power Amplifier kW] R4[Transmit Receive Switch] end subgraph S3[Receiver and Digitizer] D1[Low Noise Preamplifier] D2[Quadrature Detector] D3[Analog to Digital Converter] D4[Computer and FFT Processor] end subgraph S4[Output] O1[FID Signal Time Domain] O2[Frequency Domain Spectrum] end M4 -->|precesses at omegaL| R4 R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> M4 M4 -->|induced EMF FID| D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> O1 --> O2 M3 -.->|shims B0| M4 M2 -.->|cools| M1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block function summary:** > - **Magnet assembly** provides a stable, homogeneous $B_0$. > - **RF electronics** generate precisely timed, shaped RF pulses at the Larmor frequency. > - **Receiver chain** captures the weak Free Induction Decay (FID) signal ŌĆö typically in the $\mu$V range. > - **FFT processor** converts time-domain FID into the familiar frequency-domain NMR spectrum. --- ## 4.2 Energy Level Diagram for a Spin-1/2 Nucleus in $B_0$ ```mermaid flowchart TB A0[External Magnetic Field B0 along z axis] subgraph EL[Zeeman Splitting for I equals 1/2 nucleus] E1[Energy E_alpha equals minus 1/2 gamma hbar B0
State mI equals plus 1/2 spin up
More populated N_alpha] E2[Energy E_beta equals plus 1/2 gamma hbar B0
State mI equals minus 1/2 spin down
Less populated N_beta] end A0 --> EL E2 -.->|RF absorption delta mI equals plus 1| E1 E1 -.->|RF emission delta mI equals minus 1| E2 note1["Population difference: N_alpha minus N_beta equals N_total times gamma hbar B0 over 2 kB T
Boltzmann distribution at 300 K for 7 T: about 1 in 30000 nuclei"] EL -.-> note1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagram:** The lower level ($m_I = +1/2$, spin-aligned with $B_0$) is slightly **more populated** by Boltzmann statistics. RF absorption at exactly $\omega_L$ excites nuclei to the upper level. When the pulse ends, **relaxation** returns them to equilibrium, and the emitted coherent radiation is the **NMR signal**. --- ## 4.3 Functional Workflow ŌĆö Pulse Sequence (1D ┬╣H NMR) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Recycle Delay D1 1 to 5 seconds for full T1 recovery] --> P2[90 degree Pulse 5 to 10 microseconds] P2 --> P3[Acquire FID over Acquisition Time AQ] P3 --> P4[Apodization and Zero Filling] P4 --> P5[Fourier Transform to Frequency Domain] P5 --> P6[Phase Correction and Baseline Correction] P6 --> P7[Peak Picking and Chemical Shift Annotation] P7 --> P8[Integration for Quantitative Analysis] ``` --- # Section 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, PECHT833]` **Q: Define Larmor frequency. State the resonance condition for NMR and explain its significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Larmor frequency** $\nu_L$ is the precessional frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment about an external static magnetic field $B_0$. It is given by: $$\nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$ The **resonance condition** requires that the applied radiofrequency $\nu_{RF}$ exactly equals $\nu_L$: $$\nu_{RF} = \nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$ **Significance (1 mark):** Resonance is the precise condition under which nuclei absorb RF energy and produce the NMR signal. Since $\gamma$ is a fingerprint of the nucleus, $\nu_L$ identifies the nucleus, and minute deviations from $\nu_L$ (chemical shift) reveal the molecular/electronic environment ŌĆö the essence of NMR's analytical power. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Writing $\omega_L = \gamma B_0$ as the *frequency* in Hz. Remember $\omega$ is angular (rad/s) and $\nu$ is cyclic (Hz). Examiners **strictly deduct ┬Į mark** for unit/notation confusion. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PECHT833]` **Q: What is chemical shift? How is it measured and why is it expressed in ppm?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Chemical shift ($\delta$)** is the relative difference between the resonance frequency of a nucleus in a sample and a reference standard (TMS for $^1$H, $^{13}$C), expressed in **parts per million (ppm)**: $$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ **Why ppm?** Absolute frequency shifts are field-dependent (e.g., a 1 ppm shift at 7 T is 300 Hz, but at 14 T it is 600 Hz). Dividing by the spectrometer frequency makes $\delta$ **dimensionless and field-independent**, allowing spectra from different instruments to be compared universally. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Do NOT write $\delta = (\nu_{sample} - \nu_{ref}) \times 10^6$ ŌĆö forgetting the division by $\nu_{spec}$ is a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### ­¤ōś QUESTION A ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, PECHT833]` **Q: (a)** Derive the Larmor frequency expression starting from the torque on a nuclear magnetic moment in an external magnetic field. Explain the resonance condition and the role of the gyromagnetic ratio. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat diagrams, describe the construction and working of a continuous-wave (CW) NMR spectrometer. Compare CW and FT-NMR techniques. **(7 marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks] **Step 1 [1 mark] ŌĆö Torque expression:** A magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ in $\vec{B_0}$ experiences torque: $$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$ **Step 2 [1 mark] ŌĆö Equation of motion:** Using $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$ and $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$: $$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})$$ **Step 3 [2 marks] ŌĆö Component-wise differentiation with $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$:** $$\frac{d^2 \mu_x}{dt^2} + \omega_L^2 \mu_x = 0, \quad \omega_L^2 = \gamma^2 B_0^2$$ **Step 4 [1 mark] ŌĆö Solution and identification:** The solutions are sinusoidal with angular frequency $\omega_L$: $$\boxed{\omega_L = \gamma B_0 \implies \nu_L = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}}$$ **Step 5 [1 mark] ŌĆö Resonance condition:** Maximum absorption of RF energy occurs when $\nu_{RF} = \nu_L$ ŌĆö this is the resonance condition. **Step 6 [1 mark] ŌĆö Role of gyromagnetic ratio:** $\gamma$ is nucleus-specific (a fingerprint). It determines $\nu_L$ for a given $B_0$, allowing NMR to discriminate between different nuclei (e.g., $^1$H at 42.58 MHz/T vs $^{13}$C at 10.71 MHz/T). --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks] **CW-NMR Construction [3 marks]:** > [!NOTE] > **Components:** Magnet ($B_0$) ŌåÆ Sample probe ŌåÆ Sweep generator (varies $B_0$ slowly) ŌåÆ RF oscillator at fixed $\nu$ ŌåÆ RF detector ŌåÆ Recorder. **Working:** The RF oscillator provides constant $\nu_{RF}$. Either $B_0$ or $\nu_{RF}$ is swept slowly. When resonance occurs, the sample absorbs RF energy, producing a dip in the detector signal ŌĆö recorded as a peak on the spectrum. **Comparison Table [3 marks]:** | Feature | CW-NMR | FT-NMR | |---------|--------|--------| | Excitation | Single frequency, slow sweep | Short RF pulse, broadband | | Data acquisition | Frequency domain, one point at a time | Time domain (FID), all frequencies simultaneously | | Sensitivity | Low | **High** (signal averaging by N repetitions: S/N $\propto \sqrt{N}$) | | Time per spectrum | Minutes to hours | **Seconds** (with multi-scan averaging) | | Best suited for | Continuous monitoring | Pulsed experiments, multidimensional NMR | **Conclusion [1 mark]:** FT-NMR is the **universal modern standard** for nanomaterial characterization due to speed, sensitivity, and flexibility (MAS, CP, 2D experiments). --- ### ­¤ōś QUESTION B ŌĆö 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, PECHT833]` **Q: (a)** Explain the principle of NMR spectroscopy. Discuss the role of shielding constant and derive an expression for chemical shift. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the application of $^1$H and $^{13}$C NMR in characterizing nanoparticles and carbon-based nanomaterials (CNTs, graphene). Add a note on solid-state NMR and Magic Angle Spinning. **(7 marks)** --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (a) [7 marks] **Principle [2 marks]:** NMR relies on nuclei with non-zero spin ($I \neq 0$) absorbing RF radiation at the Larmor frequency when placed in $B_0$. Resonance frequencies depend on local electronic environments (chemical shifts) and neighboring spins (J-coupling), providing atomic-level structural information. **Shielding constant $\sigma$ [2 marks]:** Electrons surrounding a nucleus circulate under $B_0$, generating an induced opposing field $B_{ind} = -\sigma B_0$. The effective field is: $$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$ Higher electron density ŌćÆ greater $\sigma$ ŌćÆ nucleus resonates at lower frequency (upfield, lower $\delta$). **Derivation of chemical shift [3 marks]:** $$\omega_{sample} = \gamma B_0(1 - \sigma_{sample})$$ $$\omega_{ref} = \gamma B_0(1 - \sigma_{ref})$$ $$\omega_{sample} - \omega_{ref} = \gamma B_0(\sigma_{ref} - \sigma_{sample})$$ Dividing by $\omega_{spec} = \gamma B_0$ and multiplying by $10^6$: $$\delta = \frac{\omega_{sample} - \omega_{ref}}{\omega_{spec}} \times 10^6 = (\sigma_{ref} - \sigma_{sample}) \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$ --- #### Ō£ģ Model Solution ŌĆö Part (b) [7 marks] **$^1$H NMR for nanoparticles [2 marks]:** - Identifies and quantifies **organic surface ligands** (e.g., oleylamine on quantum dots, PEG on Au nanoparticles). - Determines **grafting density** by integration of ligand vs internal standard peaks. - Probes **ligand exchange dynamics** through line-broadening analysis. **$^{13}$C NMR for CNTs and graphene [2 marks]:** - **SWCNTs:** $sp^2$ carbons appear at $\delta \approx 120$ŌĆō140 ppm; defects and functional groups ($sp^3$) at $\delta \approx 60$ŌĆō90 ppm. - **Graphene oxide:** carbonyls (170ŌĆō180 ppm), aromatic C=C (120ŌĆō130 ppm), epoxides (60ŌĆō70 ppm) ŌĆö quantifies oxidation degree. - **MAS-NMR** overcomes the broad lines of solid carbon nanomaterials. **Solid-State NMR & MAS [3 marks]:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **MAS (Magic Angle Spinning):** Sample is rotated rapidly at **54.74┬░** to $B_0$. This angle satisfies $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$, eliminating **chemical shift anisotropy** and **dipolar broadening** ŌĆö yielding high-resolution spectra from solids. **Other techniques:** - **CP-MAS (Cross-Polarization):** Transfers magnetization from abundant $^1$H to dilute $^{13}$C, boosting sensitivity by up to **4├Ś**. - **DNP (Dynamic Nuclear Polarization):** Enhances signal up to **660├Ś** for surface characterization of nanoparticles. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls ŌĆö NMR Questions:** > 1. **Wrong angle for MAS** ŌĆö it is $54.74┬░$ (where $3\cos^2\theta - 1 = 0$), NOT $45┬░$ or $60┬░$. Marks deducted immediately. > 2. **Confusing $\omega$ and $\nu$** ŌĆö angular frequency vs. cyclic frequency. Specify units. > 3. **Forgetting the reference (TMS)** in chemical shift ŌĆö examiners expect explicit mention. > 4. **No derivation shown** ŌĆö for "derive" questions, partial marks are given even if the final answer is wrong, **but only if intermediate steps are visible**. > 5. **Mixing up TŌéü and TŌéé** ŌĆö TŌéü is longitudinal (spin-lattice), TŌéé is transverse (spin-spin). Each has a distinct physical meaning. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist ŌĆö Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR):** ### ­¤Ä» Core Concepts - [ ] NMR is a **resonance absorption** technique exploiting nuclear spin in $B_0$. - [ ] Only nuclei with **$I \neq 0$** are NMR-active. - [ ] **Larmor frequency:** $\omega_L = \gamma B_0$ ŌĆö the resonance condition. - [ ] For $^1$H: $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad┬Ęs$^{-1}$┬ĘT$^{-1}$ (or 42.577 MHz/T). - [ ] At 7.05 T, $^1$H resonates at **300.2 MHz**. ### ­¤Ä» Chemical Shift - [ ] Defined as $\delta = [(\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}) / \nu_{spec}] \times 10^6$ in **ppm**. - [ ] **TMS** = reference standard for $^1$H and $^{13}$C (assigned $\delta = 0$). - [ ] **Deshielded** (electron-poor) ŌćÆ **downfield**, higher $\delta$. - [ ] Typical $^1$H range: **0ŌĆō14 ppm**; $^{13}$C range: **0ŌĆō220 ppm**. ### ­¤Ä» Relaxation - [ ] **TŌéü** = spinŌĆōlattice (longitudinal) recovery; sensitive to molecular motion. - [ ] **TŌéé** = spinŌĆōspin (transverse) decay; sensitive to local field inhomogeneities. - [ ] In **nanomaterials**, TŌéé is short due to large surface-to-volume ratio ŌĆö MAS is essential. ### ­¤Ä» Instrumentation & Techniques - [ ] **FT-NMR** uses short RF pulses; **CW-NMR** uses slow frequency sweeps (mostly obsolete). - [ ] **MAS = 54.74┬░** spinning eliminates anisotropic broadening in solids. - [ ] **CP-MAS** enhances $^{13}$C sensitivity via $^1$H cross-polarization. - [ ] **DNP** boosts signal up to 660├Ś for nanoscale samples. ### ­¤Ä» Nanomaterial Applications - [ ] Surface ligand quantification on nanoparticles. - [ ] Pore structure analysis in mesoporous silica and MOFs. - [ ] Phase identification in graphene, GO, CNTs. - [ ] Drug loading quantification in nanocarriers. - [ ] Li-ion transport studies in nanostructured electrodes. ### ­¤Ä» Mnemonic for the Multiplicity Rule > "**n + 1 rule**": For $^1$H NMR, $n$ equivalent neighboring protons split a signal into **(n + 1)** peaks. For a nucleus with general spin $I$, multiplicity = $2nI + 1$. ---

topics.title: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)** is an advanced analytical technique that uses an **interferometer** combined with **Fourier Transform mathematics** to obtain the infrared absorption, emission, or photoconductivity spectrum of a solid, liquid, or gaseous sample. It simultaneously collects high-resolution spectral data over a wide spectral range, allowing identification of molecular bonds, functional groups, and chemical environments in nanomaterials. The fundamental measurement parameter in FTIR is the **wavenumber** $\tilde{\nu}$, expressed in reciprocal centimeters (**cmŌü╗┬╣**), representing the number of wavelengths per unit length. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** FTIR is a cornerstone characterization tool for nanomaterials, enabling surface functionalization analysis of nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, and polymeric nanocomposites. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Fingerprint of Molecules" Imagine each chemical bond in a molecule as a **tiny tuning fork**. When you (the IR beam) strike the molecule with the right "musical note" (frequency of infrared light), that specific bond vibrates, absorbs the energy, and produces a unique signal. Just like a human fingerprint identifies a person uniquely, the **pattern of absorbed frequencies** identifies the chemical groups uniquely. - **White light with no sample** ŌåÆ all colors pass through (flat baseline). - **White light through a sample** ŌåÆ specific colors are absorbed by the bonds, leaving dark bands at characteristic frequencies. The **Fourier Transform** part is like converting a complex song (recorded over time on a tape) into a **musical score showing the exact notes played** ŌĆö it converts the raw interferogram (time/space domain) into a clean spectrum (frequency domain). ### 1.3 Key Definitions and Physical Constants - **IR Region:** Electromagnetic spectrum from **0.78 ┬Ąm to 1000 ┬Ąm** (wavenumbers **12800 cmŌü╗┬╣ to 10 cmŌü╗┬╣**). - **Mid-IR Region (used in FTIR):** **4000 cmŌü╗┬╣ to 400 cmŌü╗┬╣**. - **Speed of light (c):** **2.998 ├Ś 10ŌüĖ m/s** or **3 ├Ś 10┬╣Ōü░ cm/s**. - **Boltzmann Constant (k):** **1.381 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬▓┬│ J/K**. - **Planck's Constant (h):** **6.626 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│Ōü┤ J┬Ęs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Mid-IR is the "Goldilocks" Zone:** Mid-IR (4000ŌĆō400 cmŌü╗┬╣) corresponds to the energy range of molecular vibrations ŌĆö the precise energies needed to excite stretching and bending of covalent bonds without ionizing or breaking the molecule. ### 1.4 The Molecular Vibration Condition A molecular vibration is **IR-active** only if it produces a change in the **dipole moment** ($\mu$) of the molecule. This is the **selection rule** for IR absorption: $$\Delta \mu \neq 0 \quad \text{(IR active)}$$ $$\Delta \mu = 0 \quad \text{(IR inactive, Raman active)}$$ For example: - **COŌéé** (asymmetric stretch): dipole changes ŌåÆ **IR active** - **NŌéé**, **OŌéé** (homonuclear diatomics): no dipole change ŌåÆ **IR inactive** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Physical Principles FTIR operates on three layered scientific principles: #### **Layer 1: Molecular Vibrations (Hooke's Law Model)** A diatomic bond can be modeled as two masses ($m_1, m_2$) connected by a spring with force constant $k$. The vibrational frequency is given by the **harmonic oscillator model**: **Step-by-step logic of the harmonic model:** 1. The bond has a natural restoring force proportional to displacement: $F = -kx$ (Hooke's Law). 2. Applying Newton's second law: $\mu \ddot{x} = -kx$, where $\mu$ is the reduced mass. 3. This second-order differential equation has a sinusoidal solution: $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi \nu_0 t)$. 4. The natural vibrational frequency depends on the **mass of the atoms** and the **stiffness of the bond**. This is the foundational equation for predicting where an IR absorption will appear. #### **Layer 2: The Michelson Interferometer** The interferometer is the heart of every FTIR instrument. It works as follows: 1. **IR Source** emits polychromatic (multi-wavelength) infrared radiation. 2. **Beam Splitter** divides the beam into two perpendicular paths (50% transmitted, 50% reflected). 3. **Fixed Mirror** reflects one beam back to the beam splitter. 4. **Moving Mirror** reflects the second beam back; its position varies with time. 5. The two beams recombine at the beam splitter and travel toward the sample and detector. 6. The recombined beam exhibits **constructive or destructive interference** depending on the **optical path difference** ($\delta$). #### **Layer 3: The Fourier Transform** The detector receives an **interferogram** ŌĆö intensity as a function of mirror displacement. To convert this into a usable spectrum, the **Fourier Cosine Transform** is applied mathematically (in real time by the instrument's computer). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Memorize the units, conversions, and selection rules. Marks are commonly awarded for correctly identifying the spectral region and units. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Use | |---|---------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Wavenumber definition | Frequency Ōåö wavelength conversion | | 2 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{\nu}{c}$ | Wavenumber from frequency | Where $c$ is the speed of light | | 3 | $\nu_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | Hooke's Law for diatomic vibration | Predicting absorption frequency | | 4 | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Reduced mass of a diatomic molecule | Used in Hooke's Law | | 5 | $I(\delta) = I_0 \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)$ | Interferogram for monochromatic source | Single-frequency interference | | 6 | $I(\delta) = \displaystyle\int_0^\infty S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu$ | Interferogram for polychromatic source | Real FTIR measurement | | 7 | $S(\nu) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta$ | Fourier Cosine Transform (FT) | Recovering spectrum from interferogram | | 8 | $\delta = 2(x - y)$ | Optical path difference | Where $x, y$ are mirror distances | | 9 | $\Delta E = h \nu = h c \tilde{\nu}$ | Quantum energy of IR photon | Energy absorbed by the bond | | 10 | $\nu_0(\text{anharmonic}) = \nu_e (v + \tfrac{1}{2}) - \nu_e x_e (v + \tfrac{1}{2})^2$ | Anharmonic oscillator correction | Real bond behavior at higher $v$ | ### 2.3 Advantages of FTIR over Dispersive IR (The "Three Big Wins") | Advantage | Name | Reason | |-----------|------|--------| | **Speed** | **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage** | All frequencies measured simultaneously | | **High throughput** | **Jacquinot Advantage** | No narrow slits required ŌåÆ more light reaches detector | | **Wavelength accuracy** | **Connes Advantage** | Internal He-Ne laser provides precise mirror-position reference | | **High S/N ratio** | ŌĆö | Signal averaged over many rapid scans | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In nanomaterial research, FTIR is used to confirm successful **surface functionalization** (e.g., -COOH, -NHŌéé groups on gold nanoparticles or carbon nanotubes), verify the **reduction of graphene oxide**, and detect **ligand binding on quantum dots**. ### 2.4 Characteristic IR Absorption Regions for Nanomaterial Bonds | Wavenumber (cmŌü╗┬╣) | Bond / Functional Group | Common in Nanomaterials | |---|---|---| | **3200ŌĆō3600** | OŌĆōH stretch (broad) | Hydroxylated CNTs, GO, SiOŌéé NPs | | **2800ŌĆō3000** | CŌĆōH stretch (sp┬│) | Organic-capped nanoparticles | | **2100ŌĆō2260** | CŌēĪC / CŌēĪN stretch | Functionalized CNTs | | **1690ŌĆō1760** | C=O stretch | Carboxylated graphene, polymers | | **1620ŌĆō1680** | C=C stretch | Graphene, GO sp┬▓ domains | | **1000ŌĆō1300** | CŌĆōO stretch | PEG-coated nanoparticles | | **500ŌĆō900** | MetalŌĆōO / MetalŌĆōS | Metal oxide/sulfide NPs | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hooke's Law for a Diatomic Vibrating Bond **Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the system.** Consider two atoms with masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ connected by a chemical bond. The bond acts as a spring with force constant $k$ (in N/m). Let $x_1$ and $x_2$ be their displacements from equilibrium. **Step 2 ŌĆö Write Newton's second law for each atom.** $$m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = -k(x_1 - x_2)$$ $$m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = -k(x_2 - x_1)$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Introduce the center-of-mass coordinate.** To simplify, switch to the **reduced mass** $\mu$ and **relative displacement** $q = x_1 - x_2$: $$\mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Reduce to single-particle form.** Substituting the reduced mass transforms the two coupled equations into one: $$\mu \ddot{q} = -k q$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Solve the differential equation.** This is a classic simple harmonic oscillator. Assume solution $q(t) = A \cos(\omega t)$: $$\mu (-A\omega^2 \cos(\omega t)) = -k A \cos(\omega t)$$ Canceling common terms: $$\mu \omega^2 = k \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Convert to vibrational frequency (Hz) and wavenumber (cmŌü╗┬╣).** $$\nu_0 = \frac{\omega}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$ $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{\nu_0}{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} \quad \text{(cm}^{-1}\text{)}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} \quad \text{where} \quad \mu = \frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Interferogram Equation (Michelson Interferometer) **Step 1 ŌĆö Define the path difference.** Let the fixed mirror be at distance $x$ and the moving mirror at distance $y$ from the beam splitter. The optical path difference is: $$\delta = 2(x - y)$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Consider a monochromatic source of wavenumber $\nu_0$.** The electric field of the recombined beam at the detector is the sum of the two reflected beams. For a path difference $\delta$, the two beams are phase-shifted by $2\pi \nu_0 \delta$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Write the total field and intensity.** The detector measures intensity (proportional to the square of the field). For a monochromatic source: $$I(\delta) = I_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta)$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Extend to a polychromatic source.** A real IR source contains a continuous distribution of wavenumbers $S(\nu)$. The total intensity is the integral (superposition) of all contributions: $$I(\delta) = \int_0^\infty S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu$$ This is the **fundamental interferogram equation** of FTIR. **Step 5 ŌĆö Recover the spectrum by inverse Fourier Cosine Transform.** Applying the inverse Fourier transform: $$S(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta$$ **Step 6 ŌĆö Sign convention and constants.** The full transform pair is: $$S(\nu) = 2 \int_0^\infty I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta$$ $$\boxed{I(\delta) = \int_0^\infty S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S(\nu) = 2 \int_0^\infty I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö Predicting the OŌĆōH Stretch **Problem:** Estimate the wavenumber of the OŌĆōH stretching vibration in water. The force constant for OŌĆōH is approximately $k = 7.7 \times 10^2$ N/m. **Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate the reduced mass.** Atomic masses: H = 1.008 u, O = 16.00 u. Convert to kg: - $m_H = 1.008 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.674 \times 10^{-27}$ kg - $m_O = 16.00 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 2.657 \times 10^{-26}$ kg $$\mu = \frac{m_H m_O}{m_H + m_O} = \frac{(1.674 \times 10^{-27})(2.657 \times 10^{-26})}{1.674 \times 10^{-27} + 2.657 \times 10^{-26}}$$ $$\mu = \frac{4.448 \times 10^{-53}}{2.824 \times 10^{-26}} = 1.575 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the Hooke equation for wavenumber.** $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2.998 \times 10^{10})} \sqrt{\frac{7.7 \times 10^{2}}{1.575 \times 10^{-27}}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the square root.** $$\sqrt{\frac{7.7 \times 10^{2}}{1.575 \times 10^{-27}}} = \sqrt{4.889 \times 10^{29}} = 6.992 \times 10^{14} \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Compute the wavenumber.** $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{6.992 \times 10^{14}}{2\pi (2.998 \times 10^{10})} = \frac{6.992 \times 10^{14}}{1.884 \times 10^{11}}$$ $$\boxed{\tilde{\nu}_0 \approx 3711 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Compare with experimental value.** The experimental OŌĆōH stretching absorption in water appears at approximately **3200ŌĆō3600 cmŌü╗┬╣** (broad). Our simple harmonic model gives ~3711 cmŌü╗┬╣, which is in the correct region. The discrepancy is due to: - **Hydrogen bonding** (broadens and shifts the band) - **Anharmonicity** (shifts the actual frequency lower) - Our model used a simple diatomic approximation for a polyatomic molecule. ### 3.4 Derivation 3: Resolution of an FTIR Spectrometer The spectral resolution of an FTIR is determined by the **maximum optical path difference** $D_{\max}$: $$\Delta \tilde{\nu} = \frac{1}{D_{\max}} = \frac{1}{2 x_{\max}}$$ where $x_{\max}$ is the maximum displacement of the moving mirror from zero path difference. For high-resolution FTIR, $x_{\max}$ is several centimeters, giving $\Delta \tilde{\nu} \sim 0.001$ cmŌü╗┬╣. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of an FTIR Spectrometer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IR Source
Globar or Nernst Glower] --> B[Beam Splitter
Ge coated KBr] B --> C[Fixed Mirror] B --> D[Moving Mirror
Linear Translator] C --> B D --> B B --> E[Sample
Compartment] E --> F[Detector
DTGS or MCT] F --> G[Amplifier and
ADC Converter] G --> H[Computer with
FFT Algorithm] H --> I[IR Spectrum
cm-1 vs Absorbance] J[He-Ne Laser
Reference] --> B H --> K[Interferogram
Display] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Michelson Interferometer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph IN[INPUT] SR[Polychromatic IR Source] end subgraph CORE[INTERFEROMETER CORE] BS[Beam Splitter 50:50] FM[Fixed Mirror] MM[Moving Mirror at distance y] LA[Laser Reference He-Ne] end subgraph OUT[OUTPUT CHAIN] SC[Sample Cell] DE[Detector DTGS] AM[Amplifier] FT[FFT Processor] SP[Final Spectrum] end SR --> BS BS --> FM BS --> MM FM --> BS MM --> BS LA --> BS BS --> SC SC --> DE DE --> AM AM --> FT FT --> SP ``` ### 4.3 FTIR Signal Processing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Interferogram
Intensity vs Mirror Position] --> B[Phase Correction
using Mertz Method] B --> C[Apodization
using Happ-Genzel or Boxcar] C --> D[Zero Filling
Interpolation] D --> E[Fast Fourier Transform
FFT Algorithm] E --> F[Background Subtraction
Atmospheric Correction] F --> G[Final Absorbance or Transmittance
Spectrum in cm-1] ``` ### 4.4 Spectral Regions of an FTIR Spectrum ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FTIR Spectrum 4000 to 400 cm-1] --> B[Functional Group Region
4000 to 1500 cm-1] A --> C[Fingerprint Region
1500 to 400 cm-1] B --> D[O-H 3200-3600] B --> E[C-H 2800-3000] B --> F[C triple bond N 2100-2260] B --> G[C double bond O 1690-1760] C --> H[C-O 1000-1300] C --> I[Metal-O 500-900] ``` ### 4.5 Application in Nanomaterial Surface Functionalization ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Nanomaterial e.g. CNT] --> B[Acid Oxidation
HNO3 plus H2SO4] B --> C[Functionalized CNT
with COOH groups] C --> D[FTIR Measurement] D --> E{Compare Spectra} E -->|New Peak at 1720 cm-1| F[Confirmation of C double bond O
Carboxyl group present] E -->|New Broad Peak at 3400 cm-1| G[Confirmation of O-H stretch] F --> H[Successful Functionalization Verified] G --> H ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** All Mermaid diagrams use alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase text labels (no markdown formatting inside quoted labels) to ensure reliable rendering across Mermaid v9+ parsers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]** Define Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR). List any two advantages of FTIR over conventional dispersive IR spectroscopy. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** FTIR is a spectroscopic technique that uses a **Michelson interferometer** to modulate infrared radiation across all frequencies simultaneously. The resulting **interferogram** is mathematically converted into a conventional IR spectrum (absorbance vs. wavenumber) using a **Fourier Transform**. **Advantages (1.5 marks ŌĆö any two):** 1. **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage:** All wavenumbers measured simultaneously ŌåÆ faster acquisition. 2. **Jacquinot Advantage:** Higher throughput of IR radiation ŌåÆ better signal-to-noise ratio. 3. **Connes Advantage:** Internal He-Ne laser provides highly accurate wavenumber calibration. 4. **Speed:** Multiple rapid scans can be co-added to improve S/N ratio. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]** Explain the **selection rule** for IR absorption. Why is homonuclear diatomic nitrogen (NŌéé) IR-inactive? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Selection Rule (1.5 marks):** A molecular vibration is **IR active** only if it produces a **change in the dipole moment** of the molecule during the vibration: $$\left( \frac{\partial \mu}{\partial q} \right) \neq 0$$ where $\mu$ is the dipole moment and $q$ is the normal coordinate of vibration. **NŌéé Case (1.5 marks):** NŌéé is a homonuclear diatomic molecule. During vibration, both atoms are identical (NŌĆōN), so the molecule possesses **no permanent dipole moment** at equilibrium, and the vibration **does not generate any change** in dipole moment. Hence, $\partial \mu / \partial q = 0$ and the vibration is **IR inactive**. Such molecules are typically characterized using **Raman spectroscopy** instead. --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With a neat labeled block diagram, describe the **working principle of a Michelson interferometer** used in FTIR. Derive the **interferogram equation** for a polychromatic source. (7 marks) **(b)** Using the **harmonic oscillator model**, derive the expression for the vibrational wavenumber of a diatomic molecule. Calculate the wavenumber of the CŌĆōH stretch given that the force constant $k = 500$ N/m and the atomic masses are $^{12}$C = 12 u, $^{1}$H = 1 u. (1 u = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg, c = $3 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s). (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1 (FTIR Spectrometer Block Diagram) above. **Working Principle (3 marks):** 1. The **IR source** (e.g., Globar) emits polychromatic infrared radiation. 2. The **beam splitter** (e.g., Ge-coated KBr) divides the beam into two equal parts ŌĆö one transmitted, one reflected. 3. The **fixed mirror** reflects one beam back to the beam splitter. 4. The **moving mirror** (translated linearly) reflects the other beam back; its position $y$ varies with time. 5. The two reflected beams recombine at the beam splitter, producing **constructive or destructive interference** depending on the optical path difference $\delta = 2(x - y)$. 6. The recombined beam passes through the **sample** and reaches the **detector** (DTGS or MCT). 7. A **He-Ne laser** provides a reference signal for precise mirror position tracking. **Derivation of Interferogram Equation (2 marks):** For a polychromatic source with spectral distribution $S(\nu)$: $$I(\delta) = \int_0^\infty S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu$$ This is the **interferogram**. The spectrum is recovered by inverse Fourier Cosine Transform: $$S(\nu) = 2 \int_0^\infty I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta$$ **(b) Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** Using the harmonic oscillator model with reduced mass $\mu = m_1 m_2 / (m_1 + m_2)$ and force constant $k$: $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}}$$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Reduced mass of CŌĆōH:** $$\mu = \frac{m_C m_H}{m_C + m_H} = \frac{(12)(1)}{12 + 1} = \frac{12}{13} = 0.923 \text{ u}$$ $$\mu = 0.923 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 1.533 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Hooke's Law:** $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi (3 \times 10^{10})} \sqrt{\frac{500}{1.533 \times 10^{-27}}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Evaluate the square root:** $$\sqrt{\frac{500}{1.533 \times 10^{-27}}} = \sqrt{3.262 \times 10^{29}} = 5.711 \times 10^{14} \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Final wavenumber:** $$\tilde{\nu}_0 = \frac{5.711 \times 10^{14}}{2\pi (3 \times 10^{10})} = \frac{5.711 \times 10^{14}}{1.885 \times 10^{11}}$$ $$\boxed{\tilde{\nu}_0 \approx 3030 \text{ cm}^{-1}}$$ This matches the experimental **CŌĆōH sp┬│ stretch** region of **2850ŌĆō2960 cmŌü╗┬╣** reasonably well (anharmonicity and coupling account for the small deviation). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Sample 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** What is the **Michelson interferometer**? Explain with a diagram how an FTIR spectrometer works. Discuss the **three major advantages** of FTIR over dispersive IR spectroscopy. (7 marks) **(b)** Explain the **sample preparation techniques** commonly used in FTIR analysis of nanomaterials. Discuss the application of FTIR in characterizing **surface-functionalized carbon nanotubes** and **graphene oxide**. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Part (a) Solution (7 Marks):** **Michelson Interferometer Definition (1 mark):** A Michelson interferometer is an optical device that splits a beam of light into two paths, reflects them from mirrors (one fixed, one moving), and recombines them to produce an **interferogram** ŌĆö a plot of intensity vs. optical path difference. **Working of FTIR (3 marks):** 1. The IR source emits broadband IR radiation. 2. The beam splitter divides the beam into two equal halves. 3. One half reflects from a fixed mirror, the other from a moving mirror. 4. The recombined beam passes through the sample; the detector records the interferogram. 5. A computer applies the **Fourier Transform** to convert the interferogram into a standard absorbance vs. wavenumber plot. **Three Advantages (3 marks):** 1. **Fellgett Advantage (Multiplex):** All wavenumbers are measured simultaneously, drastically reducing scan time. 2. **Jacquinot Advantage (Throughput):** The circular apertures of FTIR pass much more light than the narrow slits of dispersive instruments, improving the signal-to-noise ratio. 3. **Connes Advantage (Precision):** The built-in He-Ne laser acts as an internal wavelength standard, providing highly accurate and reproducible wavenumber calibration. **(b) Part (b) Solution (7 Marks):** **Sample Preparation Techniques (3 marks ŌĆö list and brief):** 1. **KBr Pellet Method:** The sample is finely ground and mixed with potassium bromide (KBr), then pressed into a transparent pellet under high pressure. KBr is transparent in the mid-IR region. Used for solid nanomaterials. 2. **Nujol Mull:** The sample is mixed with mineral oil (Nujol) to form a paste, sandwiched between NaCl/KBr windows. Avoids water interference but introduces CŌĆōH peaks from Nujol. 3. **Attenuated Total Reflectance (ATR):** The sample is placed in direct contact with a high-refractive-index crystal (e.g., diamond, ZnSe). The IR beam undergoes total internal reflection, and the evanescent wave probes the sample. Ideal for powders, thin films, and aqueous solutions. 4. **Thin Film Casting:** Nanomaterial dispersions are drop-cast onto IR-transparent substrates and dried. **Application: Surface-Functionalized CNTs (2 marks):** - Pristine CNTs show very few IR-active bands due to their symmetric structure. - After **acid oxidation** (HNOŌéā/HŌééSOŌéä), new peaks appear: - ~1720 cmŌü╗┬╣: **C=O stretch** of ŌĆōCOOH groups - ~1580 cmŌü╗┬╣: **C=C stretch** of CNT backbone - ~3400 cmŌü╗┬╣ (broad): **OŌĆōH stretch** of ŌĆōCOOH - This confirms successful **covalent functionalization**. **Application: Graphene Oxide (2 marks):** - GO shows characteristic peaks at: - ~3400 cmŌü╗┬╣: broad **OŌĆōH stretch** (hydroxyl and adsorbed water) - ~1720 cmŌü╗┬╣: **C=O stretch** (carbonyl/carboxyl) - ~1620 cmŌü╗┬╣: **C=C stretch** (sp┬▓ graphitic domains) - ~1050 cmŌü╗┬╣: **CŌĆōO stretch** (epoxy/alkoxy) - Upon **reduction to rGO**, the OŌĆōH, C=O, and CŌĆōO peaks diminish, confirming successful deoxygenation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting units on wavenumbers:** Always write cmŌü╗┬╣ explicitly. Many students write just the number and lose 0.5 marks. > 2. **Confusing "frequency" $\nu$ with "wavenumber" $\tilde{\nu}$:** Frequency is in Hz; wavenumber is in cmŌü╗┬╣. They are related by $\tilde{\nu} = \nu / c$. > 3. **Not stating the IR selection rule explicitly:** Examiners expect $\Delta \mu \neq 0$ to be written clearly. Don't just say "polar molecules absorb." > 4. **Wrong reduced mass formula:** Use $\mu = m_1 m_2 / (m_1 + m_2)$, **not** the arithmetic mean. > 5. **Forgetting $2\pi$ in Hooke's Law:** A common slip that gives an answer ~6.28 times too large. Always double-check angular vs. linear frequency. > 6. **In interferometer diagrams, not labeling the moving mirror clearly:** Examiners specifically check for this. > 7. **Confusing Fellgett, Jacquinot, and Connes advantages:** Match each name with the correct principle. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **­¤ōī Important Things to Remember ŌĆö Last-Minute KTU Revision Notes** - Ō£ģ **FTIR** = Fourier Transform + Infrared Spectroscopy ŌåÆ converts interferogram (time/space domain) to spectrum (frequency domain). - Ō£ģ **Core component:** **Michelson Interferometer** (beam splitter + fixed mirror + moving mirror). - Ō£ģ **Mathematical heart:** **Fourier Cosine Transform** pair: $$I(\delta) = \int_0^\infty S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu$$ $$S(\nu) = 2 \int_0^\infty I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\delta$$ - Ō£ģ **Selection Rule:** IR active Ōćö $\partial \mu / \partial q \neq 0$ (vibration changes dipole moment). - Ō£ģ **Hooke's Law for diatomic bond:** $\tilde{\nu}_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ where $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$. - Ō£ģ **Spectral regions:** Functional group (4000ŌĆō1500 cmŌü╗┬╣); Fingerprint (1500ŌĆō400 cmŌü╗┬╣). - Ō£ģ **Key bands for nanomaterials:** OŌĆōH (~3400), CŌĆōH (~2900), CŌēĪC (~2200), C=O (~1720), C=C (~1620), CŌĆōO (~1050), MetalŌĆōO (~500ŌĆō900). - Ō£ģ **Three advantages of FTIR:** Fellgett (speed/multiplex), Jacquinot (throughput), Connes (precision). - Ō£ģ **Sample prep methods:** KBr pellet, Nujol mull, ATR (most common for nanomaterials). - Ō£ģ **Nanomaterial applications:** Confirming functionalization of CNTs, GO, reduced GO, ligand attachment on metal NPs, polymerŌĆōnanoparticle interface studies. - Ō£ģ **Resolution relation:** $\Delta \tilde{\nu} = 1 / (2 x_{\max})$ ŌĆö larger mirror travel = higher resolution. - Ō£ģ **Remember the constant:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s (or $3 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s for exam calculations). --- *End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes ŌĆö Module 3: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)* *Course: PECHT833 ŌĆö Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology*

topics.title: X-ray Diffraction (XRD)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X-ray Diffraction (XRD)

# X-ray Diffraction (XRD) ŌĆō KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**X-ray Diffraction (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical technique used to identify the crystalline phases, lattice parameters, crystal structure, and grain size of nanomaterials by measuring the angles and intensities of X-rays diffracted from the atomic planes of a crystal. The technique is fundamentally governed by **Bragg's Law of Diffraction** and is the most important primary tool for the structural characterization of nanocrystalline solids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "X-ray Diffraction is a wave-based interference phenomenon where incident X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ (comparable to interatomic spacings, $\sim$ 0.1 nm) are scattered by the periodic electron density of a crystalline material, producing constructive interference (diffraction peaks) only at specific angles $2\theta$ that satisfy the Bragg condition $n\lambda = 2d \sin\theta$."

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy ŌĆō "The Stadium Wave"

Imagine sitting in a huge circular stadium where every spectator represents an atom in a crystal lattice. If one person (an X-ray photon) starts a wave, it will only reach the opposite side of the stadium clearly if all the rows of people are spaced at *precise intervals* matching the wavelength of the step. If the rows are unevenly spaced (amorphous material), the wave dies out. This is essentially what happens in a crystal: a regular array of atoms reflects X-rays constructively only at very specific angles.

> [!NOTE]
> **X-rays** are electromagnetic radiation with wavelength **0.01ŌĆō10 nm**, typically **1.5418 ├ģ** for Cu K$\alpha$ radiation in laboratory XRD.

### 1.3 X-rays: The Probe

| Property | Value / Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| Type | Electromagnetic radiation |
| Wavelength range | **0.01ŌĆō10 nm** |
| Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength | **$\lambda = 1.5418$ ├ģ = 0.15418 nm** |
| Energy range | **100 eV ŌĆō 100 keV** |
| Penetration | Moderate (few microns in solids) |
| Generation source | X-ray tube (cathode-anode), Synchrotron |

### 1.4 X-ray Generation (X-ray Tube)

A standard X-ray tube consists of:
- **Cathode (tungsten filament):** emits electrons by thermionic emission
- **High voltage (30ŌĆō50 kV):** accelerates electrons
- **Anode (Cu, Mo, Co, Fe target):** electrons decelerate ŌåÆ produce two types of X-rays

> [!NOTE]
> **Two types of X-ray spectra produced:**
> 1. **Continuous (Bremsstrahlung) spectrum** ŌĆō due to sudden deceleration of electrons
> 2. **Characteristic spectrum (K$\alpha$, K$\beta$)** ŌĆō due to inner-shell electron transitions (K-shell to L-shell = K$\alpha$; K-shell to M-shell = K$\beta$)

The **K$\alpha$ line is preferred** for diffraction studies because it is the most intense. Cu K$\alpha$ is the most common laboratory source.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bragg's Law of Diffraction

**Statement (W.H. Bragg & W.L. Bragg, 1913):** When a beam of monochromatic X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ is incident on a set of parallel atomic planes with interplanar spacing $d$, the reflected waves from successive planes will interfere constructively when the **path difference** between them is an **integer multiple** of the wavelength.

**Geometric Origin of Path Difference:**
Consider two parallel atomic planes (hkl) with spacing $d_{hkl}$. X-rays strike at glancing angle $\theta$. The path difference between rays reflected from successive planes is:

$$\text{Path Difference} = AB + BC = 2d\sin\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **constructive interference (diffraction peak):**
> $$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta \quad \text{...(Bragg's Law)}$$
> where $n$ = order of diffraction (usually $n=1$).

### 2.2 Cubic Crystal System Interplanar Spacing

The interplanar spacing for a cubic crystal is given by:

$$d_{hkl} = \frac{a}{\sqrt{h^2 + k^2 + l^2}}$$

| Crystal System | Interplanar Spacing Formula |
| :--- | :--- |
| Simple Cubic (SC) | $a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ |
| BCC | $a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ |
| FCC | $a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ |
| Diamond Cubic | $a\sqrt{3}/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ |
| HCP | More complex (uses $a, c$ parameters) |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam-Focused Formula Table (KEEP THIS ON YOUR FINGERTIPS):**

| # | Formula | Application | Standard Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law ŌĆō main diffraction condition | $d$ in ├ģ, $\lambda$ in ├ģ, $\theta$ in degrees |
| 2 | $d_{hkl} = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | Cubic crystal plane spacing | $a, d$ in ├ģ |
| 3 | $4\sin^2\theta = (\lambda^2/a^2)(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ | Bracketed for cubic indexing | dimensionless |
| 4 | $D = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$ | **Scherrer equation ŌĆō crystallite size** | $D$ in nm |
| 5 | $\beta \cos\theta = (K\lambda/D) + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$ | Williamson-Hall equation | strain analysis |
| 6 | $\rho_{X-ray} = \dfrac{nM}{N_A a^3}$ | Theoretical X-ray density of cubic cell | g/cm┬│ |
| 7 | $a = d_{hkl}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ | Lattice parameter from single peak | ├ģ |
| 8 | $V_{cell} = a^3$ (cubic) | Unit cell volume | ┼│ |
| 9 | $N = n(h^2+k^2+l^2)$ (last indexed) | Calculate N for unit cell volume | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta = 1/d^2$ | Reciprocal lattice spacing | ┼│ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard values in the Scherrer equation:**
> - $K$ (shape factor, Scherrer constant) = **0.9** (for spherical crystallites)
> - $\lambda$ = **1.5418 ├ģ** (Cu K$\alpha$)
> - $\beta$ = **Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM)** in **radians**
> - $\theta$ = Bragg angle in radians
> - $D$ = crystallite size (typically in nm)

### 2.4 Why Bragg's Law Works (The "How")

**Step-by-Step Logic:**
1. X-rays strike a crystal at glancing angle $\theta$
2. Each atom scatters incident X-rays in all directions (Rayleigh/Thomson scattering)
3. Reflected/scattered rays from different lattice planes have a path difference
4. **Constructive interference** ŌåÆ strong peak ŌåÆ when path difference = $n\lambda$
5. **Destructive interference** ŌåÆ no signal ŌåÆ at all other angles

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of XRD

In nanomaterials science, XRD is the **backbone** technique because it can determine:
- **Phase identification** (which compound is present?)
- **Crystal structure** (FCC, BCC, HCP, etc.)
- **Lattice parameters** (precise bond distances)
- **Crystallite size** (down to ~1ŌĆō2 nm using Scherrer equation)
- **Microstrain** (via Williamson-Hall analysis)
- **Preferred orientation / texture** (in thin films)
- **Phase quantification** (Rietveld refinement)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** X-ray Diffraction is a **bulk** technique that probes an *ensemble average*. It is NOT a direct imaging technique and does NOT show individual atoms (use TEM/STM for that). XRD gives the *coherently diffracting domain size*, not particle size if particles are polycrystalline.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law

**Setup:** Consider two parallel, equally spaced atomic planes in a crystal, separated by distance $d$. Monochromatic X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike the planes at a glancing angle $\theta$ (angle between the incident ray and the plane surface).

**Step 1:** Ray 1 reflects from the *first* plane at point A; Ray 2 penetrates and reflects from the *second* plane at point B.

**Step 2:** Draw perpendiculars from point A to the incident ray and from point B to the reflected ray.

**Step 3:** The **additional path length** that Ray 2 travels compared to Ray 1 is:
- Incoming segment (before B): from the wavefront to B = $d\sin\theta$
- Outgoing segment (after B): from B to the wavefront = $d\sin\theta$
- **Total path difference** = $2d\sin\theta$

**Step 4:** For **constructive interference**, the path difference must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta \quad \text{(Bragg's Law)}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** governing all XRD experiments.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Scherrer Equation (Crystallite Size)

The Scherrer equation relates the **broadening of an XRD peak** to the **size of the crystallite** producing that peak. It was derived by Paul Scherrer (1918).

**Step 1:** Consider a crystallite containing $N$ parallel lattice planes with spacing $d$. The total diffracting thickness is $t = Nd$.

**Step 2:** The angular range of constructive interference is determined by how the path difference varies as the angle $\theta$ deviates from the exact Bragg angle $\theta_B$. The destructive limit occurs when the path difference between the top and bottom planes differs by exactly $\lambda$:

$$2(N+1)d\sin(\theta_B + \delta\theta) - 2Nd\sin\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 3:** Since $\sin(\theta_B + \delta\theta) \approx \sin\theta_B + \delta\theta\cos\theta_B$ (small angle approximation), we get:

$$2(N+1)d(\sin\theta_B + \delta\theta\cos\theta_B) - 2Nd\sin\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 4:** Expanding the product:

$$2Nd\sin\theta_B + 2d\sin\theta_B + 2Nd\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B - 2Nd\sin\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 5:** The first and last terms cancel; since $2d\sin\theta_B = \lambda$ (from Bragg's law, with $n=1$):

$$\lambda + 2N d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 6:** Solving for $\delta\theta$:

$$2N d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B = 0 \Rightarrow \text{(apparent zero)}$$

This shows the peak has a *finite* angular range. The true broadening $\beta$ (FWHM) is:

$$\beta = \frac{\lambda}{t\cos\theta_B} \quad \text{where } t = Nd$$

**Step 7:** Introducing the dimensionless shape factor $K$ (Scherrer constant, typically 0.9):

$$\boxed{D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}}$$

This is the **Scherrer equation** ŌĆō the most important equation for KTU nanotechnology exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Smaller crystallites ŌåÆ fewer parallel planes ŌåÆ less coherent cancellation ŌåÆ broader diffraction peak. This is the foundation for using XRD to measure nanocrystal size.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solving Standard KTU Problems

**Problem Type 1:** Given $\lambda$ and $d$, find $\theta$.

**Algorithm:**
1. Apply Bragg's Law: $\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$
2. Solve: $\sin\theta = \lambda/(2d)$
3. Take inverse sine: $\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\lambda/(2d)\right)$
4. Compute $2\theta$ (the angle recorded in diffractometer)

**Problem Type 2:** Given $2\theta$ peaks, find lattice parameter $a$.

**Algorithm:**
1. From peak position, compute $\theta = (2\theta)/2$
2. Use Bragg's Law: $d = \lambda/(2\sin\theta)$
3. Apply cubic formula: $a = d\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$

**Problem Type 3:** Given peak FWHM, find crystallite size.

**Algorithm:**
1. From diffractogram, extract peak position $2\theta$ ŌåÆ $\theta$ in radians
2. Measure FWHM $\beta$ in radians
3. Apply Scherrer: $D = (0.9 \times 1.5418)/(\beta\cos\theta)$ ├ģ ŌåÆ convert to nm

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** Copper K$\alpha$ radiation ($\lambda = 1.5418$ ├ģ) is used to analyze an FCC copper sample. A diffraction peak appears at $2\theta = 43.32┬░$ corresponding to the (111) plane. Calculate: (a) the interplanar spacing, and (b) the lattice parameter of Cu.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Interplanar spacing

$$\theta = \frac{2\theta}{2} = \frac{43.32┬░}{2} = 21.66┬░$$

Applying Bragg's Law (with $n=1$):

$$d_{111} = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{1.5418 \text{ ├ģ}}{2 \times \sin(21.66┬░)}$$

$$d_{111} = \frac{1.5418}{2 \times 0.3691} = \frac{1.5418}{0.7382} = 2.088 \text{ ├ģ}$$

**Part (b):** Lattice parameter

For cubic crystals:

$$a = d_{111}\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2} = 2.088 \times \sqrt{1^2+1^2+1^2}$$

$$a = 2.088 \times \sqrt{3} = 2.088 \times 1.732 = 3.616 \text{ ├ģ}$$

This matches the known lattice parameter of Cu ($a = 3.615$ ├ģ), confirming our analysis.

### 3.5 Worked Example: Scherrer Equation Application

**Question:** A ZnO nanoparticle sample is analyzed by XRD with Cu K$\alpha$ radiation. The (101) peak appears at $2\theta = 36.25┬░$ with a FWHM of $0.42┬░$. Calculate the average crystallite size.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert $2\theta$ to $\theta$ in radians.

$$\theta = \frac{36.25┬░}{2} = 18.125┬░ = 18.125 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.3163 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Convert FWHM to radians.

$$\beta = 0.42┬░ = 0.42 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 7.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3:** Apply Scherrer equation.

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5418 \text{ ├ģ}}{7.33 \times 10^{-3} \times \cos(18.125┬░)}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3876 \text{ ├ģ}}{7.33 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9505} = \frac{1.3876}{6.967 \times 10^{-3}} = 199.2 \text{ ├ģ}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to nanometers.

$$D = 19.92 \text{ nm} \approx 20 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** $\beta$ in radians is essential. If given in degrees, ALWAYS convert first. Wrong units = wrong answer (a common KTU mark-loser).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question A1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**State Bragg's Law of X-ray diffraction and explain its significance in materials characterization.**

**Model Answer:**
Bragg's Law states that for a crystalline material, X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ incident on parallel atomic planes with interplanar spacing $d$ will produce constructive interference (diffracted beam) only when:

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Significance:**
1. It establishes the fundamental geometric condition for diffraction
2. It allows calculation of **interplanar spacing $d$** from measured $\theta$
3. It is the basis for determining **crystal structure, lattice parameters, phase identification**, and **crystallite size** in nanomaterials
4. It forms the theoretical foundation for techniques like powder XRD and rotating crystal method

---

**Question A2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]**
**Differentiate between X-ray diffraction patterns of crystalline and amorphous nanomaterials.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Crystalline | Amorphous |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Peak shape | Sharp, well-defined peaks | Broad humps (halos) |
| Peak position | Specific $2\theta$ from Bragg's law | No specific position |
| Information | d-spacing, lattice parameters, size | Short-range order only |
| Order | Long-range periodic order | No long-range order |
| Example | ZnO nanocrystal | Silica glass |

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆō Internal Choice)

---

**Question B ŌĆō Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive Bragg's Law of X-ray diffraction. Draw a neat labeled diagram showing the geometry of the derivation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 ŌĆō Mermaid Block Diagram)

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Consider a set of parallel, equally spaced atomic planes with interplanar spacing $d$. Monochromatic X-rays of wavelength $\lambda$ strike the planes at a glancing angle $\theta$.

**Step 2:** Ray 1 reflects from plane 1 (top) at point A. Ray 2 penetrates to plane 2 and reflects at point B.

**Step 3:** Draw perpendiculars from A to the incident ray and from B to the reflected ray to form the geometric path difference triangle.

**Step 4:** The path difference between Ray 1 and Ray 2 is:

$$\Delta = AB + BC = d\sin\theta + d\sin\theta = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Step 5:** For constructive interference, the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$\boxed{n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta \quad \text{(Bragg's Law)}}$$

**Step 6:** For $n=1$ (first-order diffraction), the condition simplifies to:

$$\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Diagram with labels: 2 marks
- Path difference derivation: 3 marks
- Final equation with condition: 2 marks

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the Scherrer equation for determining the crystallite size of nanocrystalline materials from XRD data.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Consider a small crystallite of thickness $t$ containing $N$ parallel lattice planes. The total thickness is $t = Nd$, where $d$ is the interplanar spacing.

**Step 2:** At the exact Bragg angle $\theta_B$, all $N$ planes diffract in phase. The total path difference across $N$ planes is:

$$\Delta_{total} = 2Nd\sin\theta_B = 2N \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} = N\lambda$$

**Step 3:** When the angle deviates by a small amount $\delta\theta$, the cumulative path difference changes. The diffraction intensity remains high as long as the additional path difference across the entire crystallite is less than $\lambda$:

$$2(N+1)d\sin(\theta_B + \delta\theta) - 2Nd\sin\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 4:** Using small-angle expansion $\sin(\theta_B + \delta\theta) \approx \sin\theta_B + \delta\theta\cos\theta_B$:

$$2(N+1)d\sin\theta_B + 2(N+1)d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B - 2Nd\sin\theta_B = \lambda$$

**Step 5:** Simplify using $2d\sin\theta_B = \lambda$:

$$(N+1)\lambda + 2(N+1)d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B - N\lambda = \lambda$$

**Step 6:** The first and third terms partially cancel, leaving:

$$\lambda + 2(N+1)d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B = \lambda$$

$$\therefore 2(N+1)d\,\delta\theta\cos\theta_B \approx \lambda \quad (\text{using } N+1 \approx N)$$

$$\delta\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{2Nd\cos\theta_B} = \frac{\lambda}{2t\cos\theta_B}$$

**Step 7:** The total angular range $2\delta\theta$ is the FWHM $\beta$:

$$\beta = \frac{\lambda}{t\cos\theta_B}$$

**Step 8:** Introducing the Scherrer constant $K$ (typically $K = 0.9$):

$$\boxed{D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta}}$$

where $D$ is the average crystallite size, $K = 0.9$, $\lambda$ is X-ray wavelength (1.5418 ├ģ for Cu K$\alpha$), $\beta$ is FWHM in radians, and $\theta$ is the Bragg angle.

---

**Question B ŌĆō Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the powder X-ray diffraction method. Discuss its advantages for characterizing nanomaterials.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** The powder XRD method uses a monochromatic X-ray beam (Cu K$\alpha$, $\lambda = 1.5418$ ├ģ) incident on a polycrystalline powder sample containing millions of randomly oriented nanocrystallites. Since all orientations are present statistically, Bragg's condition is automatically satisfied for every (hkl) plane in the sample.

**Instrumentation:**
1. **X-ray source** ŌĆō Cu target tube with K$\beta$ filter and monochromator
2. **Sample stage** ŌĆō flat plate sample holder, sample rotated to improve statistics
3. **Detector** ŌĆō scintillation counter mounted on a goniometer arm
4. **Data system** ŌĆō records intensity vs. $2\theta$ ŌåÆ **diffractogram**

**Procedure:**
1. Place finely ground powder in the sample holder
2. Mount the holder on the goniometer
3. Scan $2\theta$ from $5┬░$ to $90┬░$ (or wider)
4. Record intensity peaks at characteristic $2\theta$ values
5. Index peaks using JCPDS-ICDD database ŌåÆ identify phase

**Advantages for Nanomaterials:**
1. **Phase identification** ŌĆō unique fingerprint of every crystalline phase
2. **Crystallite size** ŌĆō Scherrer equation for sizes 1ŌĆō100 nm
3. **Lattice parameters** ŌĆō precise calculation from peak positions
4. **Strain analysis** ŌĆō Williamson-Hall plot
5. **Non-destructive** ŌĆō sample can be recovered
6. **Bulk statistics** ŌĆō averages over millions of crystallites (representative)
7. **Quantitative** ŌĆō relative phase amounts via Rietveld refinement

---

**(b) [7 Marks] A nanocrystalline CuO sample is analyzed by powder XRD using Cu K$\alpha$ radiation ($\lambda = 1.5418$ ├ģ). A diffraction peak is observed at $2\theta = 38.7┬░$ with FWHM = $0.48┬░$. Calculate: (i) the d-spacing, and (ii) the average crystallite size.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i): Calculation of d-spacing**

**Step 1:** Bragg angle:

$$\theta = \frac{2\theta}{2} = \frac{38.7┬░}{2} = 19.35┬░$$

**Step 2:** Apply Bragg's Law:

$$d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{1.5418 \text{ ├ģ}}{2 \times \sin(19.35┬░)}$$

$$\sin(19.35┬░) = 0.3314$$

$$d = \frac{1.5418}{2 \times 0.3314} = \frac{1.5418}{0.6628} = 2.327 \text{ ├ģ}$$

$$\boxed{d = 2.327 \text{ ├ģ}}$$

**Part (ii): Calculation of crystallite size**

**Step 1:** Convert FWHM to radians:

$$\beta = 0.48┬░ \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 8.378 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2:** Convert $\theta$ to radians (not strictly needed, as $\cos$ works in degrees):

$$\theta = 19.35┬░, \quad \cos(19.35┬░) = 0.9434$$

**Step 3:** Apply Scherrer equation:

$$D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta\cos\theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 1.5418 \text{ ├ģ}}{8.378 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.9434}$$

$$D = \frac{1.3876}{7.903 \times 10^{-3}} = 175.6 \text{ ├ģ}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to nanometers:

$$\boxed{D = 17.56 \text{ nm} \approx 17.6 \text{ nm}}$$

**Conclusion:** The CuO crystallites are in the nanometer regime (~17.6 nm), confirming the nanocrystalline nature of the sample.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MISTAKES THAT COST KTU STUDENTS MARKS:**
>
> 1. **Unit error in Scherrer equation:** $\beta$ MUST be in **radians**, NOT degrees. Forgetting to multiply by $\pi/180$ loses 2ŌĆō3 marks.
>
> 2. **Using $2\theta$ instead of $\theta$:** The Scherrer equation uses $\theta$ (Bragg angle), not $2\theta$ (diffractometer angle). Always halve the $2\theta$ value first.
>
> 3. **Wrong $K$ value:** Use $K = 0.9$ for most cases. Using $K = 1$ gives a slightly different (and incorrect for KTU) answer.
>
> 4. **Confusing $d$-spacing with lattice parameter $a$:** $d$ is for one specific (hkl) plane, while $a$ is the unit cell edge. They are related by $a = d\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$ for cubic systems.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to draw the diagram in derivations:** KTU examiners explicitly allocate 2 marks for a neat, labeled diagram in Bragg's Law derivation.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions:** Mention that "X-rays are monochromatic", "the crystal is perfectly periodic", and "$n=1$" for first-order diffraction.
>
> 7. **Mixing up amorphous vs crystalline XRD pattern:** Amorphous = broad hump, Crystalline = sharp peaks. Students frequently invert this.
