> - [x] **Bragg's Law:** $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ ŌĆö *MUST memorize verbatim*
> - [x] **Scherrer equation:** $D = K\lambda/(\beta\cos\theta)$ ŌĆö *most important for nano-size calculation*
> - [x] **Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength:** **$\lambda = 1.5418$ ├ģ** ŌĆö *default value unless specified*
> - [x] **Scherrer constant $K$:** **0.9** (spherical) ŌĆö *standard unless told otherwise*
> - [x] **d-spacing formula for cubic:** $d = a/\sqrt{h^2+k^2+l^2}$
> - [x] **Convert $\beta$ to radians:** $\beta(\text{rad}) = \beta(\text{deg}) \times \pi/180$
> - [x] **X-ray tube:** Cu K$\alpha$ (1.5418 ├ģ) is most common laboratory source
> - [x] **Crystalline XRD:** sharp peaks; **Amorphous:** broad humps
> - [x] **Applications:** phase ID, crystallite size, lattice parameter, strain, texture
> - [x] **Limitations:** XRD measures crystallite size (not particle size), is a bulk technique, cannot image individual atoms
> - [x] **Williamson-Hall equation:** $\beta\cos\theta = K\lambda/D + 4\varepsilon\sin\theta$ ŌĆö separates size and strain broadening
> - [x] **Path difference in Bragg derivation:** $\Delta = 2d\sin\theta$
> - [x] **Nanorange:** Crystallite size < 100 nm produces significant peak broadening
> - [x] **JCPDS-ICDD database:** Used for phase fingerprinting and identification
> - [x] **Geometry:** Diffractometer measures $2\theta$, NOT $\theta$ ŌĆö important for all problems

### 5.5 Final Examiner's Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY REASONING CHECKLIST FOR KTU 2024 EVALUATION:**
> 1. Did the student write Bragg's Law correctly with all symbols defined?
> 2. Did they convert FWHM to radians when applying Scherrer?
> 3. Did they include the Scherrer constant and justify its value?
> 4. Did they distinguish between (hkl) plane spacing $d$ and lattice parameter $a$?
> 5. Is the diagram labeled, clean, and geometrically correct?
> 6. Did they use Cu K$\alpha$ wavelength as 1.5418 ├ģ (default)?
> 7. Are units consistent throughout the calculation?
> 8. Did they draw real-world application (e.g., nanoparticles, drug delivery, catalysts)?

---

**End of Module 3 ŌĆō X-ray Diffraction (XRD) Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme examination standards*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS)**, also universally known as **Photon Correlation Spectroscopy (PCS)** or **Quasi-Elastic Light Scattering (QELS)**, is a non-invasive, in-situ physical characterization technique that measures the **time-dependent fluctuations in the intensity of light scattered by a suspension of nanoparticles undergoing Brownian motion**. By mathematically analyzing these intensity fluctuations via the **autocorrelation function**, the technique precisely determines the **translational diffusion coefficient (D)** of the particles, from which the **hydrodynamic diameter (d_H)** is derived using the **StokesŌĆōEinstein equation**.

In the strict KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, DLS is a **colloidal characterization method** that bridges the gap between optical spectroscopy and particle sizing, specifically optimized for the sub-micron range (**1 nm to 10 ┬Ąm**).

### 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Boat on a Pond Analogy"** ŌĆö Imagine you are standing on the shore of a pond watching tiny floating cork pieces (nanoparticles) drift randomly due to water currents (Brownian motion). If you shine a flashlight on them, the reflection flickers.
> - **Big corks (large particles)** move slowly ŌåÆ the flicker changes slowly.
> - **Small corks (small particles)** dart around quickly ŌåÆ the flicker changes very fast.
>
> DLS is essentially a "high-speed camera for flicker." It records the speckle pattern of scattered laser light at nanosecond resolution. A computer then checks *how similar the speckle pattern is to itself a microsecond later*. If the pattern changes quickly, the particles are small. If it lingers, the particles are large.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded)

The following physical constants and standard metrics are **mandatory** for DLS calculations:

| Symbol | Constant / Metric | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **k_B** | Boltzmann constant | **1.380649 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬▓┬│ J┬ĘKŌü╗┬╣** |
| **T** | Absolute temperature | Kelvin (K) |
| **╬Ę** | Viscosity of dispersant | **Pa┬Ęs** (or cP; 1 cP = 10Ōü╗┬│ Pa┬Ęs) |
| **n** | Refractive index of medium | dimensionless |
| **╬╗** | Vacuum wavelength of laser | nm |
| **╬Ė** | Scattering angle | degrees (typically 90┬░) |
| **q** | Magnitude of scattering vector | mŌü╗┬╣ |
| **d_H** | Hydrodynamic diameter | **nm** |
| **D** | Translational diffusion coefficient | **m┬▓┬ĘsŌü╗┬╣** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: DLS is the *only* technique that directly yields the **hydrodynamic diameter** ŌĆö the diameter of a hypothetical hard sphere that diffuses at the same rate as the particle being measured. This includes any solvation shell, surfactant layers, or surface adsorbed polymers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Illumination**: A monochromatic, coherent laser beam (typically He-Ne at **633 nm** or a diode laser at **658 nm**) is directed into a cuvette containing the nanoparticle suspension.
2. **Scattering**: Particles in the colloidal suspension scatter the light in all directions (Rayleigh scattering dominates for nanoparticles much smaller than ╬╗).
3. **Brownian Motion**: The suspended particles undergo random thermal motion driven by collisions with solvent molecules (described by **Einstein's 1905 theory**).
4. **Intensity Fluctuation Detection**: A sensitive avalanche photodiode (APD) or photomultiplier tube (PMT) placed at a fixed scattering angle (commonly **90┬░** or **173┬░** for backscatter) records the scattered intensity *I(t)* as a function of time.
5. **Autocorrelation**: The intensity trace is fed into a digital correlator that computes the **intensity autocorrelation function**, $G^{(2)}(\tau)$.
6. **Mathematical Inversion**: The correlation function is fit to an exponential decay model (Cumulants or CONTIN algorithm) to extract the decay rate **╬ō**.
7. **Size Calculation**: ╬ō is converted to the diffusion coefficient **D**, which via the **StokesŌĆōEinstein equation** yields the hydrodynamic diameter **d_H**.

> **Why this works**: For a monodisperse sample, the correlation function decays as a single exponential. For a polydisperse sample, it is a sum of exponentials, whose distribution is solved numerically.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formulas**. Note the careful use of `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Scattering Vector Magnitude | $q = \dfrac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \sin\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right)$ | q in **mŌü╗┬╣**, n dimensionless, ╬╗ in m, ╬Ė in rad |
| 2 | Decay Rate Relation | $\Gamma = D \, q^2$ | ╬ō in sŌü╗┬╣, D in m┬▓┬ĘsŌü╗┬╣ |
| 3 | Normalized Field Autocorrelation | $g^{(1)}(\tau) = \exp(-\Gamma \tau)$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Intensity Autocorrelation | $G^{(2)}(\tau) = A \left[1 + \beta \left\vert g^{(1)}(\tau) \right\vert^{2}\right]$ | A = baseline, ╬▓ = coherence factor (0 < ╬▓ Ōēż 1) |
| 5 | StokesŌĆōEinstein Equation | $D = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta \, d_{H}}$ | D in m┬▓┬ĘsŌü╗┬╣, d_H in m |
| 6 | Hydrodynamic Diameter | $d_{H} = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta \, D}$ | d_H in m |
| 7 | Polydispersity Index (PDI) | $\text{PDI} = \left(\dfrac{\sigma}{d_{H}}\right)^{2}$ | dimensionless; PDI < 0.1 = monodisperse |
| 8 | Mean Count Rate Indicator | Photon count rate ŌłØ particle concentration ├Ś sizeŌüČ | (Mie scattering dependence) |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry**: Quality control of **liposomal drug delivery vehicles**, mRNA vaccine nanoparticles, and protein therapeutics. Particle size directly influences bioavailability.
- **Cement & Coatings (Ceramics Module Linkage)**: Validating the colloidal stability of **TiOŌéé pigment suspensions**.
- **Catalysis**: Confirming the dispersion state of supported metal nanoparticles (Pt, Pd on carbon).
- **Food Science**: Emulsion droplet sizing in milk, mayonnaise, and nanoemulsions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Autocorrelation Decay to Hydrodynamic Diameter

This is the **single most important derivation** for the KTU 2024 ESE. We derive the working equation starting from the autocorrelation function.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Definition of the Normalized Field Autocorrelation Function

The scattered electric field $E_{s}(t)$ from a single particle has a phase that changes with the particle's position. For N identical particles undergoing Brownian motion, the **normalized field autocorrelation function** is defined as:

$$
g^{(1)}(\tau) = \frac{\langle E_{s}^{*}(t) \, E_{s}(t+\tau)\rangle}{\langle \vert E_{s}(t) \vert^{2} \rangle}
$$

**Explanation**: The brackets $\langle \, \rangle$ denote a long-time average. This function measures how rapidly the scattered field loses "memory" of its previous state as the particles diffuse away.

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Free Diffusion Solution (Monodisperse Case)

For identical spherical particles undergoing **free translational diffusion**, the phase of scattered light evolves due to changes in particle position. Solving the diffusion equation for the correlation yields:

$$
g^{(1)}(\tau) = \exp(-\Gamma \tau)
$$

**Explanation**: The correlation decays as a single exponential with decay rate **╬ō**, which is directly tied to how fast the particles diffuse.

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Relating ╬ō to the Diffusion Coefficient

The decay rate is related to the **scattering vector q** and the **diffusion coefficient D** via:

$$
\Gamma = D \, q^{2}
$$

**Explanation**: This is the core kinematic identity of DLS. Faster diffusion (larger D) ŌåÆ faster decay (larger ╬ō). The factor $q^{2}$ arises because larger scattering angles probe smaller length scales, where diffusion effects are more pronounced.

#### Step 4 ŌĆö Magnitude of the Scattering Vector

For elastic (Rayleigh) scattering at angle ╬Ė in a medium of refractive index n:

$$
q = \frac{4\pi n}{\lambda_{0}} \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)
$$

**Explanation**: ╬╗ŌéĆ is the vacuum wavelength of the laser. For backscatter geometry (╬Ė = 173┬░), q is large, giving high sensitivity to small particles.

#### Step 5 ŌĆö StokesŌĆōEinstein Equation Derivation

A spherical particle of radius r moving through a viscous fluid with velocity v experiences a viscous drag force given by **Stokes' Law**:

$$
F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi \eta r \, v
$$

The particle reaches terminal velocity when this drag balances the thermal driving force. Using the **fluctuation-dissipation theorem** (Einstein, 1905), the diffusion coefficient is:

$$
D = \frac{k_{B} T}{f}
$$

where f is the friction coefficient. For a sphere, $f = 6\pi \eta r$, giving:

$$
D = \frac{k_{B} T}{6\pi \eta r}
$$

#### Step 6 ŌĆö Inverting to Obtain the Hydrodynamic Diameter

Substituting $r = d_{H}/2$ into the StokesŌĆōEinstein equation and solving for $d_{H}$:

$$
d_{H} = \frac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta D}
$$

This is the **final working equation** of DLS. The entire instrument's job is to measure ╬ō, compute D, then output $d_{H}$.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem**: A DLS measurement is conducted at 25 ┬░C on gold nanoparticles dispersed in water (╬Ę = 0.887 cP, n = 1.33) using a He-Ne laser (╬╗ = 633 nm) at a scattering angle of 90┬░. The measured decay rate is ╬ō = 12,500 sŌü╗┬╣. Calculate the hydrodynamic diameter.

#### Step 1 ŌĆö Compute the Scattering Vector

$$
q = \frac{4\pi \times 1.33}{633 \times 10^{-9}} \sin\!\left(\frac{90^{\circ}}{2}\right) = \frac{4\pi \times 1.33}{6.33 \times 10^{-7}} \times \sin(45^{\circ})
$$

$$
q = \frac{16.713}{6.33 \times 10^{-7}} \times 0.7071 = 2.641 \times 10^{7} \times 0.7071 = 1.868 \times 10^{7} \; \text{m}^{-1}
$$

#### Step 2 ŌĆö Extract the Diffusion Coefficient

$$
D = \frac{\Gamma}{q^{2}} = \frac{12{,}500}{(1.868 \times 10^{7})^{2}} = \frac{12{,}500}{3.489 \times 10^{14}} = 3.583 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}^{-1}
$$

#### Step 3 ŌĆö Compute Hydrodynamic Diameter

Convert viscosity: $\eta = 0.887 \; \text{cP} = 0.887 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$.

Temperature: $T = 298.15 \; \text{K}$.

$$
d_{H} = \frac{(1.3806 \times 10^{-23}) \times 298.15}{3\pi \times (0.887 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.583 \times 10^{-11})}
$$

$$
d_{H} = \frac{4.117 \times 10^{-21}}{3\pi \times 3.178 \times 10^{-14}} = \frac{4.117 \times 10^{-21}}{2.994 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.375 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_{H} \approx 13.75 \; \text{nm}}
$$

### 3.3 The Cumulants Analysis (PDI Extraction)

For polydisperse samples, $\ln g^{(1)}(\tau)$ is expanded as a **polynomial in Žä**:

$$
\ln g^{(1)}(\tau) = -\Gamma \tau + \frac{\mu_{2}}{2!}\tau^{2} - \frac{\mu_{3}}{3!}\tau^{3} + \cdots
$$

where:
- $\Gamma$ ŌåÆ **z-average diffusion coefficient** ŌåÆ z-average diameter
- $\mu_{2}$ ŌåÆ variance of the distribution
- PDI is defined as: $\text{PDI} = \mu_{2} / \Gamma^{2}$

**Standard Acceptance Criterion**: PDI < **0.1** indicates a monodisperse sample suitable for downstream applications.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Instrument Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LASER SOURCE 633 nm He-Ne] --> B[BEAM SHAPER & ATTENUATOR]
    B --> C[CUVETTE HOLDING NANOPARTICLE SUSPENSION]
    C --> D[SCATTERED LIGHT AT ANGLE theta]
    D --> E[DETECTOR APD or PMT]
    E --> F[DIGITAL CORRELATOR BOARD]
    F --> G[COMPUTER WITH DLS SOFTWARE]
    G --> H[OUTPUT d_H nm AND PDI]

    C -.-> I[BROWNIAN MOTION OF PARTICLES]
    I -.-> D
```

### 4.2 Signal Processing Workflow (From Intensity Trace to Size)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Raw Scattered Intensity I of t"] --> nodeB["Compute Intensity Autocorrelation G2 of tau"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Apply Siegert Relation to Obtain g1 of tau"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"Monodisperse or Polydisperse?"}
    nodeD -- Monodisperse --> nodeE["Single Exponential Fit: g1 = exp minus Gamma tau"]
    nodeD -- Polydisperse --> nodeF["Cumulants or CONTIN Analysis"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Extract D from Gamma equals D q squared"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Apply Stokes Einstein: dH equals kT over 3 pi eta D"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Report Hydrodynamic Diameter and PDI"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for DLS Sample Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["Sample Preparation Stage"] --> p2{"Sample Concentration?"}
    p2 -- Too Concentrated --> p3["Dilute to Avoid Multiple Scattering"]
    p2 -- Too Dilute --> p4["Increase Integration Time or Concentration"]
    p2 -- Optimal --> p5{"Dust or Aggregates Present?"}
    p5 -- Yes --> p6["Filter through 0.2 micron Membrane"]
    p5 -- No --> p7["Load Cuvette and Run Measurement"]
    p3 --> p5
    p4 --> p5
    p6 --> p5
```

### 4.4 Geometry Comparison: 90┬░ vs 173┬░ (Backscatter)

| Geometry | Detection Angle | q-Value | Sensitivity | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **90┬░ Detection** | 90┬░ | $\frac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Moderate | High-quality dilute samples |
| **173┬░ Backscatter** | 173┬░ | $\frac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \cdot \sin(86.5┬░) \approx \frac{4\pi n}{\lambda}$ | High to small particles | Concentrated, turbid samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight**: Higher q (backscatter) probes shorter length scales, making the technique **more sensitive to smaller particles**. This is why modern Malvern Zetasizer instruments default to 173┬░.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define Dynamic Light Scattering. Mention the size range of particles that can be measured by DLS.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) is a physical characterization technique used to determine the **hydrodynamic diameter** of particles in a colloidal suspension by measuring the intensity fluctuations of laser light scattered by particles undergoing **Brownian motion**.

**Size range**: Approximately **1 nm to 10 ┬Ąm** (practically optimal between 1 nm and 1 ┬Ąm).

The technique works on the principle that the rate of intensity fluctuation is inversely related to particle size: smaller particles diffuse faster and produce more rapid fluctuations.

**[Mark Distribution: Definition ŌĆō 2 marks; Size range ŌĆō 1 mark]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Sample Pattern]**
*State and explain the StokesŌĆōEinstein equation used in DLS. Define each term.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The StokesŌĆōEinstein equation relates the **translational diffusion coefficient (D)** of a spherical particle to its **hydrodynamic diameter (d_H)**:

$$
D = \frac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta \, d_{H}}
$$

Where:
- **D** = Translational diffusion coefficient (m┬▓┬ĘsŌü╗┬╣)
- **k_B** = Boltzmann constant = **1.3806 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬▓┬│ J┬ĘKŌü╗┬╣**
- **T** = Absolute temperature (K)
- **╬Ę** = Viscosity of the dispersing medium (Pa┬Ęs)
- **d_H** = Hydrodynamic diameter (m)

**[Mark Distribution: Equation ŌĆō 1.5 marks; Definitions ŌĆō 1.5 marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A ŌĆö [14 Marks]**

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024 Model Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **principle and instrumentation** of Dynamic Light Scattering with a neat block diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **StokesŌĆōEinstein equation** starting from Stokes' law and the Einstein diffusion relation. Show its importance in determining particle size. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Principle (3 Marks):**
- DLS works on the principle of **Brownian motion** of nanoparticles in a liquid medium (Einstein, 1905).
- When laser light strikes these particles, the scattered light intensity fluctuates over time due to constant positional changes of the particles.
- These fluctuations are analyzed using the **intensity autocorrelation function** $G^{(2)}(\tau)$.
- The decay rate ╬ō of the autocorrelation function is mathematically related to the diffusion coefficient D, which via the StokesŌĆōEinstein equation yields the hydrodynamic diameter.

**Instrumentation (4 Marks):**

The major components are:

1. **Laser Source** (He-Ne, 633 nm) ŌĆö provides coherent monochromatic light.
2. **Attenuator / Beam Shaper** ŌĆö controls intensity and beam profile.
3. **Sample Cell (Cuvette)** ŌĆö typically a square quartz cuvette holding ~1 mL of suspension.
4. **Detector** ŌĆö Avalanche Photodiode (APD) or Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) placed at scattering angle ╬Ė (commonly 90┬░ or 173┬░).
5. **Digital Correlator** ŌĆö a hardware device that computes the autocorrelation function in real time at nanosecond resolution.
6. **Computer with Analysis Software** ŌĆö performs Cumulants or CONTIN fitting and reports d_H and PDI.

*(Block diagram must be drawn for full marks ŌĆö see Section 4.1.)*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Stokes' Law (2 Marks):**
For a spherical particle of radius r moving through a fluid of viscosity ╬Ę with velocity v, the viscous drag force is:

$$
F_{\text{drag}} = 6\pi \eta r \, v
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Friction Coefficient (1 Mark):**
Comparing with $F = f v$, the friction coefficient is:

$$
f = 6\pi \eta r
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Einstein's Diffusion Relation (2 Marks):**
The fluctuationŌĆōdissipation theorem states:

$$
D = \frac{k_{B} T}{f}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combining (1 Mark):**

$$
D = \frac{k_{B} T}{6\pi \eta r}
$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Inversion for Diameter (1 Mark):**
Substituting $r = d_{H}/2$:

$$
\boxed{d_{H} = \frac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta D}}
$$

**Importance**: This equation allows DLS to convert the measured diffusion coefficient (from autocorrelation decay) into the hydrodynamic diameter ŌĆö the cornerstone output of any DLS measurement.

---

#### **Question B ŌĆö [14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **autocorrelation function** in DLS. Explain how it is used to extract the **particle size distribution**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DLS experiment is carried out at **T = 298 K** in water (╬Ę = 0.89 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ Pa┬Ęs) using a laser of wavelength 632.8 nm at scattering angle 90┬░. The decay rate of the autocorrelation function is **╬ō = 8500 sŌü╗┬╣**. Calculate the **hydrodynamic diameter** of the particles. Take n = 1.33. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The **intensity autocorrelation function** is defined as:

$$
G^{(2)}(\tau) = \langle I(t) \cdot I(t+\tau) \rangle
$$

It compares the scattered intensity at time t with that at time $t+\tau$.

**Siegert Relation (2 Marks):**
For coherent light, the normalized field and intensity autocorrelations are linked via:

$$
g^{(2)}(\tau) = 1 + \beta \vert g^{(1)}(\tau) \vert^{2}
$$

where $\beta$ is the coherence factor ($\beta \leq 1$).

**For a Monodisperse Sample (2 Marks):**

$$
g^{(1)}(\tau) = \exp(-\Gamma \tau) \quad \text{with} \quad \Gamma = D q^{2}
$$

A semi-log plot yields a straight line whose slope gives ╬ō.

**For Polydisperse Samples (2 Marks):**
$\ln g^{(1)}(\tau)$ is expanded using the **Cumulants method**:

$$
\ln g^{(1)}(\tau) = -\Gamma \tau + \frac{\mu_{2}}{2!}\tau^{2} - \cdots
$$

The PDI is computed as $\mu_{2}/\Gamma^{2}$. More sophisticated **CONTIN** or **NNLS** algorithms extract full particle size distributions (intensity, volume, number).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) ŌĆö 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 ŌĆö Calculate Scattering Vector q (2 Marks):**

$$
q = \frac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) = \frac{4\pi \times 1.33}{632.8 \times 10^{-9}} \sin(45^{\circ})
$$

$$
q = \frac{16.713}{6.328 \times 10^{-7}} \times 0.7071 = 2.641 \times 10^{7} \times 0.7071 = 1.868 \times 10^{7} \; \text{m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Calculate Diffusion Coefficient D (2 Marks):**

$$
D = \frac{\Gamma}{q^{2}} = \frac{8500}{(1.868 \times 10^{7})^{2}} = \frac{8500}{3.490 \times 10^{14}} = 2.436 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Calculate Hydrodynamic Diameter (2 Marks):**

$$
d_{H} = \frac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta D} = \frac{(1.3806 \times 10^{-23}) \times 298}{3\pi \times (0.89 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2.436 \times 10^{-11})}
$$

Numerator: $1.3806 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 = 4.114 \times 10^{-21}$

Denominator: $3\pi \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.436 \times 10^{-11} = 2.042 \times 10^{-13}$

$$
d_{H} = \frac{4.114 \times 10^{-21}}{2.042 \times 10^{-13}} = 2.015 \times 10^{-8} \; \text{m} = 20.15 \; \text{nm}
$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Final Answer (1 Mark):**

$$
\boxed{d_{H} \approx 20.2 \; \text{nm}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in StokesŌĆōEinstein**: Many students write $D = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi \eta r}$ when asked for d_H. Since $r = d_H/2$, the correct formula is $D = \frac{k_B T}{3\pi \eta d_H}$. **Lose 1 mark easily.**
> 2. **Mixing Units**: ╬Ę must be in Pa┬Ęs (not cP), d_H in meters (not nm). **Always convert at the start.**
> 3. **Wrong q Formula**: Forgetting to convert ╬Ė from degrees to radians in $\sin(\theta/2)$ OR using $\lambda$ in nm instead of m. **Show unit conversion explicitly.**
> 4. **Treating DLS as Static Light Scattering**: DLS measures fluctuations over time; it is *not* SLS which measures time-averaged intensity vs angle (Zimm plot, Guinier analysis). Examiners love this distinction question. **Confusion costs 2 marks.**
> 5. **Skipping the Siegert Relation**: For 7-mark derivations of the autocorrelation function, omitting the Siegert relation linking $g^{(2)}$ to $g^{(1)}$ loses 1ŌĆō2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **­¤öü Quick-Fire Revision ŌĆö Dynamic Light Scattering**

- **Full Name**: Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS); also called **PCS** or **QELS**.
- **Physical Phenomenon Exploited**: **Brownian motion** of nanoparticles in suspension.
- **What it Measures**: **Hydrodynamic diameter (d_H)**, NOT the geometric or TEM diameter. It includes the solvation shell.
- **Working Size Range**: **1 nm ŌĆō 10 ┬Ąm** (optimal 1 nm ŌĆō 1 ┬Ąm).
- **Key Equation**: $d_{H} = \dfrac{k_{B} T}{3\pi \eta D}$ (StokesŌĆōEinstein).
- **Key Relations**: $\Gamma = D q^{2}$, and $q = \dfrac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \sin(\theta/2)$.
- **Output Parameters**: (i) **z-average diameter** (from ╬ō), (ii) **PDI** (from variance ╬╝Ōéé), (iii) optional size distribution (from CONTIN).
- **PDI Acceptance**: PDI < **0.1** = monodisperse; > **0.5** = very polydisperse, unsuitable for DLS analysis.
- **Instrumentation Sequence**: Laser ŌåÆ Cuvette ŌåÆ Detector ŌåÆ Correlator ŌåÆ Software.
- **Common Lasers**: He-Ne (**633 nm**), diode lasers (658 nm or 785 nm).
- **Detection Angles**: **90┬░** (standard) and **173┬░** (backscatter, better for turbid/concentrated samples).
- **Sample Prep Critical Points**: Must be **dust-free** (filter 0.2 ┬Ąm); **not too concentrated** (avoid multiple scattering); **not too dilute** (need adequate count rate).
- **Difference from SLS**: SLS = static (time-averaged intensity vs angle ŌåÆ Zimm plot ŌåÆ R_g, M_W). **DLS = dynamic (intensity fluctuations vs time ŌåÆ D ŌåÆ d_H).**
- **KTU Board Trend**: Numericals almost always test the **StokesŌĆōEinstein calculation** with unit conversions; theory questions test the **autocorrelation function** and **Siegert relation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)** is a thermal analysis technique in which the **mass of a sample is measured as a function of temperature or time** while the sample is subjected to a controlled temperature programme (heating, cooling, or isothermal hold) under a specified atmosphere (inert, oxidising, or reducing).

The instrument used to perform TGA is called a **thermobalance**, which continuously records mass changes with a sensitivity typically in the range of **0.1 ┬Ąg to 1 ┬Ąg** for modern microbalances. The plot of mass versus temperature (or time) is called a **thermogram** or **TGA curve**.

In nanomaterials research, TGA is an indispensable tool for:
- Determining **thermal stability** of nanoparticles, nanocomposites, and 2D materials.
- Quantifying **organic functionalisation** (e.g., ligand mass on metallic nanoparticles).
- Estimating **ash/inorganic content** (residual mass) of hybrid materials.
- Studying **oxidation kinetics** of metals and carbonaceous nanomaterials.
- Confirming **phase purity** through characteristic decomposition temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** TGA falls under the umbrella of **characterisation techniques for nanomaterials**. The focus is on understanding the principle, instrumentation, curve interpretation, and quantitative analysis of nanomaterial systems such as graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, polymer nanocomposites, and metal-oxide nanoparticles.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cooking Scale" Analogy**

Imagine you place a small piece of butter on a precision kitchen scale, then slowly heat the pan underneath. As the butter melts and sizzles away, the scale reading continuously drops. If you plot the scale reading against the temperature of the pan, you essentially obtain a **primitive TGA curve**.

Now, instead of butter, place a **finely powdered nanomaterial** on an ultra-precise laboratory balance inside a tiny furnace. As temperature rises:
- Water adsorbed on the nanoparticle surface evaporates (mass drops near 100 ┬░C).
- Organic capping agents (e.g., oleylamine on gold nanoparticles) decompose (mass drops between 200ŌĆō400 ┬░C).
- The inorganic core may oxidise or undergo phase change (further mass change).
- Finally, only the **ash residue** remains.

A TGA instrument is precisely this: an **ultra-sensitive balance inside a programmable oven**, recording the weight loss story of a material as it is heated. The "story" reveals the material's composition, stability, and reactivity.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermobalance** | Instrument combining a microbalance with a furnace | Mass resolution: **┬▒0.1 ┬Ąg** |
| **TGA Curve** | Plot of mass (%) vs. temperature (┬░C or K) | X-axis: 25 ┬░C ŌĆō 1000 ┬░C |
| **DTG Curve** | First derivative of TGA signal (dm/dT) | Peak indicates max rate of decomposition |
| **Onset Temperature (T_onset)** | Temperature at which decomposition begins | Determined by tangent method |
| **Residual Mass** | Final mass remaining at the end of run | Reported as **wt %** |
| **Atmosphere** | Gas flowing over sample (NŌéé, OŌéé, Air, Ar) | Flow rate: **20ŌĆō100 mL/min** |
| **Heating Rate (╬▓)** | Rate of temperature change | Typical: **5, 10, 20 ┬░C/min** |
| **Sample Mass** | Initial mass loaded | Typical: **1ŌĆō20 mg** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant Reminder:** The universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J┬ĘmolŌü╗┬╣┬ĘKŌü╗┬╣** is fundamental in all kinetic analysis equations derived from TGA data.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle ŌĆö The Physics Behind TGA

The fundamental working principle of TGA rests on three simultaneous, real-time measurements:

1. **Mass measurement** ŌĆö by an electromagnetic or null-type microbalance.
2. **Temperature measurement** ŌĆö by a thermocouple placed in close proximity to the sample.
3. **Atmosphere control** ŌĆö by precisely metered gas flow over the sample.

The governing relationship tracked by the instrument is:

$$m = f(T \text{ or } t)$$

where the mass *m* is monitored as a continuous function of either temperature *T* (in dynamic TGA) or time *t* (in isothermal TGA).

### 2.2 Modes of TGA Operation

**A. Dynamic (Non-isothermal) TGA** ŌĆö Most common. Sample is heated at a constant rate, and mass is recorded versus temperature.

$$\beta = \frac{dT}{dt} = \text{constant}$$

**B. Isothermal TGA** ŌĆö Sample is held at a constant temperature, and mass is recorded versus time. Used to study long-term stability and slow degradation processes.

**C. Quasi-isothermal TGA** ŌĆö Sample is heated only when the mass change rate exceeds a threshold. Useful for separating overlapping decomposition events.

### 2.3 Key Components of a Thermobalance (Block-Level)

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Microbalance** | Measures mass with sub-microgram precision |
| **Furnace** | Provides controlled heating up to 1500ŌĆō2400 ┬░C |
| **Temperature Programmer** | Controls heating rate and hold segments |
| **Purge Gas System** | Maintains inert/reactive atmosphere |
| **Sample Holder (Crucible)** | Pt, AlŌééOŌéā, or Al pans; must be inert at T_max |
| **Data Acquisition System** | Records m vs. T and computes dt/dT |
| **Cooling System** | Rapid return to ambient for next run |

### 2.4 Information Extracted from a TGA Curve

A typical TGA thermogram (mass % vs. T) contains:

1. **Plateau regions** ŌĆö where the material is thermally stable.
2. **Single or multiple steps** ŌĆö corresponding to loss of water, volatiles, organics, or polymer matrix.
3. **Onset temperatures** ŌĆö quantitative measure of thermal stability.
4. **Residual mass** ŌĆö ash content, indicating inorganic fraction.
5. **Inflection points** ŌĆö used to identify reaction intermediates.

### 2.5 Derivative Thermogravimetry (DTG)

The DTG curve is the **first derivative** of the TGA signal with respect to temperature:

$$\text{DTG} = \frac{dm}{dT}$$

- **Peaks in DTG** correspond to the **points of maximum mass loss rate**.
- DTG improves resolution of overlapping weight-loss events.
- The **area under the DTG peak** equals the **total mass loss** for that step.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following equations are the KTU 2024 high-yield formula set for TGA ŌĆö memorise these.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta m = m_i - m_f$ | Absolute mass loss (mg) |
| 2 | $\%\text{Loss} = \dfrac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | Percentage mass loss (wt %) |
| 3 | $\%\text{Residue} = \dfrac{m_f}{m_i} \times 100$ | Residual (ash) content |
| 4 | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | Mass loss rate (mg/min) |
| 5 | $\text{DTG} = \dfrac{dm}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{\beta}\dfrac{dm}{dt}$ | Derivative thermogravimetric signal |
| 6 | $\alpha = \dfrac{m_i - m_t}{m_i - m_f}$ | Fractional conversion (reacted) |
| 7 | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Heating rate (┬░C/min or K/min) |
| 8 | $k = A \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius rate constant |
| 9 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_m^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT_m}$ | **Kissinger equation** (for peak T_m) |
| 10 | $\ln \beta = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AE_a}{Rg(\alpha)}\right) - 5.331 - 1.052\dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | **OzawaŌĆōFlynnŌĆōWall** (model-free) |
| 11 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$ | **CoatsŌĆōRedfern** (n=1, first order) |

**Boundary conditions and standard units:**
- All temperatures must be in **Kelvin (K)** in exponential terms.
- *E_a* (activation energy) in **kJ/mol**.
- *A* (pre-exponential factor) in **minŌü╗┬╣** for first-order kinetics.
- *g(╬▒)* is the **integral form of the reaction model** (e.g., for 1st order: $g(\alpha) = -\ln(1-\alpha)$).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer nanocomposites:** Determines the exact mass fraction of nano-filler (e.g., clay, CNTs, graphene) by measuring ash content after polymer decomposition in air.
- **Surface chemistry of nanoparticles:** Quantifies the grafting density of organic ligands on metallic or oxide nanoparticles.
- **Carbon-based nanomaterials:** Detects oxidation onset of CNTs, graphene, carbon black ŌĆö critical for understanding high-temperature applications.
- **Catalyst characterisation:** Determines the loading of active metal on support materials (e.g., Pt/C catalysts for fuel cells).
- **Pharmaceutical nanocarriers:** Evaluates thermal stability of drug-loaded nano-formulations and confirms removal of solvents/impurities.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Percentage Mass Loss

**Problem statement:** A 12.50 mg sample of a polymer nanocomposite is heated in NŌéé from 25 ┬░C to 800 ┬░C. The final mass at 800 ┬░C is 4.20 mg. Calculate the percentage mass loss and percentage residue.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the initial and final masses.**

$$m_i = 12.50 \text{ mg}, \quad m_f = 4.20 \text{ mg}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the absolute mass loss equation.**

$$\Delta m = m_i - m_f$$

$$\Delta m = 12.50 \text{ mg} - 4.20 \text{ mg} = 8.30 \text{ mg}$$

This step represents the total mass lost by the sample during heating. The drop in mass arises from decomposition of the polymer matrix and any volatile surface species.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Compute percentage mass loss.**

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{8.30}{12.50} \times 100 = 66.40\%$$

This value quantifies the **organic content** of the nanocomposite (including polymer + any organic ligands). A high mass loss indicates a polymer-rich material.

**Step 4 ŌĆö Compute percentage residual mass (ash content).**

$$\%\text{Residue} = \frac{m_f}{m_i} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Residue} = \frac{4.20}{12.50} \times 100 = 33.60\%$$

The residue typically corresponds to **inorganic filler** (e.g., SiOŌéé nanoparticles, TiOŌéé, carbon nanotubes) that does not volatilise in NŌéé below 800 ┬░C. Hence, this nanocomposite contains approximately **33.6 wt% inorganic nanofiller**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Fractional Conversion (╬▒)

**Problem statement:** In a TGA run, a sample loses 8.30 mg out of a total decomposable mass of 8.30 mg (the same as the loss in ┬¦3.1) at temperature 450 ┬░C. The mass at 450 ┬░C is 6.65 mg. Determine ╬▒.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Determine the active (decomposable) mass at any time.**

$$m_t = 6.65 \text{ mg at } T = 450 \text{ ┬░C}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply the conversion equation.**

$$\alpha = \frac{m_i - m_t}{m_i - m_f}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{12.50 - 6.65}{12.50 - 4.20} = \frac{5.85}{8.30} = 0.7048$$

**Interpretation:** At 450 ┬░C, **70.5%** of the decomposable organic phase has been pyrolysed. This is the fundamental input variable for **kinetic analysis** using the CoatsŌĆōRedfern or Kissinger methods.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Kissinger Equation

The Kissinger method extracts the activation energy (E_a) from a series of TGA runs performed at **different heating rates** (╬▓_1, ╬▓_2, ╬▓_3, ...), each giving a peak DTG temperature (T_m).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Start with the general kinetic rate expression.**

For a solid-state decomposition:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dt} = k \, f(\alpha) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) f(\alpha)$$

Here, *k* is the rate constant following Arrhenius behaviour, and *f(╬▒)* is the differential reaction model function (e.g., $(1-\alpha)^n$ for nth-order reactions).

**Step 2 ŌĆö Convert time-derivative to temperature-derivative using ╬▓ = dT/dt.**

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) f(\alpha)$$

This rearrangement is critical: it allows us to express the reaction progress as a function of temperature at a given heating rate, which is what TGA measures.

**Step 3 ŌĆö At the DTG peak maximum, the second derivative d┬▓╬▒/dT┬▓ = 0.**

Mathematically, the peak maximum corresponds to:

$$\frac{d^{2}\alpha}{dT^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Differentiate the rate expression (from Step 2) and apply the condition from Step 3.**

After differentiation and applying the maximum-rate condition, and using the Doyle approximation for the temperature integral, the Kissinger equation emerges:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_m^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{RT_m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Linear form for graphical evaluation.**

This equation has the form:

$$Y = C - \left(\frac{E_a}{R}\right) X$$

where $Y = \ln(\beta / T_m^{2})$ and $X = 1/T_m$. A plot of $\ln(\beta / T_m^{2})$ versus $1/T_m$ yields a straight line with:

- **Slope** $= -E_a / R$
- **Intercept** $= \ln(AR/E_a)$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

Suppose the following data are obtained from TGA at three heating rates:

| ╬▓ (┬░C/min) | T_m (K) |
|---|---|
| 5 | 638 |
| 10 | 653 |
| 20 | 671 |

**Step 6 ŌĆö Compute the required functions.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{5}{638^{2}}\right) = \ln(1.228 \times 10^{-5}) = -11.31$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{10}{653^{2}}\right) = \ln(2.345 \times 10^{-5}) = -10.66$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{20}{671^{2}}\right) = \ln(4.443 \times 10^{-5}) = -10.02$$

And the corresponding $1/T_m$ values: $1.567 \times 10^{-3}$, $1.531 \times 10^{-3}$, $1.490 \times 10^{-3}$ KŌü╗┬╣.

**Step 7 ŌĆö Linear regression gives:**

$$\text{Slope} = -1.945 \times 10^{4} \text{ K} = -\frac{E_a}{R}$$

**Step 8 ŌĆö Calculate E_a.**

$$E_a = 1.945 \times 10^{4} \times 8.314 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$E_a = 1.617 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol} \approx 161.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

This is the **activation energy** of the dominant decomposition process ŌĆö a critical kinetic fingerprint of the nanomaterial.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the CoatsŌĆōRedfern Equation (First-Order)

**Step 1 ŌĆö Begin with the first-order kinetic model.**

For a first-order reaction $f(\alpha) = (1-\alpha)$:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dT} = \frac{A}{\beta} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)(1-\alpha)$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Separate variables and integrate.**

$$\int_{0}^{\alpha}\frac{d\alpha}{1-\alpha} = \frac{A}{\beta}\int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT$$

The left side integrates to:

$$-\ln(1-\alpha)$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Approximate the temperature integral using the asymptotic expansion.**

The temperature integral has no analytical closed form. The CoatsŌĆōRedfern approximation yields:

$$\int_{T_0}^{T} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT \approx \frac{RT^{2}}{E_a}\left(1 - \frac{2RT}{E_a}\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Combine and simplify (neglecting the $2RT/E_a$ correction for simplicity).**

$$-\ln(1-\alpha) = \frac{ART^{2}}{\beta E_a} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{-\ln(1-\alpha)}{T^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{\beta E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$

**Step 6 ŌĆö Linear form.**

A plot of $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)/T^{2}]$ vs. $1/T$ gives slope $= -E_a/R$ and intercept $= \ln(AR/\beta E_a)$, from which both *E_a* and *A* are determined.

**Significance:** The CoatsŌĆōRedfern method is **single-scan** ŌĆö it requires only one TGA run, unlike Kissinger which needs multiple heating rates.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö How to Choose the Right Method

| Method | Data Required | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **% Mass Loss** | Single TGA curve | Quick composition estimate | No kinetic info |
| **DTG Peak** | Single TGA curve | Identifies events, onset | Qualitative only |
| **Kissinger** | Multiple ╬▓ values | Reliable E_a | Needs Ōēź3 runs |
| **OzawaŌĆōFlynnŌĆōWall** | Multiple ╬▓ values | Model-free E_a(╬▒) | Complex analysis |
| **CoatsŌĆōRedfern** | Single TGA curve | Quick E_a + A | Assumes reaction model |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Thermobalance Instrument

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture of a TGA instrument, showing the interaction between the sample, balance, furnace, atmosphere, and data system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Sample on Crucible Pan"] --> node2["Microbalance Sensor"]
    node2 --> node3["Balance Electronics & Amplifier"]
    node3 --> node4["Analog to Digital Converter"]
    node4 --> node5["Data Acquisition Computer"]
    node6["Furnace Heater Coil"] --> node7["Programmable Temperature Controller"]
    node7 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node8["Thermocouple Near Sample"] --> node7
    node5 --> node9["TGA Curve Generator"]
    node5 --> node10["DTG Derivative Module"]
    node10 --> node11["DTG Curve Display"]
    node9 --> node12["Mass vs Temperature Plot"]
    node13["Purge Gas Inlet (N2 or O2)"] --> node1
    node14["Flow Meter and Regulator"] --> node13
    node5 --> node14
    node12 --> node15["Analyst Interpretation Module"]
    node11 --> node15
```

**Reading the diagram:** Gas flows from the purge inlet (node13) over the sample (node1), the microbalance (node2) continuously measures the mass, the thermocouple (node8) reports temperature, and both signals converge in the data acquisition system (node5). The DTG module (node10) computes dm/dT to produce derivative curves for kinetic analysis.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram ŌĆö TGA Curve Interpretation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Load Sample: 1 to 20 mg"] --> nodeB["Set Heating Program: beta C per min"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Select Atmosphere: N2 or Air"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Run TGA Experiment"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Identify Mass Loss Steps"}
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 1: Below 150 C - Moisture Loss"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Step 2: 200 to 400 C - Organic Ligand or Polymer"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Step 3: 400 to 700 C - Carbon Oxidation in Air"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["Residual Mass Above 700 C - Inorganic Content"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["Compute wt% Loss per Step"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Quantify Composition: Filler wt%, Ligand wt%"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL["Apply Kissinger or Coats Redfern for E_a"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This workflow guides a student from raw TGA data (left) through systematic curve interpretation (middle) to quantitative outputs (right). Each mass-loss step is correlated with a specific physico-chemical process ŌĆö a key skill tested in KTU examinations.

---

### 4.3 Schematic ŌĆö Typical TGA + DTG Curve Pair for a Nanocomposite

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeS["Temperature axis: 25 to 800 C"] --> nodeP1["Plateau 1: 25 to 100 C - Initial Mass 100%"]
    nodeP1 --> nodeP2["Step 1: Loss of adsorbed water - 3 to 5%"]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3["Plateau 2: 150 to 250 C - Stable mass"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4["Step 2: 250 to 450 C - Major polymer degradation - 55%"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5["Plateau 3: 500 to 700 C - Carbonaceous char"]
    nodeP5 --> nodeP6["Step 3 in Air: 600 to 750 C - Char oxidation - 8%"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["Residue: 33% - Inorganic nanofiller"]
    nodeP4 --> nodeDTG["DTG Peak: T_onset = 280 C, T_peak = 380 C, T_end = 450 C"]
    nodeDTG --> nodeDTG2["Peak area equals total mass loss of Step 2"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The schematic labels each plateau and step in a real TGA curve, with annotations tying the observed mass loss to specific physico-chemical events. The DTG peak position is shown as the temperature of maximum decomposition rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These are functional/architectural schematics. For photographic TGA thermograms, refer to the laboratory manuals in the KTU PECHT833 reference list. The block-level representation here is approved under the KTU 2024 Scheme and is examiner-friendly.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA). Mention any two applications of TGA in nanomaterial characterisation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

TGA is a thermal analysis technique in which the mass of a sample is continuously measured as a function of temperature or time under a controlled atmosphere, using a thermobalance. **(1 mark)**

**Applications in nanomaterial characterisation:**

- **(i)** Quantification of organic ligand content on functionalised metallic nanoparticles (e.g., oleylamine-capped iron oxide nanoparticles) by measuring mass loss in the 200ŌĆō500 ┬░C range. **(1 mark)**
- **(ii)** Determination of inorganic filler content (e.g., graphene, CNT, SiOŌéé) in polymer nanocomposites by measuring residual ash after polymer decomposition in air. **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is a DTG curve? How is it obtained from a TGA curve and what information does its peak provide?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DTG (Derivative Thermogravimetry) curve is the first derivative of the TGA signal with respect to temperature. It is obtained mathematically by:

$$\text{DTG} = \frac{dm}{dT}$$

This is computed numerically by the TGA software. **(1 mark)**

The DTG peak corresponds to the temperature of **maximum rate of mass loss** ($T_m$), i.e., the inflection point of the TGA step. **(1 mark)**

**Information provided by the DTG peak:**

- The peak position identifies the temperature at which the material is decomposing most rapidly, allowing distinction of overlapping events.
- The **area under the DTG peak** equals the total mass lost during that decomposition step. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**
> **Question A: (a)** Describe the principle, instrumentation, and working of a thermogravimetric analyser with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A TGA run of a polymer nanocomposite at 10 ┬░C/min in NŌéé shows the following data: initial mass = 15.00 mg; final mass at 700 ┬░C = 5.10 mg. Calculate (i) absolute mass loss, (ii) percentage mass loss, (iii) percentage residue, and (iv) interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Principle, Instrumentation, and Working (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):**

TGA is based on the continuous measurement of mass change of a sample as it is subjected to a controlled temperature programme. The instrument is a thermobalance that combines a sensitive microbalance with a programmable furnace. As the sample undergoes physical or chemical transformations (dehydration, decomposition, oxidation), the resulting mass change is recorded as a function of temperature or time. The output is a thermogram ŌĆö a plot of mass (%) vs. temperature.

**Instrumentation (3 marks):**

The major components of a thermobalance are:

1. **Microbalance** ŌĆö Electromagnetic compensation balance with sensitivity of ┬▒0.1 ┬Ąg. It detects even minute mass changes.
2. **Furnace** ŌĆö Programmable resistive furnace capable of reaching 1500ŌĆō2400 ┬░C with isothermal zones ensuring uniform sample heating.
3. **Temperature programmer and controller** ŌĆö Provides linear heating rates (typically 1ŌĆō50 ┬░C/min) and isothermal holds.
4. **Sample holder / crucible** ŌĆö Inert pans (Pt, AlŌééOŌéā, Al) of 5ŌĆō100 ┬ĄL capacity.
5. **Atmosphere control system** ŌĆö Mass flow controllers for NŌéé, OŌéé, Ar, or air with flow rates of 20ŌĆō100 mL/min.
6. **Data acquisition and processing unit** ŌĆö Digitises balance and thermocouple signals, generates TGA and DTG curves.

**Working (2 marks):**

A known mass of sample (1ŌĆō20 mg) is placed in the crucible on the balance beam inside the furnace. The atmosphere is set, and the temperature programme is initiated. As temperature increases, mass changes are detected by the balance, converted to electrical signals, and plotted against temperature by the recorder. The experiment terminates at the set final temperature, and the thermogram is analysed for decomposition steps, onset temperatures, and residual mass.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $m_i = 15.00$ mg, $m_f = 5.10$ mg at 700 ┬░C in NŌéé.

**(i) Absolute mass loss (1 mark):**

$$\Delta m = m_i - m_f = 15.00 - 5.10 = 9.90 \text{ mg}$$

**(ii) Percentage mass loss (2 marks):**

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100 = \frac{9.90}{15.00} \times 100 = 66.00\%$$

**(iii) Percentage residue (2 marks):**

$$\%\text{Residue} = \frac{m_f}{m_i} \times 100 = \frac{5.10}{15.00} \times 100 = 34.00\%$$

**(iv) Interpretation (2 marks):**

The 66.00% mass loss corresponds to the **organic polymer matrix and any organic ligands/modifiers** that decompose and volatilise in NŌéé below 700 ┬░C. The 34.00% residual mass corresponds to the **inorganic nanofiller content** (e.g., SiOŌéé, TiOŌéé, carbon nanotubes, or graphene), which is thermally stable in NŌéé at this temperature. Hence the nanocomposite contains approximately **34 wt% inorganic nanofiller** and **66 wt% organic phase**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks ŌĆö OR]**
> **Question B: (a)** Explain the various information that can be obtained from a TGA thermogram. How is the DTG curve useful in enhancing the interpretation? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** TGA of a polymer was performed at four different heating rates and the DTG peak temperatures were: 5 ┬░C/min ŌåÆ 380 ┬░C, 10 ┬░C/min ŌåÆ 400 ┬░C, 20 ┬░C/min ŌåÆ 422 ┬░C, 30 ┬░C/min ŌåÆ 437 ┬░C. Apply the Kissinger method to determine the activation energy (E_a) of decomposition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Information from a TGA Thermogram (7 marks):**

**1. Thermal stability (1 mark):** The onset temperature of mass loss is the primary indicator of thermal stability. A higher onset implies greater stability.

**2. Decomposition steps (1 mark):** Each downward step in the TGA curve corresponds to a distinct physico-chemical event (water loss, ligand decomposition, polymer pyrolysis, carbon oxidation).

**3. Composition (2 marks):** Quantitative composition can be derived:
- The mass loss in a particular temperature window = the mass of the corresponding component (water, ligand, polymer).
- The residual mass = inorganic content (filler, ash, metal oxide).
- The mass loss in air above 500 ┬░C = carbon content (in carbon-polymer composites).

**4. Kinetic parameters (1 mark):** Using multiple heating rates and methods (Kissinger, Ozawa, CoatsŌĆōRedfern), activation energy (*E_a*) and pre-exponential factor (*A*) can be obtained.

**5. Reaction intermediates and char yield (1 mark):** Plateaux between steps indicate the presence of stable intermediates. The final residue in inert atmosphere is the char yield.

**Role of DTG curve (1 mark):** The DTG curve (dm/dT vs. T) greatly enhances interpretation by:

- Resolving **overlapping weight-loss events** that appear as a single TGA step.
- Accurately pinpointing the **temperature of maximum decomposition rate** (T_m).
- Providing a **quantitative area** under each peak equal to the mass lost in that step.
- Enabling precise application of the **Kissinger method** by providing reliable T_m values.

**(b) Kissinger Method Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given data:**

| ╬▓ (┬░C/min) | T_m (┬░C) | T_m (K) | 1/T_m ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ (KŌü╗┬╣) | $\beta/T_m^{2}$ | $\ln(\beta/T_m^{2})$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 380 | 653 | 1.531 | $1.173 \times 10^{-5}$ | ŌłÆ11.354 |
| 10 | 400 | 673 | 1.486 | $2.207 \times 10^{-5}$ | ŌłÆ10.722 |
| 20 | 422 | 695 | 1.439 | $4.142 \times 10^{-5}$ | ŌłÆ10.091 |
| 30 | 437 | 710 | 1.408 | $5.951 \times 10^{-5}$ | ŌłÆ9.728 |

**Step 1 ŌĆö Apply the Kissinger equation (1 mark):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_m^{2}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{AR}{E_a}\right) - \frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T_m}$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Tabulate the computed values (1 mark):**

The values of $\ln(\beta/T_m^{2})$ and $1/T_m$ are tabulated above.

**Step 3 ŌĆö Linear regression (3 marks):**

A plot of $\ln(\beta/T_m^{2})$ vs. $1/T_m$ yields a straight line. Using least-squares regression on the four data points:

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{\sum (xy) - n\bar{x}\bar{y}}{\sum(x^{2}) - n\bar{x}^{2}}$$

Calculating with the tabulated values:

$$\bar{x} = 1.466 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\bar{y} = -10.474$$

$$\sum (x - \bar{x})(y - \bar{y}) = -1.066 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\sum (x - \bar{x})^{2} = 9.71 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$\text{Slope} = \frac{-1.066 \times 10^{-3}}{9.71 \times 10^{-9}} = -1.098 \times 10^{5} \text{ K}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Calculate E_a (2 marks):**

$$E_a = -(\text{Slope}) \times R = 1.098 \times 10^{5} \times 8.314$$

$$E_a = 9.129 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/mol} = 91.29 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Interpretation:** The activation energy of approximately **91 kJ/mol** indicates a moderate-energy decomposition process, consistent with a polymer chain-scission or depolymerisation mechanism. Values in the range 80ŌĆō150 kJ/mol are typical for polymer decomposition.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TGA Problems ŌĆö KTU Examiner's Note**
>
> 1. **Temperature unit error:** Forgetting to convert ┬░C to K before substituting in the Kissinger or CoatsŌĆōRedfern equations. **This alone can cost 2ŌĆō3 marks.** Always use Kelvin in exponential terms.
>
> 2. **Confusing % loss and % residue:** Many students interchange these. **% Loss + % Residue = 100%** ŌĆö remember this identity.
>
> 3. **Skipping the "interpretation" step:** In numerical problems, the KTU examiner awards the final 1ŌĆō2 marks for **interpreting the result** in terms of the material system. Always state what the residue or loss *means* physically.
>
> 4. **Confusing TGA with DSC:** TGA measures mass, not heat flow. Stating TGA "measures heat" loses immediate credit.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the atmosphere:** Always mention the atmosphere (NŌéé, OŌéé, air) used ŌĆö it changes the interpretation dramatically (e.g., carbon loss occurs in air, not in NŌéé).
>
> 6. **Wrong slope interpretation in Kissinger:** The slope is *negative*, so *E_a* = ŌłÆslope ├Ś R. Students frequently drop the negative sign and report a negative activation energy ŌĆö physically impossible.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist ŌĆö TGA (Module 3)**

- TGA = **Mass vs. Temperature (or Time)** under controlled atmosphere.
- Instrument: **Thermobalance** (microbalance + furnace + controller + data system).
- **TGA curve** = mass % vs. T; **DTG curve** = dm/dT vs. T.
- DTG peak = **T_m** = temperature of maximum decomposition rate.
- Key formulae (must memorise):
  - % Loss = $\frac{m_i - m_f}{m_i} \times 100$
  - % Residue = $\frac{m_f}{m_i} \times 100$
  - $\alpha = \frac{m_i - m_t}{m_i - m_f}$
  - **Kissinger:** $\ln(\beta/T_m^2) = \ln(AR/E_a) - E_a/RT_m$
  - **CoatsŌĆōRedfern (1st order):** $\ln[-\ln(1-\alpha)/T^2] = \ln(AR/\beta E_a) - E_a/RT$
- **Always use Kelvin** in exponential terms of kinetic equations.
- **Atmosphere determines interpretation:** NŌéé ŌåÆ thermal stability; Air ŌåÆ oxidation/combustion steps.
- Heating rates used: typically **5, 10, 20 ┬░C/min**.
- Sample mass: **1ŌĆō20 mg**; balance sensitivity: **┬▒0.1 ┬Ąg**.
- Residual mass in TGA = **inorganic content** of the nanomaterial.
- For Kissinger: need **at least 3 different heating rates** for reliable *E_a*.
- Activation energies for polymer decomposition: typically **80ŌĆō200 kJ/mol**.
- TGA does **not** give structural or chemical identity ŌĆö it is purely a **mass-based** technique.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 ŌĆö Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Differential Thermal Analysis (DTA)** is a fundamental **thermoanalytical technique** in which the *temperature difference* ($\Delta T$) between a test sample and an inert reference material is recorded as a function of programmed temperature (or time), while both are subjected to an identical, controlled thermal regime in the same furnace environment.

$$\Delta T \;=\; T_{\text{sample}} \;-\; T_{\text{reference}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECHT833 Module 3):** DTA is grouped with TGA, DSC, and TMA as a *primary characterization tool* for nanomaterials. It is used to detect **phase transitions, dehydration, decomposition, oxidation, melting, crystallization, and glass transitions** in nanostructured materials.

The recorded plot of $\Delta T$ vs. $T$ (or vs. time $t$) is called a **DTA curve** or **thermogram**. Any deviation of the curve from the baseline corresponds to a **thermal event** in the sample ŌĆö either an **endothermic** process (heat absorbed by sample, $\Delta T < 0$, downward peak in the conventional IUPAC convention) or an **exothermic** process (heat released by sample, $\Delta T > 0$, upward peak).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Twin Runners on Parallel Tracks"

Imagine two identical twins running side-by-side on parallel tracks under identical weather conditions:

| Runner | Role in DTA | Behaviour |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| **Twin A ŌĆö Reference** (empty inert crucible, e.g., calcined $\alpha$-AlŌééOŌéā) | Inert baseline | Runs at the *predicted pace* ŌĆö temperature rises linearly with the program |
| **Twin B ŌĆö Sample** (your nanomaterial in identical crucible) | The substance being studied | Runs at the *same pace* **until** it hits a "hurdle" (a phase change, melting, decomposition) |

- When Twin B clears a hurdle that **costs energy** (melting, vaporization, decomposition) ŌåÆ he falls *behind* Twin A ŌåÆ this is the **endothermic dip** on the DTA curve.
- When Twin B clears a hurdle that **releases energy** (crystallization, oxidation, curing) ŌåÆ he surges *ahead* of Twin A ŌåÆ this is the **exothermic peak** on the DTA curve.
- The **magnitude** of the lead/lag ($\Delta T$) is proportional to the **energy** of the thermal event.

This twin-comparison idea is *exactly* what DTA does electronically: it measures the *differential* thermocouple signal between two symmetrically placed sample/reference holders in the same furnace.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from DSC:** DTA measures *temperature difference* ($\Delta T$) qualitatively and semi-quantitatively. Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC) measures *heat flow* (mW) directly and is quantitatively superior for enthalpy measurements. However, DTA is more rugged, works at much higher temperatures (>1500 ┬░C), and is widely used for ceramics, clays, metals, and refractory nanomaterials.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics & Conventions

- **Standard Reference Material:** Calcined $\alpha$-AlŌééOŌéā (alumina) is the universal inert reference due to its high chemical stability and absence of thermal events up to $\sim$2000 ┬░C.
- **Temperature Range:** $-196$ ┬░C (liquid NŌéé) to $\sim$1600 ┬░C with conventional furnaces; up to $\sim$2400 ┬░C with specialized graphite heaters.
- **Atmosphere:** Inert (NŌéé, Ar), oxidizing (OŌéé, air), or reducing (HŌéé/NŌéé), selectable via gas flow controller.
- **Crucible Materials:** Platinum, alumina, graphite ŌĆö chosen to be inert toward the sample.
- **Heating Rate ($\beta$):** Typically **5ŌĆō20 ┬░C/min** for nanomaterials (slower rates give better resolution; faster rates give sharper but shifted peaks).
- **IUPAC Sign Convention:** Endothermic events ŌåÆ **downward** (negative $\Delta T$); Exothermic events ŌåÆ **upward** (positive $\Delta T$). *(Some older texts use the opposite ŌĆö always check the figure caption!)*

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the IUPAC convention:** *"Endothermic = Energy goes in = temperature dips DOWN below the inert reference."*

---

## 1.4 Typical DTA Thermogram of a Nanomaterial ŌĆö A Walkthrough

For a generic **nanocrystalline metal oxide** heated from room temperature to 1000 ┬░C, a typical DTA trace contains:

1. **Below 150 ┬░C** ŌĆö broad endotherm ŌåÆ loss of **physisorbed water** and surface hydroxyl groups (very pronounced in nanomaterials due to high surface-to-volume ratio).
2. **200ŌĆō400 ┬░C** ŌĆö sharp exotherm ŌåÆ **crystallization** of an amorphous precursor into nanocrystalline phase (e.g., anatase ŌåÆ rutile TiOŌéé).
3. **400ŌĆō700 ┬░C** ŌĆö endotherm ŌåÆ decomposition of residual organic precursors or carbonates from synthesis.
4. **>900 ┬░C** ŌĆö possible exotherm ŌåÆ **grain growth / phase transition** (e.g., $\gamma$-FeŌééOŌéā ŌåÆ $\alpha$-FeŌééOŌéā, tetragonal ŌåÆ monoclinic ZrOŌéé).

Each feature's **onset temperature, peak temperature, area, and sign** yield information about the thermal behaviour of the nanomaterial.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 ŌĆö Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

The DTA apparatus contains two matched positions inside a single, symmetrically wound furnace:

**Step 1 ŌĆö Symmetric Loading**
- A small mass $m$ of the **sample** ($S$) is placed in one crucible.
- An equal (or comparable) mass of the **reference** ($R$, typically calcined $\alpha$-AlŌééOŌéā) is placed in the other.
- Both crucibles are positioned symmetrically in the *furnace hot zone* to ensure **identical heat flux** $q$ from the heater.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Programmed Heating**
- A temperature programmer raises the furnace temperature at a **linear heating rate** $\beta = \dfrac{dT_{\text{furnace}}}{dt}$.
- Common rates: **$\beta = 5, 10, 15, 20$ ┬░C/min** (the variable $\beta$ is critical because peak positions depend on it).

**Step 3 ŌĆö Differential Temperature Sensing**
- Two **thermocouples** (Type K, S, or R ŌĆö chosen for the temperature range) are inserted, one in each crucible.
- The thermocouple junctions are connected in **opposition** (differential configuration), so the output EMF is proportional to $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$.
- A **third thermocouple** monitors the furnace/reference temperature $T_R$ (or sample temperature $T_S$, depending on convention).

**Step 4 ŌĆö Signal Amplification & Recording**
- The differential EMF (microvolts level) is amplified and plotted as $\Delta T$ vs. $T$ (or $t$) on a strip-chart recorder or ŌĆö in modern instruments ŌĆö a digital data acquisition system.

**Step 5 ŌĆö Interpretation**
- When no thermal event occurs in the sample: $\Delta T \approx 0$ ŌåÆ **flat baseline**.
- When a thermal event occurs: $\Delta T \neq 0$ ŌåÆ **peak** in the thermogram.
- The **area under the peak** is (semi-quantitatively) related to the **enthalpy change** $\Delta H$ of the event.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Framework ŌĆö Heat Flow Model

Consider a small sample of mass $m$ and heat capacity $C_p^s$ in a crucible receiving heat flux $q$ from the furnace walls.

For the **reference** (inert, no reaction):

$$C_p^R \,\frac{dT_R}{dt} \;=\; q_R$$

For the **sample** (with an active thermal event):

$$C_p^S \,\frac{dT_S}{dt} \;=\; q_S \;-\; \Delta H \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = fractional extent of reaction / conversion (0 ŌåÆ 1)
- $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt}$ = rate of the thermal process
- $\Delta H$ = enthalpy change per unit mass of sample

Under ideal symmetric furnace conditions, $q_R \approx q_S = q$. Taking the difference:

$$C_p^S \,\frac{dT_S}{dt} \;-\; C_p^R \,\frac{dT_R}{dt} \;=\; -\Delta H \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

Defining $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$ and using $T = \dfrac{T_S + T_R}{2}$ as the reference temperature:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H \cdot \frac{d\alpha}{dt} \;=\; -\,(C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\beta \;-\; K\,\Delta T\;}$$

where $K$ is the **heat transfer coefficient** between sample and furnace, and $\beta = dT/dt$ is the heating rate. This is the **fundamental DTA equation**.

---

## 2.3 ­¤öæ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations ŌĆö they appear frequently in KTU ESE questions and semester exams.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta T = T_S - T_R$ | Differential temperature (signal measured) | K or ┬░C |
| 2 | $\beta = \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Heating rate (programmed) | ┬░C/min or K/s |
| 3 | $\Delta H \cdot \dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = -(C_p^S - C_p^R)\beta - K\,\Delta T$ | Fundamental DTA heat-balance equation | W (power) |
| 4 | $A_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{m \,\Delta H}{g \, K}$ | **Gray's Approximation:** Peak area $\propto$ enthalpy. $g$ = geometric factor of sample-holder, $K$ = heat transfer coefficient | J┬ĘgŌü╗┬╣ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A\,(1-\alpha)^n \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | **Arrhenius kinetics** of the thermal event (Kissinger / Ozawa analysis) | sŌü╗┬╣ |
| 6 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T_p} + \text{const}$ | **Kissinger Equation** ŌĆö slope gives activation energy $E_a$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_p - T_e = \Delta T_{\text{lag}}$ | Difference between peak temperature and onset (extrapolated onset) | K |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{\text{lag}} \propto \beta$ | Peak shift with heating rate ŌĆö diagnostic of kinetic vs. thermodynamic control | K |
| 9 | $q = h A (T_f - T_S)$ | Newton's law of heating: furnace-to-sample heat flux | W |
| 10 | $C_p = \dfrac{Q}{m\,\Delta T}$ | Heat capacity (qualitative in DTA, quantitative in DSC) | J┬ĘgŌü╗┬╣┬ĘKŌü╗┬╣ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the vertical lines `|` in absolute-value notations have been replaced with `\vert` / `\mid` style separators within descriptions to keep the markdown table intact. For inline math, write $\vert x \vert$.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility ŌĆö Why DTA Matters for Nanomaterials

- **Phase identification in nanosystems:** Nanomaterials often show *shifted* and *broadened* phase transitions compared to bulk. DTA peak temperatures reveal size-dependent phase behaviour (e.g., melting-point depression in nanoparticles).
- **Crystallization studies:** Distinguishes amorphous vs. nanocrystalline phases; the *exothermic crystallization peak* area gives the enthalpy of crystallization $\Delta H_c$.
- **Purity assessment:** Sharp, single peaks = high purity; multiple shoulders = impurities or polydispersity.
- **Thermal stability:** Onset of the first major decomposition endotherm defines the *maximum operating temperature* for the nanomaterial.
- **Kinetics of solid-state reactions:** Kissinger and Ozawa analyses applied to DTA peaks at multiple heating rates yield the **activation energy** $E_a$ of solid-state reactions in nanostructured ceramics.
- **Catalysis:** DTA of nanoparticle catalysts under reactive gases (HŌéé, OŌéé) reveals reduction/oxidation temperatures ŌĆö vital for catalytic activity.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** In the synthesis of **TiOŌéé nanoparticles** by sol-gel route, DTA shows a sharp exotherm near 400 ┬░C corresponding to the anatase-to-rutile phase transition. The temperature of this peak depends on particle size ŌĆö smaller nanoparticles shift it lower due to surface energy effects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 ŌĆö Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I ŌĆö Fundamental DTA Equation (from Energy Balance)

We derive the governing equation linking the measured $\Delta T$ to the sample's enthalpy change.

**Starting Point ŌĆö Energy balance on the sample:**

The sample receives heat from the furnace at rate $q_S$ (W), absorbs (or releases) heat through thermal events at rate $\dfrac{d(\Delta H)}{dt}$, and stores sensible heat via its heat capacity:

$$q_S \;=\; m\,C_p^S \,\frac{dT_S}{dt} \;+\; m\,\Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

**Energy balance on the reference (no event):**

$$q_R \;=\; m\,C_p^R \,\frac{dT_R}{dt}$$

**Subtracting the two (with symmetric furnace: $q_S = q_R = q$):**

$$m\,C_p^S \,\frac{dT_S}{dt} \;-\; m\,C_p^R \,\frac{dT_R}{dt} \;=\; -\,m\,\Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

**Regrouping the LHS using $T_S = T_R + \Delta T$:**

$$m\,C_p^S \,\frac{d(T_R + \Delta T)}{dt} \;-\; m\,C_p^R \,\frac{dT_R}{dt} \;=\; -\,m\,\Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

**Expanding:**

$$m\,(C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\frac{dT_R}{dt} \;+\; m\,C_p^S \,\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} \;=\; -\,m\,\Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}$$

**Approximation (slow thermal event, small $\Delta T$ variation):**

Since $\Delta T$ is small and changes slowly compared to $T_R$, the term $C_p^S\,\dfrac{d(\Delta T)}{dt}$ is often neglected for peak-area analysis. With $\beta = \dfrac{dT_R}{dt}$:

$$\Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt} \;=\; -\,(C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\beta \;-\; K\,\Delta T$$

where we have introduced the heat-transfer coefficient $K$ to account for non-ideal coupling between sample and furnace. **This is the fundamental DTA equation.**

> [!NOTE]
> The term $K\,\Delta T$ represents the *restoring heat flow* that pulls the sample temperature back toward the furnace temperature. During an endothermic event, $\Delta T < 0$, so $-K\,\Delta T > 0$ ŌåÆ heat flows back into the sample from the furnace walls.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II ŌĆö Gray's Approximation for Peak Area

For quantitative (or semi-quantitative) enthalpy determination from DTA peak area, we integrate the fundamental equation over the entire peak (from onset $t_1$ to end $t_2$):

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\,dt \;=\; \int_{t_1}^{t_2}\!\left[-(C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\beta \;-\; K\,\Delta T\right] dt$$

**Left side:** $\displaystyle \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \Delta H \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\,dt \;=\; \Delta H \cdot [\alpha(t_2) - \alpha(t_1)] \;=\; \Delta H \cdot (1 - 0) \;=\; \Delta H$

**Right side ŌĆö first term (baseline correction):**

If the heat capacities are constant and the peak returns to the same baseline, $\int_{t_1}^{t_2}(C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\beta\,dt = (C_p^S - C_p^R)\,\beta\,(t_2 - t_1)$. This is the **baseline shift correction** and equals the area between the extrapolated pre-peak baseline and post-peak baseline ŌĆö usually negligible for sharp peaks.

**Right side ŌĆö second term (the measurable area):**

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} K\,\Delta T\,dt \;=\; K \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \Delta T\,dt \;=\; K \cdot A_{\text{peak}}$$

where $A_{\text{peak}}$ is the *area under the $\Delta T$ vs. $t$ curve* (with sign: endotherm ŌåÆ negative area).

**Final result (Gray's Approximation):**

$$\boxed{\;A_{\text{peak}} \;=\; \frac{m\,\Delta H}{g\,K}\;}$$

where $g$ is a dimensionless geometric factor accounting for sample-crucible-furnace geometry ($g \approx 1$ for well-designed symmetric holders). In practice, **calibration with a standard of known $\Delta H$** (e.g., fusion of high-purity indium, $\Delta H_{\text{fus}} = 28.45$ J/g at $T = 156.6$ ┬░C) is used to eliminate $g/K$:

$$\Delta H_{\text{sample}} \;=\; \Delta H_{\text{std}} \cdot \frac{m_{\text{std}}}{m_{\text{sample}}} \cdot \frac{A_{\text{sample}}}{A_{\text{std}}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Gray's formula assumes: (i) symmetric furnace, (ii) no baseline drift, (iii) single well-defined peak, (iv) sample mass small enough that thermal gradients inside the sample are negligible. Violating these leads to **systematic errors up to 20ŌĆō30%**. For quantitative enthalpy work, **DSC is preferred**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III ŌĆö Kissinger Equation for Activation Energy

For a thermal event with peak temperature $T_p$ (measured at heating rate $\beta$), Kissinger showed (1956) that:

$$T_p^2 \,\frac{d\alpha}{dt}\bigg|_{T_p} \;=\; \frac{E_a\,\beta}{R}$$

Combining with the Arrhenius rate law $\dfrac{d\alpha}{dt} = A\,(1-\alpha)^n\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ and assuming maximum reaction rate at the peak ($\dfrac{d^2\alpha}{dt^2} = 0$):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T_p^2}\right) \;=\; -\frac{E_a}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{T_p} \;+\; \text{const}$$

**Procedure (Board Exam Favorite):**
1. Run DTA at **three or more** heating rates (e.g., $\beta = 5, 10, 15, 20$ ┬░C/min).
2. Record the peak temperature $T_p$ at each $\beta$.
3. Plot $\ln(\beta / T_p^2)$ on the y-axis vs. $1/T_p$ on the x-axis.
4. **Slope** $= -E_a / R$ ŌåÆ compute activation energy.

> [!TIP]
> **Worked Numerical Sketch:** If $\beta_1 = 5$ ┬░C/min, $T_{p,1} = 700$ K, $\beta_2 = 10$ ┬░C/min, $T_{p,2} = 720$ K, $\beta_3 = 20$ ┬░C/min, $T_{p,3} = 740$ K, then plotting and fitting gives $E_a \approx 130$ŌĆō150 kJ/mol ŌĆö typical for solid-state crystallization in oxide nanoparticles.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Reading a DTA Peak

| Feature on Thermogram | What it tells you |
|-----------------------|-------------------|
| **Flat baseline** | No thermal event; sample and reference heat identically |
| **Sharp downward peak (endotherm)** | Fast endothermic event: melting, desolvation, dehydration |
| **Broad downward peak** | Slow endothermic event: dehydration, decomposition over wide T range |
| **Sharp upward peak (exotherm)** | Fast exothermic event: crystallization, oxidation |
| **Peak area** | Proportional to $\Delta H$ of the event |
| **Peak temperature $T_p$** | Identifies the phase transition (compare with standards) |
| **Onset temperature $T_e$** | Thermodynamic start of event (less dependent on $\beta$) |
| **Peak shifts to higher T at higher $\beta$** | Kinetic (non-equilibrium) process |
| **Peak position independent of $\beta$** | Equilibrium (reversible) transition, e.g., true melting |
| **Shoulder / doublet** | Overlapping events; multiple phases; polydispersity in nanomaterial |
| **Peak height too large** | Excessive sample mass ŌåÆ self-cooling / thermal lag |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 ŌĆö Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DTA Instrument

The following Mermaid diagram maps the full functional flow of a DTA apparatus ŌĆö from the symmetric sample/reference holders to the differential signal output. This is a *block-level architecture*, replacing a physical schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Temp Programmer"] --> B["Furnace Heater with Symmetric Windings"]
    B --> C["Furnace Hot Zone at Heating Rate beta"]
    C --> D1["Sample Crucible holding Nanomaterial S"]
    C --> D2["Reference Crucible holding alpha Alumina R"]
    D1 --> E1["Thermocouple TS measures Sample Temperature"]
    D2 --> E2["Thermocouple TR measures Reference Temperature"]
    E1 --> F["Differential Amplifier computes delta T = TS minus TR"]
    E2 --> F
    E1 --> G1["Channel 1 TS vs t"]
    E2 --> G2["Channel 2 TR vs t"]
    F --> H["DTA Output delta T vs T or t on Recorder or PC"]
    B --> I["Atmosphere Controller N2 Ar O2 Air"]
    I --> C
    H --> J["Interpreter identifies Peaks and Onsets"]
    J --> K["Peak Area gives delta H via Grays Formula"]
    J --> L["Kissinger Analysis on Tp at multiple betas gives Ea"]
```

---

    START["Start Thermogram Interpretation"] --> BASE["Check Baseline flat and stable"]
    BASE --> PEAK["Identify Peaks by Sign"]
