# SECTION 5 ŌĆö KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
> **Define MEMS. List any four advantages of using silicon as the substrate material in MEMS fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **MEMS (Micro Electro Mechanical Systems)** are integrated micro-scale devices that combine mechanical and electrical functionalities on the same silicon chip, typically fabricated using IC-compatible batch processing techniques with feature sizes in the range of **1 ┬Ąm to 1 mm**.

**Four advantages of silicon as a MEMS substrate:**
1. **Excellent mechanical properties** ŌĆö Young's modulus $E \approx 190$ GPa (comparable to steel) with low mechanical hysteresis and no plastic deformation until fracture.
2. **Near-zero mechanical fatigue** ŌĆö unlike metals, Si can survive billions of stress cycles, ideal for oscillators and comb drives.
3. **CMOS-compatible processing** ŌĆö batch fabrication allows millions of devices per wafer at low unit cost.
4. **Tunable electrical properties** ŌĆö resistivity can be varied across 6 orders of magnitude via n-type / p-type doping.
5. *(Bonus)* Native oxide $\text{SiO}_2$ is a perfect insulator and etch mask.

*(Any 4 correct points ŌĆö 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the list.)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
> **State the scaling law for forces in micro-scale systems. Why is it the most important consideration in MEMS design?**

**Model Answer:**

The scaling law states that when a characteristic linear dimension is reduced by a factor $s$:
- **Volume forces** (gravity, inertia) scale as $s^{-3}$.
- **Surface forces** (friction, adhesion, electrostatics on area) scale as $s^{-2}$.

Therefore, the surface-to-volume force ratio grows as $\dfrac{F_{surf}}{F_{vol}} \propto \dfrac{1}{L}$ ŌĆö meaning surface forces dominate as the device shrinks.

**Why it is the most important consideration:**
At the micro-scale, gravity becomes negligible while adhesion, viscosity, and electrostatic forces dominate. Designers must therefore use **electrostatic, surface-tension-based, or piezoresistive sensing** rather than electromagnetic or inertial (coil-based) mechanisms which are efficient only at the macro scale. Failure to apply the scaling law leads to **stiction, inoperable bearings, and unexpected failure modes** ŌĆö historically the most common cause of MEMS project failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks).** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`
> **(a) (7 marks)** Explain the working principle of a **capacitive MEMS accelerometer** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its sensitivity.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** A MEMS cantilever has length $L = 500 \mu m$, width $w = 100 \mu m$, thickness $t = 5 \mu m$, and is made of silicon ($E = 190$ GPa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m┬│). Calculate (i) the spring constant, (ii) the effective mass, and (iii) the fundamental resonant frequency. State two engineering conclusions from your result.

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part A (a)

**Working Principle of a Capacitive MEMS Accelerometer:**

A capacitive accelerometer consists of a **seismic proof mass** suspended by **flexure springs** above a fixed set of electrodes. The proof mass has perforated fingers that interdigitate with fixed stator fingers, forming a **differential capacitive half-bridge** ($C_1$ and $C_2$).

**Block-level flow:**

$$\text{Acceleration } a \;\to\; \text{Displacement } x = \frac{m a}{k} \;\to\; \Delta C = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2} \;\to\; V_{out} = V_s \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \;\to\; \text{Readout}$$

**Working steps:**
1. When the device accelerates, the proof mass lags due to inertia, producing relative displacement $x$.
2. This displacement changes the gap in one capacitor while increasing it in the other.
3. The differential capacitance change is detected by a charge amplifier or switched-capacitor ASIC.
4. The output voltage is proportional to acceleration.

**Derivation of Sensitivity:**

Let $C_1$ and $C_2$ be the two sensing capacitors. At rest, $C_1 = C_2 = C_0 = \varepsilon_0 A / d$. With displacement $x$:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - x}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + x}.$$

The differential output is proportional to:
$$\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{1}{d-x} - \frac{1}{d+x}\right] = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2 - x^2} \approx \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, x}{d^2} \quad (x \ll d).$$

The mechanical displacement is $x = m a / k$, hence:
$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta C}{a} = \frac{2 \varepsilon_0 A \, m}{k \, d^2}\;}$$

This expression is the **sensitivity** (capacitance change per unit acceleration), and the inverse dependence on $k d^2$ shows why low-spring-constant, small-gap designs are preferred. *(7 marks: 3 for principle, 2 for block flow, 2 for derivation.)*

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Part A (b)

**Given:**
$L = 500 \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $w = 100 \mu m = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m, $t = 5 \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $E = 190 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\rho = 2330$ kg/m┬│.

**(i) Spring constant of a cantilever:**
$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3} = \frac{(190 \times 10^9)(1 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{4 (5 \times 10^{-4})^3}.$$

Computing the numerator:
$$190 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.25 \times 10^{-16} = 2.375 \times 10^{-9}.$$

Denominator:
$$4 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-10} = 5.0 \times 10^{-10}.$$

$$\boxed{\;k = \frac{2.375 \times 10^{-9}}{5.0 \times 10^{-10}} = 4.75 \text{ N/m}\;}$$

**(ii) Effective mass** (for a cantilever, $m_{eff} \approx 0.24 \, m$, where $m = \rho \cdot V$):
$$m = \rho w t L = 2330 \times (1 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-6})(5 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.825 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg}.$$

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \times 5.825 \times 10^{-11} = 1.398 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg}.$$

$$\boxed{\;m_{eff} \approx 1.40 \times 10^{-11} \text{ kg}\;}$$

**(iii) Fundamental resonant frequency** (using the analytical form for a uniform cantilever, $\lambda_1^2 = 1.875$ for the 1st mode):
$$f_0 = \frac{\lambda_1^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4}}, \quad I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-4})(5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = 1.042 \times 10^{-21} \text{ m}^4.$$

$$A = w t = 5 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m}^2.$$

$$\frac{E I}{\rho A L^4} = \frac{(190 \times 10^9)(1.042 \times 10^{-21})}{(2330)(5 \times 10^{-10})(5 \times 10^{-4})^4}.$$

Numerator: $1.98 \times 10^{-10}$. Denominator: $2330 \times 5 \times 10^{-10} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-14} = 7.28 \times 10^{-20}$.

Ratio: $1.98 \times 10^{-10} / 7.28 \times 10^{-20} = 2.72 \times 10^{9}$.

$$f_0 = \frac{1.875^2}{2 \pi} \sqrt{2.72 \times 10^{9}} = \frac{3.516}{2 \pi} \times 52154 \approx 29184 \text{ Hz}.$$

$$\boxed{\;f_0 \approx 29.2 \text{ kHz}\;}$$

**Two engineering conclusions:**
1. The very low effective mass ($\sim 14$ picograms) and the moderately low $k$ yield a **kHz-class resonance**, suitable for **acoustic / vibration sensing** in smartphones and microphones.
2. The displacement under 1 g ($x = m g / k \approx 1.4 \times 10^{-11} \times 9.81 / 4.75 \approx 29$ pm) is extremely small ŌĆö requiring **on-chip amplification** to detect. *(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 1 for setup, 2 for conclusions.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö Alternative Choice.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
> **(a) (7 marks)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **bulk vs. surface micromachining** processes for MEMS fabrication. State four differences between them in a table.
>
> **(b) (7 marks)** Define **pull-in voltage** in a parallel-plate MEMS actuator and derive its expression. A device has $A = 200 \times 200 \mu m^2$, $d_0 = 2 \mu m$, $k = 5$ N/m. Calculate the pull-in voltage. Use $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B (a)

**Bulk Micromachining:**
Etches **into the bulk** of the silicon wafer to form three-dimensional microstructures (membranes, cantilevers, deep cavities). Uses anisotropic etchants like KOH, TMAH, or DRIE. The wafer itself becomes the structural material.

**Surface Micromachining:**
Builds microstructures **on top of the wafer** by sequentially depositing, patterning, and selectively removing (releasing) **sacrificial layers**. The wafer acts only as a substrate; the structural layers are deposited polysilicon, SiOŌéé, or SiŌéāNŌéä.

**Block diagrams:**

```
BULK MICROMACHINING                  SURFACE MICROMACHINING
-------------------                  ----------------------
[ Mask on SiO2 ]                    [ Deposit Sacrificial ]
[ Etch SiO2 ]                       [ Deposit Structural  ]
[ Anisotropic Etch Si (KOH) ]        [ Pattern Structural  ]
[ Release Membrane ]                [ Etch Sacrificial    ]
                                     [ Release Microstructure ]
```

**Differences Table (4 key):**

| S.No. | Aspect | Bulk Micromachining | Surface Micromachining |
|------|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Etching direction | Into the wafer (vertical/lateral deep etch) | On the wafer surface only (thin-film etch) |
| 2 | Structural material | Bulk Si of the wafer itself | Deposited poly-Si / SiN films |
| 3 | Sacrificial layer | Usually none (etch-stop techniques used) | Mandatory to release the structure |
| 4 | Thickness of structure | Equal to wafer thickness (200 ŌĆō 700 ┬Ąm) | Limited to film thickness (1 ŌĆō 25 ┬Ąm) |
| 5 | IC compatibility | Partial (high-temp KOH limits) | High (low-temp processes) |
| 6 | Applications | Pressure sensors, accelerometers | Comb drives, gyroscopes, RF MEMS |

*(7 marks: 3 for diagrams, 2 for explanations, 2 for the table.)*

---

### Model Solution ŌĆö Question B (b)

**Pull-in voltage** is the **critical voltage** at which a movable, spring-suspended parallel plate in a MEMS actuator becomes **unstable** and snaps onto the fixed substrate, because the electrostatic attraction grows faster than the spring can restore.

**Derivation** (re-stated concisely, see Section 3.2 for full derivation):
$$V_{PI} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k d_0^3}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}.$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $A = 200 \times 200 \mu m^2 = 4 \times 10^{-8}$ m┬▓, $d_0 = 2 \mu m = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $k = 5$ N/m, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

Compute each term:
- $d_0^3 = (2 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-18}$ m┬│.
- Numerator: $8 k d_0^3 = 8 \times 5 \times 8 \times 10^{-18} = 3.2 \times 10^{-16}$.
- Denominator: $27 \varepsilon_0 A = 27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 27 \times 35.416 \times 10^{-20} = 9.562 \times 10^{-18}$.

$$\frac{N}{D} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^{-16}}{9.562 \times 10^{-18}} = 33.47.$$

$$\boxed{\;V_{PI} = \sqrt{33.47} \approx 5.79 \text{ V}\;}$$

**Interpretation:** A pull-in voltage of ~5.8 V is conveniently compatible with standard CMOS supplies, enabling direct on-chip electrostatic actuation without external high-voltage drivers. *(7 marks: 2 definition, 2 derivation, 3 calculation.)*

---

## 5.3 ŌÜĀ’ĖÅ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes where students lose marks in MEMS questions:**
> 1. **Unit errors in $k$ and $f_0$:** Forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to m ŌĆö a 1 ┬Ąm vs $10^{-6}$ m slip can give spring constants wrong by **$10^{12}$**.
> 2. **Pull-in derivation skipping the stability condition** $\dfrac{dF_e}{dx} = \dfrac{dF_m}{dx}$ ŌĆö the universal $1/3$ displacement factor cannot be derived otherwise. Marks are lost if you only quote the formula.
> 3. **Confusing effective mass $m_{eff}$ with total mass $m$** ŌĆö for a cantilever, $m_{eff} = 0.24 \, m$, **not** $m$. For a fixed-fixed beam, $m_{eff} \approx 0.41 \, m$.
> 4. **Using $E$ in GPa in a formula that expects Pa** ŌĆö always convert $E = 190$ GPa to $190 \times 10^9$ Pa before substituting.
> 5. **Writing "MEMS = miniature machine"** ŌĆö it is a **system** with electrical and mechanical functions, not merely a small device.
> 6. **Confusing pull-in (electrostatic) with snap-through (mechanical buckling)** ŌĆö they are different physical phenomena.
> 7. **Listing MEMS applications without classifying** under sensing vs. actuation ŌĆö board examiners reward structured answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** MEMS = **M**icro **E**lectro **M**echanical **S**ystems ŌĆö size range **1 ┬Ąm ŌĆō 1 mm**, IC-compatible, integrates mechanical + electrical functions.
- **Core physics:** Scaling laws dictate that $F_{surf}/F_{vol} \propto 1/L$ ŌåÆ **surface forces dominate at the micro-scale**; gravity becomes negligible.
- **Most-used material:** Silicon (high $E$, low fatigue, CMOS-compatible, anisotropic etching).
- **Transduction pairs to memorize:**
  * Sensing ŌĆö piezoresistive, capacitive, piezoelectric, resonant.
  * Actuation ŌĆö electrostatic (preferred for low-power CMOS), piezoelectric, thermal bimorph, magnetic (rare at micro-scale).
- **Key formulas to keep on the tip of the pen:**
  * Cantilever $k = E w t^3 / 4 L^3$
  * Resonant frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$
  * Electrostatic force $F_e = \varepsilon_0 A V^2 / 2 d^2$
  * Pull-in voltage $V_{PI} = \sqrt{8 k d_0^3 / 27 \varepsilon_0 A}$ at displacement $d_0/3$.
- **Fabrication routes:** Bulk micromachining (etch into wafer) vs. surface micromachining (sacrificial layer release). DRIE enables high aspect ratios.
- **MEMS Ōåö NEMS transition:** Occurs at critical dimensions $\approx 100$ nm where quantum and surface effects start to dominate.
- **Standard physical constants to memorize:** $E_{Si} = 190$ GPa, $\rho_{Si} = 2330$ kg/m┬│, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\mu_{air} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa┬Ęs.
- **Engineering reality check:** Always check pull-in voltage against available supply; always check resonant frequency against the operating bandwidth; always verify that the designed displacement remains < $d_0/3$ to avoid instability.
- **MEMS applications hit-list (memorize):** airbag accelerometer, smartphone gyroscope, MEMS microphone, inkjet print head, digital micromirror (DLP), lab-on-chip, RF switch, pressure sensor.
- **Past-year emphasis:** KTU ESE questions on this topic usually test (i) the scaling law, (ii) the pull-in voltage derivation, and (iii) the resonant frequency ŌĆö practice all three before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS)** are a class of integrated nanoscale devices in which electrical and mechanical functionalities are coupled at the nanometer length scale (typically **1 nm to 100 nm** for the critical feature size). They represent the logical technological extension of **Micro Electro Mechanical Systems (MEMS)** into the sub-micron regime, fabricated using both **top-down** (lithographic) and **bottom-up** (chemical/self-assembly) techniques. Mathematically and operationally, a NEMS device is any system whose characteristic dimension *d* satisfies: $$d \ll \lambda_{\text{ph}}, \quad d \ll l_{\text{mean free path}}$$ where $\lambda_{\text{ph}}$ is the phonon wavelength and $l_{\text{mean free path}}$ is the mean free path of the charge carrier/phonon in the host material. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim-Style):** NEMS are integrated nanometer-scale devices that combine **electrical** (signal transduction) and **mechanical** (motion/deformation) functionality, exploiting quantum, surface, and size-dependent effects absent in MEMS. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy ŌĆö "The Tuning Fork at the Atomic Scale":** > Imagine taking an ordinary steel **tuning fork** (a mechanical resonator you can hold in your hand) and shrinking it by a factor of **one million** until it becomes smaller than a virus. Its *natural frequency* of vibration would skyrocket from ~440 Hz to the **GHz range** ŌĆö radio frequencies. Now replace the steel with a single **carbon nanotube (CNT)**, and you essentially have a NEMS resonator. Because the mass *m* has shrunk to attograms ($10^{-18}$ g) and the stiffness $k$ has remained relatively high, the device becomes extraordinarily sensitive to **single-molecule adsorption** ŌĆö a single virus landing on it shifts its frequency measurably. This is the heart of NEMS: ultra-light, ultra-stiff, ultra-sensitive. ## 1.3 Why NEMS? ŌĆö Driving Physical Reasoning Three fundamental physical advantages drive the transition from MEMS to NEMS: | Scaling Effect | Physical Consequence | Engineering Benefit | |---|---|---| | **Mass Reduction** | $m \propto L^3$ scales cubically | Single-molecule mass sensitivity ($\sim 10^{-21}$ g) | | **Surface-to-Volume Ratio** | $\text{SVR} \propto 1/d$ | Dominant surface forces ŌåÆ ultra-sensitive chemical/biological sensors | | **Reduced Power** | Energy $\propto m \cdot v^2$ | $10^3$ŌĆō$10^6$├Ś lower power dissipation than MEMS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics (Board-Relevant Constants):** > - **Operating Frequency:** $1$ MHz ŌĆō $10$ GHz (for MEMS: $< 1$ MHz) > - **Mass Sensitivity:** $\sim 10^{-21}$ g (zeptogram regime) > - **Power Consumption:** $\sim 10$ pW per device > - **Characteristic Dimension:** $10$ nm ŌĆō $100$ nm # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of NEMS Devices NEMS components are broadly classified into the following functional categories. KTU examiners frequently test these classifications: 1. **NEMS Resonators** ŌĆö Beam/cantilever structures vibrating at RF frequencies, used as mass, force, and gas sensors. 2. **NEMS Switches/Relays** ŌĆö Electrostatically or chemically actuated mechanical contacts for ultra-low-power logic. 3. **NEMS Sensors** ŌĆö Chemical, biological, and physical sensors exploiting surface adsorption. 4. **NEMS Actuators** ŌĆö Convert electrical signal into controlled nanoscale motion. 5. **NEMS Transducers** ŌĆö Couple mechanical motion to electrical readout (piezoresistive, capacitive, field-effect). ## 2.2 Fundamental Working Principle A generic NEMS device operates via a **three-stage transduction cycle**: - **Step 1 ŌĆö Input Coupling:** A physical, chemical, or electrical stimulus (mass, force, charge, molecule) is delivered to the mechanical element (beam, cantilever, bridge, or nanowire). - **Step 2 ŌĆö Mechanical Response:** The mechanical element deforms or shifts its natural resonance frequency $f_n$ in proportion to the stimulus. - **Step 3 ŌĆö Electrical Readout:** The mechanical change is transduced into an electrical signal (change in capacitance, resistance, or current). > [!NOTE] > **Why "Why?" matters here:** The *brilliance* of NEMS lies in the *transduction* ŌĆö a tiny mechanical change (picometers) is amplified into a large, easily measurable electrical change. The figure of merit is the **resonance frequency shift** $\Delta f_n / f_n$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}}$ | Natural resonance frequency, stiffness $k$, effective mass $m^*$ | Hz, N/m, kg | | 2 | $f_n = \dfrac{\alpha_n^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ | Cantilever beam resonance; $\alpha_1 = 1.875$ for mode 1 | $E$: Young's modulus; $I$: 2nd moment; $\rho$: density; $A$: area; $L$: length | | 3 | $k = \dfrac{3EI}{L^3}$ | Spring constant of a cantilever beam | N/m | | 4 | $m^* = 0.24 \, \rho A L$ | Effective mass of cantilever (mode 1) | kg | | 5 | $Q = \dfrac{f_n}{\Delta f_{\text{FWHM}}}$ | Quality factor (energy stored / energy lost per cycle) | Dimensionless | | 6 | $\delta m = \dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}$ | Mass resolution / sensitivity from frequency shift | kg | | 7 | $F_{\min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T k \Delta f}{\omega_n Q}}$ | Minimum detectable force (thermodynamic limit) | N; $k_B$: Boltzmann constant | | 8 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance used in NEMS readout | Farads (F) | | 9 | $F_{\text{electrostatic}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x} V^2$ | Electrostatic actuation force | N | | 10 | $R_{\text{CNT}} = \dfrac{h}{4e^2}$ | Quantum resistance of a single-walled CNT | $\approx 6.45\ \text{k}\Omega$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility in Real Systems:** > - **Mass Sensing (e.g., virus detection):** $\delta m \sim 10^{-21}$ g using CNT resonators. > - **Force Sensing (AFM tips):** $F_{\min} \sim 10^{-18}$ N at room temperature. > - **RF Communications:** NEMS filters/oscillators above 1 GHz. > - **Medical Diagnostics:** Single biomolecule (DNA, protein) detection. ## 2.4 Materials Used in NEMS Fabrication (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > **Frequently tested:** Material selection and justification. 1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs):** Exceptional $E \sim 1$ TPa, low $\rho$, high $Q$. 2. **Graphene:** 2D monolayer, $E \sim 1$ TPa, ultra-high surface area. 3. **Silicon Nanowires (SiNWs):** CMOS-compatible, mature fabrication. 4. **SiC, SiGe, AlN:** Piezoelectric, used in electromechanical coupling. 5. **Metallic Nanowires (Au, Pt):** Used in molecular-scale switches. ## 2.5 Comparison: MEMS vs. NEMS (Board-Favorite Table) | Property | MEMS | NEMS | |---|---|---| | Characteristic Size | $1 - 100\ \mu m$ | $1 - 100\ \text{nm}$ | | Operating Frequency | kHz ŌĆō MHz | MHz ŌĆō GHz | | Mass Sensitivity | $\sim 10^{-15}$ g | $\sim 10^{-21}$ g | | Power Consumption | $\mu$W | pW ŌĆō nW | | Surface Effects | Negligible | Dominant | | Fabrication | Top-down lithography | Top-down + Bottom-up | | Quantum Effects | Absent | Significant | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Resonance Frequency of a Cantilever NEMS Beam We derive the most frequently tested NEMS formula. Consider a uniform cantilever beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section (width $w$, thickness $t$), with density $\rho$ and Young's modulus $E$. ### Step 1 ŌĆö Equation of Motion (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory) The transverse displacement $u(x, t)$ of a slender beam under no external load satisfies: $$EI\dfrac{\partial^4 u}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 u}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ where $A = w t$ is the cross-sectional area, and $I = \dfrac{wt^3}{12}$ is the second moment of area about the neutral axis. *Explanation:* The first term is the **restoring bending moment per unit length**; the second is the **inertial force per unit length**. Their balance governs free vibration. ### Step 2 ŌĆö Separation of Variables Assume $u(x, t) = \phi(x) e^{i\omega t}$. Substituting: $$EI\,\phi^{(4)}(x) - \rho A \omega^2 \phi(x) = 0$$ Rearranging: $$\phi^{(4)}(x) = \beta^4 \phi(x), \quad \beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$$ ### Step 3 ŌĆö Boundary Conditions (Cantilever: Fixed at x = 0, Free at x = L) The cantilever boundary conditions are: 1. $\phi(0) = 0$ (zero displacement at fixed end) 2. $\phi'(0) = 0$ (zero slope at fixed end) 3. $\phi''(L) = 0$ (zero bending moment at free end) 4. $\phi'''(L) = 0$ (zero shear force at free end) ### Step 4 ŌĆö General Solution and Characteristic Equation The general solution to $\phi^{(4)} = \beta^4 \phi$ is: $$\phi(x) = a_1 \cos(\beta x) + a_2 \sin(\beta x) + a_3 \cosh(\beta x) + a_4 \sinh(\beta x)$$ Applying BCs (1) and (2): $a_1 + a_3 = 0$ and $a_2 + a_4 = 0$. Substituting into BCs (3) and (4) and eliminating the constants yields the **transcendental frequency equation**: $$\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$$ The roots of this equation are $\beta_n L = 1.875,\ 4.694,\ 7.855, \ldots$ ### Step 5 ŌĆö Closed-Form Resonance Frequency The $n^{\text{th}}$ mode resonance frequency is: $$\boxed{f_n = \dfrac{(\beta_n L)^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A}} = \dfrac{\alpha_n^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}}$$ where $\alpha_n = \beta_n L$ are dimensionless mode constants. *Explanation:* The $L^2$ in the denominator and $L^2$ inside the square root give a combined $L^4$ scaling ŌĆö meaning **shorter beams vibrate at much higher frequencies**, justifying the GHz operation of NEMS cantilevers. ### Step 6 ŌĆö Mode 1 Closed-Form For the fundamental mode ($\alpha_1 = 1.875$): $$f_1 = \dfrac{(1.875)^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}} \approx 0.56 \sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Insight (KTU-Level):** A silicon cantilever with $L = 100$ nm, $w = 20$ nm, $t = 20$ nm gives $f_1 \approx 1$ GHz. Reducing $L$ to 30 nm pushes $f_1$ above **10 GHz**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Sensitivity of a NEMS Resonator When a small mass $\delta m$ adsorbs on the beam, the effective mass increases: $m^* \to m^* + \delta m$. The frequency shifts from $f_n$ to $f_n + \delta f_n$. ### Step 1 ŌĆö Frequency-Mass Relationship $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}}$$ Differentiating with respect to $m^*$: $$\dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial m^*} = -\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi m^*}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}} = -\dfrac{f_n}{2 m^*}$$ ### Step 2 ŌĆö Solve for $\delta m$ $$\delta m = -2m^* \dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n} = -\dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}$$ (using $k = m^* \omega_n^2$). The negative sign indicates that **mass addition decreases frequency**, so we measure the absolute shift: $$\boxed{\vert\delta m\vert = \dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\left\vert\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}\right\vert}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Implication:** Higher $f_n$ (shorter/stiffer beam) and lower $k$ (softer beam) **both** improve mass sensitivity. This is why **ultra-thin, ultra-short CNTs and graphene ribbons** dominate cutting-edge NEMS sensors. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Detectable Force (Thermodynamic Limit) A NEMS resonator in thermal equilibrium experiences **Brownian noise force** spectral density: $$S_F^{\text{th}} = 4 k_B T k \, \dfrac{1}{\omega_n Q}$$ Over a measurement bandwidth $\Delta f$, the minimum resolvable force is: $$F_{\min} = \sqrt{S_F^{\text{th}} \cdot \Delta f} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 k_B T k \, \Delta f}{\omega_n Q}}$$ *Explanation:* Higher $Q$ and higher $\omega_n$ reduce thermal noise floor. This is why NEMS operate at GHz in vacuum to achieve $Q \sim 10^4$ and approach $F_{\min} \sim 10^{-18}$ N. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Application to Real Materials | Material | $E$ (GPa) | $\rho$ (kg/m┬│) | $\sqrt{E/\rho}$ (m/s) | $f_1$ for $L = 100$ nm beam | |---|---|---|---|---| | Si | 170 | 2330 | 8548 | $\sim 0.5$ GHz | | SiC | 450 | 3210 | 11839 | $\sim 0.7$ GHz | | Diamond | 1050 | 3515 | 17285 | $\sim 1.0$ GHz | | CNT (armchair) | 1000 | 1300 | 27735 | $\sim 1.7$ GHz | | Graphene | 1000 | 2200 (areal) | ~21000 | $\sim 1.2$ GHz | > [!NOTE] > **Board Tip:** The product $\sqrt{E/\rho}$ is the *figure of merit* for high-frequency NEMS. CNTs and graphene lead by ~2ŌĆō3├Ś over silicon. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a NEMS Device The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete functional architecture of a generic NEMS sensor/actuator system, illustrating signal flow from physical input to electrical output. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Physical Input Stimulus
Mass Molecule
Force Charge
Temperature] --> B[Mechanical Transducer Element
Cantilever Bridge
Nanowire CNT
Graphene Ribbon] B --> C[Mechanical Response
Deflection Delta X
Resonance Shift Delta F
Strain Epsilon] C --> D[Transduction Mechanism
Piezoresistive
Capacitive
Field Effect
Piezoelectric] D --> E[Signal Conditioning
Amplifier
Oscillator Circuit
Phase Locked Loop] E --> F[Electrical Output
Voltage V
Current I
Frequency F] B -. Feedback Path .-> G[Actuation Drive
Electrostatic
Piezoelectric
Thermal] G --> B F --> H[Readout System
Spectrum Analyzer
Computer DAQ
Wireless Telemetry] H --> I[Application Layer
Mass Sensor
Gas Detector
RF Filter
Logic Switch
Medical Probe] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The **main forward path** (solid arrows) flows top-to-bottom from physical stimulus to application. The **dashed feedback loop** represents the **actuation drive** that excites the mechanical element into resonance ŌĆö a hallmark of NEMS dynamic-mode operation. ## 4.2 Fabrication Route Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Top Down Approach
E beam Lithography
FIB Milling
Reactive Ion Etching] --> C[NEMS Device] A2[Bottom Up Approach
CNT Growth CVD
Nanowire VLS
Self Assembly] --> C A3[Hybrid Approach
Dielectrophoresis
Pick and Place
Nanolithography] --> C C --> D[Functional Output
Resonator
Switch
Sensor
Actuator] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section (Conceptual) of a NEMS Cantilever Resonator ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1[Substrate Anchor Fixed End] SUB[SiO2 Substrate] end subgraph S2[Free Standing Nanobeam] BEAM[CNT or SiNW or Graphene Ribbon
Length L Width w Thickness t] MASS[Adsorbed Analyte Mass Delta M] end subgraph S3[Actuation and Readout] ACT[Actuator Electrode
Electrostatic Drive] READ[Readout Electrode
Capacitive Piezoresistive] end SUB --> BEAM BEAM --> MASS ACT -. AC Voltage .-> BEAM BEAM -. Motion .-> READ READ --> SIG[Output Signal Frequency Shift] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2024, Model Paper]** **Define Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS). How do they differ from MEMS in terms of characteristic size and operating frequency?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Nano Electro Mechanical Systems (NEMS) are integrated devices that combine **electrical** and **mechanical** functionality at the nanometer scale, typically with critical dimensions in the range of **1 nm ŌĆō 100 nm**. They are actuated and sensed by electrical signals while exhibiting mechanical motion or deformation. **Differentiation from MEMS (1.5 Marks):** | Property | MEMS | NEMS | |---|---|---| | Characteristic Size | $1 - 100\ \mu m$ | $1 - 100\ \text{nm}$ | | Operating Frequency | kHz ŌĆō MHz | MHz ŌĆō GHz | | Mass Sensitivity | $\sim 10^{-15}$ g | $\sim 10^{-21}$ g | | Power Consumption | $\mu$W | pW | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **List any three materials commonly used in NEMS fabrication. Justify their suitability.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Carbon Nanotubes (CNTs) (1 Mark):** Exceptional Young's modulus ($\sim 1$ TPa), low density ($\sim 1300$ kg/m┬│), high $Q$ factor, and 1D geometry ideal for beam resonators. 2. **Silicon Nanowires (SiNWs) (1 Mark):** CMOS-compatible, well-established fabrication, and good piezoresistive response for electrical readout. 3. **Graphene (1 Mark):** 2D monolayer, atomic thickness, ultra-high surface-to-volume ratio, and ultrahigh carrier mobility for sensitive mass and gas sensing. --- ## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not** confuse $E$ (Young's modulus) with $E$ (energy) in beam equations ŌĆö write units explicitly. > 2. **Always** state boundary conditions before solving the transcendental frequency equation. > 3. **Always** include numerical units in final answers. Marks are deducted for unitless numerical values. > 4. **Do not** skip the transcendental equation derivation ŌĆö KTU board expects at least the equation $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$ in the model answer. > 5. **Sign convention:** Mass addition ŌåÆ frequency **decreases**; force application ŌåÆ beam **deflects** downward (or per chosen axis). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Derive the resonance frequency expression for a cantilever-type NEMS beam using Euler-Bernoulli theory. State clearly the boundary conditions used.** **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Equation of Motion (1.5 Marks):** The Euler-Bernoulli equation for free transverse vibration of a uniform beam is: $$EI \dfrac{\partial^4 u(x, t)}{\partial x^4} + \rho A \dfrac{\partial^2 u(x, t)}{\partial t^2} = 0$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Separation of Variables (1 Mark):** Assume $u(x, t) = \phi(x) e^{i\omega t}$: $$EI\,\phi^{(4)}(x) - \rho A \omega^2 \phi(x) = 0$$ Define $\beta^4 = \dfrac{\rho A \omega^2}{EI}$, so $\phi^{(4)} = \beta^4 \phi$. **Step 3 ŌĆö Boundary Conditions (1 Mark):** For a cantilever (fixed at $x = 0$, free at $x = L$): $\phi(0) = 0, \quad \phi'(0) = 0, \quad \phi''(L) = 0, \quad \phi'''(L) = 0$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Frequency Equation (1.5 Marks):** General solution: $\phi(x) = a_1 \cos\beta x + a_2 \sin\beta x + a_3 \cosh\beta x + a_4 \sinh\beta x$. Applying BCs yields the **transcendental equation**: $$\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$$ **Step 5 ŌĆö Final Frequency Expression (2 Marks):** The $n^{\text{th}}$ mode resonance frequency: $$f_n = \dfrac{(\alpha_n)^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}, \quad \alpha_1 = 1.875$$ --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]** **A silicon NEMS cantilever has length $L = 200$ nm, width $w = 50$ nm, and thickness $t = 30$ nm. Calculate the fundamental resonance frequency. Given: $E_{\text{Si}} = 170$ GPa, $\rho_{\text{Si}} = 2330$ kg/m┬│.** **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Compute Geometric Parameters (2 Marks):** $$A = w t = (50 \times 10^{-9})(30 \times 10^{-9}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{m}^2$$ $$I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-9})(30 \times 10^{-9})^3}{12} = 1.125 \times 10^{-32}\ \text{m}^4$$ **Step 2 ŌĆö Apply Frequency Formula (2 Marks):** $$f_1 = \dfrac{(1.875)^2}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Compute the Term Inside the Square Root (2 Marks):** $$\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4} = \dfrac{(170 \times 10^9)(1.125 \times 10^{-32})}{(2330)(1.5 \times 10^{-15})(200 \times 10^{-9})^4}$$ Denominator: $2330 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-22} = 5.592 \times 10^{-34}$ Numerator: $1.9125 \times 10^{-21}$ $$\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4} = \dfrac{1.9125 \times 10^{-21}}{5.592 \times 10^{-34}} = 3.42 \times 10^{12}\ \text{s}^{-2}$$ **Step 4 ŌĆö Final Frequency (1 Mark):** $$f_1 = \dfrac{3.516}{2\pi}\sqrt{3.42 \times 10^{12}} = 0.560 \times 1.849 \times 10^{6} \approx 1.03 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_1 \approx 1.03\ \text{MHz}}$$ (For such a long beam, MHz range is expected; shorter beams reach GHz.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) [7 Marks]** **Explain the working principle of a NEMS resonator as a mass sensor. Derive the mass sensitivity expression $\delta m = \dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}$.** **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1 ŌĆö Working Principle (2.5 Marks):** A NEMS resonator is a nanomechanical beam excited at its natural frequency $f_n$. When an analyte mass $\delta m$ adsorbs on the beam surface, the effective mass $m^*$ increases ($m^* \to m^* + \delta m$). Because $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m^*}$, the resonance frequency shifts downward by $\delta f_n$. The magnitude of the shift directly quantifies the adsorbed mass ŌĆö enabling **single-molecule detection** with $\delta m \sim 10^{-21}$ g sensitivity. **Step 2 ŌĆö Differentiation (2 Marks):** Starting from $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m^*}$: $$\dfrac{df_n}{dm^*} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k}\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\right)(m^*)^{-3/2} = -\dfrac{f_n}{2m^*}$$ **Step 3 ŌĆö Rearranging for $\delta m$ (2.5 Marks):** For small changes, $\delta f_n = \dfrac{\partial f_n}{\partial m^*}\delta m$: $$\delta m = -2m^* \dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}$$ Using $k = m^* \omega_n^2$, i.e., $m^* = k/\omega_n^2$: $$\boxed{\delta m = -\dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}}$$ The negative sign indicates that **mass addition decreases** the resonance frequency. --- #### **Part (b) [7 Marks]** **Discuss the key differences between top-down and bottom-up fabrication approaches for NEMS. Give two specific examples of each.** **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Top-Down Approach (3.5 Marks):** The top-down method starts from a bulk material and **subtractively** sculpts nanoscale features using lithographic or etching techniques. It is the dominant industrial approach due to **CMOS compatibility**. - **Advantages:** Precise dimensional control, batch fabrication, integration with electronics. - **Disadvantages:** Resolution limits, surface damage, high equipment cost. - **Examples:** 1. **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL):** Direct-write patterning of nanobeams down to ~10 nm. 2. **Reactive Ion Etching (RIE):** Anisotropic etching of silicon to form NEMS cantilevers and bridges. **Bottom-Up Approach (3.5 Marks):** The bottom-up method **builds up** NEMS structures atom-by-atom or molecule-by-molecule from chemical precursors using self-assembly or directed growth. - **Advantages:** Atomic precision, low cost, access to truly 1D structures (CNTs, nanowires). - **Disadvantages:** Lack of positional control, integration challenges. - **Examples:** 1. **Chemical Vapor Deposition (CVD) Growth of CNTs:** Vertical or horizontal CNT growth for NEMS resonators. 2. **Vapor-Liquid-Solid (VLS) Growth of SiNWs:** Catalyst-driven axial growth of single-crystal silicon nanowires. > [!NOTE] > **Hybrid Approach:** Modern NEMS fabrication frequently uses a **hybrid** method ŌĆö e.g., CVD-grown CNTs transferred onto lithographically patterned electrodes via **dielectrophoresis**. This combines the precision of top-down with the material quality of bottom-up. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision ŌĆö Memorize These for KTU Board Exam:** - [ ] **Definition:** NEMS = electrical + mechanical coupling at $1 - 100$ nm scale. - [ ] **MEMS vs NEMS:** MEMS = $\mu$m scale, kHzŌĆōMHz; NEMS = nm scale, MHzŌĆōGHz. - [ ] **Master Formula (must memorize):** $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m^*}$ - [ ] **Cantilever Formula:** $f_1 = 0.560\sqrt{\dfrac{EI}{\rho A L^4}}$ (use $\alpha_1 = 1.875$). - [ ] **Mass Sensitivity:** $\delta m = \dfrac{2k}{\omega_n^2}\dfrac{\delta f_n}{f_n}$ ŌĆö mass adsorption *decreases* $f_n$. - [ ] **Minimum Detectable Force:** $F_{\min} = \sqrt{4k_B T k \Delta f / (\omega_n Q)}$. - [ ] **Transcendental Equation (cantilever):** $\cos(\beta L)\cosh(\beta L) = -1$. - [ ] **Three Key Materials:** CNT, Graphene, SiNW ŌĆö all with $E \sim 10^2 - 10^3$ GPa. - [ ] **Two Fabrication Routes:** Top-down (EBL, RIE) vs Bottom-up (CVD, VLS). - [ ] **Four Application Domains:** Mass sensing, force sensing, RF filters, ultra-low-power switches. - [ ] **Quality Factor:** $Q = f_n / \Delta f_{\text{FWHM}}$ ŌĆö high $Q$ = sharp resonance = better sensor. - [ ] **Surface-to-Volume Ratio:** $SVR \propto 1/d$ ŌĆö dominant in NEMS, negligible in MEMS. - [ ] **Why NEMS are faster:** $f_n \propto 1/L^2$ ŌĆö shorter beam = higher frequency. - [ ] **Numerical Benchmark:** Si NEMS cantilever ($L = 100$ nm) $\Rightarrow f_1 \sim 1$ GHz. - [ ] **Power:** NEMS consume pW ŌĆö 10$^3$ŌĆō10$^6$├Ś less than MEMS. - [ ] **Quantum Resistance of CNT:** $R_Q = h/(4e^2) \approx 6.45$ k$\Omega$.

topics.title: Nanomanipulation - STM Based Atomic Manipulations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Nanomanipulation ŌĆō STM Based Atomic Manipulations

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 ŌĆō NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanomanipulation ŌĆō STM Based Atomic Manipulations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**STM-based atomic manipulation** is a precise, controlled nanomanipulation technique that uses a sharp metallic tip (typically W or Pt-Ir) brought to within a sub-nanometer distance (Ōēł **0.3 nm to 1.0 nm**) from a conducting substrate, exploiting the principle of **quantum mechanical electron tunneling** to image, push, pull, or place individual atoms or molecules on a surface with atomic-scale precision.

The technique was famously demonstrated in **1989** by **Eigler and Schweizer** at IBM Almaden Research Center, who used a low-temperature STM to manipulate individual **Xenon (Xe) atoms** on a Nickel (110) surface to spell out the letters "**IBM**" ŌĆö proving that atoms could be precisely positioned one at a time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> STM-based atomic manipulation is a *direct-write, bottom-up* nanofabrication paradigm. It is the gold-standard reference technique for:
> - Atomic-scale assembly of quantum structures
> - Construction of quantum corrals (e.g., Fe atoms on Cu(111) ŌĆō Crommie et al., 1993)
> - Single-molecular chemistry
> - Verification of theoretical models in surface science

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Atomic Pick-and-Place Machine"**
>
> Imagine you are wearing **invisible magnetic gloves** that allow you to feel and move individual grains of sand on a beach ŌĆö but every grain is **10 million times smaller** than a real sand grain, and your "hand" is a needle tip only **one atom wide at its apex**.
>
> In STM manipulation, the **tip** acts like this magical hand. When you bring the tip very close to an atom, the atom feels a tiny attractive force (analogous to a magnet). If you slide the tip across the surface, the atom "hops" along with the tip ŌĆö much like a **paperclip being pulled by a magnet** across a table. You can then drop the atom at a desired location by lifting the tip slightly.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Tip-sample separation | **0.3 nm ŌĆō 1.0 nm** |
| Tunneling current | **0.1 nA ŌĆō 10 nA** |
| Bias voltage | **+10 mV ŌĆō ┬▒4 V** |
| Tip apex radius | **< 30 nm** (atomic-scale sharpness) |
| Manipulation temperature | **4 K ŌĆō 300 K** (often UHV, 10Ōü╗┬╣Ōü░ Torr) |
| Work function (typical metals) | **4 ŌĆō 6 eV** |
| Lateral manipulation force | **10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ N to 10Ōü╗Ōü╣ N** (pico- to nano-Newton) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these numbers matter:** The exponential dependence of tunneling current on tip-sample distance means that a change of just **0.1 nm** in gap changes the current by roughly **an order of magnitude**. This is what gives STM its extraordinary vertical resolution (~**0.01 nm**).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Principle: Quantum Mechanical Tunneling

When two metallic electrodes (tip and sample) are separated by a thin insulating vacuum gap, electrons can "tunnel" through the potential barrier due to their wave-like nature. The resulting **tunneling current** is exponentially sensitive to the gap width, which is the foundation of both STM imaging and atomic manipulation.

### 2.2 Operational Steps in STM-Based Manipulation

- **Step 1 ŌĆō Imaging Mode (Constant Current / Constant Height)**
  The tip is raster-scanned across the surface. A feedback loop adjusts the tip height (in constant-current mode) to maintain a fixed tunneling current, generating a real-space topographic map of the surface with atomic resolution.

- **Step 2 ŌĆō Positioning the Tip Above a Target Atom**
  After identifying the target atom in the topographic image, the tip is precisely positioned directly above it using piezoelectric scanners (XYZ resolution Ōēł **0.001 nm**).

- **Step 3 ŌĆō Lowering the Tip (Increasing Tip-Sample Interaction)**
  The tip is brought closer than during imaging, typically by reducing the gap resistance from ~**G╬® (imaging)** to ~**10 k╬® ŌĆō 1 M╬® (manipulation)**. This strengthens the tip-atom binding force.

- **Step 4 ŌĆō Lateral Movement (Pulling / Pushing the Atom)**
  The tip is dragged laterally across the surface. Depending on the interaction regime, the atom either:
  - **Slides** along with the tip (sliding/pulling mode), or
  - **Hops** between neighboring binding sites (hopping mode).

- **Step 5 ŌĆō Atom Deposition**
  The tip is retracted to imaging height, leaving the atom at the desired position.

### 2.3 Modes of STM Atomic Manipulation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification ŌĆö memorize these three modes:**

**1. Lateral Manipulation (Sliding/Pulling Mode)**
The tip is moved parallel to the surface at a close distance. The atom remains in contact with the substrate while being dragged by the tip-atom attractive force. Typical tip-atom force: **~ 2 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬╣Ōü░ N**.

**2. Vertical Manipulation (Pick-and-Place Mode)**
The tip picks up an atom by vertical approach, lifts it off the substrate, translates it, and redeposits it. Used for transferring atoms between terraces or different surfaces.

**3. Field Emission Manipulation (Pushing Mode / Single Atom Field Ionization)**
A high bias voltage (**> +5 V**) is applied to ionize and repel atoms via field-emission forces. Used for non-contact manipulation of weakly bound adsorbates.

### 2.4 Forces Involved in Tip-Atom-Substrate Interaction

- **Van der Waals (vdW) force** ŌĆō Long-range, always attractive (ŌłØ 1/rŌüČ).
- **Short-range chemical (covalent-like) force** ŌĆō Becomes significant at distances < 0.3 nm.
- **Electrostatic force** ŌĆō Due to applied bias and local work function differences.
- **Pulsed-force / current-induced force** ŌĆō From inelastic tunneling electrons exciting atomic vibrations.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical equations for STM-based atomic manipulation (KTU board favorites):**

| Equation | Description | Variables / Units |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| $I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp\!\left(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, z\right)$ | Tunneling current (WKB approximation) | $I_t$: current [A], $V_b$: bias [V], $z$: gap [m], $\phi$: avg. work function [eV], $k \approx 1.025 \,\text{eV}^{-1/2}\,\text{├ģ}^{-1}$ |
| $k = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m_e}}{\hbar} \approx 0.512 \,\text{├ģ}^{-1}\,\text{eV}^{-1/2}$ | Decay constant in vacuum barrier | $m_e$: electron mass, $\hbar$: reduced Planck constant |
| $P(z) \propto \exp\!\left(-2\kappa z\right)$ where $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m_e\phi}}{\hbar}$ | Tunneling probability through rectangular barrier | $\kappa$: inverse decay length [mŌü╗┬╣] |
| $F_{tip-atom} = -\dfrac{\partial U(r)}{\partial r}$ | Force on adatom from tip potential | $U(r)$: interaction potential [J] |
| $R_{gap} = \dfrac{V_b}{I_t}$ | Tunneling junction resistance (key control parameter) | $R_{gap}$: [╬®]; for imaging Ōēł **G╬®**, for manipulation Ōēł **k╬®ŌĆōM╬®** |
| $\Delta I / I \approx -2\kappa \Delta z$ | Relative current change with vertical displacement | Shows **exponential sensitivity** |
| $E_{ads}$ (e.g., Xe on Ni) | Adsorption energy barrier that tip must overcome | Typically **0.1 ŌĆō 0.3 eV** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility:** STM manipulation is used to assemble atomic-scale quantum devices, build model catalysts, study single-atom magnetism, and fabricate artificial molecules (e.g., **quantum corrals** that confine surface-state electrons to mimic 2D quantum wells).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tunneling Current Using the Bardeen Formalism

The tunneling current in an STM junction can be rigorously derived using Bardeen's perturbation theory. We present it step by step.

**Setup:** Two electrodes (tip and sample) separated by a vacuum barrier of width $z$ and effective height $\phi$ (average work function). Apply bias voltage $V_b$ across the junction.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Wavefunction in the barrier region**

Inside the classically forbidden vacuum gap, the electronic wavefunction decays exponentially:

$$\psi(z) = \psi_0 \, e^{-\kappa z}$$

Here, $\kappa$ is the decay constant, which depends on the barrier height.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Energy of an electron in the barrier**

For an electron of mass $m_e$ with energy $E$ tunneling through a barrier of height $U_0 > E$:

$$E - U_0 = -\dfrac{\hbar^2 \kappa^2}{2m_e}$$

Rearranging for $\kappa$:

$$\kappa^2 = \dfrac{2m_e(U_0 - E)}{\hbar^2}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Define effective work function $\phi$**

For a metal-vacuum-metal junction at small bias, the relevant barrier height is the average work function (measured from Fermi level):

$$\phi = \dfrac{\phi_{tip} + \phi_{sample}}{2}$$

So:

$$\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$$

Plugging in values:

$$\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \cdot 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \cdot \phi \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.055 \times 10^{-34}}$$

For $\phi \approx 4$ eV:

$$\kappa \approx 1.025 \,\text{├ģ}^{-1} = 1.025 \times 10^{10} \,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Tunneling probability (WKB approximation)**

The probability of an electron tunneling through a rectangular barrier of width $z$ is:

$$P(z) = e^{-2\kappa z}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Tunneling current (low-bias limit)**

For small bias $V_b$, the current is proportional to the density of states times the tunneling probability times the applied voltage:

$$I_t = C \cdot V_b \cdot e^{-2\kappa z} = C \cdot V_b \cdot e^{-k\sqrt{\phi}\,z}$$

where $k = 2\kappa / \sqrt{\phi} \approx 1.025 \,\text{├ģ}^{-1}\,\text{eV}^{-1/2}$.

**Step 6 ŌĆö Sensitivity to gap distance**

Taking the logarithmic derivative to find the sensitivity:

$$\dfrac{d I_t}{d z} = -k\sqrt{\phi}\, I_t$$

Therefore, a fractional change in current is:

$$\dfrac{\Delta I_t}{I_t} = -k\sqrt{\phi}\, \Delta z$$

For $\phi = 4$ eV and $\Delta z = 0.1$ nm:

$$\dfrac{\Delta I_t}{I_t} = -1.025 \times 2 \times 0.1 = -0.205$$

This corresponds to roughly **~20% change** per 0.1 nm ŌĆö explaining STM's atomic vertical resolution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic summary:** Energy in eV ├Ś $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ converts to Joules; $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs; $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg. The result $\kappa \approx 1.025$ ├ģŌü╗┬╣ is universal for typical metal work functions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix ŌĆö Manipulation Mode Selection

| Mode | Tip-Substrate Gap | Resistance Regime | Force Regime | Atom Behavior |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------|---------------|
| Imaging | Ōēł 0.5 ŌĆō 1.0 nm | **G╬®** (10Ōü╣ ╬®) | vdW only | Stationary |
| Lateral Sliding | Ōēł 0.3 ŌĆō 0.5 nm | **100 k╬® ŌĆō 1 M╬®** | vdW + short-range chemical | Slides with tip |
| Vertical Pick-up | < 0.3 nm | **10 ŌĆō 100 k╬®** | Strong chemical | Attaches to tip |
| Field Emission | Ōēł 0.5 ŌĆō 1.0 nm | High bias (> 5 V) | Electrostatic repulsion | Pushed away |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** Reducing the gap resistance from G╬® to k╬® during manipulation increases tip-atom interaction by 3ŌĆō4 orders of magnitude in current density, which translates into a dramatic increase in the lateral force available to move the atom.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of STM-Based Atomic Manipulation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STM Tip Pt-Ir or W] -->|Tunneling gap 0.3 to 1.0 nm| B[Sample Surface e.g., Cu 111]
    B -->|Adsorbate atom e.g., Xe, Fe, Co| C[Adsorbate Binding Site]
    D[Piezoelectric XYZ Scanner] -->|Positioning resolution 0.001 nm| A
    E[Bias Voltage Source 10 mV to 4 V] -->|Applied across junction| A
    F[Tunneling Current Amplifier nA range] -->|Feedback signal| G[Control Electronics]
    G -->|Adjusts Z height| D
    H[UHV Chamber 10 to the power minus 10 Torr] --> A
    H --> B
    I[Manipulation Mode Selector] -->|Sets gap resistance| G
    I -->|Lateral mode| J[Lateral Sliding Pulling]
    I -->|Vertical mode| K[Vertical Pick and Place]
    I -->|Field emission mode| L[Field Emission Pushing]
    J --> M[Atom Relocated to Target Site]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Imaging Verification]
    N -->|Success or repeat| A
```

### 4.2 Energy Landscape During a Lateral Manipulation Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initial atom position] -->|Barrier E ads| B[Tip atom interaction region]
    B -->|Barrier E hop| C[Adjacent binding site]
    C -->|Barrier E ads| D[Final atom position]
    E[Tip moves laterally] --> B
    B -.->|Decreased barrier due to tip proximity| F[Atom hops with tip]
```

### 4.3 Operational State Machine for STM Manipulation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Imaging
    Imaging --> PositionOverTarget: Identify atom
    PositionOverTarget --> LowerTip: Decrease gap to manipulation range
    LowerTip --> LateralManipulation: Drag tip across surface
    LowerTip --> VerticalManipulation: Approach vertically
    LateralManipulation --> AtomRelocated: Atom slides with tip
    VerticalManipulation --> AtomPicked: Atom binds to tip
    AtomPicked --> AtomPlaced: Translate and lower tip
    AtomRelocated --> VerifyImaging: Retract tip to imaging height
    AtomPlaced --> VerifyImaging: Retract tip to imaging height
    VerifyImaging --> [*]: Success
    VerifyImaging --> PositionOverTarget: Repeat if needed
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 Model]**
**Define STM-based atomic manipulation. Mention any two key parameters that distinguish manipulation mode from imaging mode.**

**Model Answer:**
STM-based atomic manipulation is a technique using a sharp metallic tip brought within **0.3ŌĆō1.0 nm** of a conducting substrate to image, push, or pull individual atoms via quantum tunneling current feedback, achieving atomic-scale precision in positioning.
*Key distinguishing parameters:*
- **Gap resistance:** Ōēł G╬® during imaging vs. Ōēł k╬®ŌĆōM╬® during manipulation.
- **Tip-atom interaction:** Weak vdW during imaging vs. strong chemical + vdW during manipulation.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark parameter 1 + 1 mark parameter 2)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 Model]**
**State Bardeen's tunneling equation and explain why the tunneling current is exponentially sensitive to the tip-sample gap.**

**Model Answer:**
Bardeen's tunneling current:
$$I_t \propto V_b \cdot \exp(-k\sqrt{\phi}\, z)$$
where $k \approx 1.025 \,\text{├ģ}^{-1}\,\text{eV}^{-1/2}$ and $\phi$ is the average work function. The exponential dependence means that even a **0.1 nm** change in $z$ alters $I_t$ by an order of magnitude, giving STM atomic-scale vertical sensitivity. This sensitivity is precisely what enables controlled tip approach during manipulation.

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks equation + 1 mark sensitivity explanation + 0.5 mark numerical)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, PECHT833 Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of quantum mechanical tunneling as applied to STM. Derive the expression for tunneling current in the low-bias limit using the WKB approximation. Mention the role of the work function in determining the decay constant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss in detail the three modes of STM-based atomic manipulation ŌĆö lateral sliding, vertical pick-and-place, and field emission ŌĆö with suitable diagrams. Compare their typical gap resistance regimes and atom behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Quantum tunneling principle (1.5 marks): electrons traverse classically forbidden vacuum gap due to wave nature.
- Derivation:
  - Wavefunction in barrier $\psi \propto e^{-\kappa z}$ (1 mark)
  - $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e \phi}/\hbar$ (1 mark)
  - Tunneling probability $P = e^{-2\kappa z}$ (1 mark)
  - Low-bias current $I_t \propto V_b e^{-2\kappa z}$ (1.5 marks)
- Role of work function: higher $\phi$ ŌåÆ larger $\kappa$ ŌåÆ faster decay ŌåÆ lower tunneling probability; typical metals $\phi \approx 4$ŌĆō6 eV. (1 mark)

**Model Solution (b):**
- **Lateral sliding:** tip dragged parallel to surface at gap Ōēł 0.3ŌĆō0.5 nm; atom slides due to tip-atom attraction; resistance 100 k╬®ŌĆō1 M╬®. (2 marks)
- **Vertical pick-and-place:** tip approaches, picks up atom (gap < 0.3 nm), translates, redeposits; used for inter-terrace transfer; resistance 10ŌĆō100 k╬®. (2 marks)
- **Field emission:** high bias (> +5 V) creates electrostatic repulsion; non-contact pushing; suitable for weakly bound adsorbates. (2 marks)
- Comparison table (gap, resistance, behavior). (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Model Paper, PECHT833 Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a)** Describe the EiglerŌĆōSchweizer experiment (1989) on Xe atom manipulation on Ni(110). Discuss the experimental conditions and the significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an STM. How does the piezoelectric scanner enable sub-nanometer positioning of the tip? Derive the sensitivity of tunneling current to gap distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- Historical context: 1989 IBM Almaden, Eigler & Schweizer. (1 mark)
- Substrate: Ni(110); adsorbate: Xe atoms; conditions: UHV, **4 K** (liquid He cooled). (1.5 marks)
- Procedure: imaging ŌåÆ positioning tip over Xe ŌåÆ lowering tip to increase interaction ŌåÆ dragging Xe laterally to form "IBM" letters. (2 marks)
- Significance: First demonstration of single-atom positional control; birth of bottom-up nanotechnology; foundation for quantum corral, atom-by-atom assembly, molecular machines. (2.5 marks)

**Model Solution (b):**
- STM construction: tip (W or Pt-Ir), piezoelectric XYZ scanner, sample, coarse positioning (walker/louse), vibration isolation, UHV chamber. (2 marks)
- Working: tunneling current at constant value maintained via feedback ŌåÆ tip traces topography. (1 mark)
- Piezoelectric scanner: voltage applied to PZT ceramic produces ├ģngstrom-scale expansion/contraction; positioning resolution Ōēł 0.001 nm. (2 marks)
- Derivation of sensitivity:
$$\dfrac{\Delta I_t}{I_t} = -k\sqrt{\phi}\, \Delta z$$
For $\phi = 4$ eV, $k = 1.025$ ├ģŌü╗┬╣ eVŌü╗┬╣/┬▓: per 0.1 nm, $\Delta I/I \approx -0.205$. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** STM (conducting samples only) with AFM (works on insulators). AFM uses force feedback, not tunneling current.
> - **Do NOT state** that the tip physically "touches" the atom during imaging ŌĆö it is non-contact.
> - **Always specify** UHV conditions for atomic-resolution STM; atmospheric STM gives noisy images.
> - In derivations, **include the work function symbol clearly** ŌĆö losing the $\sqrt{\phi}$ dependency costs marks.
> - For manipulation mode questions, **explicitly state gap resistance values** ŌĆö vague answers lose 2ŌĆō3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4 ŌĆö STM Atomic Manipulation):**

- Ō£ģ STM = Scanning Tunneling Microscopy; first atomic-resolution instrument (Binnig & Rohrer, **1981**, Nobel Prize **1986**).
- Ō£ģ Tunneling current: $I_t \propto V_b \exp(-k\sqrt{\phi}\,z)$, with $k \approx 1.025$ ├ģŌü╗┬╣ eVŌü╗┬╣/┬▓.
- Ō£ģ Decay constant: $\kappa = \sqrt{2m_e\phi}/\hbar$.
- Ō£ģ Imaging gap Ōēł 0.5ŌĆō1.0 nm; manipulation gap Ōēł 0.3ŌĆō0.5 nm or less.
- Ō£ģ Gap resistance: G╬® (imaging) ŌåÆ k╬®ŌĆōM╬® (manipulation).
- Ō£ģ Three manipulation modes: **Lateral sliding, Vertical pick-and-place, Field emission**.
- Ō£ģ EiglerŌĆōSchweizer experiment (1989): Xe on Ni(110) at 4 K ŌåÆ "IBM" logo.
- Ō£ģ Quantum corral: Fe atoms on Cu(111) confine surface-state electrons (Crommie et al., 1993).
- Ō£ģ Key forces: van der Waals (long-range attractive), short-range chemical, electrostatic.
- Ō£ģ Work function ($\phi$) governs tunneling decay; typical metals Ōēł 4ŌĆō6 eV.
- Ō£ģ Piezoelectric scanner enables **sub-nanometer** XYZ positioning (~0.001 nm).
- Ō£ģ Tip material: W (etched) or Pt-Ir (mechanically cut); apex radius < 30 nm.
- Ō£ģ Operation environment: **UHV** (~10Ōü╗┬╣Ōü░ Torr), often cryogenic (4 KŌĆō77 K).
- Ō£ģ Manipulation forces: ~10Ōü╗┬╣┬▓ŌĆō10Ōü╗Ōü╣ N (pN to nN range).
- Ō£ģ Atomic-scale resolution: vertical Ōēł **0.01 nm**; lateral Ōēł **0.1 nm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nanolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 ŌĆö NANOLITHOGRAPHY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> **Nanolithography** is the art and science of fabricating nanoscale structures, patterns, and devices ŌĆö typically with critical dimensions **below 100 nm** ŌĆö by selectively modifying a material (the *resist*) using a directed energy source (photons, electrons, ions, or physical contact). It is the foundational enabling technology of modern **Very-Large-Scale Integration (VLSI)**, **MEMS/NEMS**, **nanophotonics**, and **quantum device fabrication**.

### Conceptual Analogy ŌĆö The "Nano-Engraver"
Imagine you are an artist carving an extremely intricate sculpture into a polished marble slab.

- The **marble slab** = the substrate (silicon wafer, glass, polymer film).
- The **acid/ink/drill** = the *resist*, a radiation-sensitive material.
- The **engraving tool** = the energy source (light, electron beam, ion beam, AFM tip, mould).
- The **blueprint/mask** = the design pattern you want to imprint.

In nanolithography, instead of a chisel we use **wavelengths of light measured in nanometres**, **focused electron beams**, or even **sharp physical tips** to "draw" features so small that thousands could fit across the width of a single human hair (Ōēł 80 ┬Ąm).

### Why Nanolithography Matters
> [!NOTE]
> **Moore's Law** drove the semiconductor industry for decades ŌĆö the number of transistors on a chip doubles roughly every **18ŌĆō24 months**. This miniaturisation is sustained *exclusively* through advances in lithographic resolution. Today, leading-edge fabs (e.g., TSMC, Intel, Samsung) manufacture chips with features down to **3 nm**, requiring **Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV)** lithography.

### Key Terminology at a Glance
| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Resist** | Radiation-sensitive polymer that changes solubility upon exposure |
| **Photomask** | Glass plate with patterned chrome that blocks light in selected regions |
| **Resolution (R)** | Smallest reproducible feature size |
| **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | Vertical tolerance for the imaging plane |
| **Throughput** | Wafers (or areas) processed per hour |
| **Numerical Aperture (NA)** | Light-gathering ability of an optical system |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Nanolithographic Techniques

Nanolithography is broadly classified into three families based on the mode of energy/material transfer:

### A) Radiation-Based Lithography
1. **Photolithography (Optical / UV / DUV / EUV)**
2. **Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)**
3. **Focused Ion Beam Lithography (FIBL)**
4. **X-Ray Lithography (XRL)**
5. **Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography (EUV, ╬╗ = 13.5 nm)**

### B) Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)
6. **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) Lithography**
7. **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) Lithography**
8. **Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)**
9. **Near-Field Scanning Optical Microscopy (NSOM) Lithography**

### C) Replica / Mould-Based Lithography
10. **Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)**
11. **Soft Lithography (Microcontact Printing, ┬ĄCP)**

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Lithography ŌĆö Why Resolution is Limited

The minimum printable feature **R** is governed by three coupled constraints:

1. **Diffraction of light / wavelength** ŌĆö smaller ╬╗ ŌåÆ smaller features.
2. **Numerical aperture (NA)** ŌĆö higher NA ŌåÆ finer features, but shallower depth of focus.
3. **Process factor (kŌéü)** ŌĆö depends on resist chemistry, illumination, masking, and process control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rayleigh's Resolution Criterion (1879)**
> $$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$
> where *kŌéü* is a process-dependent constant (typically 0.4ŌĆō0.8 for production; theoretical lower limit Ōēł 0.25).

The **Depth of Focus (DOF)** sets the vertical tolerance:
$$DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}$$
A high-NA system gives sharp features but demands an extremely flat wafer and precise focusing.

## 2.3 Photolithography in Depth

Photolithography uses **UV light** projected through a **photomask** to selectively expose a **photoresist** coated on a wafer. The process flow is universal:

1. **Substrate Preparation** ŌĆö RCA cleaning, dehydration bake (Ōēł 200 ┬░C).
2. **Resist Coating** ŌĆö Spin-coating (~ 1000ŌĆō5000 rpm) to obtain a uniform film (50 nm ŌĆō 1 ┬Ąm).
3. **Soft Bake** ŌĆö Evaporates residual solvent.
4. **Exposure** ŌĆö UV illumination through mask (contact, proximity, or projection).
5. **Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)** ŌĆö Drives diffusion-controlled reactions (especially in chemically amplified resists).
6. **Development** ŌĆö Removes exposed (positive resist) or unexposed (negative resist) regions in a developer (e.g., TMAH).
7. **Hard Bake** ŌĆö Improves etch resistance.
8. **Etching / Deposition / Ion Implantation** ŌĆö Pattern transfer into the substrate.
9. **Resist Strip** ŌĆö Removes residual resist using acetone or plasma ashing.

### Wavelength Reduction Roadmap
| Generation | ╬╗ (nm) | Approx. Min. Feature (nm) | Year |
|---|---|---|---|
| g-line (Hg lamp) | 436 | 500 ŌĆō 800 | 1980s |
| i-line (Hg lamp) | 365 | 350 ŌĆō 500 | 1990s |
| KrF Excimer | 248 | 130 ŌĆō 250 | late 1990s |
| ArF Excimer (dry) | 193 | 65 ŌĆō 130 | 2000s |
| ArF Immersion | 193 | 38 ŌĆō 45 | 2010s |
| **EUV (Sn plasma)** | **13.5** | **7 ŌĆō 3** | **2018 onwards** |

## 2.4 Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)

A focused **electron beam** (typically 1ŌĆō100 keV) directly "writes" patterns into an electron-sensitive resist (e.g., **PMMA**, **ZEP520**, **HSQ**). No mask is needed ŌĆö it is a **direct-write, maskless** technique.

**Key advantages:** Sub-10 nm resolution; maskless (rapid prototyping).
**Key disadvantages:** Very low throughput (one wafer takes hours), expensive equipment, proximity effects from electron scattering.

The de Broglie wavelength of an electron at accelerating voltage V is:

$$\lambda_e = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

For 100 kV electrons, ╬╗ Ōēł **0.0037 nm** ŌĆö far smaller than any atomic spacing. Resolution is therefore limited by **electronŌĆōresist interactions and scattering**, not wavelength.

## 2.5 Focused Ion Beam Lithography (FIBL)

Uses **GaŌü║** ions (or HeŌü║, NeŌü║) accelerated at 5ŌĆō50 keV. The heavier mass gives:
- Higher momentum transfer (sputtering, milling).
- Minimal proximity effect (ions do not backscatter like electrons).
- Direct substrate modification (no resist required).

Used for **mask repair, TEM sample preparation, circuit edit, and sub-10 nm patterning**.

## 2.6 X-Ray Lithography (XRL)

Uses **soft X-rays (╬╗ Ōēł 0.4 ŌĆō 4 nm)** from a synchrotron or laser-produced plasma. The extremely short wavelength gives:
- Diffraction-limited resolution < 25 nm.
- Large depth of focus.
- **Challenge:** X-ray masks (SiC, Si membrane with W/Au absorber) are extremely difficult to fabricate.

## 2.7 Extreme Ultraviolet Lithography (EUV)

Uses **╬╗ = 13.5 nm** photons from a **Sn droplet laser-produced plasma (LPP)** source. Operates in **vacuum** (all materials absorb EUV). Uses **Mo/Si multilayer Bragg reflectors** (Ōēł 70% reflectivity per mirror, 40+ mirrors in series ŌćÆ only Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 % throughput ŌĆö a major engineering challenge).

## 2.8 Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)

A **mechanical, mould-based** technique. A hard mould (Si, SiOŌéé, Ni, diamond) is pressed into a thermoplastic or UV-curable resist. No radiation is required.

- **Resolution:** down to **2 nm** demonstrated (Stanford, Chou group, 1997).
- **Throughput:** extremely high (parallel stamping).
- **Limitation:** mould wear, defect control, overlay alignment for multi-level devices.

Two main variants:
- **Thermal NIL (T-NIL):** heat + press.
- **UV-NIL (UV-NIL):** UV-curable liquid resist, transparent mould.

## 2.9 Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)

A sharp tip (**1ŌĆō10 nm apex radius**) is scanned across a surface to modify it locally.

| Technique | Mechanism | Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| STM lithography | Tunneling current ŌåÆ surface reaction | Atomic |
| AFM (anodic) oxidation | Tip bias ŌåÆ local oxide growth | 10ŌĆō20 nm |
| Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN) | AFM tip delivers "ink" (alkanethiols) to surface | 15ŌĆō50 nm |
| Thermal/mechanical SPL | Tip heating/ploughing | 10ŌĆō30 nm |

## 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists **every** key equation required to solve numerical problems on Nanolithography in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rayleigh Resolution | $R = k_1 \cdot \lambda / NA$ | $k_1$: process factor; ╬╗: wavelength; NA: numerical aperture |
| 2 | Depth of Focus | $DOF = k_2 \cdot \lambda / NA^2$ | $k_2 \approx 1$ |
| 3 | Photon Energy | $E = hc / \lambda$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| 4 | de Broglie Wavelength (relativistic) | $\lambda = h / \sqrt{2 m_e e V (1 + eV / 2 m_e c^2)}$ | $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg; $V$ in volts |
| 5 | de Broglie Wavelength (non-relativistic approx.) | $\lambda \approx 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ nm | $V$ in volts |
| 6 | Optical Lens NA | $NA = n \cdot \sin\theta$ | $n$: refractive index of medium; $\theta$: half-angle of cone |
| 7 | Immersion Lithography Effective ╬╗ | $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda_0 / n_{imm}$ | $n_{imm} = 1.44$ for water |
| 8 | Resist Thickness (Spin Coat) | $t = K \cdot \omega^{-1/2}$ | $\omega$: angular velocity; K depends on resist viscosity |
| 9 | Proximity Effect (EBL) ŌĆö Gaussian | $f(r) = e^{-r^2 / \alpha^2} + \eta \cdot e^{-r^2 / \beta^2}$ | $\alpha$: forward scatter; $\beta$: backscatter; $\eta$: ratio |
| 10 | Multilayer Bragg Reflector | $d = \lambda / (2 \sin\theta)$ | d: bilayer thickness; ╬Ė: incidence angle |

> **Note for examiners:** $n_{imm}$ = 1.44 corresponds to ultra-pure water used in **ArF immersion lithography** (╬╗ŌéĆ = 193 nm ŌćÆ ╬╗_eff Ōēł 134 nm).

## 2.11 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Semiconductor ICs:** every commercial microchip, smartphone SoC, and AI accelerator is fabricated using photolithography.
- **MEMS/NEMS:** NIL and EBL create sub-┬Ąm mechanical structures for sensors and actuators.
- **Photonics & Plasmonics:** EBL defines metal nanostructures for surface plasmon resonance biosensors.
- **Data storage:** heat-assisted magnetic recording (HAMR) heads use NIL.
- **Biomedical:** DPN deposits protein arrays for single-cell studies.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Rayleigh Resolution Limit of an Optical Lithography System

The **Airy disk** formed by diffraction through a circular lens aperture has its first minimum at an angle ╬Ė given by:
$$\sin\theta = 1.22 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{D}$$
where *D* is the lens diameter. For a microscope/lithography objective, the **Numerical Aperture** is defined as $NA = n \sin\theta$ (for small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$). The radius of the Airy disk at the focal plane is:
$$r_{Airy} = 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$
This is the **theoretical minimum spot radius** (no process imperfections). In real lithography, imperfections are bundled into the **process factor kŌéü**:
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$
This is the **working equation** you must memorise. Typical kŌéü values:
- $k_1 = 0.7$ ŌĆō old contact/proximity lithography
- $k_1 = 0.5$ ŌĆō modern DUV with phase-shift masks, off-axis illumination
- $k_1 = 0.25$ ŌĆō theoretical limit (single exposure)

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Electron Wavelength Used in EBL

Starting from de Broglie's relation:
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$
For an electron accelerated through a potential difference *V* (non-relativistic):
$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$
Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C:
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm}$$
At **V = 100 kV**:
$$\lambda = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{100000}} = \frac{1.227}{316.23} \approx 0.00388 \text{ nm}$$
**Conclusion:** The electron wavelength is ~ 30,000├Ś smaller than an atom. Resolution in EBL is therefore **not diffraction-limited** ŌĆö it is limited by **electron scattering in the resist and substrate** (the *proximity effect*).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Effective Wavelength in Immersion Lithography

In immersion lithography, the space between the final lens element and the wafer is filled with a high-index fluid (water, $n_{imm} = 1.44$ at 193 nm). The **effective wavelength** inside the fluid becomes:
$$\lambda_{eff} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n_{imm}}$$
For ArF source ($\lambda_0 = 193$ nm):
$$\lambda_{eff} = \frac{193}{1.44} \approx 134 \text{ nm}$$
Plugging into the Rayleigh equation with $k_1 = 0.4$, $NA = 1.35$:
$$R = 0.4 \times \frac{134}{1.35} \approx 39.7 \text{ nm}$$
This is exactly the resolution regime of the **32 nm and 22 nm CMOS nodes** produced by Intel, TSMC, and Samsung (2010ŌĆō2014).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 ŌĆö EUV Bragg Reflector Bilayer Thickness

EUV mirrors are **alternating Mo / Si multilayers** acting as Bragg reflectors. The Bragg condition for first-order reflection is:
$$m \lambda = 2 d \sin\theta$$
For $m = 1$, $\lambda = 13.5$ nm, near-normal incidence $\sin\theta \approx 1$:
$$d = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{13.5}{2} = 6.75 \text{ nm}$$
So each Mo layer Ōēł 2.3 nm and each Si layer Ōēł 4.45 nm, repeated **40ŌĆō50 times** in a single mirror. Even with this careful design, the peak reflectivity is only **Ōēł 70 %** per mirror ŌĆö and with ~ 10 mirrors in series, total throughput is only **1ŌĆō2 %**, which is why EUV sources must deliver **> 250 W** at the intermediate focus.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Choosing the Right Technique

| Technique | Resolution | Throughput | Mask? | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Optical (DUV) | 38 ŌĆō 130 nm | Very High | Yes | Moderate | Mass IC production |
| EUV | 7 ŌĆō 20 nm | High | Yes (reflective) | Extreme | Leading-edge ICs |
| EBL | < 10 nm | Low (serial) | No (direct write) | High | R&D, photomasks, photonics |
| FIBL | < 10 nm | Low (serial) | No | High | Mask repair, TEM prep |
| X-Ray (XRL) | < 25 nm | Medium | Yes (X-ray mask) | Very High | Niche, MEMS |
| NIL | 2 ŌĆō 50 nm | Very High | Mould | Moderate | Photonics, biotech, storage |
| DPN | 15 ŌĆō 50 nm | Low (parallel arrays) | No | Moderate | Bio-arrays, R&D |
| STM/AFM | Atomic | Very Low | No | Moderate | Atomic-scale research |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    H -->|Negative| J[Developer dissolves unexposed areas]
    I --> K[Hard Bake]
    J --> K[Hard Bake]
    K --> L[Etch or Implant or Deposit]
    L --> M[Resist Strip]
    M --> N[Patterned Wafer]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ESE[Electron Source: Tungsten or Field Emission] --> ACC[Accelerator 1 to 100 kV]
    ACC --> STG[Electromagnetic Stigmation and Deflection Coils]
    STG --> CLM[Column and Magnetic Lenses]
    CLM --> BLN[Beam Blanker]
    BLN --> STG
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

```
> **Reading guide:** Every node is a hardware subsystem. Light flows from the Sn plasma source ŌåÆ collector ŌåÆ mask ŌåÆ projection optics ŌåÆ wafer, with **vacuum maintained throughout** because EUV is absorbed by every material, including air.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Dec 2022, Model Question]**
**Define Nanolithography. List any four major nanolithographic techniques used for sub-100 nm patterning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Nanolithography is the process of creating nanoscale patterns (critical dimensions < 100 nm) on a substrate by selectively exposing or modifying a resist material using a directed energy source (photons, electrons, ions) or by mechanical/chemical contact.
**Four techniques (2 marks ŌĆö 0.5 each):**
1. Photolithography (UV/DUV/EUV)
2. Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)
3. Focused Ion Beam Lithography (FIBL)
4. Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)
*(Acceptable alternatives: XRL, DPN, STM lithography, AFM lithography, soft lithography.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö May 2023, Model Question]**
**State the Rayleigh resolution criterion. Explain how immersion lithography improves resolution without changing the light source.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Equation (1 mark):**
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}$$
**Explanation (2 marks):** In immersion lithography, the space between the final lens element and the wafer is filled with a high-refractive-index fluid (typically ultra-pure water, n = 1.44 for ArF). The **effective wavelength** in the medium becomes:
$$\lambda_{eff} = \frac{\lambda_0}{n_{imm}}$$
The **NA also increases** to $NA = n_{imm} \sin\theta$. Both effects together **reduce R** by a factor of $n_{imm}$ (Ōēł 1.44 for water), without changing the laser source ŌĆö yielding ~ 30ŌĆō40 % smaller features with the same light source.

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU ESE Pattern]** Every Part B question provides an **internal choice** (OR). Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two completely independent alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Module 4, Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **principle, instrumentation, and working of Electron Beam Lithography (EBL)**. Compare its resolution and throughput with conventional photolithography. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **de Broglie wavelength of an electron** accelerated through 50 kV. Hence comment on the fundamental resolution limit in EBL. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) EBL ŌĆö Principle, Instrumentation and Comparison (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):** EBL uses a focused beam of electrons (1ŌĆō100 keV) to directly "write" patterns into an electron-sensitive resist. The electron beam is scanned (rastered) across the wafer using electromagnetic deflection coils under computer control. No physical mask is required, making EBL a **maskless, direct-write technique**.

**Instrumentation (3 marks):**
- **Electron source:** Tungsten hairpin, LaBŌéå, or Schottky Field-Emission Gun (FEG). FEGs give the smallest spot (Ōēł 1 nm).
- **Acceleration column:** Electrostatic plates accelerate electrons to 1ŌĆō100 kV.
- **Electromagnetic lenses:** Condenser and objective lenses focus the beam to a spot of 1ŌĆō10 nm.
- **Beam blanker:** Deflects the beam on/off rapidly so it can be turned on only where exposure is required.
- **Deflection coils:** Steer the beam in X and Y across the field.
- **Wafer stage:** Laser-interferometer-controlled XŌĆōY stage with sub-nm positioning accuracy.
- **Detectors:** Faraday cup, secondary electron detector for in-situ imaging.
- **Vacuum system:** 10Ōü╗ŌüČ to 10Ōü╗ŌüĖ mbar to prevent electron scattering.

**Comparison (2 marks):**
| Parameter | Photolithography | EBL |
|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 38 ŌĆō 130 nm | < 10 nm (sub-10 nm routinely) |
| Throughput | ~ 100 wafers/hour | ~ 1 wafer/hour (serial writing) |
| Mask required? | Yes | No (direct write) |
| Cost | Moderate | Very high |
| Use case | Mass production | R&D, photomask fabrication |

**(b) de Broglie Wavelength of 50 keV Electron (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Relativistic de Broglie formula:
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

**Step 2:** Insert constants:
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J┬Ęs
- $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $V = 50{,}000$ V

**Step 3:** Compute $eV$:
