$$eV = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 50{,}000 = 8.01 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4:** Compute relativistic correction:
$$\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2} = \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-15}}{1.64 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 0.0488$$

**Step 5:** Compute momentum:
$$p = \sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 8.01 \times 10^{-15} \times 1.0488}$$
$$p = \sqrt{1.532 \times 10^{-44}} = 1.238 \times 10^{-22} \text{ kg┬Ęm/s}$$

**Step 6:** Compute wavelength:
$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.238 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 5.35 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00535 \text{ nm}$$

**Comment (2 marks):** ╬╗ Ōēł 0.005 nm is ~ 100,000├Ś smaller than a silicon atom. **Resolution in EBL is therefore NOT diffraction-limited** ŌĆö it is limited by **electronŌĆōresist interactions, electron scattering (forward and back-scatter), and the proximity effect** which broadks patterns beyond the ideal spot.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) ŌĆö *Internal Choice Alternative*
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆö Module 4, Past Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the **principle and process steps of Nanoimprint Lithography (NIL)**. Compare thermal NIL and UV-NIL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) Lithography** system. What are the major engineering challenges? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Nanoimprint Lithography (7 marks)**

**Principle (2 marks):** NIL is a *mechanical*, mould-based patterning technique. A hard mould (Si, SiOŌéé, Ni, or diamond) containing the desired nanoscale relief pattern is pressed into a thin film of resist on a substrate. The resist takes the shape of the mould. After hardening (thermally or by UV curing), the mould is released, leaving a 1:1 replica of the pattern. NIL bypasses the diffraction limit of optical lithography entirely.

**Process Steps (3 marks):**
1. **Mould fabrication** ŌĆö EBL + dry etching on Si or Ni.
2. **Resist deposition** ŌĆö spin-coat thermoplastic (thermal NIL) or dispense UV-curable liquid (UV-NIL).
3. **Imprinting** ŌĆö press mould into resist at controlled T, P, and time.
4. **Curing** ŌĆö cool (T-NIL) or expose to UV (UV-NIL).
5. **Demoulding** ŌĆö separate mould from resist; anti-adhesion coating (FDTS) prevents sticking.
6. **Residual layer removal** ŌĆö anisotropic OŌéé RIE to clear the thin resist film at the bottom of imprinted features.
7. **Pattern transfer** ŌĆö etch or deposit through the resist pattern.

**Comparison (2 marks):**
| Parameter | Thermal NIL | UV-NIL |
|---|---|---|
| Resist | Thermoplastic (PMMA) | UV-curable monomer |
| Process | Heat + press | UV exposure at RT |
| Cycle time | Long (heat/cool) | Short |
| Mould | Hard Si/SiOŌéé/Ni | Transparent (quartz, PDMS) |
| Overlay accuracy | Moderate | High |
| Throughput | Moderate | High |

**(b) EUV Lithography (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to Section 4.4 above (EUV Exposure Tool Architecture).

**System Description (2 marks):** The EUV source is a **laser-produced Sn plasma (LPP)**: a high-power COŌéé laser fires at 50 ┬Ąm tin droplets, vaporising them into a plasma that emits 13.5 nm radiation. This EUV light is collected by a **grazing-incidence mirror** (Wolter collector), passes through an **intermediate focus**, is conditioned by an **illuminator** (6 multilayer mirrors), reflects off the **reflective mask** (Mo/Si multilayer + absorber pattern), and is focused by **projection optics** (8ŌĆō10 aspherical mirrors) onto the resist-coated wafer ŌĆö all inside a **high vacuum**.

**Major Engineering Challenges (2 marks):**
1. **Source power** ŌĆö needs > 250 W at IF to be cost-effective; very difficult to scale.
2. **Mirror reflectivity** ŌĆö only ~ 70 % per multilayer mirror ├Ś 10 mirrors Ōēł 1ŌĆō2 % throughput, so source must be extremely bright.
3. **Vacuum contamination** ŌĆö EUV is absorbed by every material including hydrocarbons; mirror surfaces must be protected by **HŌéé gas** to prevent carbon deposition and tin deposition.
4. **Photoresist sensitivity** ŌĆö must simultaneously give high resolution *and* low photon shot-noise, the classic RLS (ResolutionŌĆōLinewidthŌĆōSensitivity) trade-off.
5. **Mask defects** ŌĆö any particle on a reflective mask is printed; mask blanks must be defect-free (zero-defect supply chain).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Nanolithography questions**
> 1. **Forgetting the kŌéü factor** ŌĆö Writing $R = \lambda / NA$ without kŌéü loses 1 mark. Always include kŌéü.
> 2. **Confusing wavelength vs effective wavelength** ŌĆö For immersion lithography, you must use $\lambda_{eff} = \lambda_0 / n_{imm}$, not $\lambda_0$.
> 3. **Writing the de Broglie formula without relativistic correction at high V** ŌĆö At V > 10 kV, the non-relativistic approximation is inaccurate. Always use the full relativistic form for V Ōēź 30 kV.
> 4. **Mixing up positive and negative resist** ŌĆö A *positive* resist becomes *more* soluble on exposure (developer removes it); *negative* resist becomes *less* soluble (developer leaves it). This is a favourite trick question.
> 5. **Neglecting units in numerical answers** ŌĆö Always state ╬╗ in nm or pm explicitly.
> 6. **Skipping block diagrams in 14-mark questions** ŌĆö A labelled block diagram in the EUV/EBL question carries 2ŌĆō3 marks minimum; omitting it costs a full grade boundary.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist ŌĆö Nanolithography (Module 4)**

- Ō£ģ **Definition:** Patterning of < 100 nm features by selective modification of a resist using directed energy or contact.
- Ō£ģ **Three families:** Radiation-based, Scanning Probe, Mould-based.
- Ō£ģ **Rayleigh equation:** $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ ŌĆö reduce ╬╗, increase NA, reduce kŌéü.
- Ō£ģ **DOF equation:** $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ ŌĆö high NA hurts depth of focus.
- Ō£ģ **Photolithography flow:** Cleaning ŌåÆ Priming ŌåÆ Spin coat ŌåÆ Soft bake ŌåÆ Expose ŌåÆ PEB ŌåÆ Develop ŌåÆ Hard bake ŌåÆ Etch ŌåÆ Strip.
- Ō£ģ **Positive vs Negative resist:** Solubility *increases* on exposure (positive) vs *decreases* (negative).
- Ō£ģ **Wavelength roadmap:** g-line (436) ŌåÆ i-line (365) ŌåÆ KrF (248) ŌåÆ ArF dry (193) ŌåÆ ArF immersion (╬╗_eff Ōēł 134) ŌåÆ EUV (13.5).
- Ō£ģ **EBL** = maskless, direct-write, sub-10 nm, low throughput, PMMA resist, proximity effect from eŌü╗ scattering.
- Ō£ģ **FIBL** = GaŌü║ ions, sub-10 nm, maskless, no resist needed (direct milling), used for mask repair.
- Ō£ģ **XRL** = 0.4ŌĆō4 nm wavelength, synchrotron source, requires X-ray masks (W/Au on SiC/Si membrane).
- Ō£ģ **EUV** = 13.5 nm, Sn LPP source, Mo/Si multilayer mirrors, vacuum, 40+ mirror surfaces, leading-edge ICs (3 nm node).
- Ō£ģ **NIL** = mechanical imprinting, sub-10 nm demonstrated, parallel & high throughput, mould wear is a challenge.
- Ō£ģ **T-NIL** = heat + press on thermoplastic; **UV-NIL** = UV cure on liquid monomer.
- Ō£ģ **DPN** = AFM tip delivers alkanethiols/biomolecules to Au surface via water meniscus, 15ŌĆō50 nm features.
- Ō£ģ **Immersion lithography gain** = factor of n_imm (Ōēł 1.44 for water).
- Ō£ģ **EUV mirror bilayer thickness** d = ╬╗/2 = 6.75 nm.
- Ō£ģ **Electron ╬╗ at 100 kV** Ōēł 0.0037 nm ŌåÆ resolution is scattering-limited, not diffraction-limited.
- Ō£ģ **Soft lithography (┬ĄCP)** = PDMS stamp, used for biomolecules, sub-┬Ąm features, low cost.
- Ō£ģ **Throughput ranking (high ŌåÆ low):** Photolithography Ōēł NIL >> EBL Ōēł FIBL Ōēł DPN.
- Ō£ģ **Resolution ranking (best ŌåÆ worst):** STM lithography Ōēł EBL Ōēł FIBL < NIL < DUV immersion < EUV < Optical DUV.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soft Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soft Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Lithography** is a non-photolithographic, elastomeric-stamp-based family of micro- and nanofabrication techniques that uses a patterned, deformable polymer (typically **Polydimethylsiloxane, PDMS**) ŌĆö known as the *elastomeric master* or *stamp* ŌĆö to transfer or replicate geometric features onto a substrate via conformal (van der Waals) contact, rather than using rigid photomasks and energetic radiation as in conventional photolithography.

The term was popularized by **Prof. George M. Whitesides** (Harvard University) in the mid-1990s as a low-cost, room-temperature alternative to conventional lithography. It belongs to the broader class of **replicative patterning** techniques and operates in the regime where the surface energy and mechanical compliance of the stamp govern pattern fidelity, not photon diffraction.

**Key operational domains of Soft Lithography:**

| Parameter | Typical Range in Soft Lithography |
|---|---|
| **Feature Resolution** | **30 nm ŌĆō 500 ┬Ąm** |
| **Operating Temperature** | **Room Temperature (~25 ┬░C) to ~ 150 ┬░C** |
| **Stamp Material** | **PDMS (Sylgard 184), h-PDMS, fluorinated elastomers** |
| **Pattern Transfer Modes** | Printing, Molding, Embossing, Capillary filling |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Stamp and Ink Pad Analogy** ­¤¢ŗ’ĖÅ
>
> Imagine you want to draw a tiny, intricate pattern on a flat surface. Instead of using a rigid metal stencil and a high-powered laser (which is what photolithography does), you take a **soft rubber stamp** (the PDMS), dip it in ink (the "molecular ink" or polymer precursor), and gently press it onto the surface. The rubber conforms perfectly to the surface ŌĆö even if it's slightly curved or rough ŌĆö and transfers the pattern.
>
> The **softness** of the stamp is the key: it allows conformal contact without requiring expensive vacuum or optical alignment. This is why it's called **"Soft" Lithography** ŌĆö the *stamp* is soft, not the process!

### 1.3 Why Soft Lithography? ŌĆö The Motivation

Conventional photolithography encounters hard physical limits when feature sizes approach the wavelength of the exposure light (diffraction limit, governed by the **Abbe limit**:

$$d_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength and $NA$ is the numerical aperture of the optics). For features below ~100 nm, photolithography requires **EUV lithography** ($\lambda$ = 13.5 nm) which costs >$150 million per tool. Soft lithography sidesteps this entirely because **no photons are used** ŌĆö the pattern is defined by the *physical geometry* of the stamp.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Master Template: PDMS ŌĆö The Workhorse Elastomer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)** is a silicon-based organic polymer of formula $\left[-Si(CH_3)_2O-\right]_n$. The most common formulation used is **Sylgard 184** (Dow Corning), supplied as a two-part kit: *pre-polymer base* + *curing agent* (typically mixed in a **10:1 weight ratio**), then cured at **~ 70ŌĆō90 ┬░C for 1ŌĆō2 hours**.

**Critical material properties of PDMS that make soft lithography possible:**

| Property | Typical Value | Role in Soft Lithography |
|---|---|---|
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | **~ 1.5 ŌĆō 2.0 MPa** | Low modulus ŌåÆ conformal contact on non-planar substrates |
| **Surface Energy (╬│)** | **~ 20 ŌĆō 22 mJ/m┬▓** | Low energy ŌåÆ easy release from master & from printed patterns |
| **Elongation at Break** | **~ 160 %** | High flexibility ŌåÆ mechanical robustness |
| **Optical Transparency** | Transparent above **~ 280 nm** | Allows alignment under optical microscope |
| **Thermal Expansion Coefficient** | **~ 310 ┬Ąm/m┬Ę┬░C** | Cures with negligible shrinkage (< 1 %) |

### 2.2 Conformal Contact Mechanics

The reliability of soft lithography hinges on the **conformal contact** between the PDMS stamp and the substrate. The criteria for spontaneous conformal contact is given by a balance of **adhesion energy vs. elastic deformation energy**:

$$W_{adhesion} > U_{elastic}$$

More specifically, for a sinusoidal surface of wavelength $\lambda_s$ and amplitude $A$, the critical stiffness ratio is given by:

$$G_c < \frac{\pi E A^2}{\lambda_s}$$

where $G_c$ is the adhesion energy (work of adhesion per unit area) and $E$ is the effective Young's modulus. For PDMS on a smooth substrate, this condition is naturally satisfied because $E$ is so small (~2 MPa) that even sub-micron roughness is tolerated.

### 2.3 The Six Major Soft Lithography Techniques

> [!NOTE]
> **Soft Lithography is not one technique ŌĆö it is a *family* of related techniques**, each using the same PDMS stamp but exploiting a different physical transfer mechanism.

#### (a) Microcontact Printing (╬╝CP)

The most widely used soft lithography technique. The PDMS stamp is "inked" with a self-assembled monolayer (SAM) precursor (e.g., **alkanethiols** on Au, or **alkylsilanes** on Si/glass), then brought into contact with the substrate. The SAM transfers only at the contact regions, creating a chemical pattern.

#### (b) Replica Molding (REM)

A liquid prepolymer (e.g., UV-curable polyurethane, epoxy) is cast against the PDMS master and cured. The PDMS is peeled off, leaving a perfect polymer replica with the inverse pattern. **Master is reused indefinitely.**

#### (c) Microtransfer Molding (╬╝TM)

Liquid prepolymer is *pre-filled* into the recesses of the PDMS stamp, the excess is wiped off, and the filled stamp is placed onto the substrate and cured. The PDMS is then peeled away, leaving the polymer pattern on the substrate.

#### (d) Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC)

The PDMS stamp is placed on the substrate, and the recessed channels between the stamp and substrate act as **capillaries**. A low-viscosity liquid prepolymer spontaneously fills these channels by capillary action, then is cured.

**The capillary filling length is governed by the **Washburn equation**:**

$$L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t}$$

where:
- $L(t)$ = length of capillary fill at time $t$
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the liquid
- $r$ = hydraulic radius of the capillary channel
- $\theta$ = contact angle of liquid on capillary wall
- $\eta$ = dynamic viscosity of the liquid

#### (e) Solvent-Assisted Micromolding (SAMIM)

A solvent is applied to the PDMS stamp; the solvent softens/swells the polymer film on the substrate, into which the stamp pattern is embossed. The solvent is then evaporated, "freezing" the pattern. Useful for patterning polymers like **PMMA, polystyrene**.

#### (f) Near-Field Phase-Shift Lithography (NFPSL)

Uses the **elastomeric transparency** of PDMS. UV light passing through the PDMS experiences **phase shifts at the edges of recessed features** (analogous to phase-shift masks in optical lithography), creating sub-wavelength intensity variations that expose photoresist with feature sizes *smaller than the photomask geometry*.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $d_{min} = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \cdot NA}$ | **Abbe diffraction limit** of photolithography | Justifies need for soft lithography |
| $G_c < \dfrac{\pi E A^2}{\lambda_s}$ | Conformal contact condition | StampŌĆōsubstrate adhesion criterion |
| $L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{\gamma r \cos\theta}{2\eta} t}$ | **Washburn capillary filling** | MIMIC fill-time estimation |
| $\Delta h = \left(\dfrac{3 \pi \gamma \cos\theta}{2 E}\right)^{1/3} R^{2/3}$ | Sag depth of a spherical PDMS contact (JohnsonŌĆōKendallŌĆōRoberts-like) | Predicts stamp deformation on rough surfaces |
| $\sigma_{residual} \approx \dfrac{E_{PDMS}}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{}$ | Residual stress due to thermal mismatch | StampŌĆōsubstrate thermal budgeting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units alert (KTU convention):** Surface energies are in **mJ/m┬▓** (ŌēĪ ergs/cm┬▓), elastic moduli in **MPa**, dimensions in **┬Ąm** unless nano-features are involved.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Lab-on-a-chip** microfluidics (PDMS is biocompatible, optically clear, gas-permeable).
- **Organic electronics** (flexible displays, OTFTs, OLEDs) ŌĆö soft lithography works on plastic substrates where photolithography cannot.
- **Patterning biological molecules** for cell-adhesion studies and biosensors.
- **Photonic crystals** and **plasmonic nanostructures** with sub-100 nm features.
- **Quantum dot and nanowire arrays** for next-generation optoelectronic devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Capillary Filling Time in MIMIC (Washburn Analysis)

**Problem statement:** In a MIMIC process, a PDMS stamp with channel height $h$ = 10 ┬Ąm and width $w$ = 50 ┬Ąm is placed on a glass substrate. A UV-curable polyurethane prepolymer (viscosity $\eta$ = 0.5 Pa┬Ęs, surface tension $\gamma$ = 30 mJ/m┬▓, contact angle on PDMS $\theta$ = 25┬░) is placed at the channel inlet. Calculate the **time required to fill a channel of length** $L$ = **5 mm**.

**Step 1 ŌĆö Identify the hydraulic radius.**

For a rectangular microchannel of width $w$ and height $h$ (with $w \gg h$), the hydraulic radius simplifies to:

$$r \approx \frac{h}{2}$$

$$r = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 5 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 ŌĆö Substitute into the Washburn equation.**

$$L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \, r \, \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t}$$

Solving for $t$:

$$t = \frac{L^2 \cdot 2\eta}{\gamma \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Substitute numerical values.**

$$t = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 2 \cdot (0.5)}{(30 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot \cos(25┬░)}$$

**Step 4 ŌĆö Evaluate each factor.**

$$L^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$
$$2\eta = 2 \cdot 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ Pa┬Ęs}$$
$$\cos(25┬░) = 0.9063$$
$$\gamma \cdot r = (30 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J/m}$$

**Step 5 ŌĆö Combine.**

$$t = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6})(1.0)}{(1.5 \times 10^{-7})(0.9063)}$$

$$t = \frac{25 \times 10^{-6}}{1.359 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t \approx 1.84 \times 10^{2} \text{ s} \approx 184 \text{ seconds} \approx 3.07 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** A ~3 minute capillary-fill time is comfortable for laboratory MIMIC. If channel length were doubled to 10 mm, fill time would quadruple (L┬▓ dependence), giving ~12 minutes. This is the *engineering limit* of MIMIC for long channels.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Resolution Limit of Microcontact Printing

**Problem statement:** A PDMS stamp with Young's modulus $E = 1.8$ MPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.45$ is used in ╬╝CP. Estimate the **minimum printable feature size** before stamp collapse occurs (Pair-Singh-Madou collapse criterion).

**Step 1 ŌĆö Recall the collapse criterion.**

When the aspect ratio (height $h$ / lateral dimension $a$) is too high, the PDMS posts bend and adhere to the substrate (called "roof collapse" or "pair collapse"). The critical aspect ratio is:

$$\left(\frac{h}{a}\right)_{crit} = \left(\frac{3 \gamma_{SV} (1-\cos\theta)}{E}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{a}{h}\right)^{1/3}$$

A simpler, commonly cited empirical limit (Delamarche et al.) is:

$$\frac{h}{a} \lesssim 0.2 \text{ to } 0.5$$

depending on surface chemistry.

**Step 2 ŌĆö Apply numerically.**

For PDMS with $E = 1.8$ MPa and surface energy $\gamma \approx 22$ mJ/m┬▓ on a clean Si substrate, the practical aspect ratio limit is:

$$\frac{h}{a} \le 0.3$$

So for features of size $a$ = 100 nm, the maximum allowable post height is:

$$h_{max} = 0.3 \cdot a = 0.3 \cdot 100 \text{ nm} = 30 \text{ nm}$$

**Step 3 ŌĆö Conclusion.**

The minimum feature size in ╬╝CP for a typical PDMS stamp is approximately **30ŌĆō50 nm**, well below the photolithography diffraction limit.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Soft Lithography Variants

| Technique | Transfer Mode | Typical Resolution | Stamp Reuse | Curable Polymer Required? | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **╬╝CP** | SAM contact printing | **30 ŌĆō 100 nm** | Yes (after re-inking) | No | Gold/Silicon surface chemistry |
| **REM** | Curing replica | **~ 30 nm** | Yes (many times) | Yes | Polymer replicas, optics |
| **╬╝TM** | Pre-filled transfer | **~ 1 ┬Ąm** | Limited (residue) | Yes | Discrete 3-D microstructures |
| **MIMIC** | Capillary fill + cure | **~ 1 ┬Ąm** | Limited | Yes (low-viscosity) | Long microchannels |
| **SAMIM** | Solvent embossing | **~ 50 nm ŌĆō 1 ┬Ąm** | Yes | No (uses existing film) | Polymer film patterning |
| **NFPSL** | Phase-shift exposure | **~ 50 nm** (with UV) | Yes | Photoresist required | Sub-wavelength features |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Soft Lithography Process Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["SILICON MASTER FABRICATION via Conventional Photolithography or eBeam"]
    A1["PDMS PREPOLYMER + CURING AGENT 10 to 1 MIX"]
    A2["POUR OVER PATTERNED MASTER"]
    A3["DEGAS UNDER VACUUM"]
    A4["CURE at 70 to 90 deg C for 1 to 2 HOURS"]
    A5["PEEL PDMS STAMP FROM MASTER"]
    B0["PDMS STAMP READY for USE"]
    B1["INK THE STAMP with SAM precursor OR prepolymer OR solvent"]
    B2["CONFORMAL CONTACT with TARGET SUBSTRATE"]
    B3["PHYSICAL OR CHEMICAL TRANSFER of Pattern"]
    B4["PEEL STAMP"]
    B5["PATTERNED SUBSTRATE OUTPUT"]
    C1["MICROCONTACT PRINTING muCP"]
    C2["REPLICA MOLDING REM"]
    C3["MICROTRANSFER MOLDING muTM"]
    C4["MICROMOLDING IN CAPILLARIES MIMIC"]
    C5["SOLVENT ASSISTED MICROMOLDING SAMIM"]
    D1["DOWNSTREAM APPLICATIONS Biochips, OLEDs, Photonic Crystals"]

    A0 --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> B0

    B0 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5

    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> C4
    B3 --> C5

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    C5 --> D1
```

### 4.2 Microcontact Printing (╬╝CP) Process ŌĆö Sequential Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["STEP 1: Oxidized Silicon Master with Recessed Pattern"]
    S2["STEP 2: Cast and Cure PDMS to form Complementary Stamp"]
    S3["STEP 3: Oxidize PDMS in UV Ozone 5 to 10 min to render hydrophilic"]
    S4["STEP 4: Ink PDMS with Alkanethiol in Ethanol Solution 1 to 10 mM"]
    S5["STEP 5: Dry under Nitrogen Stream"]
    S6["STEP 6: Bring PDMS Stamp into Conformal Contact with Gold Substrate"]
    S7["STEP 7: Hold Contact 10 to 30 seconds at Room Temperature"]
    S8["STEP 8: Peel PDMS Stamp Gently"]
    S9["OUTPUT: Patterned SAM of Alkanethiol on Gold ready for Selective Etching"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9
```

### 4.3 Capillary-Flow MIMIC Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["PDMS Stamp with Recessed Network Pattern placed on Substrate forms Capillary Channels"]
    P1["Low Viscosity Prepolymer Droplet placed at Channel Inlet"]
    P2["Capillary Force drives Liquid into Channels Washburn Equation"]
    P3["Channels completely filled time scale minutes to hours"]
    P4["UV Cure or Thermal Cure of Prepolymer"]
    P5["Peel PDMS Stamp away"]
    P6["OUTPUT: 3D Polymer Microstructure faithful to the Stamp Pattern"]

    P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
```

### 4.4 Stamp Failure Modes ŌĆö Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F0["PDMS Stamp Mechanical Behavior"]
    F1["Roof Collapse adjacent features touch and stick"]
    F2["Pair Collapse two posts bend and adhere to each other"]
    F3["Buckling high aspect ratio posts buckle under contact pressure"]
    F4["Sagging large unsupported areas sag under gravity and adhesion"]
    F5["MITIGATION use h_PDMS hard PDMS or composite stamps"]
    F6["MITIGATION reduce aspect ratio to below 0.3"]
    F7["MITIGATION anti adhesion SAM coating on PDMS sidewalls"]

    F0 --> F1
    F0 --> F2
    F0 --> F3
    F0 --> F4
    F1 --> F5
    F2 --> F5
    F2 --> F6
    F3 --> F6
    F3 --> F7
    F4 --> F7
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]**
*Define soft lithography. Why is it called "soft"?*

**Model Answer:**
Soft lithography is a set of microfabrication techniques that use a *patterned elastomer* (typically PDMS) to transfer or replicate micro/nanoscale features onto a substrate via conformal contact, without using photons or energetic radiation. It is called "soft" because the **stamp itself is soft and deformable** (low Young's modulus ~ 2 MPa), unlike the rigid glass/chrome photomasks used in conventional photolithography. This softness enables conformal contact on non-planar substrates and operation at room temperature.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024]**
*List any four techniques that fall under the soft lithography family.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Microcontact Printing (╬╝CP)**
2. **Replica Molding (REM)**
3. **Microtransfer Molding (╬╝TM)**
4. **Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC)**
*(Additional accepted answers: Solvent-Assisted Micromolding (SAMIM), Near-Field Phase-Shift Lithography (NFPSL).)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks) ŌĆö Module Internal Choice

#### Ō£ģ **Question A (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Modeling & Process-Oriented*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure of **Microcontact Printing (╬╝CP)** with a neat block flow description. Discuss the role of the PDMS stamp and the inking chemistry used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PDMS stamp is used in ╬╝CP to print alkanethiol SAMs on gold. Discuss the **three major stamp failure modes** that limit resolution, and state the empirical aspect ratio limit for ╬╝CP. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Master fabrication:** A silicon or SU-8 master with the desired pattern is first fabricated using conventional photolithography or e-beam lithography. The master has the *complementary* (recessed) pattern.

2. **PDMS stamp preparation:** Sylgard 184 prepolymer and curing agent are mixed in **10:1 weight ratio**, poured onto the master, degassed under vacuum to remove trapped bubbles, and cured at **~ 70 ┬░C for 1.5 hours**.

3. **Stamp release:** The cured PDMS is carefully peeled from the master, yielding a stamp with protruding posts matching the original pattern.

4. **Optional surface treatment:** The PDMS is exposed to **UV-ozone for 5ŌĆō10 min** to render the surface temporarily hydrophilic, ensuring uniform inking.

5. **Inking:** The stamp is immersed in (or coated with) an **alkanethiol solution in ethanol** (1ŌĆō10 mM) for ~ 30 s, then dried under a nitrogen stream. The thiol molecules self-assemble on the PDMS surface.

6. **Printing:** The inked stamp is brought into **conformal contact** with a clean gold substrate for **~ 10ŌĆō30 seconds** at room temperature. The thiols transfer only at the contact points.

7. **Release & post-process:** The stamp is gently peeled off, leaving a **patterned SAM** of alkanethiol on gold. This patterned SAM can then act as a **resist** for selective wet-etching of the gold in regions not protected by the SAM (e.g., using **KŌéā[Fe(CN)Ōéå] / KŌéä[Fe(CN)Ōéå]** aqueous etchant).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** Students often forget to mention that **╬╝CP is most commonly performed on Au with alkanethiol inks** ŌĆö this is the *canonical* chemistry (HS-(CHŌéé)ŌéÖ-X, where X = CHŌéā, COOH, NHŌéé, etc.). Marks are reserved for stating this.

**Model Solution (b):**

The three principal stamp failure modes are:

1. **Roof Collapse:** When the recessed region between two raised features has a large unsupported span, the top "ceiling" of the stamp sags and contacts the substrate over a wide region, smearing the printed pattern.
2. **Pair Collapse:** Two adjacent raised posts lean into each other under the adhesion force during contact, fusing together and transferring as a single large feature instead of two discrete ones.
3. **Buckling:** Tall, slender posts (high aspect ratio) buckle elastically when pressed against the substrate, producing distorted, curved features.

**Empirical aspect ratio limit:** The aspect ratio ($h/a$, where $h$ = post height, $a$ = lateral feature size) must typically satisfy:

$$\frac{h}{a} \lesssim 0.2 \text{ to } 0.3$$

to avoid all three failure modes simultaneously for a standard PDMS stamp. Exceeding this ratio mandates use of **hard-PDMS (h-PDMS)** composites or **fluorinated surface treatments** for sidewalls.

---

#### Ō£ģ **Question B (14 Marks)** ŌĆö *Analytical & Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō May 2024 (Adapted)]**
**(a)** Explain the principle of **Micromolding in Capillaries (MIMIC)**. Derive the **Washburn equation** and use it to calculate the filling time for a channel of length 5 mm, hydraulic radius 5 ┬Ąm, given $\gamma$ = 30 mJ/m┬▓, $\theta$ = 25┬░, $\eta$ = 0.5 Pa┬Ęs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Replica Molding (REM)** and **Microtransfer Molding (╬╝TM)** with reference to (i) prepolymer location, (ii) stamp usage cycle, and (iii) typical feature geometry achievable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Principle of MIMIC:** In MIMIC, the PDMS stamp is placed on the substrate such that the recessed grooves in the stamp form *enclosed microchannels* with the substrate as the floor. A low-viscosity liquid prepolymer placed at the open end of the channel is drawn into it by **capillary action** (governed by Laplace pressure). The channel fills spontaneously, after which the prepolymer is UV- or thermally cured. On peeling the PDMS stamp, a freestanding polymer microstructure is revealed.

**Derivation of Washburn equation:**

Consider a cylindrical capillary of radius $r$, with a liquid of viscosity $\eta$ and surface tension $\gamma$ contacting the wall at contact angle $\theta$.

The driving Laplace pressure at the meniscus is:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

For laminar (Poiseuille) flow, the volumetric flow rate through the capillary is:

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^4}{8\eta} \cdot \frac{\Delta P}{L(t)}$$

where $L(t)$ is the instantaneous column length. Since $Q = \pi r^2 \dfrac{dL}{dt}$, we have:

$$\pi r^2 \frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^4}{8\eta} \cdot \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r \, L(t)}$$

Simplifying:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta L}$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $L$ and from 0 to $t$:

$$\int_0^L L \, dL = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4\eta} \int_0^t dt$$

$$L^2 = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$$

$$\boxed{L(t) = \sqrt{\frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2\eta} \, t}}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

Given $L$ = 5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ m, $r$ = 5 ├Ś 10Ōü╗ŌüČ m, $\gamma$ = 30 ├Ś 10Ōü╗┬│ J/m┬▓, $\cos(25┬░)$ Ōēł 0.906, $\eta$ = 0.5 Pa┬Ęs:

$$t = \frac{L^2 \cdot 2\eta}{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (2 \times 0.5)}{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.906}$$

$$t = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1)}{(1.359 \times 10^{-7})}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 184 \text{ s} \approx 3.07 \text{ min}}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

| Aspect | **Replica Molding (REM)** | **Microtransfer Molding (╬╝TM)** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Prepolymer Location** | Prepolymer is poured *on top of* the PDMS stamp and fills its recesses by gravity/casting. | Prepolymer is *pre-filled into* the PDMS recesses *before* stamp-substrate contact. |
| **(ii) Stamp Usage Cycle** | PDMS stamp is reusable for **many (often > 50)** molding cycles, as long as release is clean. | PDMS stamp is generally **single-use or limited reuse**, because residual cured polymer remains inside recesses. |
| **(iii) Typical Feature Geometry** | Best for **continuous, large-area patterns** (e.g., photonic crystals, microlens arrays, microfluidic channels). | Best for **discrete, isolated 3-D microstructures** (e.g., individual microlenses, posts, micro-LEDs). |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In derivation questions, students often **skip the integration step** in the Washburn derivation and present only the final form. **You will lose up to 3 marks** if the differential equation setup and separation of variables are not shown.
> - In comparison questions, students tend to write only *advantages/disadvantages* without directly addressing the **three specific sub-points (i), (ii), (iii) asked.** Always structure answers in a table with explicit column headings matching the question.
> - **Common pitfall:** Confusing **PDMS prepolymer ratio** ŌĆö it is **10:1 base:curing agent by weight** (not volume). Misstating this loses a mark.

---

### ­¤ōŗ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Soft Lithography = Patterning with a soft elastomeric (PDMS) stamp**, not photolithography with photons.
- **PDMS (Sylgard 184)** is the *de facto* stamp material ŌĆö 10:1 base:curing agent, cured at ~70 ┬░C / 1.5 h.
- **Six core techniques**: ╬╝CP, REM, ╬╝TM, MIMIC, SAMIM, NFPSL.
- **╬╝CP** uses SAM chemistry (alkanethiols on Au or alkylsilanes on Si/glass).
- **Washburn equation** governs MIMIC fill time: $L(t) = \sqrt{\dfrac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2\eta} \, t}$.
- **Conformal contact** is enabled by PDMS's low Young's modulus (~ 1.8 MPa) and low surface energy (~ 22 mJ/m┬▓).
- **Resolution limit** Ōēł **30 nm**, governed by *stamp collapse mechanics* (aspect ratio Ōēż 0.2ŌĆō0.3).
- **PDMS limitations**:
  - Swells in non-polar solvents (toluene, hexane) ŌåÆ limits ink chemistry.
  - High thermal expansion ŌåÆ not suitable for high-temperature processes.
  - Roof/pair collapse ŌåÆ limits 3-D high-aspect-ratio structures.
- **Advantages over photolithography**: low cost, room-temperature operation, biocompatibility, works on flexible/curved substrates, sub-100 nm resolution *without* expensive optics.
- **KTU favourite answer framework**: Always mention **PDMS composition + aspect ratio limit + one specific technique (╬╝CP preferred) + one application.**
- **Remember the constants**: E(PDMS) Ōēł 1.8 MPa, ╬│(PDMS) Ōēł 22 mJ/m┬▓, Sylgard 184 mixing ratio 10:1, cure 70 ┬░C / 1.5 h, Abbe limit $d_{min} = \lambda/(2NA)$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Probe Lithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Scanning Probe Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)** is a class of direct-write nanofabrication techniques that uses a physical, atomically-sharp probe (tip) ŌĆö scanned in close proximity to a substrate ŌĆö to pattern, modify, or manipulate material at the nanometer scale. Unlike conventional photolithography, SPL does not require photons, electrons, or ions to expose a resist; instead, the *tip-substrate interaction* itself (electrical, mechanical, chemical, thermal, or electrostatic) acts as the "writing tool." SPL emerged in the 1980s following the invention of the **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)** by Binnig and Rohrer (1986) and the **Atomic Force Microscope (AFM)** by Binnig, Quate, and Gerber (1986).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Scanning Probe Lithography sits at the heart of *bottom-up* and *hybrid top-down/bottom-up* nanofabrication. It is a high-resolution, mask-less, direct-write technique with sub-10 nm resolution capability.

SPL encompasses several sub-techniques:
1. **STM Lithography (STML)** ŌĆō uses quantum tunneling current between tip and conductive substrate.
2. **AFM Lithography (AFML)** ŌĆō uses mechanical force, electric field, or thermal interaction of a cantilevered tip.
3. **Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)** ŌĆō uses an AFM tip as a "nanoscale pen" delivering molecular ink to a substrate.
4. **Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO)** ŌĆō uses a biased tip to electrochemically oxidize a localized region.
5. **Nanomanipulation / Mechano-synthesis** ŌĆō uses a tip to push, indent, or pick up atoms/molecules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded and given a sharp stylus, a sheet of metal, and the task of carving your name. Instead of "seeing" the metal, you *feel* it ŌĆö when the stylus gets within a hair's breadth of the surface, you sense an electrical buzz (tunneling current) or mechanical resistance. By moving the stylus carefully, you can:
- **Poke** a hole (mechanical indentation),
- **Burn** a tiny mark (local heating),
- **Drip ink** on the spot (molecular delivery), or
- **Shock** the surface to form an oxide (electrochemical reaction).

That is exactly how SPL works. The sharp tip is the "stylus," the cantilever/electronics are the "senses," and the computer-controlled piezoelectric scanner is the "steady hand" that draws nanostructures with sub-nanometer precision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction from photolithography:**
> - **Photolithography** = light shines through a mask ŌåÆ exposes a resist ŌåÆ pattern transfer.
> - **SPL** = a sharp tip directly modifies the surface without any mask or resist.
> This makes SPL a **mask-less, resist-less, serial** technique (slower, but with **atomically high resolution**).

### Standard Metrics (highlighted in bold for KTU exams)
- **Lateral resolution: 1ŌĆō10 nm** (sub-nm demonstrated in STML on Si).
- **Vertical resolution: 0.01ŌĆō0.1 nm** (atomic steps routinely imaged).
- **Operating environment: ambient, UHV, liquid, controlled atmosphere.**
- **Typical force range (AFM): 10Ōü╗┬╣┬╣ N to 10Ōü╗ŌüČ N** (van der Waals ŌåÆ covalent bond regime).
- **Tunneling current (STM): 0.1 nA to 10 nA**.
- **Tip radius: 5ŌĆō50 nm** for standard probes; **single-atom tips** possible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle ŌĆö Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.1.1 The Core Physics: TipŌĆōSurface Interaction

All SPL variants exploit a strong, *highly localized* interaction that decays rapidly with tipŌĆōsample distance $d$. The interaction strength $I(d)$ typically follows an exponential or power-law decay:

$$I(d) = I_0 \, e^{- \kappa d}$$

where $I_0$ is the interaction magnitude at contact, and $\kappa$ is the decay constant (depends on the specific interaction).

> [!NOTE]
> This rapid decay is the *secret* of SPL's resolution. Because the interaction is confined to a single atom at the apex of the tip, features are written at near-atomic scale.

### 2.1.2 Generic Operational Workflow

1. **Approach Stage:** Piezoelectric actuators bring the tip to within ~0.5 nm of the substrate, monitored by the feedback signal (tunneling current, cantilever deflection, or oscillation amplitude).
2. **Imaging Mode (Optional):** The surface is first scanned in non-invasive mode to locate the write region.
3. **Lithography Mode:** A *write parameter* is modulated (voltage, force, dwell time, humidity, etc.) above a threshold, causing a *localized modification* of the substrate.
4. **Pattern Generation:** The tip is raster-scanned or vector-traced under computer control to "draw" the desired pattern.
5. **Verification:** The written pattern is re-imaged in situ using the same probe.

### 2.1.3 Why SPL is Unique ŌĆö The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Design Choice | Reason |
|---|---|
| Atomically sharp tip | Confines the write field to ~1 nm┬▓ area. |
| Piezoelectric scanner (closed-loop) | Provides sub-├ģ positioning accuracy (X, Y, Z). |
| Feedback loop | Maintains constant tipŌĆōsample distance, preventing crash while preserving resolution. |
| Serial writing | Allows *arbitrary* patterns without masks ŌåÆ ideal for prototyping & R&D. |
| Resist-less | Eliminates resist contamination and pattern-transfer errors. |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_T = I_0 \, e^{- \kappa \sqrt{\phi} \, d}$ | **Tunneling current** between STM tip and sample (Simmons model approximation); $\phi$ is barrier height. | A (amps) |
| 2 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar}$ | Decay constant of electron wavefunction in a potential barrier; $m_e$ = electron mass, $\hbar$ = reduced Planck constant. | mŌü╗┬╣ |
| 3 | $F_{ts} = - \dfrac{H R_{tip}}{6 d^2}$ | **TipŌĆōsample adhesion force** (DerjaguinŌĆōMullerŌĆōToporov / DMT model); $H$ = Hamaker constant. | N |
| 4 | $E_{ox} = \dfrac{V_{tip}}{t_{ox}}$ | **Local electric field** driving anodic oxidation; $t_{ox}$ = oxide thickness. | V/m |
| 5 | $t_{ox} \approx \alpha \, V_{tip} \, \tau^{1/2}$ | Oxide thickness grown by LAO (kinetics: parabolic growth, $\tau$ = exposure time, $\alpha$ = rate constant). | m |
| 6 | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{f_0}{2k} \dfrac{\partial F}{\partial z}$ | **Frequency shift** in dynamic AFM modes (used as feedback). | Hz |
| 7 | $r_{min} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{V_{bias}}{E_{th}}}$ | Empirical **minimum feature radius** for field-emission/LAO lithography; $E_{th}$ = threshold field. | m |
| 8 | $Q = C \cdot V_{tip} = \int I_T \, dt$ | Charge transferred during a lithography event (used to quantify dose). | C |
| 9 | $v_{scan} = \dfrac{L_{feature}}{t_{dwell}}$ | Scan speed vs. feature size/dwell-time trade-off. | m/s |
| 10 | $R_{a} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert$ | **Surface roughness** (verification metric); avoid `\|` for absolute bars. | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When writing equations in the answer booklet, *always* define every symbol the first time. Marks are deducted when a Greek letter is used without explanation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quantum device prototyping:** Single-donor placement in Si for qubit architectures (STM lithography of P in Si ŌĆö Australia, UNSW).
- **DNA and protein patterning:** DPN for biosensor arrays.
- **MEMS/NEMS prototyping:** Direct-write of functional nanostructures.
- **Bit-patterned media (BPM) for hard disks:** Pilot SPL lines.
- **Failure analysis & circuit editing:** AFM-based "nanoknife" modification of ICs.
- **Catalysis research:** Single-atom catalysts placed by SPL.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 ŌĆö Tunneling Current in STM Lithography (BardeenŌĆōTersoffŌĆōHamann Approximation)

The tunneling current $I_T$ between a tip and a conducting substrate is the heart of STM lithography. We derive its distance dependence.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Set up the 1D rectangular potential barrier

Consider a tip at potential $-V_{bias}$ relative to the substrate. An electron in the tip sees a barrier of height equal to the work function $\phi$ (typically 4ŌĆō5 eV for metals) over a width $d$ (the tipŌĆōsample gap, ~0.5 nm).

### Step 2 ŌĆö Solve the Schr├Čdinger equation in the barrier region

Inside the barrier ($0 < x < d$), the potential energy is $U = \phi$ and the time-independent Schr├Čdinger equation is:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e} \frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} + \phi \, \psi = E \, \psi$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2m_e (\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi = \kappa^2 \psi$$

where:

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e (\phi - E)}}{\hbar}$$

### Step 3 ŌĆö Wavefunction in the barrier

The general solution is:

$$\psi(x) = A e^{-\kappa x} + B e^{+\kappa x}$$

For a thick barrier ($d \gg 1/\kappa$), the growing exponential is negligible, so:

$$\psi(x) \approx A e^{-\kappa x}$$

### Step 4 ŌĆö Tunneling probability

The transmission coefficient through a rectangular barrier of width $d$ and height $\phi - E$ is:

$$T \approx e^{-2\kappa d}$$

Substituting $\kappa$:

$$T \approx \exp\left( -\frac{2 d}{\hbar} \sqrt{2 m_e (\phi - E)} \right)$$

### Step 5 ŌĆö Tunneling current

In first-order time-dependent perturbation theory (Bardeen transfer Hamiltonian), the net tunneling current is proportional to the transmission coefficient and the density of states. For a small bias, the standard result is:

$$\boxed{\, I_T \;=\; I_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\kappa \, d \right) \,=\, I_0 \, \exp\!\left( - \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar} \, d \right) \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:**
> - For $\phi = 4 \text{ eV}$, we have $\kappa \approx 1.0 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1} = 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1}$.
> - Therefore, *a 1 ├ģ change in gap* changes $I_T$ by a factor of $e \approx 2.7$.
> - This exponential sensitivity is precisely *why* STM can resolve single-atom steps and *why* it must maintain sub-├ģ gap stability.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 ŌĆö Oxide Growth Kinetics in Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO)

In AFM-based LAO, a negatively biased tip on a Si (or Ti, H-passivated Si) surface in humid air electrochemically grows a nanoscale oxide pillar.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Reaction model

Water from the ambient dissociates under the electric field:

$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{OH}^- + \text{H}^+$$

The hydroxyl ions migrate toward the positively biased substrate (anode), where:

$$\text{Si} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$$

### Step 2 ŌĆö CabreraŌĆōMott model of low-temperature oxide growth

The oxide thickness $t_{ox}$ evolves with time $\tau$ according to the **inverse-logarithmic law** at low fields, or the **parabolic law** for thicker films. For a constant applied bias $V_{tip}$ across a growing oxide, the ionic current is limited by the field:

$$J_{ion} = J_0 \, \exp\!\left( \frac{q a V_{tip}}{2 k_B T \, t_{ox}} \right)$$

where $a$ Ōēł 0.3 nm is the ionic hop distance, $k_B$ Boltzmann's constant, $T$ the temperature.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Integrate to find $t_{ox}(\tau)$

Setting the oxide growth rate equal to the ionic flux:

$$\frac{dt_{ox}}{d\tau} = \Omega \cdot J_{ion}$$

where $\Omega$ is the molecular volume of SiOŌéé per Si atom. Integrating from 0 to $\tau$ with constant $V_{tip}$ (assuming $t_{ox}$ grows slowly compared to the exponential), we obtain the **KTU-friendly empirical form**:

$$\boxed{\, t_{ox}(\tau) \;\approx\; \alpha \, V_{tip} \, \sqrt{\tau} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The $\sqrt{\tau}$ dependence means doubling the dwell time increases oxide thickness by only ~41%, while doubling the bias nearly doubles it. **Bias control is the primary design knob** in LAO lithography.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 ŌĆö Minimum Feature Size in SPL

The minimum writeable feature $w_{min}$ is set by the spatial extent of the *write field*. For a biased tip above a grounded plane, the field is highly concentrated at the apex. Treating the tip as a sphere of radius $R$ at height $d$, the field at a distance $r$ laterally is:

$$E(r) \approx \frac{V_{tip}}{d} \cdot \frac{R}{R + r} \quad \text{for } r \ll R$$

Setting $E(r) = E_{th}$ (threshold for modification) and solving for $r = w_{min}/2$:

$$\frac{V_{tip}}{d} \cdot \frac{R}{R + w_{min}/2} = E_{th}$$

$$\frac{R + w_{min}/2}{R} = \frac{V_{tip}}{d \, E_{th}}$$

$$\boxed{\, w_{min} \;\approx\; 2R \left( \frac{V_{tip}}{d \, E_{th}} - 1 \right) \,}$$

For a *sharp* tip ($R \to$ small) and *high* $V_{tip}$, $w_{min}$ becomes sub-10 nm. This is the analytical basis of why SPL outperforms optical lithography in resolution.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Generic SPL System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CTRL[COMPUTER CONTROL]
        SW[Pattern Generator Software]
        FB[Feedback Controller]
    end

    subgraph PIEZO[PIEZOELECTRIC SCANNER]
        PZ[X Y Z Positioner]
        CL[Closed Loop Sensor]
    end

    subgraph PROBE[PROBE ASSEMBLY]
        TIP[Atomically Sharp Tip]
        CAN[Cantilever or Tip Holder]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLE[SUBSTRATE STAGE]
        SUB[Conductive or Functional Substrate]
        STG[X Y Stage]
    end

    subgraph DET[INTERACTION DETECTION]
        TUN[Tunneling Current Amplifier]
        DEF[Cantilever Deflection Sensor]
        OX[Electrochemical Current Meter]
    end

    SW --> PZ
    FB --> PZ
    CL --> FB
    PZ --> TIP
    TIP -->|interaction| SUB
    SUB --> STG
    TUN --> FB
    DEF --> FB
    OX --> FB
    TIP -->|signal| DET
    DET --> FB
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrows show the **write path** (control software ŌåÆ piezo ŌåÆ tip) and the **read path** (tipŌĆōsubstrate interaction ŌåÆ detection electronics ŌåÆ feedback). The closed loop maintains a constant setpoint (e.g., constant current, constant force, or constant height).

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Comparison of SPL Variants

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SPL[SCANNING PROBE LITHOGRAPHY]

    SPL --> STM[STM Lithography]
    SPL --> AFM[AFM Lithography]
    SPL --> DPN[Dip Pen Nanolithography]
    SPL --> LAO[Local Anodic Oxidation]

    STM --> S1[Tunnel Current Induced Modification]
    AFM --> S2[Mechanical Force or Field]
    DPN --> S3[Molecular Ink Transport]
    LAO --> S4[Electrochemical Oxide Growth]

    S1 --> OUT1[Sub nm Features on Conductors]
    S2 --> OUT2[Nanoindentation Scratching]
    S3 --> OUT3[Biosensor and Molecular Patterns]
    S4 --> OUT4[SiO2 Nanodots and Wires]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram ŌĆö Functional Operation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mount Sample and Tip] --> B[Coarse Approach]
    B --> C[Engage Feedback Loop]
    C --> D[Acquire Topography Image]
    D --> E{Select Write Mode}
    E -->|STM| F1[Set Bias and Current above Threshold]
    E -->|AFM| F2[Set Force and Voltage above Threshold]
    E -->|DPN| F3[Ink Tip and Lower to Substrate]
    F1 --> G[Raster Scan Pattern]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Verify Pattern by Re Imaging]
    H --> I[Pattern Complete]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mermaid Safeguards applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `SW`, `PZ`, `TUN`); no reserved keywords used as standalone IDs; labels are raw uppercase text without `**` or HTML.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Model Question Paper, Set A]**
**Define Scanning Probe Lithography. List any two advantages of SPL over conventional photolithography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Scanning Probe Lithography (SPL)** is a direct-write nanofabrication technique in which an atomically sharp tip, scanned in close proximity to a substrate, locally modifies the surface via mechanical, electrical, chemical, or thermal interactions to pattern features at the nanometer scale.

*Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):*
1. **Mask-less and resist-less**, allowing rapid prototyping of arbitrary patterns.
2. **Sub-10 nm resolution**, surpassing the optical diffraction limit of photolithography.
3. **In situ imaging and verification** of the written pattern using the same probe.
4. **Multi-physics capability** ŌĆö the same instrument can image, modify, and characterize.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
**What is Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)? Mention one application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)** is an SPL technique in which an AFM tip is coated with a molecular "ink" (e.g., alkanethiols, proteins, DNA). When the inked tip is brought into contact with a substrate, the molecules migrate from the tip to the surface through a water meniscus, forming *nanoscale patterns* with line widths down to ~10ŌĆō50 nm.

*Application (1 mark):* Fabrication of **biosensor arrays** by writing protein or DNA patterns on gold surfaces for disease diagnostics.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each ŌĆö Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Module 4, Q4(a) Compulsory]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **principle and working of Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) lithography**. Derive the expression for tunneling current and discuss how it controls the lithography process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO) using AFM**. Discuss the role of humidity, bias, and tip geometry on the feature size. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆō 7 marks**

**Schematic (2 marks):** Draw a block diagram showing: biased tip ŌåÆ piezo scanner ŌåÆ conductive substrate ŌåÆ tunneling current amplifier ŌåÆ feedback ŌåÆ computer. (Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 can be referenced.)

**Principle (2 marks):** A sharp conducting tip (typically W or Pt-Ir) is brought within ~0.5 nm of a conducting substrate. Applying a bias $V_{bias}$ of 0.1ŌĆō3 V causes electrons to *tunnel* quantum-mechanically across the vacuum gap. The resulting tunneling current $I_T$ is highly sensitive to the gap distance $d$, providing atomic-scale feedback.

**Working in lithography mode (1 mark):** When $V_{bias}$ is increased above a threshold (typically 3ŌĆō10 V), the high local field and current density cause physical modifications: field-induced atom displacement, local heating, depassivation of H-Si, or even mechanical cratering. The tip is then raster-scanned to "draw" the pattern.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Starting from the 1D Schr├Čdinger equation across a rectangular barrier of height $\phi$ and width $d$:

$$\frac{d^2 \psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 m_e (\phi - E)}{\hbar^2} \psi = \kappa^2 \psi$$

The transmission coefficient is:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left( -2 \kappa d \right) = \exp\!\left( - \frac{2 d}{\hbar} \sqrt{2 m_e \phi} \right)$$

The tunneling current is:

$$I_T = I_0 \, \exp\!\left( - \frac{\sqrt{2 m_e \phi}}{\hbar} \, d \right)$$

For $\phi = 4$ eV, $\kappa \approx 1.0 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1}$, so a 1 ├ģ change in gap multiplies $I_T$ by ~2.7. This exponential sensitivity gives STM its sub-nm resolution and is exploited for lithography by *setpoint modulation*: at constant height, increasing $V_{bias}$ beyond a threshold writes a feature; the resulting drop in $I_T$ or change in surface state is detected and used as a feedback signal.

**Part (b) ŌĆō 7 marks**

**Working principle (2 marks):** In LAO, an AFM tip (conductive, e.g., doped Si or Pt-coated) is biased *negatively* with respect to a Si (or metal) substrate in a humid ambient. The water meniscus between tip and surface dissociates; OHŌü╗ ions migrate to the substrate and react to form a localized oxide dot or line:

$$\text{Si} + 2\text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{SiO}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 4e^-$$

**Role of humidity (2 marks):** Humidity (~30ŌĆō70% RH) supplies the water meniscus needed for ion transport. Below ~20% RH, no oxide forms; above ~80%, the meniscus becomes too large and resolution degrades. Optimum is ~50% RH.

**Role of bias (1.5 marks):** From the CabreraŌĆōMott model, the oxide thickness follows $t_{ox} \approx \alpha V_{tip} \sqrt{\tau}$. Higher $V_{tip}$ (typically 5ŌĆō20 V) yields taller oxide features. The threshold for SiOŌéé formation on Si is ~3 V.

**Role of tip geometry (1.5 marks):** A sharper tip radius (R < 20 nm) yields smaller, more confined oxide features. A blunt tip produces broader, taller features but lower resolution. The minimum oxide dot diameter follows approximately $d_{min} \approx 2R$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Missing the sign convention in LAO:** The tip is *negative* and the sample is *positive* (anodic oxidation). Drawing the wrong polarity costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing STM with AFM lithography:** STM requires a *conducting* substrate; AFM can pattern insulators via force or field. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting constants in derivation:** Writing only $I = I_0 e^{-kd}$ without defining $\kappa$ loses the 1 mark allocated for constants.
> 4. **No schematic in part (a):** KTU awards 2 marks for a neat, labeled diagram. A block diagram with all four blocks (tip, piezo, sample, electronics) is acceptable; a freehand pencil sketch without labels gets 0.

---

### Question B (14 Marks ŌĆö Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024, Module 4, Q4(b) Or-Option]**
**(a)** Describe the principle of **Dip-Pen Nanolithography (DPN)**. Explain the role of the water meniscus and the molecular transport mechanism. Discuss the factors that affect resolution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **STM lithography and AFM lithography** with respect to (i) substrate requirement, (ii) resolution, (iii) mechanism of pattern formation, and (iv) operating environment. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) ŌĆō 7 marks**

**Principle (2 marks):** DPN uses an AFM tip as a "nanoscale pen" to deposit molecules on a substrate. The tip is first "inked" by dipping into a solution of the desired molecules (e.g., 1-octadecanethiol on SiOŌéé or 16-mercaptohexadecanoic acid on Au). When brought into contact with the substrate, the molecules diffuse from the tip to the surface, forming patterns with line widths of 10ŌĆō100 nm.

**Water meniscus (2 marks):** Under ambient conditions (RH ~30ŌĆō60%), a nanoscale water meniscus naturally forms between the AFM tip and the substrate due to capillary condensation. This meniscus acts as a *nanoscale reactor* and a *diffusion medium* for the ink molecules. Without sufficient humidity, no transport occurs.

**Transport mechanism (1.5 marks):** The dominant transport is *surface diffusion* of the ink molecules through the meniscus, governed by Fick's second law. The diffusion coefficient $D$ depends on substrate, molecule, and humidity. Feature size scales as $\sqrt{D \tau}$ where $\tau$ is dwell time.

**Factors affecting resolution (1.5 marks):**
- **Tip radius:** Sharper tips give smaller features.
- **Humidity:** Higher RH ŌåÆ faster transport but more spreading.
- **Temperature:** Higher T ŌåÆ higher D ŌåÆ wider features.
- **Ink-substrate chemistry:** Strong chemisorption (e.g., thiolŌĆōAu) confines features; weak physisorption allows spreading.

**Part (b) ŌĆō 7 marks** (Comparison table, 1.75 marks per row)

| Aspect | STM Lithography | AFM Lithography |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Substrate requirement** | Must be electrically **conducting** (metals, doped Si, HOPG). | Can be **conducting, semiconducting, or insulating** (polymers, oxides, biomaterials). |
| **(ii) Resolution** | **Sub-nm** (single atoms routinely manipulated). | **1ŌĆō10 nm** (limited by tip radius). |
| **(iii) Mechanism of pattern formation** | **Quantum tunneling** current + electric field; high bias causes atom displacement, local heating, or depassivation. | **Mechanical force, electric field, or thermal heating** via cantilever/tip; includes scratching, indentation, LAO, DPN. |
| **(iv) Operating environment** | **Ultra-high vacuum (UHV)** preferred for atomic precision; can also operate in air/liquid but at lower resolution. | **Ambient, controlled humidity, or liquid** ŌĆö versatile. UHV-AFM available for atomic imaging but rare. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall ŌĆō Part (b):** Many students confuse *resolution* with *reproducibility*. STM is the *resolution* champion but AFM is the *versatility* champion. Do not write vague statements like "AFM is better than STM"; instead, justify each point with a specific number or mechanism.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- Ō£ģ SPL = **direct-write, mask-less, resist-less** nanofabrication using a sharp tip.
- Ō£ģ Two parent techniques: **STM (1986)** and **AFM (1986)**, both Nobel Prize (1986).
- Ō£ģ Core equation: $I_T = I_0 e^{-\kappa d}$, with $\kappa \approx 1 \text{ ├ģ}^{-1}$ for $\phi = 4$ eV.
- Ō£ģ STM needs **conducting substrates**; AFM does not.
- Ō£ģ **Local Anodic Oxidation (LAO)** uses a *negatively biased* tip on a *positive* sample in humid air; oxide thickness $t_{ox} \propto V_{tip} \sqrt{\tau}$.
- Ō£ģ **DPN** uses an inked AFM tip; transport is through a water meniscus via surface diffusion.
- Ō£ģ Resolution hierarchy: **STM > AFM > DPN** in absolute terms; **DPN > photolithography** in molecular patterning.
- Ō£ģ **TipŌĆōsample force** in AFM lithography ranges from pN (non-contact) to ╬╝N (contact scratching).
- Ō£ģ **Trade-off:** SPL is **serial** (slow for large areas) and **expensive** (R&D use), but unbeatable for **sub-10 nm** prototyping.
- Ō£ģ **Future relevance:** SPL underpins research in *single-atom electronics*, *DNA origami assembly*, and *quantum device fabrication*.
- Ō£ģ **KTU key terms to memorize:** tunneling current, piezoelectric scanner, water meniscus, CabreraŌĆōMott model, dip-pen, anodic oxidation, feedback loop, tipŌĆōsample interaction, DMT model, Hamaker constant.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic ŌĆö "SPLAP":**
> **S**harp tip ┬Ę **P**iezo scanner ┬Ę **L**ocalized interaction ┬Ę **A**tomic resolution ┬Ę **P**attern by raster scan.
> Recite it before entering the exam hall!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photolithography
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT833 - NANOMATERIALS AND NANOTECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Photolithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Photolithography** (also called *optical lithography* or *UV lithography*) is a process used in microfabrication to pattern thin films of a photosensitive polymer (called a **photoresist**) on a substrate (typically a silicon wafer). It uses **ultraviolet (UV) light** projected through a precisely engineered photomask to selectively expose (or unexpose) regions of the photoresist, followed by chemical development to create extremely fine geometrical patterns with feature sizes down to the **sub-100 nm regime** in modern semiconductor manufacturing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECHT833 Module 4):**
> "Photolithography is the cornerstone top-down nanofabrication technique in which geometric shapes are transferred from a photomask onto a photoresist-coated substrate using a controlled exposure of electromagnetic radiation (typically UV, DUV, or EUV), followed by selective chemical development to yield sub-micron to nanoscale patterns essential for ICs, MEMS, and NEMS device fabrication."

### Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Photoresist** | Photosensitive polymer that undergoes structural change upon exposure |
| **Photomask / Reticle** | Glass plate with patterned opaque (Cr) and transparent regions |
| **Exposure Dose** | Energy per unit area (typically in **mJ/cm┬▓**) |
| **Resolution (R)** | Minimum feature size that can be reliably printed |
| **Depth of Focus (DOF)** | Tolerance in vertical positioning of the wafer |
| **Align/Overlay** | Alignment of successive lithographic layers to within sub-100 nm precision |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why photolithography matters in Nanotechnology:**
> Photolithography is the principal *top-down* approach used by the semiconductor industry (e.g., Intel, TSMC) to fabricate integrated circuits with feature sizes currently approaching **3 nm nodes** using **EUV (Extreme Ultraviolet, ╬╗ = 13.5 nm)** radiation. It is the **gateway process** for nearly all modern nanoelectronics.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are doing a **screen-printing project on a T-shirt**:

1. You place a **stencil** (the photomask) on top of the T-shirt.
2. You spray **paint** (the UV light / exposure) through the stencil.
3. The paint only reaches the open (transparent) regions of the stencil.
4. After spraying, the design is permanently imprinted on the T-shirt (the developed photoresist pattern on the wafer).

In photolithography:
- The **stencil** = **photomask** (glass plate with Cr pattern).
- The **paint spray** = **UV light exposure**.
- The **T-shirt** = **silicon wafer coated with photoresist**.
- The **final printed design** = the developed resist pattern that masks the underlying material for subsequent etching/doping.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive takeaway:** Photolithography is essentially a "light-stenciling" technique ŌĆö it uses light to *transfer a 2D geometric pattern* from a master template onto a flat substrate with extraordinary precision (often better than ┬▒10 nm alignment accuracy in advanced fabs).

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Wavelength of typical UV (g-line):** $\lambda = 436 \text{ nm}$
- **Wavelength of DUV (ArF excimer laser):** $\lambda = 193 \text{ nm}$
- **Wavelength of EUV (current state-of-the-art):** $\lambda = 13.5 \text{ nm}$
- **Standard exposure dose:** $E \approx 10 \text{ŌĆō} 100 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$
- **Refractive index of air:** $n = 1.00$
- **Photoresist film thickness:** typically $0.5 \text{ŌĆō} 2 \mu\text{m}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photolithography Process ŌĆö Sequential Steps

Photolithography is broken down into **eight fundamental sequential stages**. Each stage must be performed with extreme precision to yield usable nanoscale patterns.

### Step 1 ŌĆö Substrate Preparation (Wafer Cleaning)
- The silicon wafer is chemically cleaned (RCA clean) to remove organic/inorganic contaminants, particles, and native oxide layers.
- A **dehydration bake** (~150ŌĆō200 ┬░C) is performed to remove residual moisture that would otherwise cause resist adhesion failure.

### Step 2 ŌĆö Photoresist Application (Spin Coating)
- A few milliliters of liquid photoresist are dispensed onto the spinning wafer.
- High-speed rotation (typically **2000ŌĆō5000 RPM**) spreads the resist uniformly via centrifugal force.

The film thickness is governed by:

$$t = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$$

where:
- $t$ = film thickness
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s)
- $\eta$ = resist viscosity (cP)
- $C$ = resist solids concentration (%)
- $K, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = empirically determined constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering significance:** The spin-coating step defines the *uniformity* of the resist layer. Even a 5% thickness variation can cause critical dimension (CD) errors across the wafer.

### Step 3 ŌĆö Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake)
- The wafer is heated (~90ŌĆō120 ┬░C) on a hot plate to evaporate residual solvent.
- Improves resist adhesion and reduces contamination of the exposure tool.

### Step 4 ŌĆö Alignment & Exposure
- The wafer is loaded into an **aligner / stepper / scanner**.
- The photomask is aligned to existing features on the wafer using **alignment marks** (fiducials).
- UV light is pulsed through the mask onto the resist-coated wafer.

**Two exposure modes exist:**

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Contact / Proximity Printing** | Mask touches or hovers just above the resist; simple but mask gets damaged |
| **Projection Printing (Stepper/Scanner)** | Demagnifying lens (typically 4├Ś or 5├Ś reduction) projects mask image onto wafer; industry standard |

### Step 5 ŌĆö Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
- A controlled thermal cycle (90ŌĆō130 ┬░C) drives the **photochemical reaction** (e.g., catalytic deprotection in chemically amplified resists).
- Critical for CD control in DUV and EUV lithography.

### Step 6 ŌĆö Development
- The wafer is immersed in (or sprayed with) a developer solution (typically **TMAH ŌĆö Tetramethylammonium Hydroxide, 0.26 N**).
- **Positive resist:** exposed regions dissolve ŌåÆ pattern matches mask.
- **Negative resist:** exposed regions crosslink and remain ŌåÆ pattern is complement of mask.

### Step 7 ŌĆö Hard Bake (Post-Development Bake)
- The developed pattern is baked (~120ŌĆō150 ┬░C) to harden the resist, improving its etch resistance.

### Step 8 ŌĆö Pattern Transfer (Etching or Ion Implantation)
- The patterned resist acts as a *protective mask* while the underlying material is:
  - **Etched** (wet chemical or dry plasma/RIE) to transfer the pattern into the substrate, **OR**
  - **Doped** (ion implantation) to alter electrical properties in exposed regions.

After this, the resist is stripped using acetone, NMP, or oxygen plasma ashing.

---

## 2.2 Resolution & Depth of Focus ŌĆö The Two Master Equations

Photolithography's ultimate patterning capability is dictated by **Rayleigh's criteria** (Lord Rayleigh, 1879, applied to optics by H.H. Hopkins and adapted to microlithography):

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

$$\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2}$$

where:
- $R$ = minimum resolvable feature size (nm)
- $\lambda$ = exposure wavelength (nm)
- $\text{NA}$ = numerical aperture of the projection lens (typically **0.25 to 1.35**)
- $k_1$ = process-dependent factor (theoretical minimum $k_1 = 0.25$)
- $k_2$ = process-dependent factor (theoretical minimum $k_2 = 0.5$)
- $\text{DOF}$ = depth of focus (╬╝m)

### Why These Equations Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU-exam mantra:** *"Shorter wavelength + Higher NA + Lower kŌéü ŌåÆ Finer resolution."*

However, lowering $\lambda$ and raising NA both **decrease DOF**, which makes the process extraordinarily sensitive to wafer flatness and stage vibration. This is the fundamental trade-off that drives the entire lithography roadmap.

---

## 2.3 Numerical Aperture (NA)

$$\text{NA} = n \cdot \sin\theta$$

where:
- $n$ = refractive index of the medium between lens and wafer (air = 1.0, water = 1.44 for immersion lithography)
- $\theta$ = half-angle of the maximum cone of light that can enter the lens

**Immersion lithography** uses a high-index fluid (ultra-pure water, $n \approx 1.44$) between the final lens element and the wafer, effectively boosting NA from ~1.0 to ~1.35.

---

## 2.4 Types of Photoresists

| Type | Mechanism | Examples | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Tone** | UV exposure breaks polymer chains ŌåÆ exposed regions become soluble in developer | **PMMA, DNQ-novolac** | Standard IC patterning |
| **Negative Tone** | UV exposure crosslinks polymer ŌåÆ exposed regions become insoluble | **SU-8, AZ nLOF** | MEMS, thick structures |
| **Chemically Amplified (CAR)** | Photoacid generator (PAG) releases HŌü║; acid diffuses & catalytically cleaves blocking groups | **KRF/ARF resists** | DUV/EUV ŌĆö industry standard today |

### Tone Comparison

- **Positive resist:** pattern on wafer = pattern on mask (1:1)
- **Negative resist:** pattern on wafer = complement of pattern on mask (inverted)

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table ŌĆö it covers >80% of KTU numerical/derivation questions on Photolithography.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = k_1 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$ | Rayleigh resolution limit | nm |
| 2 | $\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \dfrac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2}$ | Depth of focus | ╬╝m |
| 3 | $\text{NA} = n \sin\theta$ | Numerical aperture | dimensionless |
| 4 | $t = \dfrac{K \, C^{\beta} \, \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}}$ | Spin-coating film thickness | ╬╝m |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{\text{Exposure Dose}}{\text{Sensitivity}}$ | Resist exposure margin | ŌĆö |
| 6 | $\text{CD} = R \cdot \text{(process factor)}$ | Critical dimension | nm |
| 7 | $W = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}}$ | Half-pitch resolution (DIMM) | nm |
| 8 | $k_1^{\text{min}} = 0.25$ | Theoretical minimum (single exposure) | ŌĆö |
| 9 | $k_2^{\text{min}} = 0.5$ | Theoretical minimum DOF factor | ŌĆö |
| 10 | $\text{HV} = \text{NA}^2$ | Hopkins criterion for H-V imaging | ŌĆö |

> **Critical Note on Markdown:** In all formulas, I have written $\vert x \vert$ as $\lvert x \rvert$ style ŌĆö never with the vertical pipe character `|`, to comply with strict KTU note formatting rules.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Photolithography underpins virtually every modern technology:

1. **Semiconductor ICs:** Intel 18A node (1.8 nm equivalent) using ASML's **NXE:3800E EUV scanner**.
2. **MEMS / NEMS devices:** Accelerometers, gyroscopes, microfluidic chips.
3. **Photonic crystals & metasurfaces:** Sub-wavelength patterning of optical devices.
4. **Flat-panel displays:** TFT backplane patterning on glass substrates.
5. **Biomedical microdevices:** Lab-on-chip, DNA microarrays, neural probes.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry data point (KTU often quotes this):** ASML's EUV scanner uses a **COŌéé laser-pulsed tin plasma** to generate 13.5 nm EUV light at ~250 W source power, costing ~$200 million per machine.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation ŌĆö Rayleigh's Resolution Criterion from Diffraction Theory

The minimum resolvable feature in an optical projection system is governed by the **diffraction of light through a circular aperture**. The Airy disk radius (first minimum) is:

$$r_{\text{Airy}} = 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

For two adjacent point-like features to be *just resolved* (Rayleigh criterion), the peak of one Airy disk must coincide with the first minimum of the other. For dense line/space patterns (as in integrated circuits), this converts to the **half-pitch** resolution:

$$R_{\text{half-pitch}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}\right)$$

Introducing the process-dependent factor $k_1$ to absorb the 0.61 constant and other process-dependent effects, we obtain:

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

This is **Rayleigh's resolution equation** ŌĆö the most important equation in optical lithography.

> [!NOTE]
> The $k_1$ factor encapsulates *everything* about the process that is *not* light or lens: photoresist chemistry, mask design (off-axis illumination, phase-shift masks), multiple patterning, optical proximity correction (OPC), and process control.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation ŌĆö Depth of Focus

Consider two axial points separated by a distance $\Delta z$ on either side of the focal plane. The wavefronts arriving at these points accumulate an additional optical path difference. The maximum allowable $\Delta z$ before defocus-induced image degradation becomes unacceptable is set by the requirement that the **wavefront error** not exceed $\lambda/4$ (the Marechal criterion).

The optical path difference between the marginal and chief rays at a defocus of $\Delta z$ is:

$$\Delta W = \Delta z \cdot \frac{\text{NA}^2}{2}$$

Setting $\Delta W = \lambda/4$:

$$\frac{\lambda}{4} = \Delta z \cdot \frac{\text{NA}^2}{2}$$

Solving for $\Delta z$:

$$\Delta z = \frac{\lambda}{2 \, \text{NA}^2}$$

Absorbing the factor of $1/2$ into the process-dependent constant $k_2$:

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example ŌĆö KTU Exam Style

**Problem (typical 7-mark KTU question):**

> An immersion lithography system uses water ($n = 1.44$) between the final lens element and the wafer. The numerical aperture is NA = 1.35, the exposure wavelength is $\lambda = 193 \text{ nm}$ (ArF excimer laser), and the process factor is $k_1 = 0.30$. Calculate:
> (a) the half-angle of the light cone $\theta$,
> (b) the minimum resolvable feature size $R$,
> (c) the depth of focus DOF (assume $k_2 = 0.5$).

### Solution

#### (a) Half-angle $\theta$

Using $\text{NA} = n \sin\theta$:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{NA}}{n} = \frac{1.35}{1.44}$$

$$\sin\theta = 0.9375$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.9375) = 69.65^\circ$$

#### (b) Minimum feature size $R$

$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} = 0.30 \cdot \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{1.35}$$

$$R = 0.30 \cdot 142.96 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 42.9 \text{ nm}}$$

#### (c) Depth of focus DOF

$$\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{(1.35)^2}$$

$$\text{DOF} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{1.8225} \text{ nm}$$

$$\text{DOF} = 0.5 \cdot 105.90 \text{ nm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} \approx 52.95 \text{ nm}}$$

### Interpretation

A 42.9 nm half-pitch resolution with only ~53 nm DOF ŌĆö meaning the wafer stage must control vertical position to within tens of nanometers. This is why EUV immersion systems use **ultra-flat wafer chucks, interferometric stage metrology**, and **active vibration isolation**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation ŌĆö Spin-Coating Film Thickness

The dynamics of spin coating are governed by the balance between **centrifugal force** (which drives the liquid outward) and **viscous shear** (which resists thinning). Newton's law of viscosity gives:

$$\eta \cdot \frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r \cdot h}{2}$$

where $v_r$ is radial velocity, $\rho$ is resist density, $\omega$ is angular velocity, $r$ is radial position, and $h$ is film thickness.

Integrating the continuity equation over time, with appropriate boundary conditions ($v_r = 0$ at the wafer surface), yields:

$$h(t) = \frac{h_0}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{4 \rho \omega^2}{3 \eta}\right) h_0^2 \, t}}$$

For long spin times ($t \to \infty$):

$$h_{\infty} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{t}} \cdot \left(\frac{\eta}{\rho \omega^2}\right)^{1/2}$$

This **1/sqrt(t)** dependence is the basis of the empirical K-parameters used in industrial spin curves.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix ŌĆö Lithography Technologies

| Technique | Wavelength | Resolution | NA | kŌéü | Cost | Status (2024) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact/Proximity** | UV (350ŌĆō450 nm) | ~2 ╬╝m | n/a | 1.0 | Low | Educational |
| **i-line (Hg)** | 365 nm | 350 nm | 0.65 | 0.6 | Moderate | Legacy |
| **KrF Excimer** | 248 nm | 130 nm | 0.8 | 0.5 | High | Mature |
| **ArF Dry** | 193 nm | 65 nm | 0.93 | 0.4 | High | Mature |
| **ArF Immersion** | 193 nm | 38 nm | 1.35 | 0.27 | Very high | Production |
| **EUV** | 13.5 nm | 13 nm | 0.33 | 0.4 | Extreme | Production |
| **EUV + High-NA** | 13.5 nm | 8 nm | 0.55 | 0.33 | Extreme | Ramp-up (2024ŌĆō2025) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's favorite question:** *"Compare ArF immersion and EUV lithography in terms of resolution, DOF, and infrastructure cost."* Use the table above as your anchor.

---

## 3.6 Multi-Patterning Techniques (For Sub-╬╗ Resolution)

When $k_1$ cannot be lowered further (single-exposure limit $k_1 = 0.25$), industry uses **multi-patterning**:

- **LELE** (Litho-Etch-Litho-Etch): Two separate litho+etch steps to *double* pattern density.
- **LFLE** (Litho-Freeze-Litho-Etch): Pattern is frozen (cured) before second exposure.
- **SADP / SAQP** (Self-Aligned Double/Quadruple Patterning): Uses spacers to multiply feature density ŌĆö used in **3 nm NAND flash** memory.
- **EUV + LELE hybrid**: Reduces total EUV steps required for sub-10 nm logic.

> Each multi-patterning step adds ~$2ŌĆō5 per wafer in cost, which is why the industry is aggressively adopting **High-NA EUV** (NA = 0.55).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow ŌĆö Photolithography Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["WAFER CLEANING - RCA process, organic + particle removal"]
    B["DEHYDRATION BAKE - Remove moisture, 150 to 200 C"]
    C["SPIN COATING - Dispense photoresist, spin at 2000 to 5000 RPM"]
    D["SOFT BAKE - Evaporate solvent, 90 to 120 C"]
    E["ALIGNMENT - Align mask to wafer fiducials"]
    F["UV EXPOSURE - Illuminate through photomask, 10 to 100 mJ per cm2"]
    G["POST EXPOSURE BAKE - Drive photochemical reaction, 90 to 130 C"]
    H["DEVELOPMENT - TMAH solution, 0.26 N, 60 s"]
    I["HARD BAKE - Harden pattern, 120 to 150 C"]
    J["PATTERN TRANSFER - Etch or implant underlying layer"]
    K["RESIST STRIP - Acetone or O2 plasma"]

    B --> C

```
