# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A ŌĆö Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023]`
**Define photolithography. List the major steps involved in the photolithographic process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Photolithography is a top-down nanofabrication technique used to transfer geometric patterns from a photomask onto a photoresist-coated substrate using UV light exposure, followed by chemical development.

**Major steps (list any 4):**
1. Substrate cleaning and dehydration bake
2. Photoresist application (spin coating)
3. Soft bake (pre-exposure bake)
4. Mask alignment and UV exposure
5. Post-exposure bake (PEB)
6. Development in TMAH solution
7. Hard bake
8. Pattern transfer (etching/doping) and resist strip

*Mark split: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for step listing.*

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2024]`
**Differentiate between positive and negative photoresists.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Positive Photoresist | Negative Photoresist |
|---|---|---|
| **Tone** | Exposed region dissolves in developer | Exposed region crosslinks and remains |
| **Final pattern** | Same as mask openings | Complement of mask openings |
| **Examples** | PMMA, DNQ-novolac | SU-8, AZ nLOF |
| **Resolution** | Higher (no swelling) | Lower (swelling during development) |
| **Etch resistance** | Lower | Higher |
| **Use case** | IC, fine-line patterning | MEMS, thick resists |

---

## 5.2 Part B ŌĆö Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts typically (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. The student answers ONE full question (either Q-A or Q-B).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain the Rayleigh criteria for resolution and depth of focus in photolithography. Derive the relevant expressions and discuss the strategies to improve resolution in modern semiconductor manufacturing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Rayleigh Resolution Criterion:**
The minimum feature size resolvable by an optical projection system is limited by diffraction. For a circular lens aperture, the Airy disk first-minimum radius is:

$$r_{\text{Airy}} = 0.61 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}$$

For dense periodic patterns (line/space arrays), the half-pitch resolution becomes:

$$\boxed{R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}}}$$

where $k_1$ is a process-dependent factor with theoretical minimum $k_1 = 0.25$ for single exposure.

**Depth of Focus Derivation:**
Starting from the Marechal criterion (acceptable wavefront error Ōēż $\lambda/4$) and the optical path difference at defocus $\Delta z$:

$$\Delta W = \frac{\Delta z \cdot \text{NA}^2}{2} = \frac{\lambda}{4}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2}}$$

with theoretical minimum $k_2 = 0.5$.

**Resolution Improvement Strategies (4 key approaches ŌĆö list with explanation):**

1. **Wavelength reduction:** Move from i-line (365 nm) ŌåÆ KrF (248 nm) ŌåÆ ArF (193 nm) ŌåÆ EUV (13.5 nm). The 27├Ś wavelength reduction from i-line to EUV is the primary driver of the lithography roadmap.

2. **Numerical aperture enhancement:** Use larger lens apertures and **immersion lithography** with high-index fluids (water, $n = 1.44$). Industry has reached NA = 1.35; High-NA EUV targets NA = 0.55.

3. **$k_1$ reduction via Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RET):**
   - **Off-axis illumination (OAI)**
   - **Phase-shift masks (PSM)**
   - **Optical proximity correction (OPC)**
   - **Multiple patterning** (LELE, SADP, SAQP)

4. **Advanced photoresists:** **Chemically amplified resists (CARs)** with photoacid generators (PAGs) provide high sensitivity and improved line-edge roughness.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**A photolithography system uses an ArF excimer laser source ($\lambda = 193$ nm) with a projection lens of NA = 0.85 in air ($n = 1.0$). The process factor is $k_1 = 0.40$. Calculate:**
**(i) The half-angle of the maximum cone of light collected by the lens.**
**(ii) The minimum resolvable feature size.**
**(iii) The depth of focus (assume $k_2 = 0.5$).**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Half-angle $\theta$:**
$$\sin\theta = \frac{\text{NA}}{n} = \frac{0.85}{1.0} = 0.85$$
$$\theta = \arcsin(0.85) = 58.21^\circ$$

**(ii) Resolution $R$:**
$$R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}} = 0.40 \cdot \frac{193 \text{ nm}}{0.85} = 0.40 \cdot 227.06 \text{ nm}$$
$$\boxed{R = 90.82 \text{ nm}}$$

**(iii) Depth of focus DOF:**
$$\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\text{NA}^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{(0.85)^2} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{193}{0.7225}$$
$$\text{DOF} = 0.5 \cdot 267.13 = 133.56 \text{ nm}$$
$$\boxed{\text{DOF} \approx 133.6 \text{ nm}}$$

*Conclusion:* This system resolves ~91 nm features with ~134 nm depth of focus ŌĆö typical of mid-2000s production-grade ArF dry lithography.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam ŌĆō June 2023, Model Paper]`

#### Part (a) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the different types of photoresists used in modern lithography. Compare chemically amplified resists with conventional DNQ-novolac resists.**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Photoresists:**

**1. Based on tone:**
- **Positive tone:** exposed regions become soluble in developer (DNQ-novolac, PMMA, polybutene sulfone).
- **Negative tone:** exposed regions crosslink and become insoluble (SU-8, AZ nLOF, polyisoprene with bisazide).

**2. Based on chemistry:**
- **Conventional (non-chemically-amplified):**
  - **DNQ-novolac:** Diazonaphthoquinone (DNQ) photoactive compound in a novolac resin matrix. Upon UV exposure, DNQ converts to indenecarboxylic acid, making the exposed region soluble in aqueous TMAH developer. Used for i-line and g-line lithography (350ŌĆō450 nm).
- **Chemically amplified resists (CARs):**
  - Contain a **photoacid generator (PAG)** which, upon exposure, releases a proton (HŌü║).
  - The acid catalyzes a cascade of chemical reactions (deprotection of t-BOC groups in KrF resists, cleavage of blocking groups in ArF resists).
  - **Single photon ŌåÆ single acid ŌåÆ many reactions ŌåÆ high sensitivity.**

**Comparison Table (DNQ-novolac vs CAR):**

| Parameter | DNQ-Novolac | Chemically Amplified (CAR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Wavelength** | i-line (365 nm) / g-line (436 nm) | KrF (248 nm), ArF (193 nm), EUV (13.5 nm) |
| **Sensitivity** | ~50ŌĆō200 mJ/cm┬▓ | ~10ŌĆō50 mJ/cm┬▓ (10ŌĆō100├Ś higher) |
| **Mechanism** | Direct photochemistry | Photoacid catalysis |
| **Post-exposure bake (PEB)** | Not strictly required | Critical (drives acid diffusion & reaction) |
| **Resolution** | ~350 nm (i-line) | Sub-30 nm achievable |
| **Line edge roughness** | Moderate | Higher (acid diffusion control critical) |
| **Environmental sensitivity** | Low | High (amine contamination causes T-topping) |

**Why CARs dominate modern lithography:**
The intrinsic photosensitivity of non-CAR materials is too low to provide practical throughput at DUV/EUV. CARs amplify a single photon event into thousands of chemical events, giving them 10ŌĆō100├Ś higher sensitivity ŌĆö a *prerequisite* for economical high-volume manufacturing at sub-100 nm nodes.

---

#### Part (b) ŌĆö 7 Marks
**Explain with neat sketches: (i) contact, (ii) proximity, and (iii) projection printing methods in photolithography. State their merits and demerits.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Contact Printing:**
- The photomask is placed **directly in physical contact** with the photoresist-coated wafer.
- A uniform UV flood exposure transfers the mask pattern 1:1 onto the wafer.

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| High resolution (~1 ╬╝m) | Mask damage due to contact ŌĆö short mask lifetime |
| Simple, low-cost equipment | Particle contamination causes defects |
| 1:1 pattern transfer (no optics required) | Limited to small-area wafers in research |

**(ii) Proximity Printing:**
- A small **gap** (typically 10ŌĆō50 ╬╝m) is maintained between the mask and the wafer.
- Light diffracts through the gap, slightly degrading resolution but preventing mask damage.

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| Mask lifetime extended | Lower resolution than contact due to diffraction |
| Reduced defect density | Resolution: $R \approx \sqrt{\lambda \cdot g}$ where $g$ = gap |

**(iii) Projection Printing (Stepper/Scanner):**
- A **demagnifying projection lens** (typically 4├Ś or 5├Ś reduction) images the mask onto the wafer.
- Industry standard for sub-micron to sub-10 nm patterning.

| Merits | Demerits |
|---|---|
| High resolution (sub-30 nm with EUV) | Extremely expensive equipment (~$150ŌĆō200M per EUV scanner) |
| Long mask lifetime (no contact) | Complex optics with multilayer Mo/Si mirrors |
| Large-area stepping possible | Requires sophisticated alignment metrology |
| 4├ŚŌĆō5├Ś reduction relaxes mask fabrication specs | Throughput limited by stage acceleration limits |

**Resolution scaling for proximity printing** (parallel plate diffraction):

$$R \approx 1.5 \cdot \sqrt{\lambda \cdot g + \left(\frac{\lambda}{2 \cdot \text{NA}}\right)^2}$$

This shows that as gap $g$ increases, resolution degrades due to Fresnel diffraction ŌĆö explaining why **contact/proximity printing is unsuitable for sub-micron features**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks in Photolithography questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $k_1$ values:** The theoretical minimum is $k_1 = 0.25$ for single exposure. Real-world processes use $k_1 = 0.4$ŌĆō$0.5$. Specifying the wrong value yields incorrect resolution numbers.
>
> 2. **Mixing up resolution and DOF dependencies:** Resolution scales as $\lambda / \text{NA}$ (linear in NA), but DOF scales as $\lambda / \text{NA}^2$ (quadratic in NA). Many students incorrectly apply the same scaling to both.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $n$ factor for immersion systems:** When water ($n = 1.44$) is used, NA = $n \sin\theta$, not $\sin\theta$. The half-angle $\theta$ is *smaller* in immersion than in dry for the same NA, but the NA value itself is higher.
>
> 4. **Writing the equation in markdown with literal pipes:** Writing $\vert x \vert$ using vertical bars inside a markdown table breaks the table formatting. Always use LaTeX `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` inside math mode.
>
> 5. **Tone confusion in positive vs negative resist questions:** Always state *which region* dissolves. Positive = exposed dissolves. Negative = exposed remains.
>
> 6. **Skipping the derivation of DOF:** Many students state the DOF formula without deriving it from the $\lambda/4$ Marechal criterion. KTU examiners award at least 2ŌĆō3 marks for the derivation steps.
>
> 7. **Spelling:** "Photolithography" (not "Photolitography" or "Photolythography"). "Photoresist" (one word). "Chemically amplified" (not "Chemicaly").
>
> 8. **Missing units in numerical answers:** Always specify nm for resolution, ╬╝m or nm for DOF, ┬░ for angles, and mJ/cm┬▓ for exposure dose.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap ŌĆö Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photolithography ŌĆö High-Density Topic Recap (revise in <5 minutes)**

- [x] **Definition:** Photolithography is a top-down pattern-transfer technique using UV light through a photomask onto a photoresist.
- [x] **Eight sequential steps:** Clean ŌåÆ Dehydrate ŌåÆ Spin coat ŌåÆ Soft bake ŌåÆ Align+Expose ŌåÆ PEB ŌåÆ Develop ŌåÆ Hard bake ŌåÆ Pattern transfer ŌåÆ Strip.
- [x] **Rayleigh Resolution:** $R = k_1 \cdot \lambda / \text{NA}$, with $k_1^{\min} = 0.25$.
- [x] **Depth of Focus:** $\text{DOF} = k_2 \cdot \lambda / \text{NA}^2$, with $k_2^{\min} = 0.5$.
- [x] **Numerical Aperture:** $\text{NA} = n \sin\theta$. Immersion with water ($n = 1.44$) pushes NA to 1.35.
- [x] **Positive resist:** exposed region dissolves ŌåÆ final pattern matches mask open regions.
- [x] **Negative resist:** exposed region crosslinks and remains ŌåÆ final pattern is complement of mask.
- [x] **Chemically Amplified Resists (CARs):** PAG releases HŌü║ ŌåÆ acid catalyzes deprotection ŌåÆ 10ŌĆō100├Ś sensitivity boost ŌåÆ enables DUV/EUV.
- [x] **Exposure modes:** Contact, Proximity, Projection (industry standard). Projection uses 4├Ś/5├Ś demagnifying lens.
- [x] **Resolution roadmap:** i-line (365 nm) ŌåÆ KrF (248 nm) ŌåÆ ArF dry (193 nm) ŌåÆ ArF immersion (193 nm, NA 1.35) ŌåÆ EUV (13.5 nm) ŌåÆ High-NA EUV (NA 0.55).
- [x] **Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RET):** Off-axis illumination, Phase-shift masks, OPC, Multi-patterning (LELE, SADP, SAQP).
- [x] **EUV lithography:** $\lambda = 13.5$ nm, generated by COŌéé laser-pulsed tin plasma, multilayer Mo/Si mirrors, enables sub-13 nm half-pitch.
- [x] **Developer:** TMAH (0.26 N) is industry standard for positive resist.
- [x] **Typical exposure dose:** 10ŌĆō100 mJ/cm┬▓.
- [x] **Photoresist thickness:** 0.5ŌĆō2 ╬╝m for standard processes; SU-8 can reach 100s of ╬╝m.
- [x] **Trade-off:** Shorter wavelength + Higher NA ŌåÆ better resolution but worse (smaller) DOF.
- [x] **Why photolithography matters:** It is the foundational process for the entire $500+ billion semiconductor industry.
